topics.title: Dynamic Testing Principles
topics.html:
[!info]
Dynamic hormone testing uses stimulation or suppression to reveal endocrine reserve, autonomy, or resistance that baseline measurements cannot. Mastering protocols, cut-offs, and interpretation is core FCPS/MRCP knowledge.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Select the correct dynamic test for a given clinical question
  • [ ] Apply standard protocols (dose, timing, sampling)
  • [ ] Interpret results using validated cut-offs
  • [ ] Identify false positives/negatives and confounding factors
  • [ ] Explain the physiological basis of each test

2. Principles of Dynamic Testing

Test TypePhysiologyWhen to Use
StimulationProvocative agent → Hormone release → Tests reserve/capacitySuspected deficiency (e.g., GH, cortisol, gonadotropins)
SuppressionInhibitory agent → Hormone suppression → Tests autonomySuspected excess/autonomy (e.g., Cushing, acromegaly, autonomous nodules)
ProvocationPhysiological stressor → Hormone responsePhysiological reserve testing (e.g., hypoglycaemia for GH/cortisol)
Replacement/WithdrawalHormone given/withdrawn → Observe axis responseDiagnostic clarification (e.g., thyroxine suppression historical)

3. Standard Stimulation Tests

1. Synacthen (ACTH) Test — Adrenal Reserve

ParameterStandard Test (250µg)Low-Dose Test (1µg)
Dose250µg tetracosactide IM/IV1µg tetracosactide IV
IndicationGeneral screening (high sensitivity)Secondary AI (more physiological)
SamplingBaseline, 30min, 60minBaseline, 20min, 30min, 60min
Normal ResponseCortisol ≥500-550 nmol/L (18-20 µg/dL) at 30/60minSame cut-off
Adrenal InsufficiencyCortisol <400-500 nmol/L at 30/60minSame cut-off
LimitationsSupraphysiological; may miss partial AIMore sensitive for secondary AI; technically demanding

Interpretation:

  • Cortisol <100 nmol/L at baseline + <400 at 30/60min = Adrenal Insufficiency
  • Cortisol >500 nmol/L at 30/60min = Normal adrenal reserve
  • Indeterminate (100-500 nmol/L) = Repeat or low-dose test

Contraindications: Known severe AI (give hydrocortisone first), acute illness.

2. GH Stimulation Tests — GH Deficiency

TestProtocolCut-off (Peak GH)Indication
Insulin Tolerance Test (ITT)IV Insulin 0.1 U/kg → Hypoglycaemia <2.2 mmol/L<3 µg/L (adults); <5 µg/L (children)Gold standard; Tests GH + cortisol simultaneously
GlucagonGlucagon 1mg IM/IV → Sampling 0, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180min<3 µg/L (adults)Safer than ITT; Preferred if ITT contraindicated
ArginineArginine 0.5g/kg IV over 30min<0.4 µg/L (children)Alternative
ClonidineClonidine 0.15mg/m² (max 0.25mg) PO<6.8 µg/LHistorical
GHRH + ArginineGHRH 1µg/kg + Arginine<9 µg/L (adults)Most sensitive for adult GHD

ITT Protocol — Gold Standard:

  1. Fast overnight, IV access, ECG monitoring
  2. Baseline: Glucose, GH, Cortisol
  3. Insulin 0.1 U/kg IV bolus
  4. Sampling: q15-30min for 2h (Glucose, GH, Cortisol)
  5. Target: Glucose <2.2 mmol/L (symptomatic hypoglycaemia)
  6. Recovery: IV Glucose + monitors

Contraindications for ITT: IHD, Cerebrovascular disease, Epilepsy, Severe hypopituitarism (cortisol <100), Age >60, Pregnancy.

3. Gonadotropin Stimulation Tests

TestProtocolInterpretation
GnRH TestGnRH 100µg IV/SC → LH/FSH at 0, 20, 40, 60, 90, 120minPrepubertal: FSH > LH; Puberty: LH > FSH; PCOS: LH > FSH (2:1); Hypogonadotropic: Blunted
hCG Stimulation (Male)hCG 1500-5000 IU IM × 3-5 days → Testosterone at 24h post-lastTestosterone rise >1.5x baseline = Leydig cell response; No rise = Primary testicular failure
Clomiphene ChallengeClomiphene 100mg/day × 5 days → FSH/LH/Oestradiol day 3 & day 10Ovarian reserve: ΔOestradiol > 400 pmol/L = Good reserve

4. Thyroid Axis Stimulation

TestProtocolInterpretation
TRH StimulationTRH 200-500µg IV → TSH at 0, 20, 40, 60, 90, 120minHyperthyroidism: Blunted TSH; Primary Hypo: Exaggerated/prolonged TSH; Central Hypo: Blunted/delayed; TSH-oma: Paradoxical rise
Recombinant Human TSH (rhTSH)rhTSH 0.9mg IM × 2 days → Radioiodine uptake / ThyroglobulinThyroid remnant ablation prep; Diagnostic scanning

5. Adrenal Steroid Stimulation — CAH Diagnosis

TestProtocolInterpretation
ACTH Stimulation (21-OH Def)Synacthen 250µg → 17-OH Progesterone, 11-Deoxycortisol, Androstenedione at 0, 30, 60min17-OHP >30 nmol/L (1000 ng/dL) = 21-OH deficiency; >100 = Severe
ACTH Stimulation (11β-OH Def)Synacthen 250µg → 11-Deoxycortisol, DOC at 0, 60min11-Deoxycortisol ↑↑
ACTH Stimulation (17α-OH Def)Synacthen 250µg → 17-OH Pregnenolone, 17-OH ProgesteroneBoth ↑↑

4. Standard Suppression Tests

1. Dexamethasone Suppression Tests — Cushing Syndrome

Low-Dose DST (LDDST) — Screening

ProtocolOvernight (1mg at 23:00 → Cortisol 09:00) OR 48h (0.5mg q6h × 8 doses → Cortisol 6h post-last)
Cut-offCortisol <50 nmol/L (1.8 µg/dL) = Normal suppression
Positive (Non-suppression)Cortisol ≥50 nmol/L → Proceed to HDDST

High-Dose DST (HDDST) — Differential Diagnosis

Protocol48h: 2mg q6h × 8 doses (or overnight 8mg at 23:00)
Cushing Disease (Pituitary ACTH)Cortisol suppresses >50% (or <50% of baseline)
Ectopic ACTH / Adrenal CushingNo suppression (<50% suppression)
False PositivesObesity, Depression, Alcohol, Drugs (Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Rifampicin) → Induce CYP3A4 → Faster dexamethasone clearance

DST Interpretation Summary

ResultInterpretation
LDDST Suppressed (<50)Normal (No Cushing)
LDDST Not Suppressed + HDDST SuppressedCushing Disease (Pituitary ACTH)
LDDST Not Suppressed + HDDST Not SuppressedEctopic ACTH or Adrenal Cushing → Check ACTH level

2. OGTT for GH Suppression — Acromegaly

Protocol75g Glucose PO → GH at 0, 30, 60, 90, 120min
NormalGH suppressed <1 µg/L at 60-120min
AcromegalyGH not suppressed ≥1 µg/L (gold standard)
Partial SuppressionGH falls but >1 µg/L = Active acromegaly

3. Thyroid Hormone Suppression (Historical)

TestProtocolReplaced By
T3 Suppression TestT3 25µg q12h × 10 days → RAI uptakeTRH Stimulation / TRAb / RAI Scan
T4 SuppressionT4 100µg/day × 7 daysSame

4. Fludrocortisone Suppression — Primary Aldosteronism

ProtocolFludrocortisone 0.1mg q6h × 4 days + High salt diet → Aldosterone, Renin, K+
PositiveAldosterone not suppressed (remains >150 pmol/L)

5. Saline Infusion Test — Primary Aldosteronism

Protocol2L 0.9% NaCl IV over 4h → Aldosterone, Renin
PositiveAldosterone >140 pmol/L (or >280 pmol/L per some guidelines) after infusion

5. Provocative / Physiological Stress Tests

1. Insulin Tolerance Test (ITT) — Gold Standard for GH + Cortisol Reserve

AspectDetails
PhysiologyHypoglycaemia → Stress → CRH → ACTH → Cortisol + GHRH → GH
ProtocolInsulin 0.1 U/kg IV → Target glucose <2.2 mmol/L
Cortisol Cut-offPeak >500-550 nmol/L = Normal
GH Cut-offPeak >3 µg/L (adults) = Normal
ContraindicationsIHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Severe hypopituitarism, Age >60, Pregnancy

2. Water Deprivation Test — Diabetes Insipidus

PhaseProtocol
Fluid DeprivationNPO + Fluid restriction → Weight, Serum/Urine Osmolality q1-2h until ↑ >5% weight loss or Urine Osm > Plasma Osm
Desmopressin ChallengeDDAVP 2µg SC/IV → Urine Osmolality at 60min
Central DIUrine Osm ↑ >50% after DDAVP
Nephrogenic DINo response to DDAVP
Primary PolydipsiaUrine Osm concentrates during deprivation (no need for DDAVP)

3. Solute Infusion Test — Aldosteronism

See Fludrocortisone Suppression / Saline Infusion above.

6. Replacement / Withdrawal Tests

TestProtocolUse
Thyroxine Withdrawal + rhTSHStop T4 × 4-6wks OR rhTSH 0.9mg IM × 2 daysPre-ablation scanning; Avoid hypothyroidism
Thyroxine Suppression (Historical)T4 100µg/day × 7d → RAI uptakeObsolete
Testosterone/Estrogen ChallengePrime with sex steroid → GnRH testDelayed puberty assessment

7. Specialised Dynamic Tests

1. Secretin Stimulation — Gastrinoma (ZES)

ProtocolSecretin 2 U/kg IV → Gastrin at 0, 2, 5, 10, 15, 30min
PositiveGastrin rise >120 pg/mL (or >200 pg/mL from baseline)
MEN1 Association20-25% of gastrinomas

2. Calcium Stimulation (SACI) — Insulinoma Localisation

ProtocolSelective arterial calcium injection → Hepatic venous insulin sampling
PositiveInsulin step-up >2x baseline in hepatic vein draining tumour region

3. Arginine Stimulation — Glucagonoma/GH

ProtocolArginine 5g IV (children) / 30g IV (adults) → Glucagon/GH
GlucagonomaGlucagon rise >2x baseline

4. Lupron/GnRH Agonist Stimulation — Gonadotropin-independent Precocious Puberty

ProtocolLeuprolide 0.2mg/kg SC → LH/FSH at 1-2h
PositiveLH >5 IU/L (pubertal response)

8. Common Pitfalls & Confounders

TestPitfallCorrection
DSTDrugs inducing CYP3A4 (Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Rifampicin, St John's Wort) → Faster dexamethasone clearance → False non-suppressionCheck medications; Use CYP3A4-independent confirmatory tests (late-night salivary cortisol, 24h UFC)
DSTObesity, Depression, Alcohol → False non-suppressionConfirm with 24h UFC + Late-night salivary cortisol
DSTPregnancy, OCP → ↑ CBG → ↑ Total cortisol (free normal) → False non-suppression if total cortisol usedUse free cortisol (salivary) or 24h UFC
SynacthenEarly secondary AI may pass 250µg testUse Low-dose (1µg) Synacthen
ITTCounter-regulatory failure in long-standing hypopituitarismUse Glucagon test instead
GH StimulationObesity → Blunted GH response (false positive GHD)Use BMI-adjusted cut-offs; Confirm with IGF-1
TRH TestDopamine agonists, Dopamine antagonists affect TSHStop drugs 48h prior
Water DeprivationPrimary polydipsia can mimic partial DIDesmopressin response + Clinical context
SynacthenOestrogen/OCP → ↑ CBG → ↑ Total cortisol (free normal)Measure Free cortisol (salivary) or 24h UFC

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Synacthen standard dose250µg IM/IV; Cortisol ≥500-550 nmol/L at 30/60min = Normal
Synacthen low-dose1µg; More sensitive for secondary AI; Technically demanding
ITTGold standard for GH + cortisol; Insulin 0.1 U/kg → Glucose <2.2; Contraindicated: IHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Age >60
GH cut-off (ITT)Peak GH <3 µg/L = GHD (adults); <5 µg/L (children)
LDDST1mg overnight or 0.5mg q6h × 48h; Cortisol <50 nmol/L = Normal suppression
HDDST2mg q6h × 48h; >50% suppression = Cushing Disease; No suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal
OGTT for Acromegaly75g glucose; GH <1 µg/L at 60-120min = Normal; ≥1 = Acromegaly
ITT ContraindicationsIHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Severe hypopituitarism, Age >60, Pregnancy
Water DeprivationCentral DI: DDAVP → Urine Osm ↑ >50%; Nephrogenic: No response; Polydipsia: Concentrates
Water Deprivation StopWeight loss >5% or Urine Osm > Plasma Osm or Haemodynamic instability
Synacthen for CAH17-OHP >30 nmol/L (1000 ng/dL) = 21-OH deficiency
Drugs affecting DSTEnzyme inducers (Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Rifampicin) → False non-suppression
DST in Pregnancy/OCP↑ CBG → ↑ Total cortisol; Use Salivary cortisol or 24h UFC
GH Stimulation in ObesityBlunted GH → False positive GHD; Use BMI-adjusted cut-offs + IGF-1

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
LDDST vs HDDSTLDDST = Screening (is it Cushing?); HDDST = Differential (Pituitary vs Ectopic/Adrenal)
Synacthen 250µg vs 1µg250µg = Standard screening; 1µg = More sensitive for secondary/early AI
GH StimulationITT = Gold standard but dangerous; Glucagon = Safer alternative; GHRH+Arg = Most sensitive for adults
DST FailureMost common cause = Drugs (enzyme inducers); Obesity/Depression/Alcohol also
Cortisol Cut-offsDST: <50 nmol/L; Synacthen: ≥500-550 nmol/L; ITT: >500-550 nmol/L; Morning: 200-700 nmol/L
ITT vs GlucagonITT tests cortisol + GH simultaneously; Glucagon safer but only GH well-validated
OGTT for AcromegalyOnly validated suppression test for acromegaly; GH <1 µg/L = Normal

Mnemonic: DYNAMIC TESTS BY AXIS

  • HPA: Synacthen, ITT, DST (LD/HD), Water Deprivation
  • HPT: TRH stim, TSH suppression (historical)
  • HPG: GnRH, hCG stim, Clomiphene challenge
  • GH/IGF-1: ITT, Glucagon, Arginine, GHRH+Arg, OGTT (suppression)
  • Adrenal/Steroid: Synacthen (CAH), DST, Saline/Fludro suppression (Conn)
  • Insulinoma: 72h Fast, Calcium stimulation (SACI)
  • Gastrinoma: Secretin stimulation
  • DI: Water Deprivation + DDAVP
  • Insulinoma: 72h Fast, SACI

11. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Dynamic Testing))

Stimulation (Deficiency)

HPA: Synacthen (250µg/1µg), ITT

GH: ITT (Gold), Glucagon, Arginine, GHRH+Arg

HPG: GnRH, hCG, Clomiphene

Thyroid: TRH, rhTSH

CAH: ACTH stim (17-OHP, 11-Deoxycortisol)

Suppression (Excess/Autonomy)

Cushing: LDDST (Screen) → HDDST (Diff)

Acromegaly: OGTT (GH suppression)

Conn: Saline Infusion, Fludrocortisone

Historical: T3/T4 suppression

Provocation/Physiological

ITT: Hypoglycaemia → GH + Cortisol

Water Deprivation + DDAVP → DI

Localization

SACI (Insulinoma), Secretin (Gastrinoma), Calcium (Insulinoma)

Pitfalls

DST: Enzyme inducers, Obesity, Pregnancy/OCP

Synacthen: 1µg for secondary AI

GH tests: Obesity blunts GH

ITT: Contraindicated (IHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Age>60)

DST in Pregnancy/OCP: Use Salivary/24h UFC

```

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
Synacthen250µg: Cortisol ≥500-550 at 30/60 = Normal; 1µg for secondary AI
ITTGold Standard GH+Cortisol; Insulin 0.1U/kg → Glucose <2.2; Contra: IHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Age>60
GH Cut-offsITT: <3 µg/L (adults); Glucagon: <3; GHRH+Arg: <9; BMI-adjusted if obese
LDDST1mg overnight; Cortisol <50 = Normal; ≥50 = Cushing possible
HDDST2mg q6h × 48h; >50% suppression = Cushing Disease; No suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal
OGTT Acromegaly75g glucose; GH <1 µg/L at 60-120 = Normal; ≥1 = Acromegaly
Water DeprivationCentral DI: DDAVP → Urine Osm ↑>50%; Nephrogenic: No response; Polydipsia: Concentrates
CAH Stimulation17-OHP >30 nmol/L (1000 ng/dL) = 21-OH deficiency
ITT ContraindicationsIHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Severe hypopit, Age>60, Pregnancy
DST PitfallsEnzyme inducers (Phenytoin, Rifampicin); Obesity; Depression; Alcohol; Pregnancy/OCP → Use Salivary/24h UFC

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

16. MCQs (10)

A. >=500-550 nmol/L

B. >=400 nmol/L

C. >=600 nmol/L

D. >=300 nmol/L

A. Cortisol <400-500 nmol/L at 30/60 min

B. Cortisol <300 nmol/L

C. Cortisol <200 nmol/L

D. Cortisol <600 nmol/L

  1. Low-dose (1 mcg) Synacthen used for?

A. Suspected secondary/tertiary AI (more physiological)

B. Primary AI screening

C. Cushing syndrome

D. CAH diagnosis

  1. Overnight 1 mg DST: cortisol <50 nmol/L?

A. Excludes Cushing

B. Diagnoses Cushing

C. Suggests Cushing

D. Inconclusive

  1. High-dose (8 mg) DST:

A. Suppresses pituitary (Cushing disease) not ectopic/adrenal

B. Suppresses all Cushing

C. Only suppresses ectopic

  1. OGTT for acromegaly: GH suppresses to?

A. <1 µg/L (or <0.4 µg/L modern assays)

B. <5 µg/L

C. <10 µg/L

  1. Water deprivation: in central DI after desmopressin?

B. Urine osmolality does not change

D. Serum sodium normalises

A. No significant rise in urine osmolality

B. Rise >50%

C. Rise >100%

D. Hyponatraemia

  1. TRH test: primary hypothyroidism TSH response?

A. Exaggerated and prolonged

B. Blunted

C. Absent

D. Normal

  1. ITT for GH/cortisol: hypoglycaemia target?

A. <2.2 mmol/L

B. <3.0 mmol/L

C. <3.5 mmol/L

D. <4.0 mmol/L

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Suspected secondary AI. Best dynamic test?

A. Low-dose (1 mcg) Synacthen test

B. Standard 250 mcg Synacthen

C. ITT

D. Overnight DST

E. Glucagon stim test

  1. Suspected acromegaly, IGF-1 elevated. Confirmatory?

A. OGTT with GH suppression

B. Random GH

C. GHRH test

D. IGFBP-3

E. TRH test

  1. Polyuria: serum osmolality 300, urine 150. Post water deprivation urine 600. Post DDAVP 620. Diagnosis?

A. Nephrogenic DI

B. Central DI

C. Primary polydipsia

D. SIADH

E. Partial central DI

  1. Suspected Cushing: overnight DST cortisol 120 nmol/L. Next step?

A. 24h UFC + midnight salivary cortisol

B. High-dose DST

C. CRH test

D. IPSS

E. Adrenal CT

  1. Cushing disease confirmed. Differentiate pituitary vs ectopic?

A. High-dose DST (suppresses pituitary) or CRH test / IPSS

B. MRI only

C. ACTH level only

D. Adrenal CT

E. 24h UFC

  1. Suspected primary hyperparathyroidism. Best initial test?

A. PTH + adjusted calcium

B. 24h urinary calcium

C. PTHrP

D. Vitamin D

E. Phosphate

  1. Suspected phaeochromocytoma. Best biochemical test?

A. Plasma free metanephrines (or 24h urinary metanephrines)

B. Plasma catecholamines

C. VMA

D. Clonidine suppression test

E. Saline infusion test

  1. Suspected SIADH. Euvolaemic, hyponatraemic, urine osmolality >100, urine Na+ >20. Confirm?

A. Normal thyroid/adrenal/pituitary + exclude diuretics

B. Water deprivation test

C. DDAVP test

D. Serum osmolality >275

E. Serum uric acid

  1. Suspected acromegaly. Random GH 8 µg/L, IGF-1 elevated. Diagnosis?

A. Confirmed (no OGTT needed if IGF-1 >1.3x ULN + clinical)

B. Needs OGTT

C. Needs GHRH test

D. Needs MRI only

E. Needs TRH test

  1. Suspected central DI. Water dep → urine osmolality 400 → DDAVP → 750. Diagnosis?

A. Central DI

B. Nephrogenic DI

C. Primary polydipsia

D. Partial central DI

E. SIADH

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Synacthen 250 mcg

A: Screening AI; Normal: Cortisol ≥500-550 at 30/60 min; AI: <400-500

  • Q: Synacthen 1 mcg

A: More sensitive for secondary/tertiary AI; Same cut-offs

  • Q: Overnight 1 mg DST

A: Screening Cushing; Normal: Cortisol <50 nmol/L; Cushing: Not suppressed

  • Q: High-dose 8 mg DST (48h)

A: Cushing disease: Suppresses cortisol >50%; Ectopic/Adrenal: No suppression

  • Q: OGTT acromegaly

A: 75g glucose; Normal: GH <1 µg/L; Acromegaly: No suppression (paradoxical rise)

  • Q: Water deprivation

A: Central DI: No concentrate → DDAVP → ↑Uosm >50%; Nephrogenic: No DDAVP response; Primary polydipsia: Some concentration

  • Q: TRH test

A: Primary hypothyroidism: Exaggerated TSH; Secondary: Blunted; Hyperthyroidism: No rise

  • Q: ITT

A: Gold standard for GH reserve & HPA; Target glucose <2.2 mmol/L; GH rise >5-7 µg/L; Cortisol rise >500-550

  • Q: Glucagon stim test

A: Alternative to ITT; GH & cortisol rise; Safer in elderly/cardiac

  • Q: Clonidine suppression

A: Phaeochromocytoma: Normal ↓catecholamines; Phaeo: No suppression

  • Q: Saline infusion test

A: Primary aldosteronism: Aldosterone not suppressed; Normal: Aldosterone suppressed

  • Q: Captopril challenge

A: Renovascular HTN screening; Not endocrine dynamic test per se

  • Q: DDAVP test

A: Central DI: Urine osmol ↑ >50%; Nephrogenic: No change

  • Q: CRH test

A: Cushing disease: ACTH rise >35-50%; Ectopic: No rise

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. >=500-550 nmol/L — Synacthen 250 mcg normal cortisol at 30 min?
  1. Cortisol <400-500 nmol/L at 30/60 min — Synacthen diagnostic of AI?
  1. Suspected secondary/tertiary AI (more physiological) — Low-dose (1 mcg) Synacthen used for?
  1. Excludes Cushing — Overnight 1 mg DST: cortisol <50 nmol/L?
  1. Suppresses pituitary (Cushing disease) not ectopic/adrenal — High-dose (8 mg) DST:
  1. <1 µg/L (or <0.4 µg/L modern assays) — OGTT for acromegaly: GH suppresses to?
  1. Urine osmolality increases >50% — Water deprivation: in central DI after desmopressin?
  1. No significant rise in urine osmolality — Nephrogenic DI: desmopressin causes?
  1. Exaggerated and prolonged — TRH test: primary hypothyroidism TSH response?
  1. <2.2 mmol/L — ITT for GH/cortisol: hypoglycaemia target?

SBAs

  1. Low-dose (1 mcg) Synacthen test — Suspected secondary AI. Best dynamic test?
  1. OGTT with GH suppression — Suspected acromegaly, IGF-1 elevated. Confirmatory?
  1. Nephrogenic DI — Polyuria: serum osmolality 300, urine 150. Post water deprivation urine 600. Post DDAVP 620. Diagnosis?
  1. 24h UFC + midnight salivary cortisol — Suspected Cushing: overnight DST cortisol 120 nmol/L. Next step?
  1. High-dose DST (suppresses pituitary) or CRH test / IPSS — Cushing disease confirmed. Differentiate pituitary vs ectopic?
  1. PTH + adjusted calcium — Suspected primary hyperparathyroidism. Best initial test?
  1. Plasma free metanephrines (or 24h urinary metanephrines) — Suspected phaeochromocytoma. Best biochemical test?
  1. Normal thyroid/adrenal/pituitary + exclude diuretics — Suspected SIADH. Euvolaemic, hyponatraemic, urine osmolality >100, urine Na+ >20. Confirm?
  1. Confirmed (no OGTT needed if IGF-1 >1.3x ULN + clinical) — Suspected acromegaly. Random GH 8 µg/L, IGF-1 elevated. Diagnosis?
  1. Central DI — Suspected central DI. Water dep → urine osmolality 400 → DDAVP → 750. Diagnosis?

topics.title: Endocrine Investigation Algorithms
topics.html:
[!info]
A systematic algorithmic approach is the standard of care in endocrinology. This note provides the step-by-step flowcharts used in clinical practice to move from clinical suspicion → biochemical confirmation → dynamic testing → imaging → genetic testing → MDT discussion.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply the universal endocrine investigation algorithm (Paired Hormone → Feedback Logic → Dynamic Test → Imaging → Genetics)
  • [ ] Execute specific algorithms for the 12 most common endocrine presentations
  • [ ] Avoid common investigation pitfalls (wrong test, wrong timing, wrong interpretation)
  • [ ] Structure a viva-ready investigation plan for any endocrine scenario

2. The Universal Endocrine Investigation Algorithm

```

CLINICAL SUSPICION OF ENDOCRINE DISORDER

│

▼

STEP 1: PAIRED HORMONE MEASUREMENT

• Measure TARGET HORMONE + TROPIC HORMONE simultaneously

• Examples: TSH + fT4, ACTH + Cortisol, LH/FSH + Testosterone/Oestradiol, PTH + Calcium

│

▼

STEP 2: FEEDBACK LOGIC ANALYSIS

│

├── PRIMARY DISORDER: Target Hormone ABNORMAL + Tropic Hormone OPPOSITE direction

│ (e.g., High T4 + Low TSH = Primary Hyperthyroidism)

│

├── SECONDARY DISORDER: Target Hormone LOW + Tropic Hormone LOW/INAPPROPRIATELY NORMAL

│ (e.g., Low T4 + Low/Normal TSH = Central Hypothyroidism)

│

└── TERTIARY DISORDER: Same as secondary but RELEASING HORMONE LOW (steroid withdrawal, hypothalamic lesion)

│

▼

STEP 3: DYNAMIC TESTING (if baseline equivocal or need confirmation)

├── DEFICIENCY SUSPECTED → STIMULATION TEST

│ (Synacthen for AI, ITT/Glucagon for GHD, GnRH for Hypogonadism, TRH for Central Hypo)

│

├── EXCESS/AUTONOMY SUSPECTED → SUPPRESSION TEST

│ (LDDST/HDDST for Cushing, OGTT for Acromegaly, Saline/Fludro for Conn)

│

└── DI/WEAKNESS → PROVOCATION TEST

(Water Deprivation + DDAVP, ITT for GH/Cortisol)

│

▼

STEP 4: LOCALISATION IMAGING

• Pituitary: MRI Pituitary (with gadolinium, 3mm slices)

• Adrenal: CT Abdomen (adrenal protocol, 1mm slices, washout)

• Thyroid: US Thyroid (TI-RADS) → FNA (Bethesda)

• Adrenal (functioning): Adrenal Venous Sampling (AVS) for Conn

• NET: ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (gold standard)

│

▼

STEP 5: GENETIC TESTING (if syndromic, familial, young onset)

• MEN1, RET, MEN1, CDKN1B, CaSR, GNAS, GNAS1, KCNJ11, ABCC8, etc.

│

▼

STEP 6: MDT DISCUSSION

• Endocrinologist, Surgeon, Radiologist, Pathologist, Geneticist, Oncologist (if malignant)

│

▼

TREATMENT PLAN + SURVEILLANCE SCHEDULE

```

3. Presentation-Specific Algorithms

Algorithm 1: HYPERTHYROIDISM

```

Clinical Thyrotoxicosis (Symptoms + Signs)

│

▼

TSH + fT4 + fT3

│

├── TSH SUPPRESSED + fT4/fT3 ↑ → PRIMARY HYPERTHYROIDISM

│ │

│ ├── TRAb POSITIVE → GRAVES DISEASE

│ │ Management: Carbimazole/PTU → RAI or Surgery

│ │

│ ├── TRAb NEGATIVE + Thyroid US + RAI Uptake

│ │ ├── Diffuse ↑ uptake → GRAVES (Seronegative)

│ │ ├── Focal ↑ uptake → TOXIC ADENOMA

│ │ └── Multifocal ↑ uptake → TOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITRE

│ │

│ ├── LOW RAI Uptake + Painful Thyroid → SUBACUTE THYROIDITIS

│ ├── LOW RAI Uptake + Painless + Postpartum → POSTPARTUM THYROIDITIS

│ ├── LOW RAI Uptake + Amiodarone/Lithium → DRUG-INDUCED

│ └── LOW RAI Uptake + No Pain → SILENT THYROIDITIS / FACTITIOUS

│

└── TSH NORMAL/HIGH + fT4/fT3 ↑ → SECONDARY HYPERTHYROIDISM (TSH-OMA)

TSH NORMAL/HIGH + fT4/fT3 ↑ + Pituitary MRI → TSH-SECRETING ADENOMA

```

Algorithm 2: HYPOTHYROIDISM

```

Symptoms + Signs of Hypothyroidism

│

▼

TSH + fT4

│

├── TSH HIGH + fT4 LOW → PRIMARY HYPOTHYROIDISM

│ ├── Anti-TPO/TgAb POSITIVE → HASHIMOTO THYROIDITIS

│ ├── Post-thyroidectomy / Post-RAI → IATROGENIC

│ ├── Iodine Deficiency / Excess → NUTRITIONAL

│ └── Drug-Induced (Lithium, Amiodarone, Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors)

│

├── TSH LOW/NORMAL + fT4 LOW → CENTRAL HYPOTHYROIDISM

│ ├── Pituitary MRI → Macroadenoma / Empty Sella / Infiltration

│ ├── Post-Surgery / Radiotherapy → IATROGENIC

│ └── Genetic (PROP1, POU1F1, LHX3) → CONGENITAL

│

└── TSH HIGH + fT4 NORMAL → SUBCLINICAL HYPOTHYROIDISM

TSH >10 → Treat; TSH 5-10 + Ab+/Pregnant/Lipids/Symptoms → Treat; Else Monitor

```

Algorithm 3: THYROID NODULE

```

Thyroid Nodule Detected (Clinical or Incidental)

│

▼

TSH

│

├── TSH SUPPRESSED → HYPERFUNCTIONING ("HOT") NODULE

│ → RAI Scan → Focal Uptake → TOXIC ADENOMA → Surgery/RAI

│

└── TSH NORMAL/HIGH → "COLD" NODULE

│

▼

THYROID ULTRASOUND (TI-RADS)

│

├── TR1/TR2 → BENIGN → Surveillance (1-2yr)

├── TR3 → FNA if ≥2.5cm

├── TR4 → FNA if ≥1.5cm

└── TR5 → FNA if ≥1cm

│

▼

FNA CYTOLOGY (BETHESDA)

│

├── I (Non-diagnostic) → Repeat FNA

├── II (Benign) → Surveillance

├── III (AUS/FLUS) → Repeat FNA / Molecular Testing

├── IV (FN/SFN) → Diagnostic Lobectomy / Molecular

├── V (Suspicious) → Near-Total Thyroidectomy

└── VI (Malignant) → Total Thyroidectomy ± RAI

```

Algorithm 4: CUSHING SYNDROME

```

Clinical Suspicion (Features + Screening)

│

▼

CONFIRM: 24h UFC ×2 + Late-night Salivary Cortisol ×2 + 1mg DST

│

├── NEGATIVE → Cushing Excluded

│

└── POSITIVE → CONFIRMED CUSHING

│

▼

MEASURE PLASMA ACTH (09:00, EDTA, Ice)

│

├── ACTH LOW (<10 pg/mL) → ACTH-INDEPENDENT

│ ├── Adrenal CT → Unilateral Mass → ADRENAL ADENOMA/CARCINOMA → Surgery

│ ├── Bilateral Hyperplasia → PPNAD / AIMAH → Genetic Testing

│ └── No Mass → Ectopic CRH (Rare)

│

└── ACTH NORMAL/HIGH (>10-20) → ACTH-DEPENDENT

│

├── HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)

│ ├── SUPPRESSES >50% → CUSHING DISEASE

│ │ MRI Pituitary (3mm slices)

│ │ ├── Microadenoma (<10mm) → Transsphenoidal Surgery

│ │ └── Macroadenoma → Surgery ± IPSS if equivocal

│ │

│ └── NO SUPPRESSION → ECTOPIC ACTH / ADRENAL

│ │

│ ├── ACTH HIGH → ECTOPIC ACTH

│ │ CT Chest/Abd/Pelvis → ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET → Bronchoscopy

│ │

│ └── ACTH LOW → ADRENAL CUSHING (Re-check Imaging)

│

└── IF HDDST EQUIVOCAL → CRH TEST / IPSS / ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET

```

Algorithm 5: ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

```

Clinical Suspicion (Fatigue, Hypotension, Hyponatraemia, Hyperkalaemia, Hyperpigmentation)

│

▼

0900 CORTISOL + ACTH (Paired)

│

├── CORTISOL <100 + ACTH HIGH → PRIMARY AI (ADDISON)

│ Anti-21-OH Ab / Adrenal CT → AUTOIMMUNE / TB / HIV / HAEMORRHAGE

│

├── CORTISOL <100 + ACTH LOW/NORMAL → SECONDARY/TERTIARY AI

│ ├── MRI Pituitary → Macroadenoma / Empty Sella / Infiltration

│ ├── Post-Surgery/Radiotherapy → IATROGENIC

│ └── Steroid Withdrawal → TERTIARY (HPA Suppression)

│

├── CORTISOL 100-500 + ACTH HIGH → EARLY PRIMARY AI

│

├── CORTISOL 100-500 + ACTH LOW/NORMAL → EQUIVOCAL

│ → SYNACTHEN TEST (250µg or 1µg)

│

└── CORTISOL >500 + ACTH LOW → NORMAL

(If clinical suspicion persists → Synacthen Test)

```

Algorithm 5b: ADRENAL CRISIS — EMERGENCY ALGORITHM

```

ACUTE HYPOTENSION + HYPONATRAEMIA + HYPERKALAEMIA + HYPOGLYCAEMIA

IN PATIENT WITH KNOWN/UNKNOWN ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

│

▼

IMMEDIATE: HYDROCORTISONE 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl OVER 1 HOUR

│

├── HYDROCORTISONE 50-100mg IV Q6H

├── FLUIDS: 2-3L 0.9% NaCl OVER 24H

├── GLUCOSE 5-10% IF HYPOGLYCAEMIC

├── MONITOR: U&Es, Glucose Q1-2H, CARDIAC MONITOR

├── TREAT PRECIPITANT (ANTIBIOTICS, FLUIDS, STRESS DOSES)

└── DO NOT DELAY HYDROCORTISONE FOR TESTS

```

Algorithm 6: HYPERCALCAEMIA

```

Corrected Calcium >2.6 mmol/L (or Ionised >1.3)

│

▼

PTH + Phosphate + Alk Phos + 25-OH Vit D + U&Es + Protein

│

├── PTH HIGH / INAPPROPRIATELY NORMAL → PTH-MEDIATED

│ ├── 24h Urine Calcium/Creatinine >0.02 → PRIMARY HYPERPARATHYROIDISM

│ │ Sestamibi/US → Surgery (if criteria met)

│ ├── 24h Urine Ca/Cr <0.02 → FHH (CaSR MUTATION)

│ │ Genetic Testing → Family Screening

│ └── PTH HIGH + CKD/Hx SECONDARY → TERTIARY HPT → Surgery

│

└── PTH SUPPRESSED (<10 pg/mL) → NON-PTH MEDIATED

├── PTHrP HIGH → HUMORAL HYPERCALCAEMIA OF MALIGNANCY

│ (Squamous Ca, Breast, RCC, Bladder, Ovarian)

├── 1,25-OH Vit D HIGH → GRANULOMATOUS (Sarcoid, TB, Lymphoma)

├── 25-OH Vit D HIGH → VIT D TOXICITY

└── MEDICATIONS (Thiazides, Lithium, Vit A, Theophylline)

```

Algorithm 7: HYPOCALCAEMIA

```

Corrected Calcium <2.15 mmol/L (or Ionised <1.1)

│

▼

PTH + Phosphate + Alk Phos + 25-OH Vit D + Mg + U&Es

│

├── PTH LOW → HYPOPARATHYROIDISM

│ ├── Post-Thyroidectomy → TRANSIENT (days-weeks) vs PERMANENT

│ ├── AUTOIMMUNE (APS-1, CaSR Ab) → Chronic

│ ├── MAGNESIUM DEFICIENCY → Functional Hypoparathyroidism

│ └── AUTOIMMUNE POLYGLANDULAR SYNDROME

│

├── PTH HIGH → SECONDARY HPT / PSEUDOHYPOPARATHYROIDISM

│ ├── CKD → Phosphate Binders, Vit D Analogues, Cinacalcet

│ ├── VIT D DEFICIENCY (25-OH D <30) → Cholecalciferol/Calcifediol

│ ├── PSEUDOHYPOPARATHYROIDISM (PHP1a) → PTH ↑, Ca ↓, Phos ↑, GNAS

│ └── ACUTE PANCREATITIS → Ca Saponification

│

└── PTH INAPPROPRIATELY NORMAL → Mg Deficiency / Early Secondary

Check Mg → Replace Mg first

```

Algorithm 8: HYPERNATRAEMIA / DIABETES INSIPIDUS

```

Serum Na+ >145 mmol/L + Polyuria (>3L/day) + Thirst

│

▼

Serum Osmolality + Urine Osmolality + Urine Na+

│

├── Serum Osm HIGH (>295) + Urine Osm LOW (<300) → DIABETES INSIPIDUS

│ │

│ ├── WATER DEPRIVATION TEST + DESMOPRESSIN

│ │ ├── URINE OSM ↑ >50% → CENTRAL DI → Desmopressin

│ │ └── NO RESPONSE → NEPHROGENIC DI → Thiazide + Low Salt + Amiloride

│ │

│ └── CAUSES: Pituitary Surgery/Trauma/Infiltration (Central); Li/Demeclocycline/HyperCa (Nephrogenic)

│

└── Serum Osm HIGH + Urine Osm HIGH → OSMOTIC DIURESIS (Hyperglycaemia, Mannitol, Urea)

```

Algorithm 8b: HYPONATRAEMIA / SIADH

```

Serum Na+ <135 mmol/L

│

▼

ASSESS VOLUME STATUS

│

├── HYPOVOLAEMIC (Low JVP, Dry Mucosa, Low BP)

│ Urine Na+ <20 → EXTRA-RENAL LOSSES (GI, Skin, Third Space)

│ Urine Na+ >20 → RENAL LOSSES (Diuretics, AKI, Cerebral Salt Wasting, Adrenal Insufficiency)

│

├── HYPERVOLAEMIC (Oedema, High JVP)

│ Heart Failure / Cirrhosis / Nephrotic Syndrome

│

└── EUVOLAEMIC (Normal JVP, No Oedema)

│

├── Serum Osm <275 + Urine Osm >100 + Urine Na+ >20 → SIADH

│ Exclude: Hypothyroidism, Adrenal Insufficiency, Drugs (SSRI, Carbamazepine)

│ Management: Fluid Restriction <1L → Demeclocycline → Tolvaptan (Severe)

│

├── Hypothyroidism / Adrenal Insufficiency → Treat Primary

├── RENAL FAILURE → Dialysis

├── BEER POTOMANIA / TEA-TOAST DIET → Low Solute Intake

└── RESET OSMOSTAT → Asymptomatic, Stable Na+ 125-130

```

Algorithm 9: HYPOKALAEMIA

```

Serum K+ <3.5 mmol/L

│

▼

ASSESS: BP, Acid-Base, Urine K+, Creatinine, Medications

│

├── HYPERSECRETION OF ALDOSTERONE (Primary Aldosteronism)

│ BP HIGH + Metabolic Alkalosis + Low Renin

│ → ARR (Aldosterone/Renin Ratio) >70 → Confirmatory Tests → CT Adrenal → AVS

│

├── RENAL LOSSES Without Aldosterone Excess

│ Diuretics (Loop/Thiazide) → Most Common

│ RTA (Type 1/2), Tubulointerstitial Disease

│ Magnesium Deficiency (Refractory Hypokalaemia) → Replace Mg First

│

├── GI LOSSES

│ Diarrhoea, Vomiting, Laxative Abuse → Urine K+ <20 (Appropriate Conservation)

│

├── INTRACELLULAR SHIFT

│ Insulin, Beta-agonists, Alkalosis, Refeeding Syndrome

│

└── HYPOKALAEMIC PERIODIC PARALYSIS (Genetic, Thyrotoxicosis)

```

Algorithm 10: INCIDENTAL ADRENAL MASS

```

Adrenal Incidentaloma on Imaging

│

▼

HORMONAL WORKUP (All Three)

│

├── 1mg DST (Cushing) → Cortisol <50 nmol/L = Excluded

├── Metanephrines (Phaeo) → Plasma Free Metanephrines Normal = Excluded

├── Aldosterone/Renin (Conn) → ARR Normal = Excluded

│

├── ALL NORMAL → NON-FUNCTIONING

│ │

│ ├── SIZE <4cm + BENIGN FEATURES (<10 HU, Washout >60%) → SURVEILLANCE

│ │ CT 6-12mo → Yearly ×2 → Hormonal Recheck 1yr

│ │

│ └── SIZE ≥4cm OR SUSPICIOUS (>10 HU, Washout <40%, Heterogeneous) → SURGERY

│

└── ANY ABNORMAL → FUNCTIONING → SPECIFIC MANAGEMENT

├── Cushing → Surgery

├── Phaeo → Alpha-blockade → Surgery

├── Conn → AVS → Surgery (Unilateral) / Medical (Bilateral)

```

Algorithm 10b: PHAEOCHROMOCYTOMA — PRE-OP PREPARATION

```

Confirmed Phaeochromocytoma (Biochemistry + Imaging)

│

▼

ALPHA-BLOCKADE (Phenoxybenzamine) 10mg BD → TITRATE 20-40mg BD OVER 2+ WEEKS

│

├── TARGET: SBP <130/80 SUPINE, <120/70 STANDING

├── HEART RATE 60-80 BPM

│

└── AFTER ALPHA-BLOCKADE ESTABLISHED → BETA-BLOCKADE (Propranolol/Metoprolol)

(NEVER BETA-BLOCKADE FIRST — UNOPPOSED α → HYPERTENSIVE CRISIS)

│

│

▼

SURGERY (Laparoscopic Adrenalectomy)

│

├── INTRAOP: PROPHYLACTIC OCTREOTIDE 200-500µg IV (PREVENT CARCINOID-LIKE CRISIS)

├── POST-OP: MONITOR GLUCOSE, BP, ELECTROLYTES

└── GENETIC TESTING (RET, VHL, SDHx, NF1, MAX, TMEM127) → FAMILY SCREENING

```

Algorithm 11: PROLACTINOMA

```

Hyperprolactinaemia (Prolactin >500 mIU/L)

│

▼

EXCLUDE CAUSES:

• Drugs (Antipsychotics, Metoclopramide, Antidepressants, OCP)

• Hypothyroidism (TRH-driven)

• Renal Failure

• Macroprolactinaemia (PEG Precipitation)

• Pregnancy

• Nipple Stimulation/Stress

│

▼

MRI PITUITARY (3mm slices, Gadolinium)

│

├── MICROADENOMA (<10mm) → CABERGOLINE 0.25mg BD → TITRATE

│ Target: Prolactin Normal + Tumour Shrinkage

│ WITHDRAWAL: After 2-3yr Normal Prolactin + No Tumour on MRI

│

├── MACROADENOMA (≥10mm) → CABERGOLINE (Higher Doses) → Surgery if Resistant/Intolerant/Apoplexy

│ VISUAL FIELDS MANDATORY (Bi-temporal Hemianopia)

│

└── NO TUMOUR + Prolactin >1000 → MACROPROLACTINAEMIA OR STALK EFFECT (Large Non-Functioning)

```

Algorithm 12: MALE HYPOGONADISM

```

Symptoms (Low Libido, ED, Fatigue, Infertility, Reduced Hair, Osteoporosis)

│

▼

TOTAL TESTOSTERONE (09:00, Fasting) ×2

│

├── Testosterone <12 nmol/L (or <8 on 2 occasions) + Symptoms → HYPOGONADISM

│ │

│ ├── LH/FSH HIGH → PRIMARY (TESTICULAR)

│ │ Karyotype (Klinefelter), Mumps, Chemo/Radiotherapy, Autoimmune, Genetic

│ │

│ └── LH/FSH LOW/NORMAL → SECONDARY (PITUITARY/HYPOTHALAMIC)

│ MRI Pituitary → Macroadenoma / Hyperprolactinaemia / Kallmann / Functional

│

└── Testosterone 12-15 + Symptoms → BORDERLINE

Repeat + Free Testosterone (SHBG) + LH/FSH

```

4. MCQs (10)

  1. First step in endocrine workup?

A. Baseline hormone + paired tropic hormone (e.g., TSH + fT4)

B. Dynamic testing

C. Imaging

D. Genetic testing

E. Biopsy

  1. High target hormone + high tropic hormone suggests?

A. Primary gland hyperfunction

B. Autonomous gland (tumour)

C. Primary gland failure

D. Central failure

E. Ectopic secretion

  1. High target hormone + low tropic hormone suggests?

A. Autonomous gland (tumour)

B. Primary gland hyperfunction

C. Primary gland failure

D. Central failure

E. Ectopic secretion

  1. Low target hormone + high tropic hormone suggests?

A. Primary gland failure

B. Primary gland hyperfunction

C. Autonomous gland

D. Central failure

E. Ectopic secretion

  1. Low target hormone + low tropic hormone suggests?

A. Central (hypothalamic-pituitary) failure

B. Primary gland failure

C. Primary gland hyperfunction

D. Autonomous gland

E. Ectopic secretion

  1. For suspected deficiency, which dynamic test?

A. Stimulation test

B. Suppression test

C. Replacement test

D. Provocation test

E. Clamp test

  1. For suspected excess, which dynamic test?

A. Suppression test

B. Stimulation test

C. Replacement test

D. Provocation test

E. Clamp test

  1. Random GH is unreliable because?

A. GH is pulsatile

B. GH has long half-life

C. GH is protein-bound

D. GH is not secreted

E. GH is cleared renally

  1. Random prolactin: avoid which confounder?

A. Stress, drugs, macroprolactin

B. Time of day

C. Fasting state

D. Posture

E. Pregnancy

  1. Aldosterone/renin ratio reliability requires?

A. Standardised posture, off interfering drugs

B. Fasting

C. Morning sample only

D. No medication washout

E. Supine only

5. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Polyuria/polydipsia. First-line algorithm?

A. ADH axis → Water deprivation → Desmopressin test

B. Serum osmolality → MRI → ADH

C. Urine osmolality → CT → ADH

D. Serum Na+ → Water loading → MRI

E. Urine volume → DDAVP → MRI

  1. Hypokalaemia + hypertension. First-line?

A. Aldosterone/Renin → CT adrenal → AVS

B. CT adrenal → Aldosterone → AVS

C. AVS → Aldosterone → CT

D. Renin → AVS → CT

E. K+ → Renin → CT

  1. Hypercalcaemia. Algorithm?

A. PTH → PTHrP → Vit D → Bone scan

B. Calcium → PTH → CT → MRI

C. PTH → CT → PTHrP → Bone scan

D. Vit D → PTH → PTHrP → Scan

E. CT → PTH → PTHrP → Scan

  1. Cushing syndrome workup?

A. 1mg DST → Late-night salivary cortisol → ACTH

B. ACTH → CT → DST

C. UFC → MRI → CRH

D. CRH → IPSS → DST

E. Salivary cortisol → DST → ACTH

  1. Adrenal insufficiency workup?

A. 0900 cortisol → Synacthen test → ACTH

B. ACTH → Synacthen → Cortisol

C. Synacthen → ACTH → Cortisol

D. Cortisol → ACTH → Synacthen

E. 24h UFC → ACTH → Synacthen

  1. Hyperthyroidism algorithm?

A. TSH → fT4/fT3 → TRAb → RAI uptake

B. TRAb → TSH → RAI → fT4

C. fT4 → TSH → TRAb → RAI

D. RAI → TSH → TRAb → fT4

E. fT3 → TSH → TRAb → I131

  1. Hypothyroidism algorithm?

A. TSH → fT4 → Anti-TPO → US

B. Anti-TPO → TSH → fT4 → US

C. fT4 → TSH → Anti-TPO

D. US → TSH → fT4

E. TPO → TSH → fT4 → US

  1. Pituitary adenoma workup?

A. Hormone panel → MRI pituitary ± visual fields

B. MRI → Hormone panel → Visual fields

C. Visual fields → MRI → Hormones

D. CT → MRI → Hormones

E. Hormones → CT → MRI

  1. Thyroid nodule algorithm?

A. TSH → US (TI-RADS) → FNA (Bethesda)

B. US → TSH → FNA → Bethesda

C. FNA → Bethesda → TSH → US

D. Bethesda → FNA → US → TSH

E. MRI → TSH → US → FNA

  1. Male hypogonadism algorithm?

A. LH/FSH/Testosterone → Prolactin → MRI if secondary

B. Testosterone → MRI → LH/FSH

C. Prolactin → MRI → Testosterone

D. MRI → Testosterone → LH/FSH

E. FSH → Testosterone → inhibin B

6. Flashcards

  • Q: Endocrine axis logic

A: High target + High tropic = Primary hyperfunction; High target + Low tropic = Autonomous; Low target + High tropic = Primary failure; Low target + Low tropic = Central failure

  • Q: Stimulation vs Suppression

A: Deficiency → Stimulation test; Excess → Suppression test

  • Q: Paired hormones always

A: TSH+fT4, ACTH+cortisol, LH/FSH+testosterone/oestradiol

  • Q: Random GH unreliable

A: Pulsatile → use IGF-1 or OGTT suppression

  • Q: Random prolactin issues

A: Stress, drugs, macroprolactin → repeat fasting, check macroprolactin

  • Q: ARR standardisation

A: Seated/standing, off interfering drugs (spironolactone, ACEi, beta-blockers)

  • Q: Drug review essential

A: Steroids, OCP, PPIs, dopamine agonists, beta-blockers, ACEi alter results

  • Q: Polyuria algorithm

A: ADH axis → Water dep → DDAVP → Central vs Nephrogenic DI vs Primary polydipsia

  • Q: HypoK + HTN algorithm

A: ARR → CT adrenal → AVS → Unilateral vs Bilateral → Surgery vs MR antagonist

  • Q: HyperCa algorithm

A: PTH → if high = primary HPT; if low → PTHrP/Vit D/Bone scan → Malignancy/Granulomatous

  • Q: Cushing algorithm

A: 1mg DST → Late-night salivary cortisol → ACTH → Imaging/IPSS

  • Q: AI algorithm

A: 0900 cortisol → Synacthen → ACTH → Primary vs Secondary/Tertiary

  • Q: Thyroid algorithm

A: TSH → fT4/fT3 → TRAb/RAI → Graves vs Toxic nodule vs Thyroiditis

  • Q: Pituitary algorithm

A: Hormone panel → MRI ± Visual fields → Micro vs Macro, Functioning vs Non-functioning

  • Q: Thyroid nodule

A: TI-RADS US → FNA Bethesda → Risk stratification → Management

7. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Baseline hormone + paired tropic hormone (e.g., TSH + fT4) — First step in endocrine workup?
  1. Primary gland hyperfunction — High target hormone + high tropic hormone suggests?
  1. Autonomous gland (tumour) — High target hormone + low tropic hormone suggests?
  1. Primary gland failure — Low target hormone + high tropic hormone suggests?
  1. Central (hypothalamic-pituitary) failure — Low target hormone + low tropic hormone suggests?
  1. Stimulation test — For suspected deficiency, which dynamic test?
  1. Suppression test — For suspected excess, which dynamic test?
  1. GH is pulsatile — Random GH is unreliable because?
  1. Stress, drugs, macroprolactin — Random prolactin: avoid which confounder?
  1. Standardised posture, off interfering drugs — Aldosterone/renin ratio reliability requires?

SBAs

  1. ADH axis → Water deprivation → Desmopressin test — Polyuria/polydipsia. First-line algorithm?
  1. Aldosterone/Renin → CT adrenal → AVS — Hypokalaemia + hypertension. First-line?
  1. PTH → PTHrP → Vit D → Bone scan — Hypercalcaemia. Algorithm?
  1. 1mg DST → Late-night salivary cortisol → ACTH — Cushing syndrome workup?
  1. 0900 cortisol → Synacthen test → ACTH — Adrenal insufficiency workup?
  1. TSH → fT4/fT3 → TRAb → RAI uptake — Hyperthyroidism algorithm?
  1. TSH → fT4 → Anti-TPO → US — Hypothyroidism algorithm?
  1. Hormone panel → MRI pituitary ± visual fields — Pituitary adenoma workup?
  1. TSH → US (TI-RADS) → FNA (Bethesda) — Thyroid nodule algorithm?
  1. LH/FSH/Testosterone → Prolactin → MRI if secondary — Male hypogonadism algorithm?

8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

topics.title: Feedback Loops
topics.html:
[!info]
Feedback loops are the central homeostatic mechanism of the endocrine system. Negative feedback maintains stability; positive feedback drives transitions; ultra-short loops provide fine-tuning. The pattern of hormone levels (tropic vs target) reveals the level of pathology.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify feedback loops by type (negative, positive, ultra-short, short, long)
  • [ ] Apply feedback logic to distinguish primary, secondary, and tertiary endocrine disorders
  • [ ] Predict hormone patterns in feedback disruption (tumours, resistance, exogenous administration)
  • [ ] Interpret dynamic tests through the lens of feedback physiology
  • [ ] Explain clinical consequences of feedback failure (Cushing, thyrotoxicosis, adrenal insufficiency)

2. Feedback Loop Types

Loop TypeDirectionComponentsExampleClinical Relevance
Long-loop NegativeTarget hormone → Hypothalamus + PituitaryTarget gland hormone → inhibits releasing hormone + tropic hormoneCortisol → CRH + ACTH; T3/T4 → TRH + TSH; Oestradiol/Testosterone → GnRH + LH/FSHPrimary vs Secondary diagnosis: High target + Low tropic = Primary; Low target + Low tropic = Secondary
Short-loop NegativePituitary hormone → HypothalamusTropic hormone → inhibits its own releasing hormoneACTH → CRH; TSH → TRH; LH/FSH → GnRHPituitary tumour autonomy = loss of short-loop feedback
Ultra-short-loop NegativeHypothalamic hormone → Hypothalamus (autocrine)Releasing hormone → inhibits its own secretionCRH → CRH; TRH → TRH; GnRH → GnRHFine-tuning; prevents runaway secretion
Positive FeedbackTarget hormone → stimulates releasing/tropic hormoneTarget hormone → ↑ releasing hormone → ↑ tropic hormoneOestradiol → GnRH/LH surge (mid-cycle); Oxytocin → uterine contractions → more oxytocinOvulation; Parturition; Lactation (suckling → prolactin/oxytocin)
Feed-forwardAnticipatory regulationUpstream signal → primes downstream responseMeal → GIP/GLP-1 → Insulin (before glucose rises); Light → SCN → Cortisol awakening responseAnticipatory homeostasis; explains cephalic phase insulin release

3. Negative Feedback — The Dominant Principle

General Architecture

```

Stimulus → Releasing Hormone (Hypothalamus)

↓

Tropic Hormone (Pituitary)

↓

Target Hormone (End-organ)

↓

┌─────────────────────────────────────┐

│ NEGATIVE FEEDBACK │

│ Target Hormone ──────┬──→ Inhibits │

│ │ Releasing │

│ └──→ Inhibits │

│ Tropic │

└─────────────────────────────────────┘

```

Feedback Sensitivity & Set Points

ConceptDefinitionClinical Example
Set PointHormone level at which feedback is 50% maximalTSH set point shifts in thyroid disease
GainSteepness of feedback responseHigh gain = tight control (thyroid); Low gain = wide variation (cortisol)
HysteresisDifferent set points for increasing vs decreasing stimulusNot prominent in human endocrine axes

4. Major Endocrine Axes — Feedback Patterns

HPA Axis (Cortisol)

```

CRH (Hypothalamus) → ACTH (Pituitary) → Cortisol (Adrenal)

↑______________|______________|

Negative Feedback

```

LevelCortisolACTHCRHInterpretation
NormalNormalNormalNormal—
Primary AI (Addison)↓↓↑↑↑↑Adrenal failure
Secondary AI (Pituitary)↓↓/N↑Pituitary failure
Tertiary AI (Hypothalamic/Steroid)↓↓/N↓Steroid withdrawal, hypothalamic lesion
Cushing Disease↑↑N/↑↓Pituitary ACTH adenoma
Adrenal Cushing↑↑↓↓↓↓Adrenal autonomy
Ectopic ACTH↑↑↑↑↓Ectopic ACTH source

HPT Axis (Thyroid)

```

TRH (Hypothalamus) → TSH (Pituitary) → T4/T3 (Thyroid)

↑______________|______________|

Negative Feedback (mainly T3 at pituitary)

```

LevelTSHfT4/fT3Interpretation
NormalNormalNormal—
Primary Hyper↓↓ (suppressed)↑↑Graves, Toxic nodule, Thyroiditis
Primary Hypo↑↑↓Hashimoto, Post-ablation, Iodine deficiency
Central Hypo↓/N (inappropriately low)↓↓Pituitary/Hypothalamic disease
Subclinical Hyper↓↓NormalEarly Graves, Toxic nodule, Excess thyroxine
Subclinical Hypo↑NormalEarly Hashimoto
T3 Thyrotoxicosis↓↓fT4 Normal, fT3 ↑Early Graves, T3-secreting nodule

HPG Axis (Gonadal)

```

GnRH (Hypothalamus) → LH/FSH (Pituitary) → Testosterone/Oestradiol (Gonads)

↑_______________|________________|

Negative Feedback (Oestradiol/Testosterone + Inhibin)

```

LevelLH/FSHSex SteroidInterpretation
Primary Hypogonadism↑↑↓Klinefelter, Turner, Mumps, Chemo
Secondary Hypogonadism↓/N↓Pituitary adenoma, Hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann, Functional
PCOSLH ↑↑, FSH N/↓ (LH:FSH >2:1)Testosterone ↑Ovarian resistance + insulin resistance
Menopause↑↑↓↓ (Oestradiol)Ovarian failure

GH/IGF-1 Axis

```

GHRH → GH (Pituitary) → IGF-1 (Liver)

↑____________|____________|

Somatostatin inhibits GH

IGF-1 negative feedback at pituitary + hypothalamus

```

5. Positive Feedback — Key Physiological Examples

SystemTriggerMechanismOutcome
Ovulation (Mid-cycle LH Surge)Rising Oestradiol (>200 pg/mL for >50h)Oestradiol switches from negative → positive feedback on pituitary → LH surgeOvulation
ParturitionFetal head → Cervical stretchOxytocin → Uterine contraction → More cervical stretch → More oxytocinLabour & Delivery
LactationSuckling → Nipple stimulationProlactin + Oxytocin release → Milk ejection → More sucklingMilk production/ejection

6. Feedback Failure — Clinical Patterns

1. Hormone Resistance (Receptor/Post-receptor Defect)

ConditionHormone PatternMechanism
Thyroid Hormone Resistance (THRβ mutation)fT4/fT3 ↑↑, TSH N/↑ (inappropriately normal)Impaired negative feedback at pituitary
Androgen Insensitivity (AR mutation)Testosterone ↑↑, LH ↑↑Impaired feedback at hypothalamus/pituitary
Pseudohypoparathyroidism (GNAS mutation)PTH ↑↑, Calcium ↓, Phosphate ↑End-organ resistance to PTH
Glucocorticoid Resistance (GR mutation)Cortisol ↑↑, ACTH ↑↑Impaired feedback

2. Autonomous Secretion (Tumours)

TumourFeedback StatusHormone Pattern
Adrenal Adenoma (Cushing)Lost (autonomous)Cortisol ↑, ACTH ↓↓
ProlactinomaLost (dopamine resistance)Prolactin ↑↑, LH/FSH ↓
TSH-omaLost (TRH resistance)TSH N/↑, fT4/fT3 ↑
InsulinomaLost (glucose sensing lost)Insulin ↑ despite hypoglycaemia

3. Ectopic Hormone Production

SourceHormoneFeedback Effect
Small Cell Lung CaACTHACTH ↑↑ → Cortisol ↑↑ (feedback intact but overwhelmed)
Squamous CaPTHrPPTHrP ↑ → Calcium ↑ → PTH Suppressed (intact)
Renal Cell CaEPOEPO ↑ → Hb ↑ (feedback intact)

4. Exogenous Hormone Administration

Steroid TypeHPA Axis SuppressionRecovery
Short-course (<3 weeks)MinimalRapid
Long-course (>3 weeks)Significant (adrenal atrophy)Weeks-months; taper required
Inhaled/Topical (high dose)SignificantVariable
Physiological replacementNo suppressionN/A

7. Dynamic Testing — Feedback in Action

TestFeedback PrincipleInterpretation
Dexamethasone Suppression (DST)Exogenous glucocorticoid → Negative feedback → ↓ ACTH → ↓ CortisolNon-suppression = Autonomous cortisol (Cushing) or ectopic ACTH
TRH StimulationTRH → TSH release (if pituitary intact) → fT4 riseBlunted TSH = Hyperthyroidism / Pituitary failure; Exaggerated = Primary hypothyroidism
GnRH StimulationGnRH → LH/FSH releasePrepubertal: FSH > LH; Puberty: LH > FSH; PCOS: LH > FSH; Hypogonadotropic: Low response
Synacthen (ACTH) TestExogenous ACTH → Cortisol rise (if adrenal intact)Cortisol <400-500 nmol/L at 30min = Adrenal insufficiency
Water Deprivation + DesmopressinADH feedback → Urine concentrationCentral DI: Response to desmopressin; Nephrogenic DI: No response

8. Feedback in Pregnancy — Unique Adaptations

AxisChangeMechanism
HPT↑ TBG (oestrogen) → ↑ Total T4/T3; fT4/fT3 reliable; hCG cross-reacts with TSH → TSH ↓ 1st trimesterhCG weak TSH agonist; TBG ↑ 2-3x
HPA↑ CBG (oestrogen) → ↑ Total cortisol; Free cortisol ↑ 2-3x; HPA axis hyperplasiaOestrogen ↑ CBG synthesis; Placental CRH
HPGProlactin ↑ (oestrogen stimulates lactotrophs); FSH/LH suppressedOestrogen negative feedback + high progesterone
Prolactin↑↑ (10-20x)Oestrogen stimulates lactotroph hyperplasia

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Primary vs SecondaryPrimary: Target hormone abnormal + Tropic hormone opposite direction; Secondary: Both same direction (low)
Addison vs Secondary AIAddison: Cortisol ↓ + ACTH ↑↑ + Hyperpigmentation; Secondary: Cortisol ↓ + ACTH ↓/N + No hyperpigmentation
Cushing Disease vs Adrenal CushingCushing Disease: ACTH N/↑; Adrenal Cushing: ACTH ↓↓
Primary HypothyroidismTSH ↑↑, fT4 ↓ (most common thyroid pattern)
Central HypothyroidismTSH ↓/N (inappropriately low), fT4 ↓ — Think pituitary tumour
Subclinical HyperthyroidismTSH suppressed, fT4/fT3 normal — Treat if TSH <0.1, elderly, cardiac risk
Subclinical HypothyroidismTSH ↑, fT4 normal — Treat if TSH >10, antibodies +ve, pregnancy, lipid abnormality
Positive FeedbackOestradiol → LH surge (ovulation); Oxytocin (parturition); Prolactin (lactation)
Steroid withdrawalHPA suppression after >3 weeks; Taper over weeks-months
Thyroid Hormone ResistancefT4/fT3 ↑, TSH N/↑ (inappropriately normal) — THRβ mutation

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Primary vs Secondary"Primary = problem in the gland itself" → Target hormone bad, Tropic hormone tries to compensate (opposite direction)
ACTH in CushingCushing Disease = Pituitary ACTH ↑; Adrenal Cushing = ACTH suppressed; Ectopic = ACTH very high
TSH in Central HypoTSH is NOT elevated (inappropriately normal/low) despite low fT4 — this IS the clue
Positive FeedbackNot "bad" feedback — physiological drive for ovulation/parturition; Oestradiol flips from inhibitor to stimulator at high sustained levels
Subclinical ThyroidBiochemical only (TSH abnormal, fT4/fT3 normal); Treat based on TSH level, age, antibodies, pregnancy, cardiac risk
Steroid Taper>3 weeks steroids = HPA suppression; Taper over weeks-months; Stress doses for illness/surgery

Mnemonic: FEEDBACK LOOP TYPES

  • Long-loop = Target → Hypo + Pit (Cortisol→CRH+ACTH)
  • Short-loop = Pituitary → Hypothalamus (ACTH→CRH)
  • Ultra-short = Self-inhibition (CRH→CRH)
  • Positive = Oestrogen→LH surge; Oxytocin→Labour
  • Feed-forward = Meal→Incretins→Insulin

Mnemonic: PRIMARY vs SECONDARY HORMONE PATTERNS

  • Primary: Problem in the Peripheral gland → Target bad, Tropic Opposite
  • Secondary: Systemic (central) failure → Target low, Tropic Same (low)
  • Tertiary: Top (hypothalamus) failure → Same as secondary but releasing hormone low

11. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Feedback Loops))

Negative (Dominant)

Long-loop: Target → Hypo + Pit

Cortisol → CRH + ACTH

T3/T4 → TRH + TSH

Sex steroids → GnRH + LH/FSH

Short-loop: Pit → Hypo

ACTH → CRH; TSH → TRH; LH/FSH → GnRH

Ultra-short: Self-inhibition

CRH → CRH; TRH → TRH; GnRH → GnRH

Positive (Physiological Drive)

Oestrogen → LH Surge (Ovulation)

Oxytocin → Labour

Prolactin → Lactation

Feed-forward (Anticipation)

Meal → Incretins → Insulin

Light → Cortisol Awakening

Failure Patterns

Resistance: Hormone ↑↑, Tropic ↑/N (THRβ, AR, PTH, GR)

Autonomous Tumour: Hormone ↑, Tropic ↓ (Adrenal Cushing, Prolactinoma)

Ectopic: Hormone ↑↑, Feedback intact but overwhelmed

Exogenous Steroids: HPA Suppression (>3 weeks)

Axis Patterns

HPA: Cortisol ↔ ACTH/CRH

HPT: T3/T4 ↔ TSH/TRH

HPG: Sex steroids ↔ LH/FSH/GnRH

GH: IGF-1 ↔ GH/GHRH/Somatostatin

```

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
Feedback TypesLong: Target→Hypo+Pit; Short: Pit→Hypo; Ultra-short: Self; Positive: Ovulation/Labour
Primary PatternTarget bad + Tropic opposite (e.g., Cortisol↓ + ACTH↑ = Addison)
Secondary PatternTarget bad + Tropic same/low (e.g., Cortisol↓ + ACTH↓ = Secondary AI)
Tertiary PatternLike secondary but CRH/Releasing hormone low (steroid withdrawal)
Cushing PatternsDisease: ACTH N/↑; Adrenal: ACTH ↓↓; Ectopic: ACTH ↑↑
Thyroid PatternsPrimary Hypo: TSH↑↑, fT4↓; Central: TSH↓/N, fT4↓; Subclinical: TSH abnormal, fT4 normal
Positive FeedbackOestrogen→LH surge (ovulation); Oxytocin (labour); Prolactin (lactation)
Resistance SyndromesHormone↑↑ + Tropic↑/N (THRβ, AR, PTH, GR)
Steroid Withdrawal>3 weeks = HPA suppression; Taper over weeks-months
Key DiscriminatorsACTH in Cushing; TSH in thyroid; LH/FSH in hypogonadism

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Cortisol inhibiting CRH is which feedback type?

A. Long-loop negative feedback

B. Short-loop negative feedback

C. Ultra-short-loop feedback

D. Positive feedback

  1. ACTH inhibiting CRH is?

A. Short-loop negative feedback

B. Long-loop negative feedback

C. Positive feedback

D. Feedforward

  1. Oestrogen causing LH surge is?

A. Positive feedback

B. Negative feedback

C. Ultra-short-loop

D. Short-loop

  1. In primary hypothyroidism, TSH is?

A. Elevated

B. Suppressed

C. Normal

D. Undetectable

  1. In secondary hypothyroidism, TSH is?

A. Low/Normal (inappropriately)

B. Elevated

C. Suppressed

D. Pulsatile

  1. TRH test in secondary hypothyroidism shows?

A. Blunted TSH response

B. Exaggerated TSH response

C. Normal TSH response

D. Delayed peak

  1. Dexamethasone suppression test: failure to suppress cortisol suggests?

A. Cushing syndrome

B. Adrenal insufficiency

C. Normal HPA axis

D. Pituitary adenoma

  1. In Cushing disease, high-dose dexamethasone:

A. Suppresses cortisol

B. Does not suppress cortisol

C. Elevates ACTH

D. Suppresses ACTH only

  1. Thyroid hormone feedback on TSH is?

A. Long-loop negative feedback

B. Short-loop

C. Ultra-short-loop

D. Positive feedback

  1. Autonomous gland hyperfunction results in?

A. High hormone + low/normal tropic hormone

B. Low hormone + high tropic hormone

C. High hormone + high tropic hormone

D. Low hormone + low tropic hormone

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Low cortisol, high ACTH, high CRH. Diagnosis?

A. Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's)

B. Secondary adrenal insufficiency

C. Cushing disease

D. Ectopic ACTH syndrome

E. Pseudo-Cushing

  1. High cortisol, low ACTH, low CRH. Diagnosis?

A. Adrenal Cushing (ACTH-independent)

B. Cushing disease

C. Ectopic ACTH

D. Pseudo-Cushing

E. Factitious Cushing

  1. Thyroid hormone resistance: TSH level?

A. Inappropriately normal/elevated with high fT4/fT3

B. Suppressed

C. Normal with low fT4

D. Elevated with low fT3

E. Low with high fT3

  1. 40yo woman: amenorrhoea, low oestradiol, low LH/FSH. Diagnosis?

A. Hypothalamic amenorrhoea (functional)

B. Premature ovarian insufficiency

C. PCOS

D. Prolactinoma

E. Sheehan syndrome

  1. Acromegaly: GH high, IGF-1 high, OGTT fails to suppress GH. Mechanism?

A. Loss of negative feedback by IGF-1 on somatotroph adenoma

B. GHRH excess

C. Ghrelin excess

D. Somatostatin deficiency

E. TRH excess

  1. Primary hyperparathyroidism: PTH high → Calcium?

A. High (hypercalcaemia)

B. Low (hypocalcaemia)

C. Normal

D. Variable

E. Low-normal

  1. Long-term steroids: low cortisol, low ACTH. Diagnosis?

A. Tertiary (steroid-withdrawal) adrenal insufficiency

B. Primary AI

C. Cushing syndrome

D. Secondary AI (pituitary)

E. Adrenal crisis

  1. SIADH: ADH high despite?

A. Low plasma osmolality

B. High plasma osmolality

C. Normal plasma osmolality

D. Low volume

E. High volume

  1. Prolactinoma: Prolactin high → GnRH/LH/FSH?

A. Suppressed (via dopamine/prolactin effect on hypothalamus)

B. Elevated

C. Normal

D. Pulsatile only

E. Absent

  1. Cushing disease: midnight salivary cortisol?

A. Elevated (loss of diurnal rhythm)

B. Normal

C. Low

D. Variable

E. Suppressed

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Long-loop negative feedback

A: Target hormone → inhibits tropic hormone (e.g., Cortisol → ↓CRH/↓ACTH)

  • Q: Short-loop negative feedback

A: Tropic hormone → inhibits releasing hormone (e.g., ACTH → ↓CRH)

  • Q: Ultra-short-loop feedback

A: Releasing hormone → self-inhibition (e.g., CRH → ↓CRH)

  • Q: Positive feedback

A: Oestrogen → ↑LH surge (mid-cycle); Oxytocin → ↑Oxytocin in labour

  • Q: Primary gland failure

A: Target hormone LOW, Tropic hormone HIGH (e.g., Primary hypothyroidism: ↓T4, ↑TSH)

  • Q: Central (secondary/tertiary) failure

A: Target hormone LOW, Tropic hormone LOW/NORMAL (inappropriately)

  • Q: Autonomous gland hyperfunction

A: Target hormone HIGH, Tropic hormone LOW (e.g., Adrenal adenoma: ↑Cortisol, ↓ACTH)

  • Q: Dexamethasone suppression

A: Low dose: Suppresses normal HPA; High dose: Suppresses pituitary (Cushing disease) but not ectopic/adrenal

  • Q: TRH test

A: Normal: TSH ↑; Primary hypo: TSH ↑↑ (exaggerated); Secondary: TSH blunted/absent; Hyperthyroidism: TSH no rise

  • Q: Synacthen test

A: Normal: Cortisol ≥500-550 nmol/L; AI: Cortisol <400-500 nmol/L

  • Q: OGTT in acromegaly

A: Normal: GH suppresses <1 µg/L; Acromegaly: GH fails to suppress (↑ or paradoxical ↑)

  • Q: Water deprivation test

A: Normal: Urine osmolality >800; Central DI: No concentrate → DDAVP → ↑Uosm; Nephrogenic DI: No response to DDAVP

  • Q: TRH test in secondary hypothyroidism

A: Blunted/delayed TSH response (pituitary dysfunction)

  • Q: Dexamethasone in Cushing disease vs ectopic

A: High-dose DST: Cushing disease → cortisol suppression (>50%); Ectopic → no suppression

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Long-loop negative feedback — Cortisol inhibiting CRH is which feedback type?
  1. Short-loop negative feedback — ACTH inhibiting CRH is?
  1. Positive feedback — Oestrogen causing LH surge is?
  1. Elevated — In primary hypothyroidism, TSH is?
  1. Low/Normal (inappropriately) — In secondary hypothyroidism, TSH is?
  1. Blunted TSH response — TRH test in secondary hypothyroidism shows?
  1. Cushing syndrome — Dexamethasone suppression test: failure to suppress cortisol suggests?
  1. Suppresses cortisol — In Cushing disease, high-dose dexamethasone:
  1. Long-loop negative feedback — Thyroid hormone feedback on TSH is?
  1. High hormone + low/normal tropic hormone — Autonomous gland hyperfunction results in?

SBAs

  1. Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's) — Low cortisol, high ACTH, high CRH. Diagnosis?
  1. Adrenal Cushing (ACTH-independent) — High cortisol, low ACTH, low CRH. Diagnosis?
  1. Inappropriately normal/elevated with high fT4/fT3 — Thyroid hormone resistance: TSH level?
  1. Hypothalamic amenorrhoea (functional) — 40yo woman: amenorrhoea, low oestradiol, low LH/FSH. Diagnosis?
  1. Loss of negative feedback by IGF-1 on somatotroph adenoma — Acromegaly: GH high, IGF-1 high, OGTT fails to suppress GH. Mechanism?
  1. High (hypercalcaemia) — Primary hyperparathyroidism: PTH high → Calcium?
  1. Tertiary (steroid-withdrawal) adrenal insufficiency — Long-term steroids: low cortisol, low ACTH. Diagnosis?
  1. Low plasma osmolality — SIADH: ADH high despite?
  1. Suppressed (via dopamine/prolactin effect on hypothalamus) — Prolactinoma: Prolactin high → GnRH/LH/FSH?
  1. Elevated (loss of diurnal rhythm) — Cushing disease: midnight salivary cortisol?

topics.title: Hormone Synthesis Secretion And Transport
topics.html:
[!info]
Hormone classification dictates mechanism of action, secretion regulation, half-life, transport in blood, and assay methodology. Understanding this is foundational for all endocrine pathology.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify hormones by chemical structure and mechanism of action
  • [ ] Describe synthesis pathways for peptide, steroid, and amine hormones
  • [ ] Explain secretion mechanisms (regulated vs constitutive)
  • [ ] Identify transport proteins and their clinical significance
  • [ ] Apply knowledge to interpret hormone assays and dynamic tests

2. Hormone Classification by Chemical Structure

ClassExamplesSynthesis SitePrecursorMechanism of ActionHalf-lifeTransport in Blood
Peptide/ProteinInsulin, GH, ACTH, TSH, ADH, Oxytocin, PTH, CalcitoninRough ER → GolgiPreprohormone → Prohormone → Active hormoneCell surface receptors → cAMP, IP3/DAG, JAK-STATMinutes (3-30 min)Free (unbound)
SteroidCortisol, Aldosterone, Oestradiol, Testosterone, Vit D, 1,25(OH)₂DSmooth ER / MitochondriaCholesterolIntracellular nuclear receptors → Gene transcriptionHours (60-90 min)Bound to binding globulins (CBG, SHBG, DBP) + Albumin
Amine (Tyrosine-derived)T4/T3, Catecholamines (Adrenaline, Noradrenaline)Thyroid follicular cells / Adrenal medullaTyrosineMixed: T4/T3 = nuclear; Catecholamines = surface (α/β adrenergic)T4: Days (5-7d); Catecholamines: MinutesT4/T3: TBG, Transthyretin, Albumin; Catecholamines: Free
EicosanoidProstaglandins, Leukotrienes, ThromboxanesMembrane phospholipidsArachidonic acidLocal autocrine/paracrine (GPCRs)SecondsLocal (not systemic)

3. Peptide Hormone Synthesis Pathway

```

Gene Transcription → Preprohormone (signal peptide + prohormone)

↓

Signal peptide cleaved in ER → Prohormone

↓

Golgi Processing → Prohormone convertases (PC1/3, PC2) → Active hormone + C-peptide fragments

↓

Packaged into Secretory Granules (immature → mature)

↓

Regulated Secretion (stimulus → Ca²⁺ influx → Exocytosis)

```

StepEnzyme/ProcessClinical Relevance
Preprohormone → ProhormoneSignal peptidaseMutations → misfolding (e.g., proinsulin mutations → diabetes)
Prohormone → Active hormoneProhormone convertases (PC1/3, PC2)PC1/3 deficiency → obesity, hypogonadism, cortisol deficiency
Granule maturationCarboxypeptidase ECPE deficiency → obesity in mice
Regulated secretionCa²⁺-dependent exocytosisSulfonylureas close KATP → Ca²⁺ influx → Insulin secretion

Key Example — Insulin: Preproinsulin (110aa) → Proinsulin (86aa, C-peptide connecting A&B chains) → Insulin (51aa, A&B chains linked by disulphide bonds) + C-peptide (31aa). C-peptide = marker of endogenous insulin secretion.

4. Steroid Hormone Synthesis Pathway

Cholesterol → Steroid Hormones (Adrenal Cortex / Gonads / Placenta)

```

Cholesterol → (STAR protein) → Inner mitochondrial membrane

↓

CYP11A1 (P450scc) → Pregnenolone (rate-limiting step)

↓

├─→ Δ5 Pathway (Zona fasciculata/reticularis)

│ 17α-hydroxylase (CYP17A1) → 17-OH Pregnenolone

│ 17,20-lyase (CYP17A1) → DHEA → Androstenedione → Testosterone/Oestradiol

│ 21-hydroxylase (CYP21A2) → 17-OH Progesterone → 11-Deoxycortisol → Cortisol (CYP11B1)

│ 21-hydroxylase → Deoxycorticosterone → Aldosterone (CYP11B2)

│

└─→ Δ4 Pathway (Zona glomerulosa)

Progesterone → 21-hydroxylase → Deoxycorticosterone → Aldosterone

```

Key Enzymes & Clinical Deficiencies

EnzymeGeneProductDeficiency Syndrome
STARSTARCholesterol transferLipoid CAH (lethal, salt-wasting)
CYP11A1CYP11A1PregnenoloneLipoid CAH (rare)
CYP17A1 (17α-hydroxylase/17,20-lyase)CYP17A117-OH steroids, DHEA17α-hydroxylase deficiency → HTN, sexual infantilism
CYP21A2 (21-hydroxylase)CYP21A211-Deoxycortisol, DOC21-hydroxylase deficiency (95% CAH) → Salt-wasting/virilising
CYP11B1 (11β-hydroxylase)CYP11B1Cortisol11β-hydroxylase deficiency → HTN + virilisation
CYP11B2 (Aldosterone synthase)CYP11B2AldosteroneAldosterone deficiency / excess
5α-reductaseSRD5A2DHT5α-reductase deficiency → 46,XY DSD
AromataseCYP19A1OestradiolAromatase deficiency → virilisation in pregnancy

5. Amine Hormone Synthesis

Thyroid Hormones (T4/T3) — Thyroid Follicular Cells

```

Iodide uptake (NIS) → Thyroglobulin synthesis (RER) → Iodination (TPO + H₂O₂) → MIT + DIT → Coupling (TPO) → T4 (2 DIT) / T3 (MIT+DIT) → Stored in colloid

↓

TSH stimulation → Endocytosis of colloid → Lysosomal proteolysis → T4/T3 released into circulation

```

StepEnzyme/ProcessClinical Relevance
Iodide uptakeNIS (Sodium-Iodide Symporter)Perchlorate discharge test; NIS mutations → dyshormonogenesis
IodinationTPO (Thyroid Peroxidase)Anti-TPO antibodies (Hashimoto); PTU inhibits TPO
CouplingTPOTPO mutations → dyshormonogenesis
ProteolysisLysosomal enzymesIodine excess inhibits (Wolff-Chaikoff effect)
DeiodinationDIO1, DIO2 (activate T4→T3); DIO3 (inactivate)Selenium deficiency impairs DIOs; Amiodarone inhibits DIO1

Key Ratios: T4:T3 secretion ≈ 10:1 (but T3 is 4x more potent). Peripheral conversion (DIO1/DIO2) provides ~80% of circulating T3.

Catecholamines — Adrenal Medulla / Sympathetic Neurones

```

Tyrosine → (Tyrosine hydroxylase) → L-DOPA → (Dopa decarboxylase) → Dopamine → (Dopamine β-hydroxylase) → Noradrenaline → (PNMT) → Adrenaline

```

EnzymeLocationClinical Relevance
Tyrosine hydroxylaseCytoplasmRate-limiting; inhibited by Metyrosine
PNMTAdrenal medulla (cortisol-induced)PNMT in adrenal → distinguishes adrenal vs extra-adrenal phaeo
MAO/COMTMetabolismMetabolites: VMA, Metanephrines (measured in urine/plasma)

6. Hormone Secretion Patterns

PatternExamplesMechanism
PulsatileGH, LH, FSH, ACTH, TSH, ProlactinHypothalamic pulse generator; essential for receptor sensitivity
Circadian/DiurnalCortisol (peak 08:00, nadir 24:00), Prolactin (sleep), GH (sleep)SCN → Hypothalamic drive
UltradianCortisol (hourly pulses), GnRH (60-90 min pulses)Pulse frequency encodes information (e.g., LH pulse freq → FSH/LH ratio)
Stress-responsiveACTH, Cortisol, Adrenaline, GH, ProlactinCRH, sympathetic activation
Feedback-regulatedThyroid (TSH-T4), Gonadal (GnRH-LH/FSH-sex steroids), Adrenal (ACTH-cortisol)Negative feedback dominant

7. Transport Proteins in Blood

Binding ProteinHormones Bound% BoundHalf-life EffectClinical Relevance
CBG (Corticosteroid Binding Globulin / Transcortin)Cortisol, Corticosterone, Progesterone~90%Prolongs t½↑ in pregnancy, OCP, hyperoestrogenism → ↑ total cortisol (free normal)
SHBG (Sex Hormone Binding Globulin)Testosterone, Oestradiol, DHT~60-70%Prolongs t½↓ in insulin resistance, obesity, hypothyroidism; ↑ in hyperthyroidism, OCP
TBG (Thyroxine Binding Globulin)T4 > T3T4: 99.97%; T3: 99.7%Prolongs t½ (T4: 5-7d)↑ in pregnancy, OCP → ↑ total T4/T3 (free normal); ↓ in nephrotic, steroids
Transthyretin (Prealbumin)T4 (minor)~10-15%MinorMutations → familial amyloid polyneuropathy
AlbuminCortisol, T4, T3, Steroid hormones (low affinity)RemainingMinor↓ in nephrotic, malnutrition, liver disease
DBP (Vitamin D Binding Protein)25-OH D, 1,25(OH)₂D>99%Prolongs t½GC gene polymorphisms affect levels

Free Hormone Hypothesis: Only free (unbound) hormone is biologically active. Total hormone assays can be misleading if binding proteins altered.

8. Clearance and Half-life

HormonePrimary ClearanceHalf-lifeClinical Implication
Peptide (Insulin, ACTH, GH)Renal filtration, hepatic/renal degradationMinutes (3-30)Frequent dosing needed; C-peptide (t½ 30min) > Insulin (t½ 5min)
Steroid (Cortisol, Aldosterone)Hepatic conjugation (glucuronidation/sulphation) → Renal excretion60-90 minOnce/twice daily dosing; Renal/hepatic impairment → accumulation
Thyroid (T4)Deiodination, conjugation, biliary excretion5-7 daysSlow onset/offset; steady state ~6 weeks
Thyroid (T3)Deiodination, conjugation1 dayFaster onset; used in myxoedema coma
CatecholaminesMAO/COMT → VMA, MetanephrinesMinutesMetabolites measured (not parent hormone)

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Peptide hormone synthesisPreprohormone → Prohormone (ER) → Active hormone + C-peptide (Golgi) → Secretory granules
Steroid synthesisCholesterol → Pregnenolone (STAR + CYP11A1, rate-limiting) → Δ5/Δ4 pathways
Rate-limiting steroid stepCholesterol → Pregnenolone (STAR + CYP11A1)
21-hydroxylase deficiencyMost common CAH (95%) → Salt-wasting (aldosterone ↓) + Virilisation (androgens ↑)
11β-hydroxylase deficiencyHTN (DOC ↑) + Virilisation (androgens ↑)
17α-hydroxylase deficiencyHTN (mineralocorticoid excess) + Sexual infantilism (sex steroids ↓)
T4 synthesisIodide → NIS → TPO (iodination + coupling) → Thyroglobulin colloid → TSH → Endocytosis → T4/T3 release
TPO functionIodination + Coupling; Anti-TPO in Hashimoto; PTU inhibits
DeiodinasesDIO1/DIO2 = T4→T3 (activation); DIO3 = Inactivation; Selenium-dependent
STAR proteinCholesterol transfer to inner mitochondrial membrane; Mutations → Lipoid CAH
Catecholamine synthesisTyrosine → L-DOPA → Dopamine → Noradrenaline → (PNMT) → Adrenaline
PNMTCortisol-induced; Adrenal medulla specific; Distinguishes adrenal vs extra-adrenal phaeo
Free hormone hypothesisOnly unbound hormone active; Measure free T4/T3, not total if TBG altered
CBG/SHBG/TBG↑ in pregnancy/OCP → Total hormone ↑ but Free normal
Half-livesPeptides: minutes; Steroids: hours; T4: days (5-7d); T3: 1 day; Catecholamines: minutes

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Total vs Free hormoneTotal = Free + Bound; Only Free is active. If TBG/CBG/SHBG altered (pregnancy, OCP, nephrotic), Total misleads → Measure Free
C-peptide vs InsulinC-peptide = endogenous secretion marker (not in exogenous insulin); t½ 30min vs Insulin 5min; No hepatic extraction
T4 vs T3 half-lifeT4: 5-7 days (bound to TBG); T3: 1 day (less bound); T3 = 4x potency
DHEA vs AndrostenedioneDHEA = 17,20-lyase product (adrenal); Androstenedione = DHEA conversion (adrenal/gonadal)
Cortisol vs CorticosteroneCortisol = 11β-hydroxylase product (human glucocorticoid); Corticosterone = rodent glucocorticoid

Mnemonic: STEROID SYNTHESIS PATHWAY

  • STAR moves cholesterol in
  • The first step is CYP11A1 (Pregnenolone)
  • Enzyme CYP17A1 makes sex steroids
  • Rate-limiting = STAR + CYP11A1
  • Other enzymes: CYP21 (cortisol/aldo), CYP11B1/B2
  • Inherited deficiencies = CAH types
  • Drug PTU inhibits TPO (not steroid synthesis)

Mnemonic: THYROID HORMONE SYNTHESIS

  • NIS brings iodide in
  • Iodination by TPO
  • Thyroglobulin stores it
  • Release by TSH (endocytosis)
  • Organification = TPO iodination + coupling
  • Inactivation by DIO3
  • Deiodination: DIO1/DIO2 make T3

15. Flashcards

  • Q: Peptide hormone synthesis

A: Preprohormone → Prohormone (ER) → Active hormone + C-peptide (Golgi) → Granules → Regulated secretion (Ca2+)

  • Q: Steroid synthesis rate-limiting

A: Cholesterol → Pregnenolone via STAR + CYP11A1 (P450scc) in inner mitochondrial membrane

  • Q: 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95% CAH)

A: ↓ Cortisol, ↓ Aldosterone → ↑ 17-OH Progesterone → ↑ Androgens → Salt-wasting + Virilisation

  • Q: 11β-hydroxylase deficiency

A: ↓ Cortisol → ↑ 11-Deoxycortisol + ↑ DOC (mineralocorticoid) → HTN + Virilisation

  • Q: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency

A: ↓ Sex steroids → Sexual infantilism; ↑ Mineralocorticoids (DOC) → HTN

  • Q: T4 synthesis steps

A: Iodide → NIS → TPO (iodination + coupling on Thyroglobulin) → Colloid storage → TSH → Endocytosis → T4/T3 release

  • Q: Deiodinases

A: DIO1/DIO2 (Se-dependent): T4→T3 activation; DIO3: Inactivation (rT3, T2)

  • Q: Catecholamine pathway

A: Tyrosine → DOPA → Dopamine → Noradrenaline → (PNMT, cortisol-induced) → Adrenaline

  • Q: Transport proteins

A: CBG: Cortisol (~90%); SHBG: Testosterone/Oestradiol; TBG: T4>T3 (99.97%); Albumin: Low affinity carrier

  • Q: Free hormone hypothesis

A: Only free (unbound) hormone is biologically active; Total hormone misleading if binding proteins altered

  • Q: Half-lives

A: Peptides: 3-30 min; Steroids: 60-90 min; T4: 5-7 d; T3: 1 d; Catecholamines: min

  • Q: STAR protein

A: Cholesterol transfer to inner mitochondrial membrane; Mutations → Lipoid CAH (lethal salt-wasting)

  • Q: Anti-TPO antibodies

A: Hashimoto's (90-95%), Graves (80%), Postpartum thyroiditis; PTU inhibits TPO

  • Q: C-peptide vs Insulin

A: C-peptide: Endogenous secretion marker, t½ 30 min, no hepatic extraction; Insulin: t½ 5 min

16. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A. Peptide/protein hormones (e.g., insulin, GH, ACTH) — Peptide hormones are synthesised as preprohormones in the rough ER; steroids are synthesised in smooth ER/mitochondria from cholesterol; thyroid hormones from tyrosine/iodide in follicular cells; catecholamines from tyrosine in adrenal medulla.
  1. A. Cholesterol → Pregnenolone (STAR + CYP11A1) — The conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone by STAR and CYP11A1 (P450scc) is the rate-limiting step in all steroid hormone synthesis.
  1. A. CYP21A2 — 21-hydroxylase deficiency accounts for ~95% of congenital adrenal hyperplasia cases.
  1. A. 5-7 days; TBG — T4 is bound to thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG), giving it a half-life of 5-7 days. T3 has a half-life of ~1 day.
  1. A. TBG, CBG, SHBG (all increased) — Oestrogen (pregnancy, OCP) increases hepatic synthesis of TBG, CBG, and SHBG, raising total hormone levels while free levels remain normal.
  1. A. Endogenous insulin secretion — C-peptide is co-secreted with insulin in equimolar amounts but is not present in exogenous insulin preparations, making it a marker of endogenous beta-cell function.
  1. A. DIO1 and DIO2 — Both DIO1 (liver, kidney) and DIO2 (brain, pituitary, brown fat) convert T4 to T3 (activation). DIO3 inactivates T4/T3.
  1. A. CYP17A1 — CYP17A1 has both 17α-hydroxylase and 17,20-lyase activities; the latter is required for sex steroid synthesis.
  1. A. Cortisol — PNMT (phenylethanolamine N-methyltransferase) is cortisol-induced and converts noradrenaline to adrenaline specifically in the adrenal medulla.
  1. A. Only unbound hormone is biologically active — The free hormone hypothesis states that only the unbound fraction of hormone can cross cell membranes and exert biological effects.

SBAs

  1. A. Increased TBG → elevated total T4, free T4 normal — OCP increases TBG, raising total T4; free T4 and TSH remain normal. This is a classic "euthyroid hyperthyroxinaemia" pattern.
  1. A. C-peptide level — C-peptide is not present in exogenous insulin and has a longer half-life; elevated C-peptide with hypoglycaemia confirms endogenous hyperinsulinaemia.
  1. A. 17-OH Progesterone → Androstenedione/Testosterone — In 21-hydroxylase deficiency, 17-OH progesterone accumulates and is shunted to androgen synthesis, causing virilisation.
  1. A. Adrenal crisis (HPA suppression) — >3 weeks of exogenous steroids causes HPA axis suppression; abrupt withdrawal leads to adrenal crisis due to inability to mount stress cortisol response.
  1. A. T4 (5-7 days) — T4 bound to TBG has the longest half-life (5-7 days) compared to T3 (1 day), cortisol (60-90 min), insulin (5 min), ACTH (10 min).
  1. A. Hashimoto's thyroiditis — Anti-TPO antibodies are present in 90-95% of Hashimoto's, ~80% of Graves, and postpartum thyroiditis.
  1. A. 17α-hydroxylase deficiency — Impaired sex steroid synthesis (sexual infantilism) with mineralocorticoid excess (DOC) causing hypertension and hypokalaemia.
  1. A. PNMT converts noradrenaline to adrenaline in adrenal medulla — PNMT is adrenal medulla-specific (cortisol-induced), distinguishing adrenal from extra-adrenal phaeochromocytoma.
  1. A. Peripheral T4 to T3 conversion (DIOs) — Deiodinases (DIO1, DIO2) are selenoenzymes; selenium deficiency impairs peripheral T4→T3 conversion.
  1. A. Low albumin → low total T4, free T4 normal — Nephrotic syndrome reduces albumin (low-affinity thyroid hormone carrier), lowering total T4 but free T4 and TSH remain normal.
