topics.title: Empty Sella Syndrome
topics.html:
[!info]
Empty Sella Syndrome (ESS) = Enlarged Sella Turcica with Flattened/Attenuated Pituitary Gland. Primary (Idiopathic) vs Secondary (Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan). Most are Asymptomatic Incidental Findings; Hypopituitarism in 10-15%. Not a tumour.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define Empty Sella Syndrome and classify Primary vs Secondary
  • [ ] Recognise radiological criteria on MRI/CT
  • [ ] Differentiate from Pituitary Adenoma, Arachnoid Cyst, Craniopharyngioma
  • [ ] Assess pituitary function and manage hypopituitarism if present
  • [ ] Counsel patients on prognosis and surveillance

2. Definition & Classification

TypeDefinitionMechanism
Primary (Idiopathic) ESSEnlarged Sella + Atresia of Diaphragma Sellae → CSF Pulsations → Pituitary FlatteningCongenital Diaphragma Sellae Defect → CSF Pulsations → Pituitary Atrophy
Secondary ESSPost-Destructive (Surgery, Radiotherapy, Haemorrhage, Infection, Sheehan Syndrome)Pituitary Destruction → CSF Fills Sella
FeaturePrimary ESSSecondary ESS
AetiologyCongenital Diaphragma Sellae DefectPituitary Destruction (Surgery, RT, Apoplexy, Sheehan, Infection)
DemographicsWomen > Men (4:1); 30-60 yearsAny Age; Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan
Pituitary Height<2mm (Flattened)Variable
Pituitary FunctionMostly Normal (85-90%)Hypopituitarism Common (50-70%)
Associated ConditionsObesity, Hypertension, Idiopathic Intracranial HypertensionSheehan, Post-Surgery, RT, Apoplexy

3. Radiological Diagnosis (MRI)

Diagnostic Criteria (MRI)

CriterionFinding
Sella VolumeEnlarged (Volume >1000 mm³ or Area >500 mm²)
Pituitary Height<2mm (Flattened against Sella Floor)
Pituitary Volume<20% of Sella Volume
CSF SignalCSF Signal Intensity Filling Sella (T1 Hypointense, T2 Hyperintense)
Pituitary StalkCentral / Slightly Deviated; Intact
Diaphragma SellaeIncompetent/Absent (CSF Pulsations Visible)

Imaging Modalities

ModalityRole
MRI (T1/T2, Sagittal/Coronal, 3mm)Gold Standard; Pituitary Gland, Stalk, Optic Chiasm, Diaphragma
CT (Thin Slices)Bony Sella Enlargement; Calcifications; If MRI Contraindicated

Differential Diagnosis on Imaging

FindingEmpty SellaPituitary AdenomaArachnoid CystCraniopharyngioma
Pituitary GlandFlattened (<2mm)Mass Expanding SellaAbsent/DisplacedDisplaced/Invaded
CSF in SellaYes (Fills Sella)No (Solid/Cystic)Yes (CSF Signal)Cystic + Solid (Calcifications)
Pituitary StalkCentralDisplaced/EncasedDisplacedDisplaced/Invaded
Diaphragma SellaeIncompetentIntactIntactIntact/Disrupted
EnhancementNone (Pituitary Thin)Avid EnhancementNo EnhancementRim + Solid Enhancement + Calcifications

4. Clinical Presentation

ScenarioFrequencyDetails
Asymptomatic Incidentaloma85-90% (Primary ESS)Found on MRI for Headache/Dizziness/Trauma
Headache20-30%Non-specific; May Relate to Intracranial Hypertension
Visual Disturbances<10%Chiasmal Compression (Rare; ESS Usually No Chiasmal Compression)
Hypopituitarism10-15% (Primary ESS); 50-70% (Secondary ESS)GH > Gonadotrophs > TSH > ACTH
Cerebrospinal Fluid LeakRareCSF Rhinorrhoea (Defective Diaphragma Sellae)
Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)AssociationObese Women; Papilloedema; ESS Common in IIH

Hypopituitarism in ESS

AxisFrequency (Primary ESS)Frequency (Secondary ESS)
GH10-15%40-50%
Gonadotrophs5-10%30-40%
TSH5-10%20-30%
ACTH<5%10-20%

Order of Loss: GH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH (Same as General Hypopituitarism)

5. Aetiology & Pathophysiology

Primary (Idiopathic) ESS

MechanismDetails
Diaphragma Sellae DefectCongenital Incompetence/Absence of Diaphragma Sellae
CSF PulsationsPulsatile CSF Pressure → Pituitary Flattening Against Sella Floor
Risk FactorsFemale Gender, Obesity, Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH), Hypertension
Pituitary FunctionMostly Preserved (Pituitary Tissue Compressed but Viable)

Secondary ESS

CauseMechanism
Pituitary SurgeryTranssphenoidal Resection → Pituitary Volume Loss
RadiotherapyRadiation-Induced Atrophy + Fibrosis
Pituitary ApoplexyHaemorrhagic Infarction → Necrosis + CSF Replacement
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis → Empty Sella
Radiation NecrosisDelayed (Years Post-RT)
Infection/InflammationMeningitis, Sarcoidosis, Lymphocytic Hypophysitis

6. Clinical Presentation

SymptomFrequencySignificance
Asymptomatic80-90% (Primary)Incidental MRI Finding
Headache20-30%Non-specific; May Relate to IIH
Visual Disturbances<5%ESS Usually Does Not Compress Chiasm (Unlike Adenoma)
Hypopituitarism10-15% (Primary)Screen if Symptomatic
CSF RhinorrhoeaRareDefective Diaphragma Sellae
Secondary ESS50-70% HypopituitarismSheehan, Post-Surgery, RT, Apoplexy

7. Diagnosis

MRI Diagnostic Criteria (Primary ESS)

CriterionThreshold
Sella EnlargementAnteroposterior Diameter >12mm (or Volume >1000 mm³)
Pituitary Height<2mm (or <3mm in some definitions)
Pituitary Volume<20% of Sella Volume
CSF in SellaCSF Signal Intensity (T2 Hyperintense) Filling Sella
Pituitary StalkCentral / Slightly Deviated, Intact
Diaphragma SellaeIncompetent/Absent (Visible CSF Pulsations)

Assessment of Pituitary Function (All ESS)

AxisTestsInterpretation
GH/IGF-1IGF-1 (Age-Adjusted)Low = GHD
GonadalLH/FSH + Testosterone/OestradiolLow = Central Hypogonadism
ThyroidfT4 + TSHLow fT4 + Low/Normal TSH = Central Hypo
Adrenal09:00 Cortisol + ACTHLow Cortisol + Low/Normal ACTH = Secondary AI
ProlactinProlactinNormal or Mildly ↑ (Stalk Effect)

8. Management

Asymptomatic Primary ESS (Normal Pituitary Function)

ApproachDetails
ReassuranceNot a Tumour; Benign Radiological Finding
SurveillanceAnnual Clinical Review for 2-3 Years; Repeat MRI if New Symptoms
Hormonal ScreeningBaseline Hormones; Repeat if Symptoms Develop

Symptomatic Primary ESS (Hypopituitarism)

DeficiencyReplacement
GH DeficiencyRH-GH 0.1-0.3 mg/day SC (Adults); 0.025-0.035 mg/kg/day (Children)
Gonadotroph DeficiencyTestosterone (Male) / HRT (Female)
TSH DeficiencyLevothyroxine (Start BEFORE Glucocorticoid)
ACTH DeficiencyHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
MonitoringAnnual Hormones for 2-3 Years; Then if Stable, Less Frequent

Secondary ESS (Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan)

ApproachDetails
Full Hormonal AssessmentAll Anterior Pituitary Axes + Prolactin
Aggressive ReplacementHigher Likelihood of Panhypopituitarism
Sheehan SyndromeAgalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Hair Loss; Empty Sella on MRI; Lifelong Replacement
Post-RTSurveillance for 10+ Years (Delayed Hypopituitarism)
Post-SurgeryImmediate Post-op Hormonal Assessment; Replace if Deficient

9. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionKey Differentiators
Pituitary AdenomaSolid Mass Expanding Sella; Enhancement; Stalk Displacement
Arachnoid CystCystic, CSF Signal, Thin Wall, No Pituitary Tissue
CraniopharyngiomaSuprasellar; Calcifications; Rim Enhancement; Stalk Displacement
Rathke's Cleft CystMidline Cystic; No Enhancement; Between Ant/Post Pituitary
MeningiomaDural Tail; Homogeneous Enhancement; Suprasellar
Hypothalamic GliomaSuprasellar; Infiltrative; Optic Pathway Involvement

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ESS DefinitionSella Enlarged + Pituitary Flattened (<2mm) + CSF-filled Sella
Primary vs SecondaryPrimary = Idiopathic (Diaphragma Defect); Secondary = Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan
Primary ESS DemographicsWomen 4:1; Age 30-60; Obesity, Hypertension, IIH Association
Symptoms85-90% Asymptomatic (Incidental); Headache 20-30%; Visual Loss Rare
Hypopituitarism in Primary ESS10-15% (Mostly GH/Gonadotrophs); ACTH Rare
Hypopituitarism in Secondary ESS50-70% (Sheehan, Post-Surgery, RT)
ESS vs AdenomaESS = Flattened Pituitary + CSF in Sella; Adenoma = Mass + Enhancement
ESS vs Arachnoid CystArachnoid Cyst = Cystic + Thin Wall; ESS = Pituitary Tissue Present (Flattened)
Pituitary Stalk in ESSCentral / Intact (vs Displaced in Adenoma)
Visual Fields in ESSUsually Normal (No Chiasmal Compression)
CSF RhinorrhoeaRare; Defective Diaphragma Sellae
IIH AssociationESS Common in IIH (Obese Women, Papilloedema)
Management Primary ESSAsymptomatic = Reassure + Annual Review; Symptomatic = Replace Deficiencies

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
ESS vs Pituitary AdenomaESS = Flattened Pituitary + CSF in Sella; Adenoma = Mass + Enhancement
ESS vs Arachnoid CystArachnoid Cyst = Pure Cystic CSF; ESS = Flattened Pituitary Tissue Present
Primary vs Secondary ESSPrimary = Idiopathic (Women, Obesity, IIH); Secondary = Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan
ESS vs SheehanSheehan = Postpartum Necrosis → Secondary ESS; Primary ESS = Idiopathic
ESS vs Rathke's CystRathke's = Midline Cyst Between Ant/Post Pituitary; ESS = Flattened Pituitary in Sella
Visual Fields in ESSUsually Normal (No Chiasmal Compression)
Pituitary Stalk in ESSCentral/Intact (vs Displaced in Adenoma)

Mnemonic: EMPTY SELLA

  • Empty = Pituitary Flattened <2mm
  • Mostly Women (4:1)
  • Primary (Idiopathic) vs Secondary (Surgery/RT/Sheehan)
  • Thyroid/Adrenal/Gonadal Usually Normal (Primary)
  • Young Women: Obesity + IIH Association
  • Sella Enlarged + CSF Filled
  • ESS = Incidentaloma 85-90%
  • Leads to Hypopituitarism Rarely (10-15% Primary)
  • Leads to HYPOPIT in Secondary (50-70%)
  • As Symptomatic = Replace Deficiencies

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Obese women 30-50yr with hypertension — Primary empty sella demographics:
  1. MRI: CSF signal in sella (empty sella) vs enhancing mass (adenoma) — Empty sella vs pituitary adenoma:
  1. Physiological pituitary enlargement may fill sella; monitor for hypopituitarism — Empty sella and pregnancy:

SBAs

  1. Reassure; screen for hypopituitarism annually; no surgery — 40yo woman: incidental empty sella on MRI, normal hormones. Management?
  1. Hormone replacement (levothyroxine, sex hormones, hydrocortisone); monitor — Same patient: 2yr later develops fatigue, amenorrhoea. Hormones: low LH/FSH, TSH, cortisol. Management?
  1. Secondary empty sella (post-surgical atrophy) — Post-pituitary surgery: empty sella on MRI, panhypopituitarism. Cause?
  1. Ophthalmology referral; consider surgery if chiasm compression — Empty sella with visual field defects. Management?
  1. Screen for hypopituitarism; physiological pituitary enlargement may fill sella — Empty sella in pregnancy. Monitoring?

topics.title: Fsh Lh Gonadal Axis
topics.html:
[!info]
HPG axis = GnRH → FSH/LH → Oestradiol/Testosterone. Central to puberty, reproduction, gonadal function, and sex steroid feedback. Dysregulation → Hypogonadism, PCOS, precocious/delayed puberty, infertility.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe the HPG axis anatomy, hormone synthesis, and regulation
  • [ ] Interpret FSH/LH patterns for primary vs secondary hypogonadism
  • [ ] Apply GnRH stimulation and sex steroid feedback tests
  • [ ] Diagnose and manage hypogonadism, PCOS, and puberty disorders
  • [ ] Interpret inhibin B, anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) in fertility assessment

2. HPG Axis — Anatomy & Physiology

Hormone Synthesis & Secretion

StepHormoneSourceRegulation
1. GnRHGonadotropin-Releasing HormoneHypothalamus (Preoptic Area)Pulsatile; Kisspeptin (key upstream); Sex steroids (negative/positive feedback)
2. FSHFollicle-Stimulating HormoneAnterior Pituitary (Gonadotrophs)GnRH (stimulatory); Inhibin B (selective negative feedback); Oestradiol/Testosterone (general)
3. LHLuteinizing HormoneAnterior Pituitary (Gonadotrophs)GnRH (stimulatory); Oestradiol/Testosterone (negative/positive feedback)
4. Sex SteroidsOestradiol / TestosteroneGonads (Ovary: Theca+Granulosa; Testis: Leydig)LH/FSH (stimulatory); Negative feedback on GnRH/LH/FSH; Inhibin B (FSH specific)

Hormone Structure

HormoneStructureShared Subunit
FSHα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (FSH-specific)α-subunit (shared with LH, TSH, hCG)
LHα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (LH-specific)α-subunit (shared with FSH, TSH, hCG)
hCGα-subunit + β-subunitSimilar to LH (cross-reacts with LH receptor)

3. HPG Axis Regulation

Feedback Loops

```

GnRH (Hypothalamus) → FSH + LH (Pituitary) → Oestradiol/Testosterone (Gonads)

↑_______________|________________|

Negative Feedback (Oestradiol/Testosterone + Inhibin)

Positive Feedback (Oestradiol → LH Surge)

```

Feedback Patterns

Gonadal StatusLH/FSHOestradiol/TestosteroneInhibin BKey Features
Primary Hypogonadism↑↑↓↓Gonadal failure
Secondary Hypogonadism↓/N↓N/↓Pituitary/Hypothalamic failure
PCOSLH ↑↑, FSH N/↓ (LH:FSH >2:1)Testosterone ↑N/↓Ovarian resistance + Insulin resistance
Menopause↑↑↓↓ (Oestradiol)↓Ovarian failure
PregnancySuppressedOestradiol/Progesterone ↑↑↓High oestrogen/progesterone negative feedback
Ageing MaleSlight ↑Gradual ↓Slight ↓Late-onset hypogonadism

4. Gonadotropin Physiology

GnRH

FeatureDetails
SourceHypothalamus (Medial preoptic area)
SecretionPulsatile (every 60-90 min); Frequency encodes signal
RegulationKisspeptin (Key upstream stimulator); Sex steroids (Negative/Positive feedback); Stress, Energy status, Leptin (modulatory)
Pulse FrequencyLow (1-2/h) → FSH dominance; High (1/30-60min) → LH dominance

FSH

FeatureDetails
Structureα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (FSH-specific)
Primary RegulatorGnRH (frequency-dependent); Inhibin B (selective negative feedback)
Actions — FemaleFolliculogenesis (Granulosa cells); Oestradiol synthesis (with LH); Inhibin B production
Actions — MaleSpermatogenesis (Sertoli cells); Inhibin B production
RegulationSelective negative feedback by Inhibin B (Granulosa/Sertoli cells)

LH

FeatureDetails
Structureα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (LH-specific)
Primary RegulatorGnRH (frequency-dependent); Oestradiol/Testosterone (negative/positive feedback)
Actions — FemaleTheca cells → Androgen synthesis → Granulosa → Oestradiol; Ovulation (LH surge); Corpus luteum → Progesterone
Actions — MaleLeydig cells → Testosterone production
Positive FeedbackSustained Oestradiol >200 pg/mL × 50h → LH Surge (Ovulation)

5. Gonadal Steroid Physiology

Testosterone

FeatureMaleFemale
SourceLeydig cells (95%), Adrenal (5%)Ovary (Theca), Adrenal
BindingSHBG ~60%, Albumin ~38%, Free ~2%SHBG ↑ in women
Metabolism5α-Reductase → DHT (potent); Aromatase → OestradiolAromatisation
RegulationLH → Leydig cells → Testosterone; Negative feedback on LH/FSHPart of ovarian steroidogenesis

Oestradiol

FeatureFemaleMale
SourceOvary (Granulosa: Aromatisation of Androstenedione)Testis (Sertoli), Adipose (Aromatase)
BindingSHBG ~60%, Albumin ~38%, Free ~2%SHBG ~60%
RegulationFSH + LH → Theca Androgens → Granulosa Aromatase → OestradiolAromatase in Adipose, Brain, Bone

6. HPG Axis Patterns — Diagnostic Key

Hypogonadism Classification

TypeLH/FSHTestosterone/OestradiolInhibin B/AMHCommon Causes
Primary (Hypergonadotropic)↑↑↓↓Klinefelter, Turner, Mumps, Chemo, Autoimmune, Genetic
Secondary (Hypogonadotropic)↓/N↓N/↓Pituitary adenoma, Hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann, Functional
Functional (Stress/Weight/Exercise)↓/N↓/N↓Anorexia, Athletics, Stress, Obesity

Male Hypogonadism — Key Patterns

TypeTestosteroneLH/FSHClinical Features
Primary<12 nmol/L↑↑Klinefelter, Mumps, Chemo, Autoimmune, Ageing
Secondary<12 nmol/L↓/NPituitary adenoma, Hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann, Obesity
Late-Onset (LOH)8-12 nmol/LN/↑Ageing, Obesity, Comorbidities

Female Hypogonadism — Key Patterns

TypeOestradiolLH/FSHClinical Features
POI (<40y)↓↑↑Turner, FMR1, Autoimmune, Chemo, Idiopathic
Secondary Amenorrhoea↓↓/NPCOS, Hyperprolactinaemia, Functional, Thyroid
Menopause↓↓↑↑Natural ovarian failure

7. PCOS — Rotterdam Criteria (2 of 3)

CriterionDefinition
1. Oligo/anovulation<8 cycles/year or Cycle >35 days
2. HyperandrogenismClinical (Hirsutism, Acne, Alopecia) OR Biochemical (Testosterone >2.5-3 nmol/L, FAI >5)
3. Polycystic Ovaries (US)≥20 follicles (2-9mm) per ovary OR Ovarian volume >10 mL

Must Exclude: CAH (17-OHP), Androgen-secreting tumours, Cushing, Thyroid disease, Hyperprolactinaemia.

8. PCOS — Management

TargetIntervention
Menstrual RegulationCOCP (Oestradiol + Cyproterone/Drospirenone) OR Cyclical Progesterone (Medroxyprogesterone 10mg 10d/month)
Hirsutism/AcneCOCP → Add Anti-androgen (Spironolactone 50-100mg, Cyproterone, Flutamide) + Topical Eflornithine
Metabolic/IRMetformin 500mg BD → 1g BD (if BMI ≥25 or IGT)
Weight Loss5-10% wt loss → Restores ovulation in 50%
Ovulation InductionLetrozole 2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (1st line) → Clomifene → Gonadotrophins → IVF
Long-termScreen T2DM (OGTT q1-3y), Lipids, BP, Endometrial cancer risk

9. Puberty & Growth

Normal Puberty Onset

SexThelarche/Genital GrowthPeak Height VelocityMenarche/Spermarche
Female8-13 years (Avg 10-11)11-12 years12-13 years (Avg 12.5)
Male9-14 years (Avg 11-12)13-14 years13-14 years (Spermarche)

Delayed Puberty

DefinitionFemale: No thelarche by 13; Male: No testicular enlargement by 14
Constitutional DelayMost common; Family history; Normal growth velocity; Bone age delayed
Hypogonadotropic HypogonadismKallmann syndrome (Anosmia + HH); Isolated HH; Chronic disease
Hypergonadotropic HypogonadismTurner (45,X), Klinefelter (47,XXY), Autoimmune, Chemotherapy

Precocious Puberty

DefinitionFemale: <8 years; Male: <9 years
Central (GnRH-dependent)True puberty early; CPP; Idiopathic, CNS tumours, Hamartoma
Peripheral (GnRH-independent)McCune-Albright (GNAS), Adrenal androgen excess, Ovarian/Testicular tumours

10. Infertility — Endocrine Workup

PartnerTests
FemaleTSH, Prolactin, FSH/LH (Day 2-4), Oestradiol (Day 2-4), AMH, Progesterone (Day 21), US Pelvis (AFC, Fibroids), HSG
MaleSemen Analysis (WHO 2021), Testosterone, LH, FSH, Prolactin, US Scrotum, Genetic (if severe)
TimingInvestigate after 12 months (or 6 months if female >35)

11. Contraception — Endocrine Methods

MethodEfficacy (Typical)Key Contraindications
COCP91%VTE, Breast cancer, Migraine with aura, Smoker >35, BP >160/100
POP (Desogestrel)91%Active breast cancer, Unexplained bleeding
Injectable (DMPA)94%Osteoporosis risk (long-term), Weight gain, Delayed fertility return
Implant (ENG)99.9%Active VTE, Breast cancer
IUS (LNG 52mg)99.8%Uterine anomaly, Active pelvic infection
Copper IUD99.2%Heavy periods, Copper allergy, Wilson's disease

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
HPG AxisGnRH → FSH/LH → Oestradiol/Testosterone; Negative feedback + Positive feedback (LH surge)
GnRH PulsatilityLow freq (1-2/h) → FSH dominant; High freq (1/30-60min) → LH dominant
Primary HypogonadismLH/FSH ↑↑, Testosterone/Oestradiol ↓ — Gonadal failure
Secondary HypogonadismLH/FSH ↓/N, Testosterone/Oestradiol ↓ — Pituitary/Hypothalamic failure
PCOS2/3 Rotterdam: Oligo-ovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCO morphology
PCOS 1st Line OvulationLetrozole 2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (Superior to Clomifene)
Male HypogonadismTestosterone <12 nmol/L; LH/FSH ↑ = Primary; LH/FSH ↓ = Secondary
Kallmann SyndromeHypogonadotropic hypogonadism + Anosmia (KAL1/ANOS1 gene)
POIFSH >40 IU/L on 2 occasions 4-6wk apart; Age <40; HRT until 50
Menopause HRTOestrogen only (Hysterectomy); Combined (Intact uterus); Lowest effective dose
InfertilityInvestigate after 12mo (6mo if female >35); AMH = Ovarian reserve marker
AMHGranulosa cell product; Reflects antral follicle count; ↓ with age

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
FSH vs LH in PCOSLH > FSH (LH:FSH >2:1); FSH relatively lower
Primary vs Secondary HypogonadismPrimary = Gonad failure → LH/FSH ↑; Secondary = Pituitary failure → LH/FSH ↓
GnRH Pulse FrequencyLow freq (1-2/h) → FSH dominant; High freq (1/30-60min) → LH dominant
AMH vs FSHAMH = Ovarian reserve (stable through cycle); FSH = Cycle-dependent (Day 2-4)
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole superior for ovulation in PCOS (higher live birth, lower multiples)
Kallmann SyndromeHypogonadotropic hypogonadism + Anosmia (KAL1/ANOS1 gene)
FAIFree Androgen Index = (Testosterone/SHBG) × 100; >5 = Hyperandrogenism

14. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((FSH/LH/Gonadal Axis))

HPG Axis

GnRH (Pulsatile) → FSH + LH → Sex Steroids

Feedback: Negative (Steroids + Inhibin) + Positive (Oestradiol→LH Surge)

Hormones

GnRH: Pulsatile, Kisspeptin regulator

FSH: Folliculogenesis, Spermatogenesis, Inhibin B

LH: Ovulation, Leydig cells, Theca cells

Inhibin B: Selective FSH feedback

AMH: Ovarian reserve (Granulosa cells)

Patterns

Primary Hypo: LH/FSH ↑↑, Steroid ↓

Secondary Hypo: LH/FSH ↓/N, Steroid ↓

PCOS: LH ↑↑, LH:FSH >2:1, Androgens ↑

PCOS

Rotterdam: 2/3 (Oligo + Hyperandrogen + PCO)

1st Line Ovulation: Letrozole

Metabolic: Metformin if BMI≥25/IGT

Puberty

Delayed: Constitutional vs Hypogonadotropic

Precocious: Central (CPP) vs Peripheral (PPP)

Infertility

Female: AMH, AFC, TSH, PRL, FSH/LH, HSG

Male: Semen Analysis, Testosterone, LH/FSH

Contraception

COCP, POP, Implant, IUS, DMPA, Cu-IUD

```

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point

GnRH PulsatilityLow freq (1-2/h) → FSH dominant; High freq (1/30-60min) → LH dominant

PCOS2/3 Rotterdam: Oligo-ovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCO morphology

Kallmann SyndromeHypogonadotropic hypogonadism + Anosmia (KAL1/ANOS1 gene)
POIFSH >40 IU/L on 2 occasions 4-6wk apart; Age <40; HRT until 50
Menopause HRTOestrogen only (Hysterectomy); Combined (Intact uterus); Lowest effective dose
InfertilityInvestigate after 12mo (6mo if female >35); AMH = Ovarian reserve marker
AMHGranulosa cell product; Reflects antral follicle count; ↓ with age

16. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
FSH vs LH in PCOSLH > FSH (LH:FSH >2:1); FSH relatively lower
Primary vs Secondary HypogonadismPrimary = Gonad failure → LH/FSH ↑; Secondary = Pituitary failure → LH/FSH ↓

17. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((FSH/LH/Gonadal Axis))

HPG Axis

GnRH (Pulsatile) → FSH + LH → Sex Steroids

Feedback: Negative (Steroids + Inhibin) + Positive (Oestradiol→LH Surge)

Hormones

GnRH: Pulsatile, Kisspeptin regulator

FSH: Folliculogenesis, Spermatogenesis, Inhibin B

LH: Ovulation, Leydig cells, Theca cells

Inhibin B: Selective FSH feedback

AMH: Ovarian reserve (Granulosa cells)

Patterns
```

18. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis|Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[ACTH/Cortisol Axis]], [[TSH/Thyroid Axis]], [[GH/IGF-1 Axis]], [[Male Hypogonadism]], [[Female Hypogonadism]], [[PCOS]], [[Feedback Loops]]

19. MCQs (10)

  1. GnRH pulse frequency determines gonadotropin profile: high frequency favours:

A. LH

B. FSH

C. Prolactin

D. TSH

E. GH

  1. FSH acts on which gonadal cells:

A. Sertoli cells (testis) + Granulosa cells (ovary)

B. Leydig cells

C. Theca cells

D. Adrenal cortex

E. Pituitary

  1. LH acts on which gonadal cells:

A. Leydig cells (testis) + Theca cells (ovary)

B. Sertoli cells

C. Granulosa cells

D. Adrenal medulla

E. Thyroid

  1. Inhibin B is produced by:

A. Sertoli cells (M) + Granulosa cells (F)

B. Leydig cells

C. Theca cells

D. Adrenal

E. Pituitary

  1. Testosterone negative feedback primarily on:

A. Hypothalamus (GnRH pulse) + Pituitary (LH)

B. FSH only

C. Prolactin

D. TSH

E. ACTH

  1. Estradiol negative feedback (early follicular):

A. Pituitary (↓ FSH > LH)

B. Hypothalamus only

C. Adrenal

D. Thyroid

E. No feedback

  1. Estradiol positive feedback (mid-cycle):

A. High sustained estradiol → ↑ GnRH/LH surge

B. Low estradiol

C. Progesterone only

D. Inhibin only

E. Testosterone

  1. Kallmann syndrome:

A. GnRH deficiency + anosmia (impaired neuronal migration)

B. Pituitary failure

C. Gonadal failure

D. Thyroid failure

E. Adrenal failure

  1. PCOS gonadotropin pattern:

A. LH:FSH ratio >2-3:1 (↑ LH amplitude/frequency)

B. FSH > LH

C. Normal LH:FSH

D. Low LH and FSH

E. Only FSH elevated

  1. Menopause gonadotropins:

A. FSH > LH; both markedly elevated (loss of inhibin/estradiol feedback)

B. LH > FSH

C. Normal

D. Low

E. Only FSH elevated

20. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 25yo man: delayed puberty, anosmia, low testosterone, low LH/FSH. MRI: absent olfactory bulbs. Diagnosis?

A. Kallmann syndrome (GnRH deficiency + anosmia)

B. Pituitary macroadenoma

C. Primary hypogonadism

D. Constitutional delay

E. Hyperprolactinaemia

  1. 30yo woman: oligomenorrhoea, hirsutism, LH 18, FSH 6, testosterone 3.5. Ultrasound: polycystic ovaries. Diagnosis?

A. PCOS (Rotterdam criteria: 2/3 oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, PCO morphology)

B. CAH

C. Cushing

D. Ovarian tumour

E. Thyroid disorder

  1. 52yo woman: amenorrhoea 12 months, flushes, FSH 85, LH 45, estradiol <50. Management?

A. HRT if symptomatic (oestrogen + progestogen if uterus intact); bone protection

B. Combined OCP

C. Clomiphene

D. GnRH agonist

E. No treatment needed

  1. Male hypogonadism: low testosterone, high LH/FSH. Cause?

A. Primary testicular failure (Klinefelter, mumps, chemo, trauma)

B. Pituitary tumour

C. Kallmann

D. Hyperprolactinaemia

E. Obesity

  1. Female athlete: amenorrhoea, low BMI, low LH/FSH, low estradiol. Diagnosis?

A. Functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea (energy deficit)

B. PCOS

C. Premature ovarian insufficiency

D. Prolactinoma

E. Thyroid disease

21. Flashcards

  • Q: GnRH pulse generator

A: Hypothalamic arcuate nucleus (KNDy neurons: kisspeptin/neurokinin B/dynorphin)

  • Q: GnRH frequency

A: High freq → LH; Low freq → FSH

  • Q: FSH action

A: Sertoli (M): inhibin B, spermatogenesis support; Granulosa (F): follicular growth, estradiol

  • Q: LH action

A: Leydig (M): testosterone; Theca (F): androgens → granulosa aromatase → estradiol

  • Q: Inhibin B

A: Sertoli/Granulosa → selectively suppresses FSH

  • Q: Testosterone feedback

A: Hypothalamus (↓ GnRH pulse) + Pituitary (↓ LH)

  • Q: Estradiol feedback

A: Early follicular: -ve on FSH; Mid-cycle sustained high: +ve LH surge

  • Q: Progesterone feedback

A: Luteal phase: -ve on GnRH pulse frequency (↓ LH pulse)

  • Q: Kallmann syndrome

A: GnRH deficiency + anosmia; X-linked (KAL1/ANOS1) or autosomal; treat: hCG/FSH or testosterone

  • Q: PCOS gonadotropins

A: LH:FSH >2-3:1; ↑ LH pulse amplitude/frequency; ↑ ovarian androgen production

  • Q: Menopause

A: Loss of inhibin B + estradiol → FSH ↑↑ > LH ↑; FSH > LH

  • Q: Premature ovarian insufficiency

A: <40y; FSH >25 on 2 occasions 4wk apart; karyotype, FMR1, autoimmune screen

  • Q: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea

A: Energy deficit (low BMI, exercise, stress) → ↓ GnRH pulse → low LH/FSH/estradiol

  • Q: Male hypogonadism types

A: Primary: high LH/FSH, low T; Secondary: low/normal LH/FSH, low T

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. LH — GnRH pulse frequency determines gonadotropin profile: high frequency favours:
  1. Sertoli cells (testis) + Granulosa cells (ovary) — FSH acts on which gonadal cells:
  1. Leydig cells (testis) + Theca cells (ovary) — LH acts on which gonadal cells:
  1. Sertoli cells (M) + Granulosa cells (F) — Inhibin B is produced by:
  1. Hypothalamus (GnRH pulse) + Pituitary (LH) — Testosterone negative feedback primarily on:
  1. Pituitary (↓ FSH > LH) — Estradiol negative feedback (early follicular):
  1. High sustained estradiol → ↑ GnRH/LH surge — Estradiol positive feedback (mid-cycle):
  1. GnRH deficiency + anosmia (impaired neuronal migration) — Kallmann syndrome:
  1. LH:FSH ratio >2-3:1 (↑ LH amplitude/frequency) — PCOS gonadotropin pattern:
  1. FSH > LH; both markedly elevated (loss of inhibin/estradiol feedback) — Menopause gonadotropins:

SBAs

  1. Kallmann syndrome (GnRH deficiency + anosmia) — 25yo man: delayed puberty, anosmia, low testosterone, low LH/FSH. MRI: absent olfactory bulbs. Diagnosis?
  1. PCOS (Rotterdam criteria: 2/3 oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, PCO morphology) — 30yo woman: oligomenorrhoea, hirsutism, LH 18, FSH 6, testosterone 3.5. Ultrasound: polycystic ovaries. Diagnosis?
  1. HRT if symptomatic (oestrogen + progestogen if uterus intact); bone protection — 52yo woman: amenorrhoea 12 months, flushes, FSH 85, LH 45, estradiol <50. Management?
  1. Primary testicular failure (Klinefelter, mumps, chemo, trauma) — Male hypogonadism: low testosterone, high LH/FSH. Cause?
  1. Functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea (energy deficit) — Female athlete: amenorrhoea, low BMI, low LH/FSH, low estradiol. Diagnosis?

topics.title: Gh Igf 1 Axis
topics.html:
[!info]
GH/IGF-1 axis = GHRH/Somatostatin → GH → IGF-1 (Liver). Central to growth, metabolism, and body composition. Dysregulation → Acromegaly (excess), Growth Hormone Deficiency (deficiency).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe GH/IGF-1 axis anatomy, synthesis, and regulation
  • [ ] Interpret IGF-1 and GH levels for acromegaly and GHD diagnosis
  • [ ] Apply dynamic tests (OGTT suppression, ITT, Glucagon, GHRH+Arginine)
  • [ ] Differentiate acromegaly from gigantism; GHD in children vs adults
  • [ ] Outline treatment algorithms for acromegaly and GHD

2. GH/IGF-1 Axis — Anatomy & Physiology

Hormone Synthesis & Secretion

StepHormoneSourceRegulation
1. GHRHGrowth Hormone-Releasing HormoneHypothalamus (Arcuate Nucleus)Stress, Exercise, Hypoglycaemia, Protein meal ↑; Somatostatin, IGF-1, Glucose, Obesity ↓
2. SomatostatinGrowth Hormone-Inhibiting Hormone (GHIH)Hypothalamus (Periventricular Nucleus), Pancreatin D-cells, GI tractGlucose, Obesity, IGF-1, Ageing ↑; Exercise, Hypoglycaemia ↓
3. GHGrowth Hormone (Somatotropin)Anterior Pituitary (Somatotrophs, 40-50% of cells)GHRH (stimulatory); Somatostatin (inhibitory); Ghrelin (stimulatory); IGF-1 (negative feedback); Glucose, Obesity, Ageing (inhibitory)
4. IGF-1Insulin-like Growth Factor-1 (Somatomedin C)Liver (main source); Peripheral tissues (autocrine/paracrine)GH (stimulatory); Insulin, Nutrition (permissive); Ageing, Malnutrition ↓

GH Synthesis & Secretion

FeatureDetails
Structure191 amino acid polypeptide; 22kDa (major) + 20kDa variant (10%)
GeneGH1 (chromosome 17); GH2 (placental GH)
SecretionPulsatile (major peak during slow-wave sleep); Contra-pulsatile to Somatostatin
RegulationStimulatory: GHRH, Ghrelin, Hypoglycaemia, Exercise, Protein meal, Stress, Deep sleep, Sex steroids
Inhibitory: Somatostatin (GHIH), IGF-1 (negative feedback), Glucose, Insulin, Obesity, Free Fatty Acids, Ageing
Half-life20-30 minutes (short); Pulsatile (every 3-5 hours)

GH Secretion Across Lifespan

AgeGH SecretionIGF-1 Level
ChildhoodModerate, pulsatileRising
PubertyPeak secretion (sex steroid amplification)Peak
Young AdultDecliningHigh normal
AgeingProgressive decline (somatopause)Declines

3. IGF-1 (Insulin-like Growth Factor-1 / Somatomedin C)

FeatureDetails
Structure70 amino acid polypeptide; 7.6 kDa; Homologous to Proinsulin
SynthesisLiver (main endocrine source, ~75%); Peripheral tissues (autocrine/paracrine)
RegulationGH (primary driver); Insulin (permissive); Nutrition (protein/energy); Ageing ↓
Binding>99% bound to IGFBP-3 (main carrier) + ALS (Acid-Labile Subunit) → Ternary complex
Half-life12-15 hours (vs GH 20-30 min); Stable, reflects integrated GH exposure
BioactivityFree IGF-1 (1%) = biologically active; Mediates most GH anabolic effects

IGF-1 Binding Proteins

Binding Protein% Total IGF-1Function
IGFBP-3~80%Main carrier; prolongs half-life; GH-dependent
IGFBP-1~1%Insulin regulates; ↑ in fasting/catabolism
IGFBP-2~10%↑ in malnutrition, GH deficiency
ALS (Acid-Labile Subunit)Binds IGFBP-3Forms ternary complex (IGF-1 + IGFBP-3 + ALS); GH-dependent

4. GH/IGF-1 Axis Regulation

Stimulatory Factors

FactorMechanism
GHRHHypothalamus → Pituitary (GHRH-R) → ↑ cAMP → GH release
GhrelinStomach → Pituitary (GHS-R1a) → ↑ GH (potent secretagogue)
HypoglycaemiaGlucose ↑ → ↑ CRH/GHRH → ↑ GH
Exercise↑ Catecholamines, Lactate, β-Endorphin → ↑ GH
Protein MealAmino acids (Arginine, Lysine) → ↑ GH
Deep SleepSlow-wave sleep → ↑ GHRH, ↓ Somatostatin → Nocturnal GH surge
Sex SteroidsOestrogen/Testosterone → ↑ GH amplitude (pubertal peak)

Inhibitory Factors

FactorMechanism
Somatostatin (GHIH)Hypothalamus → Pituitary (SSTR2/5) → ↓ cAMP → ↓ GH
IGF-1Negative feedback at Pituitary + Hypothalamus (↑ Somatostatin)
Glucose / Insulin↓ GH secretion (physiological post-prandial suppression)
Obesity↑ FGF21, Leptin resistance → ↓ GH
Free Fatty Acids↑ FFA → ↓ GH secretion
Ageing↓ GHRH, ↑ Somatostatin, ↓ IGF-1 → "Somatopause"

5. GH Secretion Patterns

PatternDetails
PulsatileEvery 3-5 hours; Amplitude > Frequency
Nocturnal SurgeMajor peak during slow-wave sleep (first 2-3 hours); 50-70% of daily GH
Diurnal RhythmPeak 00:00-03:00; Nadir mid-morning
PubertyPeak GH secretion (sex steroid amplification); 2-3x adult levels
AgeingProgressive decline ("Somatopause"); ↓ Amplitude, preserved frequency

6. IGF-1 — Clinical Applications

UseInterpretation
Screening for AcromegalyIGF-1 (age-adjusted) — Single best test; ↑ = Acromegaly
Monitoring AcromegalyIGF-1 normal = Biochemical control; GH not suppressed on OGTT = Active disease
GH Deficiency (Adult)Low IGF-1 + Clinical features → Dynamic testing
GH Deficiency (Child)Low IGF-1 + Low GH peak on stimulation
Catabolic StatesLow IGF-1 in malnutrition, burns, sepsis, hepatic failure

IGF-1 Cut-offs

PopulationIGF-1 (Age-Adjusted)
NormalWithin 2 SD of age-matched mean
AcromegalyAbove upper limit (often 2-3x ULN)
GHD (Adult)Below lower limit + Clinical features

7. Acromegaly — Diagnosis & Management

Clinical Features

SystemFeatures
FacialCoarse features, Prognathism, Macroglossia, Wide spacing teeth
ExtremitiesAcral enlargement (hands, feet), Ring/shoe size increase
SkinThickened, Oily, Hyperhidrosis, Skin tags, Acanthosis nigricans
CardiovascularCardiomyopathy, Hypertension, Sleep apnoea, Arrhythmias
MetabolicImpaired Glucose Tolerance / Diabetes (30-50%); Dyslipidaemia
JointsArthropathy, Carpal tunnel syndrome
Neoplasia RiskColonic polyps/cancer (↑ 2-3x); Thyroid nodules

Diagnostic Criteria

TestNormalAcromegaly
IGF-1 (Age-Adjusted)Within reference rangeAbove upper limit (often 2-3x ULN)
OGTT (75g Glucose)GH suppressed <1 µg/L at 60-120minGH not suppressed ≥1 µg/L (Gold Standard)
Random GH<0.4 µg/L (nadir)>1 µg/L (if IGF-1 elevated)
MRI PituitaryNormalMacroadenoma (>10mm in 90%)

Differential Diagnosis of Elevated IGF-1

ConditionIGF-1GHNotes
Acromegaly↑↑Not suppressed on OGTTPituitary macroadenoma
Pubertal Gigantism↑↑↑Open epiphyses
Pregnancy↑ (Placental GH)N/APhysiological
Hepatic Failure↓ (impaired synthesis)↑ (reduced clearance)Discordant
Renal Failure↑ (reduced clearance)↑ (reduced clearance)
Malnutrition/Anorexia↓↓↑↑ (paradoxical)GH resistance

8. Acromegaly — Treatment Algorithm

```

Acromegaly Diagnosed (↑ IGF-1 + GH not suppressed on OGTT)

│

▼

MRI PITUITARY (3mm slices) → Macroadenoma >10mm (90%) / Microadenoma

│

▼

PRIMARY TREATMENT: TRANSSPHENOIDAL SURGERY (TSS)

│

├── CURE (Post-op IGF-1 normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L)

│ → Lifelong Surveillance (Annual IGF-1 + OGTT)

│

├── RESIDUAL DISEASE (IGF-1 elevated / GH not suppressed)

│ │

│ ├── RE-OPERATION (if accessible residual)

│ └── MEDICAL THERAPY

│ ├── SOMATOSTATIN ANALOGUES (1st line medical)

│ │ Octreotide LAR 20-40mg IM q4wk

│ │ Lanreotide Autogel 60-120mg SC q4wk

│ │ Pasireotide LAR 40-60mg IM q4wk (2nd line)

│ │

│ ├── PEGVISOMANT (GH Receptor Antagonist)

│ │ 10-30mg SC daily (if SST resistant)

│ │

│ └── DOPAMINE AGONISTS (Adjunct)

│ Cabergoline 0.5-3mg/week

│

└── RADIOTHERAPY (If Surgery + Medical fail)

Stereotactic / Conventional; Latent effect 5-10yr

Risk: Hypopituitarism, Optic neuropathy, Secondary tumours

```

Medical Therapy — Agents Comparison

AgentMechanismDoseIndication
Octreotide LARSSTR2/5 agonist20-40mg IM q4wk1st line medical
Lanreotide AutogelSSTR2/5 agonist60-120mg SC q4wk1st line medical
Pasireotide LARSSTR1/2/3/5 agonist40-60mg IM q4wk2nd line (if Octreotide/Lanreotide fail)
PegvisomantGH Receptor Antagonist10-30mg SC dailyRefractory; Normalises IGF-1
CabergolineD2 Agonist0.5-3mg weeklyAdjunct (partial effect)
Pegvisomant + SSTCombination—Severe refractory

Cure Criteria (Post-Surgery / Medical)

ParameterTarget
IGF-1Normal (Age-adjusted)
OGTT GH<1 µg/L at 60-120min
Random GH<0.4 µg/L (nadir)
ClinicalResolution of features (soft tissue, metabolic)

9. Growth Hormone Deficiency (GHD)

Classification

TypeDefinition
Childhood-Onset GHD (CO-GHD)Diagnosed <18yr; May persist into adulthood
Adult-Onset GHD (AO-GHD)Diagnosed ≥18yr; Usually pituitary tumour/surgery/radiotherapy

Diagnosis — Adult GHD

Stimulation Tests (Require ≥2 tests usually)

TestProtocolPeak GH Cut-off
ITT (Gold Standard)Insulin 0.1 U/kg IV → Glucose <2.2 mmol/L<3 µg/L (adults); <5 µg/L (children)
Glucagon1mg IM/IV → Sample 0, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180min<3 µg/L (adults)
Arginine0.5g/kg IV over 30min<0.4 µg/L (children)
GHRH + ArginineGHRH 1µg/kg + Arginine 0.5g/kg IV<9 µg/L (adults; most sensitive)
Clonidine0.15mg/m² PO<6.8 µg/L (historical)

ITT Protocol (Gold Standard)

  1. Fast overnight, IV access, ECG monitoring
  2. Baseline: Glucose, GH, Cortisol
  3. Insulin 0.1 U/kg IV bolus
  4. Sampling: q15-30min for 2h (Glucose, GH, Cortisol)
  5. Target: Glucose <2.2 mmol/L (symptomatic hypoglycaemia)
  6. Recovery: IV Glucose + monitors

ITT Contraindications: IHD, Cerebrovascular disease, Epilepsy, Severe hypopituitarism (cortisol <100), Age >60, Pregnancy.

GH Deficiency — Diagnosis Criteria

PopulationCriteria
Adult (AO-GHD)Peak GH <3 µg/L on ITT/Glucagon OR <9 µg/L on GHRH+Arg PLUS ≥3 of: ↑ Visceral fat, ↓ BMD, ↓ QoL, Dyslipidaemia, ↓ Muscle mass
Child (CO-GHD)Height < -2 SD; Growth velocity < -1 SD; Peak GH <5 µg/L on 2 tests; Low IGF-1

10. GHD — Treatment

PopulationRH-GH DoseMonitoring
Adult0.1-0.3 mg/day SC (titrate to IGF-1 mid-normal range)IGF-1 q1-3mo initially; then 6-monthly
Child0.025-0.035 mg/kg/day SC (approx 0.2-0.5 mg/day)Height velocity, IGF-1, Bone age q6-12mo
Transition (CO-GHD → Adult)Re-test GH status; Continue if persistent GHDIGF-1, BMD, Lipids, QoL

GHD Treatment Contraindications

AbsoluteRelative
Active malignancyActive proliferative retinopathy
Acute critical illnessSevere obesity
Prader-Willi (paediatric)Severe insulin resistance

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
GH SecretionPulsatile, major peak slow-wave sleep; 50-70% daily GH at night
IGF-1Single best test for Acromegaly; Age-adjusted; t½ 12-15h; Bound to IGFBP-3 + ALS
Acromegaly DiagnosisOGTT: GH not suppressed <1 µg/L (Gold Standard); IGF-1 elevated
Acromegaly 1st Line TxTranssphenoidal Surgery (cure if microadenoma)
Acromegaly MedicalSomatostatin Analogues (Octreotide LAR/Lanreotide) 1st line; Pegvisomant if refractory
GHD Adult DiagnosisITT: Peak GH <3 µg/L (Gold Standard) or GHRH+Arg <9 µg/L
GHD Adult TreatmentRH-GH 0.1-0.3 mg/day SC; Titrate to IGF-1 mid-normal
IGF-1Liver-derived, t½ 12-15h; Bound to IGFBP-3 + ALS; t½ 12-15h
GH PulsatilityMajor peak slow-wave sleep; 50-70% daily GH at night
Acromegaly ComplicationsCardiomyopathy, Sleep apnoea, Diabetes (30-50%), Colonic polyps (↑2-3x)
Gigantism vs AcromegalyGigantism = Pre-epiphyseal fusion; Acromegaly = Post-fusion

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
IGF-1 vs GH for AcromegalyIGF-1 = Screening (stable); OGTT GH = Confirmation (dynamic)
GH RandomUseless (pulsatile); Use IGF-1 or Dynamic tests
GHD DiagnosisITT = Gold Standard; GHRH+Arginine = Most sensitive for adults
Acromegaly vs GigantismGigantism = Pre-epiphyseal fusion; Acromegaly = Post-fusion
IGF-1 in Acromegaly↑↑ (stable); GH = pulsatile (use mean or OGTT)
GHD TreatmentRH-GH SC daily; Titrate to IGF-1 mid-normal; Monitor IGF-1, BMD, Lipids
GHD Adult vs ChildAdult: Metabolic phenotype; Child: Growth failure
PegvisomantGH Receptor Antagonist → Normalises IGF-1 but ↑ GH levels
PasireotideSSTR1/2/3/5 agonist; ↑ Hyperglycaemia risk (2nd line)

17. MCQs (10)

  1. GH-IGF-1 axis:

18. SBA Questions (10)

A. Transsphenoidal surgery

B. Octreotide LAR

C. Pegvisomant

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Acromegaly cause

A: GH-secreting pituitary adenoma (>95%)

  • Q: Screening

A: IGF-1 (age-adjusted); confirm with OGTT GH suppression <1 ug/L

  • Q: OGTT

A: Normal: GH suppresses <1 ug/L; acromegaly: no suppression

  • Q: Surgery

A: Transsphenoidal 1st line; remission if IGF-1 normal

  • Q: Medical Rx

A: SSA (octreotide/lanreotide) 2nd line; pegvisomant if SSA fails

  • Q: Pegvisomant

A: GH receptor antagonist; normalises IGF-1; no tumour shrinkage

  • Q: Complications

A: Cardiomyopathy, sleep apnoea, colonic polyps, diabetes, HTN, arthropathy

  • Q: Monitoring

A: IGF-1, GH, glucose, echo, sleep study, colonoscopy q6-12mo

  • Q: Recurrence

A: IGF-1 rise -> repeat MRI -> SSA/pegvisomant/radiotherapy

  • Q: Life expectancy

A: Reduced if uncontrolled; normal if controlled

topics.title: Hypopituitarism
topics.html:
[!info]
Hypopituitarism = Deficiency of one or more anterior pituitary hormones. Caused by pituitary/hypothalamic damage. Order of loss: GH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH (most to least vulnerable). Panhypopituitarism = All axes deficient.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define hypopituitarism and classify by severity (partial vs panhypopituitarism)
  • [ ] Identify aetiologies (pituitary, hypothalamic, systemic)
  • [ ] Apply the hierarchical pattern of hormone loss to clinical presentations
  • [ ] Prescribe appropriate hormone replacement regimens
  • [ ] Manage special situations (pregnancy, surgery, adrenal crisis, Sheehan syndrome)

2. Definition & Classification

CategoryDefinition
Partial HypopituitarismDeficiency of 1-2 anterior pituitary axes
PanhypopituitarismDeficiency of all anterior pituitary axes
Selective DeficiencyIsolated single hormone deficiency (e.g., isolated GHD, isolated ACTH deficiency)

3. Aetiology

CategorySpecific Causes
Pituitary AdenomaMacroadenoma (compression), Apoplexy, Surgery, Radiotherapy
HypothalamicCraniopharyngioma, Meningioma, Glioma, Sarcoidosis, LCH, Trauma
VascularPituitary Apoplexy, Sheehan Syndrome (Postpartum Necrosis), Carotid Aneurysm
Inflammatory/InfiltrativeSarcoidosis, LCH, Haemochromatosis, TB, Metastases, Lymphoma
GeneticPROP1, POU1F1, LHX3, HESX1, SOX3, OTX2
AutoimmuneLymphocytic Hypophysitis (Postpartum), Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndromes (APS-1, APS-2)
IatrogenicSurgery (Transsphenoidal), Radiotherapy (Delayed), Drugs (Checkpoint Inhibitors)
TraumaBasal Skull Fracture, Diffuse Axonal Injury

4. Pattern of Hormone Loss — Hierarchical Vulnerability

Order of LossHormoneMechanismClinical Presentation
1st (Most Vulnerable)GHSomatotrophs most sensitive to compression/ischaemiaChildren: Growth Failure; Adults: Metabolic Syndrome, ↓ QoL, ↑ Visceral Fat
2ndGonadotrophs (LH/FSH)Gonadotrophs sensitive to compressionInfertility, Amenorrhoea, Low Libido, Low Testosterone/Oestradiol
3rdTSHThyrotrophs less sensitiveCentral Hypothyroidism (Fatigue, Cold Intolerance, Weight Gain)
4th (Least Vulnerable)ACTHCorticotrophs most resistantAdrenal Insufficiency (Fatigue, Hypotension, Hyponatraemia, Hyperkalaemia)

Mnemonic: Go Go To Adrenal (GH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH)

5. Aetiology — Detailed

Pituitary Adenoma (Most Common Cause)

TypeMechanism
MacroadenomaDirect Compression of Normal Pituitary Tissue
Pituitary ApoplexyHaemorrhagic Infarction → Acute Hypopituitarism
Post-SurgicalTranssphenoidal Resection (Damage to Normal Pituitary)
Post-RadiotherapyDelayed (5-10yr) Progressive Hypopituitarism

Hypothalamic / Suprasellar

ConditionMechanism
CraniopharyngiomaCompression of Pituitary Stalk + Hypothalamus
MeningiomaSuprasellar Compression
InfiltrativeSarcoidosis, LCH, Haemochromatosis, TB, Metastases
VascularCarotid Aneurysm (Compress Pituitary Stalk)

Vascular

ConditionDetails
Pituitary ApoplexyAcute Haemorrhage/Infarction → Acute Panhypopituitarism
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis (Vasospasm + Enlarged Gland)
Carotid AneurysmCompression of Pituitary Stalk

Infiltrative / Inflammatory

ConditionFeatures
SarcoidosisNeurosarcoid; CSF ACE ↑; Pituitary Stalk Thickening
LCHDiabetes Insipidus + Anterior Hypopituitarism; Bone Lesions
HaemochromatosisIron Deposition in Pituitary + Liver + Heart + Pancreas
Lymphocytic HypophysitisPostpartum; Autoimmune; MRI: Diffuse Pituitary Enlargement
Autoimmune Polyendocrine SyndromesAPS-1 (AIRE), APS-2 (Addison + Thyroid + DM1)

Genetic

GeneInheritancePhenotype
PROP1ARMultiple Pituitary Hormone Deficiency (CPHD)
POU1F1ARGH + TSH + PRL Deficiency
LHX3ARCPHD + Rigid Cervical Spine
HESX1AR/ADSepto-optic Dysplasia + CPHD
SOX3X-linkedCPHD + Mental Retardation
OTX2ADCPHD + Eye Anomalies

6. Clinical Presentation by Axis

GH Deficiency

PopulationFeatures
ChildrenGrowth Failure (Height < -2 SDS), ↓ Growth Velocity, ↓ Bone Age, Hypoglycaemia
Adults↑ Visceral Fat, ↓ Lean Mass, ↓ BMD, ↓ QoL, Dyslipidaemia, ↓ Exercise Capacity, ↑ Cardiovascular Risk

Gonadotropin Deficiency

SexFeatures
MaleLow Libido, ED, Infertility, ↓ Beard/Body Hair, ↓ Muscle Mass, Hot Flushes, Osteoporosis
FemaleSecondary Amenorrhoea (OLIGO/AMEN), Infertility, Hot Flushes, Vaginal Atrophy, Osteoporosis

TSH Deficiency (Central Hypothyroidism)

FeaturesDetails
BiochemistryLow fT4 + Inappropriately Low/Normal TSH
ClinicalFatigue, Cold Intolerance, Weight Gain, Constipation, Bradycardia, Hyperlipidaemia
DifferentiationPrimary Hypo: TSH ↑↑; Central: TSH Low/Normal + fT4 Low

ACTH Deficiency (Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency)

FeatureDetails
BiochemistryLow Cortisol + Low/Inappropriately Normal ACTH
ClinicalFatigue, Weakness, Anorexia, Weight Loss, Hypotension, Hypoglycaemia, Hyponatraemia, NO Hyperpigmentation (ACTH Low)
DifferentiationPrimary: Cortisol ↓ + ACTH ↑↑ + Hyperpigmentation; Secondary: Cortisol ↓ + ACTH ↓/N + No Pigmentation

7. Common Aetiology-Specific Presentations

Sheehan Syndrome (Postpartum Pituitary Necrosis)

FeatureDetails
PathophysiologyPituitary Enlarges 2x in Pregnancy → Vasospasm/Thrombosis Postpartum → Infarction
Risk FactorsPostpartum Haemorrhage, Hypotension, DIC, Multiple Gestation
OnsetAcute (Days) or Subacute/Insidious (Months-Years)
Classic TriadAgallactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Loss of Pubic/Axillary Hair
Hormones LostGH > Gonadotrophs > TSH > ACTH (ACTH often preserved initially)
DiagnosisMRI: Pituitary Infarction → Empty Sella; Hormonal Panels
ManagementLifelong Hormone Replacement; Glucocorticoid Priority; GH if Indicated

Lymphocytic Hypophysitis

FeatureDetails
TimingPostpartum (3-6 months) or Peripartum
PathologyAutoimmune Lymphocytic Infiltration of Pituitary
MRIDiffuse Pituitary Enlargement, Stalk Thickening, Homogeneous Enhancement
HormonesACTH Deficiency (Most Common), TSH, Gonadotrophs; Prolactin ↑ (Stalk Effect)
ManagementHigh-Dose Steroids (Pred 1mg/kg) + Hormone Replacement; Taper Over Months

Pituitary Apoplexy

FeatureDetails
DefinitionAcute Haemorrhage/Infarction in Pituitary Adenoma
TriggersCoagulopathy, Hypertension, Pregnancy, Trauma, Anticoagulation, Dynamic Testing
ClinicalSudden Severe Headache, Visual Loss, Ophthalmoplegia, Hypotension, Altered Consciousness
Emergency MgmtHydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT + Fluids + Dexamethasone 4-8mg IV q6h + Urgent MRI → TSS if Deteriorating

8. Diagnosis — Investigations

Hormonal Panel

AxisTestsInterpretation
GH/IGF-1IGF-1 (Age-Adjusted), GH (Random/Stim)↓ IGF-1 + Low GH Peak on Stim = GHD
GonadalTestosterone/Oestradiol + LH/FSHLow Steroid + Low/Normal LH/FSH = Secondary Hypogonadism
ThyroidfT4 + TSHLow fT4 + Low/Normal TSH = Central Hypothyroidism
Adrenal09:00 Cortisol + ACTHLow Cortisol + Low/Normal ACTH = Secondary AI
ProlactinProlactinNormal/Low (unless Stalk Effect)
GH StimITT / Glucagon / GHRH+ArgPeak GH <3 µg/L (Adults) = GHD

Imaging

ModalityIndication
MRI Pituitary (3mm, Gadolinium)Gold Standard; Pituitary Stalk, Adenoma, Infiltration
CTIf MRI Contraindicated; Bony Detail (Craniopharyngioma)
Visual Fields (Goldmann/Humphrey)Bitemporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)

Genetic Testing

GeneIndication
PROP1Multiple Pituitary Hormone Deficiency (CPHD)
POU1F1GH + TSH + PRL Deficiency
LHX3CPHD + Rigid Cervical Spine
HESX1Septo-optic Dysplasia + CPHD
SOX3CPHD + Mental Retardation (X-linked)

9. Hormone Replacement Therapy

Glucocorticoid (ACTH Deficiency)

RegimenDose
Hydrocortisone15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM (or 10mg TDS)
Prednisolone5mg OD (Longer half-life)
Stress DosingMinor: 25mg IV; Moderate: 50-100mg IV; Major: 100mg IV + 50mg q6h
MonitoringWeight, BP, Glucose, BP, Skin, Bone (DEXA q2-3yr)

Thyroxine (TSH Deficiency)

RegimenDose
Levothyroxine1.6 µg/kg/day (e.g., 100-150µg)
InitiationStart Low (25-50µg) → Titrate q4-6wk by 25µg
TargetfT4 Upper Half of Normal; TSH N/A (Pituitary)
SpecialInitiate BEFORE Glucocorticoid (Avoid Adrenal Crisis)

Sex Steroid Replacement

PopulationRegimen
Male (Secondary Hypogonadism)Testosterone Gel 5g/day (50mg) / IM Nebido 1000mg q10-14wk / Enantate 250mg q3-4wk
Female (POI/Secondary Hypogonadism)HRT (Oestradiol + Progestogen) until Age 50; Fertility: hCG/FSH if Desired
Fertility (Male)hCG 1500-5000 IU 2-3x/week OR rFSH + hCG

GH Replacement (Confirmed GHD)

PopulationDoseMonitoring
Adult0.1-0.3 mg/day SC → Titrate to IGF-1 Mid-NormalIGF-1 q1-3mo ×1yr, then 6-monthly
Child0.025-0.035 mg/kg/day SCHeight Velocity, IGF-1, Bone Age q6-12mo

10. Special Situations

Pregnancy in Hypopituitarism

AxisManagement
GlucocorticoidIncrease 20-40% in 2nd/3rd Trimester; Stress Doses for Delivery
Thyroxine↑ Dose 30% at +ve Test; Monitor TSH/fT4 q4-6wk
Sex SteroidsContinue HRT; Stop Gonadotrophins; Discuss Fertility Plans
GHStop (Contraindicated in Pregnancy)
DeliveryStress Dose Hydrocortisone (100mg IV q6h) During Labour

Surgery in Hypopituitarism

PhaseManagement
Pre-opStress Dose Hydrocortisone 100mg IV; Thyroxine continued
Peri-op50-100mg Hydrocortisone IV q6-8h × 24-48h; Taper
Post-opResume Usual Doses; Monitor U&Es, Glucose, BP

Radiotherapy-Induced Hypopituitarism

TimelineFeatures
6-12 monthsGH Deficiency (First)
2-5 yearsGonadotroph Deficiency
5-10 yearsTSH Deficiency
>10 yearsACTH Deficiency (Rare)
SurveillanceAnnual Hormones for 10+ Years Post-RT

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Hormone Loss OrderGH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH (Most → Least Vulnerable)
PanhypopituitarismAll Axes Deficient
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Necrosis; Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Hair Loss; MRI: Empty Sella
Lymphocytic HypophysitisPostpartum; Diffuse Enlargement + Stalk Thickening; ACTH Deficiency Common
Pituitary ApoplexySudden Headache + Visual Loss + Ophthalmoplegia + Hypotension → Hydrocortisone 100mg IV + Dexamethasone + Urgent MRI → Surgery
Central HypothyroidismLow fT4 + Low/Normal TSH (Inappropriately Normal)
Secondary Adrenal InsufficiencyLow Cortisol + Low ACTH; No Hyperpigmentation
GHD OrderGH Lost First; ACTH Lost Last
Replacement OrderThyroxine BEFORE Glucocorticoid (Avoid Adrenal Crisis)
Stress DosingMinor 25mg; Moderate 50-100mg; Major 100mg + 50mg q6h
Pituitary RadiotherapyDelayed Hypopituitarism: GH (1-2yr) → Gonad (2-5yr) → TSH (5-10yr) → ACTH (10+yr)
Pregnancy in Hypopit↑ Thyroxine 30%; ↑ Hydrocortisone 20-40% in 2nd/3rd Tri; Stop GH; Stress Doses Delivery

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Primary vs Secondary AIPrimary: Cortisol↓ + ACTH↑↑ + Hyperpigmentation; Secondary: Cortisol↓ + ACTH↓ + NO Hyperpigmentation
Central vs Primary HypothyroidismCentral: fT4↓ + TSH Low/Normal; Primary: TSH↑↑ + fT4↓
Hormone Loss OrderGH → Gonad → TSH → ACTH (Mnemonic: Go Go To Adrenal)
Replacement PriorityThyroxine FIRST, then Glucocorticoid (Avoid Adrenal Crisis)
ITT in HypopituitarismContraindicated (Cortisol <100); Use Glucagon/GHRH+Arg
GHD in Adults↑ Visceral Fat, ↓ BMD, ↓ QoL, ↑ CV Risk
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis → Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Hair Loss; MRI: Empty Sella
Replacement PriorityThyroxine BEFORE Glucocorticoid (Prevents Adrenal Crisis)
Stress DosesMinor 25mg; Moderate 50-100mg; Major 100mg + 50mg q6h

topics.title: Pituitary Adenomas Functioning
topics.html:
[!info]
Functioning pituitary adenomas secrete excessive hormone(s), producing characteristic clinical syndromes. Most common: Prolactinoma (40-50%), then GH-secreting (20%), ACTH-secreting (10-15%), TSH-oma (1-2%), Gonadotroph (<5%). Silent corticotroph/Non-functioning = 25-30%.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify functioning pituitary adenomas by hormone secretion
  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation of each functioning adenoma type
  • [ ] Apply appropriate investigations (hormones, dynamic tests, imaging)
  • [ ] Outline management algorithms for each functioning adenoma
  • [ ] Recognise and manage pituitary apoplexy as emergency

2. Classification of Pituitary Adenomas by Function

TypeHormone(s)PrevalenceCell of OriginClinical Syndrome
ProlactinomaProlactin40-50%LactotrophHyperprolactinaemia, Hypogonadism, Galactorrhoea
Somatotroph (GH) AdenomaGH20%SomatotrophAcromegaly (adult) / Gigantism (child)
Corticotroph (ACTH) AdenomaACTH10-15%CorticotrophCushing Disease
Thyrotroph (TSH) AdenomaTSH1-2%ThyrotrophSecondary Hyperthyroidism
Gonadotroph AdenomaFSH/LH/α-subunit<5%GonadotrophOften asymptomatic / Mass effect
Mixed/PlurihormonalMultiple (e.g., GH+PRL)5-10%PluripotentOverlapping syndromes
Silent CorticotrophACTH (clinically silent)10-15%CorticotrophNon-functioning phenotype

3. Prolactinoma

Diagnosis

FeatureMicroprolactinoma (<10mm)Macroprolactinoma (≥10mm)
Prolactin Level500-5000 mIU/L>5000 mIU/L (often >10,000)
PresentationGalactorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Infertility, Low libidoVisual field defects, Headache, Hypopituitarism
MRIMicroadenoma (<10mm)Macroadenoma (≥10mm), Chiasmal compression

Management

ScenarioTreatment
MicroprolactinomaCabergoline 0.25mg BD → Titrate to 0.5-1mg BD (Max 2mg BD)
MacroprolactinomaCabergoline 0.5mg BD → Titrate to 1-2mg BD (Max 3mg BD); Surgery if resistant/intolerant/apoplexy
Pregnancy + MicroStop Cabergoline (Low growth risk)
Pregnancy + MacroContinue Cabergoline (Growth risk >10%)

Cabergoline Dose Titration:

  • Micro: 0.25mg BD → 0.5mg BD → 1mg BD (Max 2mg BD)
  • Macro: 0.5mg BD → 1mg BD → 1.5-2mg BD (Max 3mg BD)

Withdrawal: After 2-3 years normal Prolactin + No tumour on MRI → Taper over 3-6 months

4. Acromegaly (GH-secreting Adenoma)

Clinical Features

SystemFeatures
FacialCoarse features, Prognathism, Macroglossia, Wide teeth spacing
ExtremitiesAcral enlargement (Hands/Feet), Ring/Shoe size increase
SkinThickened, Oily, Hyperhidrosis, Skin tags, Acanthosis nigricans
CVSCardiomyopathy, Hypertension, Sleep apnoea
MetabolicImpaired Glucose Tolerance / Diabetes (30-50%)
JointsArthropathy, Carpal tunnel syndrome

Diagnosis

TestNormalAcromegaly
IGF-1 (Age-Adjusted)Normal↑↑ (2-3x ULN)
OGTT (75g Glucose)GH <1 µg/L at 60-120minGH not suppressed ≥1 µg/L (Gold Standard)
Random GH<0.4 µg/L>1 µg/L
MRI PituitaryNormalMacroadenoma >10mm (90%)

Management Algorithm

```

Acromegaly (↑ IGF-1 + GH not suppressed on OGTT)

│

▼

MRI PITUITARY → Macroadenoma >10mm (90%)

│

▼

TSS (Transsphenoidal Surgery) — 1st Line

│

├── CURE (IGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1) → Surveillance

│

└── RESIDUAL → Medical Therapy

│

├── Somatostatin Analogues (Octreotide LAR/Lanreotide) — 1st Line Medical

├── Pegvisomant (GH Receptor Antagonist) — If SST Refractory

├── Dopamine Agonists (Cabergoline) — Adjunct

└── Radiotherapy (Stereotactic/Conventional) — Last Resort

```

Medical Therapy:

  • Octreotide LAR 20-40mg IM q4wk / Lanreotide 60-120mg SC q4wk
  • Pasireotide 40-60mg IM q4wk (2nd line, higher hyperglycaemia risk)
  • Pegvisomant 10-30mg SC daily (GH Receptor Antagonist)
  • Cabergoline 0.5-3mg/week (Adjunct)

Cure Criteria: IGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L + Random GH <0.4 µg/L

5. Cushing Disease (ACTH-secreting Adenoma)

Clinical Features

FeatureDetails
Cushingoid HabitusCentral obesity, Moon face, Buffalo hump, Striae, Bruising
MetabolicDiabetes, Hypertension, Dyslipidaemia, Proximal Myopathy
PsychiatricDepression, Anxiety, Cognitive impairment
SkinThin, Fragile, Acne, Hirsutism, Poor wound healing

Diagnosis

StepInvestigationCut-off
Screening24h UFC ×2 + Late-night Salivary Cortisol ×2 + 1mg DSTUFC >ULN; DST Cortisol ≥50 nmol/L
Confirm ACTH-DependentACTH (09:00, EDTA)Normal/High (>10-20 pg/mL)
DifferentiateHDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)>50% Suppression = Cushing Disease
If EquivocalCRH Test / IPSS / ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PETCentral:Peripheral ACTH Gradient >2 = Pituitary

Management

StepTreatment
1st LineTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS) — Cure rate 80-90% (Micro), 50-60% (Macro)
Residual/RecurrentRepeat TSS / Bilateral Adrenalectomy / Medical (Ketoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat) / Radiotherapy
Adjuvant MedicalKetoconazole (200-400mg q6h), Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Pasireotide

6. TSH-secreting Adenoma (TSH-oma)

Clinical Features

FeatureDetails
HyperthyroidismfT4/fT3 ↑↑, TSH Inappropriately Normal/High
PresentationTypical thyrotoxicosis + Goitre (often large)
Pituitary MRIMacroadenoma (Usually >10mm)

Diagnosis

TestFinding
TSHNormal/High despite fT4/fT3 ↑↑
TRH TestParadoxical TSH Rise
α-SubunitElevated (Shared with FSH/LH)
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma

Management

StepTreatment
1st LineTranssphenoidal Surgery
Pre-op / UnresectableSomatostatin Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide) — Normalises TSH/T4 in 70%
RadiotherapyIf Residual/Recurrent

7. Gonadotroph Adenoma

FeatureDetails
HormonesFSH, LH, α-subunit (often elevated)
ClinicalUsually Silent (Mass effect dominant); May cause Hypogonadism (Compression)
PresentationHeadache, Visual Field Defects, Incidentaloma
Biochemistry↑ FSH/LH/α-subunit; Low/Normal Sex Steroids
TreatmentTranssphenoidal Surgery (if Macro/Visual Defects)

8. Silent Corticotroph Adenoma

FeatureDetails
DefinitionACTH immuno-positive, Clinically Silent (No Cushing Features)
PresentationIncidentaloma, Visual Field Defects, Headache, Hypopituitarism
ACTH StainingPositive on Immunohistochemistry
BiochemistryNormal Cortisol, Normal/↑ ACTH (Often Non-suppressible)
ManagementSurgery if Macroadenoma / Visual Defects / Hypopituitarism

9. Mixed / Plurihormonal Adenomas

CombinationClinical Picture
GH + PRLAcromegaly + Hyperprolactinaemia (Most common mixed)
ACTH + PRLCushing + Hyperprolactinaemia
TSH + GHCentral Hyperthyroidism + Acromegaly
ManagementTarget Dominant Hormone; Surgery Often Required

10. Pituitary Apoplexy — Emergency

FeatureDetails
DefinitionAcute Haemorrhage/Infarction in Pituitary Adenoma
TriggersCoagulopathy, Hypertension, Pregnancy, Trauma, Anticoagulation, Dynamic Testing
PresentationSudden Severe Headache, Visual Loss (Chiasmal), Ophthalmoplegia (CN III/IV/VI), Hypotension (Adrenal Crisis), Hypopituitarism
Emergency ManagementHigh-dose Dexamethasone (4-8mg IV q6h), IV Fluids, Hydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT → 50mg q6h, Urgent MRI, Transsphenoidal Surgery if Deteriorating

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Most Common FunctioningProlactinoma (40-50%)
Prolactinoma SizeMicro <10mm; Macro ≥10mm
Cabergoline DoseMicro: 0.25mg BD → 1mg BD (Max 2mg BD); Macro: 0.5mg BD → 2mg BD (Max 3mg BD)
Acromegaly DiagnosisOGTT: GH not <1 µg/L; IGF-1 ↑↑
Acromegaly 1st LineTranssphenoidal Surgery
Cushing Disease vs AdrenalACTH: Disease = N/↑; Adrenal = ↓↓; Ectopic = ↑↑
TSH-omafT4/fT3 ↑ + TSH Inappropriately Normal/High
Silent CorticotrophACTH+ on IHC, No Cushing Features
Pituitary ApoplexySudden Headache + Visual Loss + CN Palsies + Hypotension → Dexamethasone + Hydrocortisone + Urgent Surgery
Silent AdenomaNon-functioning + ACTH+ on IHC → Surgery if Macro/Visual Defects
Mixed AdenomaGH+PRL most common; Treat Dominant Hormone

12. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Functioning Pituitary Adenomas))

Prolactinoma (40-50%)

Micro: Cabergoline 0.25→1mg BD

Macro: Cabergoline 0.5-2mg BD

Acromegaly (GH, 20%)

OGTT GH <1 = Normal; >1 = Acromegaly

Surgery 1st; Medical: SST Analogues → Pegvisomant

Cushing Disease (ACTH, 10-15%)

HDDST >50% suppression = Pituitary

Surgery 1st; Bilateral Adrenalectomy if fail

TSH-oma (1-2%)

fT4/fT3 ↑ + TSH Normal/High

Surgery + SST Analogues

Gonadotroph

Silent usually; Surgery if Macro

Silent Corticotroph

ACTH+ on IHC, No Cushing

Apoplexy

Sudden Headache + Visual Loss + CN Palsies + Hypotension

Dexamethasone + Hydrocortisone + Urgent Surgery

```

14. MCQs (10)

  1. Pituitary functioning adenomas:

A. Hormone-secreting pituitary adenomas (prolactinoma, acromegaly, Cushing, TSHoma, gonadotroph)

B. Non-functioning adenomas

C. Craniopharyngioma

D. Meningioma

E. Metastasis

  1. Prolactinoma =

A. Prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma; commonest functioning adenoma (~40%)

B. GH-secreting

C. ACTH-secreting

D. TSH-secreting

E. Non-functioning

  1. Acromegaly =

A. GH-secreting adenoma; IGF-1 elevated

B. Prolactin-secreting

C. ACTH-secreting

D. TSH-secreting

E. Non-functioning

  1. Cushing disease =

A. ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma; 70% endogenous Cushing

B. Adrenal adenoma

C. Ectopic ACTH

D. Exogenous steroids

E. CAH

  1. TSH-secreting adenoma:

A. Rare (<1%); hyperthyroidism with inappropriately normal/raised TSH

B. Common

C. Always with hyperprolactinaemia

D. Associated with acromegaly

E. Always with Cushing

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma:

A. Secretes LH/FSH/alpha-subunit; often clinically non-functioning; mass effects

B. Functioning prolactinoma

C. Functioning acromegaly

D. Functioning Cushing

E. Functioning TSHoma

  1. Functioning adenoma treatment:

A. Prolactinoma: cabergoline; Acromegaly: surgery -> SSA/pegvisomant; Cushing: surgery; TSHoma: surgery; Gonadotroph: surgery

B. All: cabergoline

C. All: surgery

D. All: radiotherapy

E. All: observation

  1. Prolactinoma cabergoline:

A. 0.25mg BD titrate to 1-2mg/week; normalises prolactin; shrink tumour 80%

B. 1mg OD

C. 10mg weekly

D. IV only

E. Short-term only

  1. Acromegaly SSA:

A. Octreotide LAR/lanreotide; reduce GH/IGF-1; shrink tumour 30-50%

B. No effect on tumour

C. Only for Cushing

D. Only for prolactinoma

E. Oral only

  1. Cushing disease surgery:

A. Transsphenoidal 1st line; remission 80-90% microadenoma

B. Bilateral adrenalectomy

C. Ketoconazole only

D. Radiotherapy 1st line

E. Observation

15. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 30yo woman: amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, prolactin 2000, MRI 8mm microadenoma. Treatment?

A. Cabergoline 0.25mg BD titrate

B. Surgery

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Bromocriptine

  1. Same patient: macroadenoma 15mm, visual field defect. Urgent?

A. Surgery (decompression) -> then cabergoline

B. Cabergoline only

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Radiotherapy

  1. 45yo man: acromegaly, 12mm macroadenoma, IGF-1 3x ULN. Treatment?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery

B. Octreotide LAR

C. Pegvisomant

D. Radiotherapy

E. Cabergoline

  1. 35yo woman: Cushing features, ACTH 80, MRI 6mm microadenoma. Treatment?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery

B. Ketoconazole

C. Bilateral adrenalectomy

D. Radiotherapy

E. Pasireotide

  1. TSHoma: hyperthyroidism, TSH 5, fT4 40, MRI 10mm adenoma. Treatment?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Somatostatin analogues

16. Flashcards

  • Q: Functioning adenomas

A: Prolactinoma (40%), Acromegaly (15%), Cushing (10%), TSHoma (<1%), Gonadotroph (rare)

  • Q: Prolactinoma

A: Prolactin >2000; cabergoline 0.25mg BD; shrink 80%; surgery if macroadenoma + visual loss

  • Q: Acromegaly

A: IGF-1 screen; OGTT GH suppression; surgery 1st line; SSA/pegvisomant if residual

  • Q: Cushing disease

A: ACTH microadenoma; transsphenoidal surgery; remission 80-90% micro

  • Q: TSHoma

A: Rare; hyperthyroidism + inappropriately normal/raised TSH; surgery

  • Q: Gonadotroph adenoma

A: Often clinically non-functioning; LH/FSH/alpha-subunit; mass effects

  • Q: Cabergoline

A: 0.25mg BD titrate; prolactin normalisation; tumour shrinkage 80%

  • Q: Octreotide/lanreotide

A: SSA 2nd line acromegaly; reduce GH/IGF-1; shrink tumour

  • Q: Pegvisomant

A: GH receptor antagonist; normalises IGF-1; no tumour shrinkage

  • Q: Functioning adenoma Rx

A: Prolactinoma: cabergoline; Acromegaly: surgery -> SSA/pegvisomant; Cushing: surgery; TSHoma: surgery

17. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Hormone-secreting pituitary adenomas (prolactinoma, acromegaly, Cushing, TSHoma, gonadotroph) — Pituitary functioning adenomas:
  1. Prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma; commonest functioning adenoma (~40%) — Prolactinoma =
  1. GH-secreting adenoma; IGF-1 elevated — Acromegaly =
  1. ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma; 70% endogenous Cushing — Cushing disease =
  1. Rare (<1%); hyperthyroidism with inappropriately normal/raised TSH — TSH-secreting adenoma:
  1. Secretes LH/FSH/alpha-subunit; often clinically non-functioning; mass effects — Gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. Prolactinoma: cabergoline; Acromegaly: surgery -> SSA/pegvisomant; Cushing: surgery; TSHoma: surgery; Gonadotroph: surgery — Functioning adenoma treatment:
  1. 0.25mg BD titrate to 1-2mg/week; normalises prolactin; shrink tumour 80% — Prolactinoma cabergoline:
  1. Octreotide LAR/lanreotide; reduce GH/IGF-1; shrink tumour 30-50% — Acromegaly SSA:
  1. Transsphenoidal 1st line; remission 80-90% microadenoma — Cushing disease surgery:

SBAs

  1. Cabergoline 0.25mg BD titrate — 30yo woman: amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, prolactin 2000, MRI 8mm microadenoma. Treatment?
  1. Surgery (decompression) -> then cabergoline — Same patient: macroadenoma 15mm, visual field defect. Urgent?
  1. Transsphenoidal surgery — 45yo man: acromegaly, 12mm macroadenoma, IGF-1 3x ULN. Treatment?
  1. Transsphenoidal surgery — 35yo woman: Cushing features, ACTH 80, MRI 6mm microadenoma. Treatment?
  1. Transsphenoidal surgery — TSHoma: hyperthyroidism, TSH 5, fT4 40, MRI 10mm adenoma. Treatment?

topics.title: Pituitary Adenomas Non Functioning
topics.html:
[!info]
Non-functioning pituitary adenomas (NFPAs) = No clinical hormone excess. Most are null cell or gonadotroph (α-subunit/FSH/LH secretion without clinical effect). Account for 25-30% of pituitary adenomas. Present with mass effect, not hormone excess.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define non-functioning pituitary adenoma (NFPA)
  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation (mass effect, hypopituitarism, stalk effect)
  • [ ] Apply appropriate investigations (hormones, MRI, visual fields)
  • [ ] Apply management algorithms (observation vs surgery vs radiotherapy)
  • [ ] Understand surveillance protocols post-surgery

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
DefinitionPituitary adenoma without clinical hormone excess syndrome
Cell TypesNull cell (70-80%) → No hormone; Gonadotroph (15-25%) → α-subunit/FSH/LH (subclinical)
Prevalence25-30% of all pituitary adenomas (2nd most common after prolactinoma)
DemographicsPeak 50-60 years; Slight male predominance
Size at PresentationMacroadenoma (>10mm) in 80-90% (Present late due to lack of hormone excess)

3. Pathology & Immunohistochemistry

ImmunohistochemistryStaining PatternSignificance
All Hormones (ACTH, TSH, GH, PRL, FSH, LH, TSH, α-subunit)NegativeNull cell adenoma
FSH/LH/α-subunitPositive (Focal/Diffuse)Gonadotroph Adenoma (Often clinically silent)
Single HormoneSingle PositiveFunctioning Adenoma (Exclude)

Diagnosis of Exclusion: NFPA diagnosed after excluding functioning adenomas (Normal PRL, GH/IGF-1, ACTH/Cortisol, TSH/fT4, FSH/LH/Testosterone/Oestradiol).

4. Clinical Presentation

PresentationFrequencyMechanism
Visual Field Defects60-80%Chiasmal compression (Bi-temporal hemianopia)
Headache50-70%Dural stretch, Cavernous sinus invasion
Hypopituitarism50-60%Compression of Normal Pituitary Tissue
Incidentaloma10-20%Imaging for unrelated reason
Cranial Nerve Palsies10-20%CN III/IV/VI (Cavernous Sinus Invasion)

Hypopituitarism Pattern (NFPA)

Order of Loss (Typical)HormoneFrequency
1stGHMost common (80-90%)
2ndGonadotrophs (LH/FSH)70-80%
3rdTSH40-50%
4thACTH20-30%
VariableProlactinMay be ↑ (Stalk Effect)

Stalk Effect: Tumour compresses pituitary stalk → ↓ Dopamine delivery → Mild Hyperprolactinaemia (Prolactin usually <2000 mIU/L).

5. Investigations

Hormonal Profile (Rule Out Functioning Adenoma)

TestExpected in NFPA
ProlactinNormal or Mildly Elevated (<2000 mIU/L) (Stalk Effect)
GH / IGF-1Normal
ACTH / 09:00 CortisolNormal
TSH / fT4Normal
FSH / LH / Testosterone / OestradiolNormal or Low (Secondary Hypogonadism from Compression)
α-subunitMay be ↑ (Gonadotroph Adenoma)

Imaging

ModalityProtocolPurpose
MRI Pituitary3mm slices, Gadolinium, Coronal/SagittalGold Standard; Tumour Size, Extension, Cavernous Sinus Invasion
Visual FieldsGoldmann Perimetry / HumphreyBaseline; Quaterly Post-op
CT (Rare)Thin Slices, ContrastIf MRI Contraindicated

Cavernous Sinus Invasion Grading (Knosp Classification)

GradeDescriptionSurgical Resectability
Grade 0Tumour ≤ Intercarotid LineComplete Resection Likely
Grade 1Tumour > Intercarotid Line, ≤ Tangent to Lateral ICAComplete Possible
Grade 2Tumour > Tangent to Lateral ICA, ≤ Tangent to Medial ICAPartial Resection
Grade 3Tumour > Tangent to Medial ICA, ≤ Tangent to MCCSubtotal
Grade 4Tumour > Tangent to MCCGross Total Unlikely

6. Management Algorithm

```

Non-Functioning Pituitary Adenoma (Confirmed)

│

├── MICROADENOMA (<10mm) + ASYMPTOMATIC

│ → OBSERVATION

│ MRI 6-12mo → Yearly ×2 → Then 2-5yr

│ Hormonal Surveillance Annual

│

├── MACROADENOMA (≥10mm) + ASYMPTOMATIC

│ → SURGERY RECOMMENDED (High risk of future mass effect)

│ Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)

│

├── VISUAL FIELD DEFECTS (Any Size)

│ → URGENT TRANSSEPHENOIDAL SURGERY (TSS)

│

├── HYPOPITUITARISM (Symptomatic)

│ → SURGERY (TSS) + Hormone Replacement

│

├── CRANIAL NERVE PALSY (CN III/IV/VI)

│ → SURGERY (TSS) ± Cavernous Sinus Exploration

│

└── RECURRENT / RESIDUAL POST-SURGERY

├── ASYMPTOMATIC RESIDUAL (<1cm) → Observation

├── SYMPTOMATIC / GROWING → Re-operation / Radiotherapy

└── NOT SURGICAL CANDIDATE → Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS) / Fractionated RT

```

7. Surgical Management

Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS) — Gold Standard

AspectDetails
ApproachEndoscopic Endonasal (Preferred) / Microscopic Sub-labial
GoalGross Total Resection (GTR) if Possible; Decompression if Cavernous Sinus Invasion
Success RatesGTR: 70-80% (Grade 0-1); Decompression: 90-95%
ComplicationsCSF Leak (2-5%), DI (Transient 10-20%, Permanent 1-2%), New Hypopituitarism (5-10%), Epistaxis, Meningitis
Post-op MonitoringVisual Fields (Pre/Post), Pituitary Hormones (Day 1, 3mo, 6mo, 1yr), MRI (3mo, 1yr, then yearly×5)

Cavernous Sinus Invasion (Knosp Classification)

GradeDescriptionResection Goal
Grade 0Tumour ≤ Intercarotid LineComplete
Grade 1> Intercarotid, ≤ Tangent Lateral ICAComplete Possible
Grade 2> Tangent Lateral ICA, ≤ Tangent Medial ICASubtotal
Grade 3> Tangent Medial, ≤ Tangent MCCSubtotal
Grade 4> Tangent MCCDecompression Only

Knosp ≥3 → Subtotal resection expected; Adjuvant radiotherapy for residual.

8. Radiotherapy

IndicationTypeTiming
Residual/Recurrent Post-SurgeryStereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS) / Fractionated RTAfter Surgical Recovery (3-6mo)
Not Surgical CandidateSRS / Fractionated RTPrimary Treatment
Aggressive/AtypicalFractionated RTAdjuvant Post-Surgery
RT TypeDoseTargetComplications
SRS (Gamma Knife/CyberKnife)12-15 Gy (Single)Tumour MarginHypopituitarism (10-30% at 10yr), Optic Neuropathy (<1%)
Fractionated RT45-50 Gy / 25-28 FractionsTumour BedLower Optic Risk; More Convenient for Large Residual

Radiotherapy Effect: Tumour control 80-90% at 10yr; Hypopituitarism develops in 30-50% at 10-15 years.

9. Surveillance & Follow-up

Post-Surgery Surveillance Schedule

TimepointInvestigations
Pre-opFull Hormones, MRI, Visual Fields
Day 1-2 Post-opCortisol, Electrolytes, Glucose, Visual Fields
6 WeeksFull Hormone Panel, Visual Fields
3 MonthsPituitary Hormones, MRI, Visual Fields
6 MonthsHormones, MRI (if residual)
1 YearHormones, MRI, Visual Fields
Annually (Years 2-5)Hormones, MRI (Alternate Years), Visual Fields
Long-term (>5yr)Hormones q2yr, MRI q2-3yr, Visual Fields q2yr

Hormonal Replacement Post-Surgery

DeficiencyReplacement
ACTH/CortisolHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
TSH/fT4Levothyroxine 1.6 µg/kg/day
GonadotrophsTestosterone / Oestradiol + Progestogen
GHConsider if Severe GHD (ITT Peak <3 µg/L)
ProlactinNot Replaced (Except Lactation)

10. Recurrence & Long-term Outcomes

OutcomeRate
Recurrence (GTR)10-15% at 10 years
Recurrence (Subtotal)30-50% at 5 years
New Hypopituitarism (Post-SRS)5-10%
New Hypopituitarism (Post-RT)30-50% at 10-15 years
Malignant TransformationExtremely Rare (<0.1%)

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
NFPA DefinitionNo clinical hormone excess; 25-30% of adenomas; Macroadenoma >80% at diagnosis
Cell TypesNull cell (70-80%) + Gonadotroph (α-subunit/FSH/LH positive but clinically silent)
PresentationMass Effect (Visual Fields, Headache, Cranial Nerves) + Hypopituitarism
Stalk EffectProlactin Mildly Elevated (<2000 mIU/L); Normalises Post-Surgery
Hypopituitarism OrderGH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH
Surgery IndicationMacroadenoma / Visual Fields / Hypopituitarism / Cranial Nerve Palsy
TSS SuccessGTR 70-80% (Knosp 0-1); Decompression 90-95%
Knosp ≥3Subtotal Resection; Adjuvant RT
RadiotherapyResidual/Recurrent/Not Surgical Candidate; Hypopituitarism Risk 30-50% at 10yr
SurveillanceMRI + Hormones + Visual Fields; Yearly x5 then 2-3yrly
Recurrence10-15% (GTR); 30-50% (Subtotal Resection)

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
NFPA vs Silent CorticotrophSilent Corticotroph = ACTH+ on IHC + Silent Cushing; NFPA = All Hormones Negative (except α-subunit/FSH/LH)
NFPA vs ProlactinomaProlactinoma: Prolactin >500 (Micro) / >5000 (Macro); NFPA: Prolactin <2000 (Stalk Effect)
NFPA vs FunctioningNFPA = No Syndrome; Functioning = Hormone Excess Syndrome
Stalk Effect Prolactin<2000 mIU/L; Normalises Post-Surgery; No Galactorrhoea
Gonadotroph vs Null CellGonadotroph = α-subunit/FSH/LH IHC Positive; Null Cell = All Negative

Mnemonic: NFPA MANAGEMENT

  • Non-functioning = No Hormone Syndrome
  • First: Functioning Excluded (Normal Hormones)
  • Presentation: Pressure (Visual, Headache, CN Palsy) + Pituitary Failure
  • Algorithm: Micro → Observe; Macro + Symptoms → Surgery (TSS)
  • Recurrence: Receed if GTR; Recur if Subtotal → RT

13. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Non-Functioning Pituitary Adenoma))

Definition

No Clinical Hormone Excess

Presentation

Headache

Hypopituitarism (GH→Gonad→TSH→ACTH)

Stalk Effect → Mild ↑PRL (<2000)

Diagnosis

Visual Fields (Goldmann/Humphrey)

Knosp Classification (CS Invasion)

Management

Micro + Asymptomatic → Observe (MRI 6-12m → 2-3yrly)

Macro + Asymptomatic → TSS

Visual Fields / Hypopituitarism → Urgent TSS

Knosp ≥3 → Subtotal + RT

Surgery

TSS (Endoscopic Preferred)

Complications: CSF Leak, DI, New Hypopit

Surveillance

```

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point

PathologyNull Cell (70-80%); Gonadotroph (α-subunit/FSH/LH) 15-25%

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis|Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning]], [[Pituitary Apoplexy]], [[Hypopituitarism]], [[Empty Sella Syndrome]], [[Craniopharyngioma]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Non-functioning pituitary adenoma =

A. No clinically apparent hormone excess; mass effects (visual loss, hypopituitarism)

B. Prolactin-secreting

C. GH-secreting

D. ACTH-secreting

E. TSH-secreting

  1. Non-functioning adenoma incidence:

A. ~30-40% of pituitary adenomas

B. <10%

C. 50-60%

D. 80-90%

E. 100%

  1. Non-functioning adenoma presentation:

A. Visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia), headache, hypopituitarism

B. Hyperprolactinaemia

C. Acromegaly

D. Cushing syndrome

E. Hyperthyroidism

  1. Non-functioning adenoma mildly elevated prolactin:

A. Stalk effect (disinhibition) <100 mIU/L; NOT true prolactinoma

B. True prolactinoma

C. Cushing disease

D. Acromegaly

E. TSHoma

  1. Non-functioning adenoma treatment:

A. Surgery (transsphenoidal) if >1cm or visual loss/hypopituitarism; observation if small/asymptomatic

B. Cabergoline

C. Somatostatin analogues

D. Radiotherapy only

E. Observation only

  1. Non-functioning adenoma surgery:

A. Transsphenoidal decompression; improves visual fields in 80-90%

B. Craniotomy only

C. Radiotherapy alone

D. Cabergoline

E. Observation

  1. Non-functioning adenoma recurrence:

A. 10-20% at 10 years; monitor MRI annually x5yr then less frequently

B. Never recurs

C. Always recurs

D. Only if radiotherapy

E. Only if macroadenoma

  1. Non-functioning adenoma hypopituitarism:

A. Pre-op: assess all axes; post-op: most recover; permanent deficit in 20-30%

B. All recover

C. Never recover

D. Always permanent

E. No hypopituitarism

  1. Incidentaloma vs non-functioning:

A. Incidentaloma: asymptomatic, <1cm; asymptomatic MFPA: <1cm, no hormones; same entity essentially

B. Completely different

C. Incidentaloma always functioning

D. Non-functioning always symptomatic

E. No overlap

  1. Non-functioning adenoma surveillance:

A. Annual MRI x3-5yr then less frequent; visual fields; hormone panel

B. No surveillance

C. MRI every 5yr

D. Hormones only

E. Visual fields only

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 50yo woman: incidental 8mm pituitary lesion, normal hormones, normal visual fields. Management?

A. Annual MRI x3yr; hormone panel; observe

B. Surgery

C. Cabergoline

D. RT

E. No follow-up

  1. Same patient: 3yr later lesion 14mm, bitemporal hemianopia. Management?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery (visual loss + >1cm)

B. Observation

C. Cabergoline

D. RT

E. Repeat MRI in 1yr

  1. 30yo man: 15mm pituitary lesion, prolactin 80 (mild), normal visual fields. Diagnosis?

A. Non-functioning adenoma (stalk effect; prolactin <100)

B. Prolactinoma

C. Cushing disease

D. Acromegaly

E. TSHoma

  1. Same patient: treatment?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery (size >1cm); NOT cabergoline

B. Cabergoline

C. RT

D. Observation

E. Bromocriptine

  1. Non-functioning adenoma post-surgery: cortisol 200, TSH normal, LH/FSH low. Management?

A. Hydrocortisone + sex hormone replacement; monitor

B. Levothyroxine only

C. Sex hormones only

D. No replacement

E. Cabergoline

19. Flashcards

A: 30-40% of pituitary adenomas

A: Visual loss, headache, hypopituitarism (NO hormone excess)

A: Stalk effect <100 mIU/L; NOT prolactinoma

A: Transsphenoidal decompression; visual fields improve 80-90%

A: 10-20% at 10yr; annual MRI x5yr

A: Asymptomatic, <1cm; same as asymptomatic non-functioning
