topics.title: Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer
topics.html:
[!info]
Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer (ATC) = Undifferentiated Thyroid Carcinoma. Rare (1-2% of Thyroid Cancers) but Highly Aggressive. Median Survival 3-6 Months. Presentation: Rapidly Growing Neck Mass, Airway Compromise, Dysphagia, Hoarseness. Multimodal Therapy (Surgery + RT + Chemo) — Palliative in Most.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation and differentiate from other thyroid malignancies
  • [ ] Apply staging (All Stage IV) and prognostic factors
  • [ ] Outline multimodal management algorithm
  • [ ] Manage airway emergencies and palliative care

2. Epidemiology & Pathophysiology

FeatureDetails
Incidence1-2% of Thyroid Cancers
Age>60 Years (Mean 70)
SexFemale > Male (2-3:1)
Risk FactorsPre-existing DTC (Dedifferentiation), Radiation Exposure, Goitre, Iodine Deficiency
GeneticsTP53 (80%), TERT Promoter, PI3K/AKT, RAS, BRAF (From DTC Background)

3. Clinical Presentation

FeatureFrequencyDetails
Rapidly Growing Neck Mass90-100%Weeks Duration; Hard, Fixed, Infiltrative
Airway Compromise50-70%Stridor, Dyspnoea, Orthopnoea
Dysphagia40-60%Oesophageal Compression
Hoarseness30-50%Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Invasion
Cervical Lymphadenopathy40-60%Fixed, Matted Nodes
Distant Mets at Diagnosis40-50%Lung (90%), Bone, Brain, Liver
Thyroid Storm / ThyrotoxicosisRareEctopic TSH/Thyroid Hormone Secretion

4. Staging (AJCC 8th) — All Stage IV

Stage GroupCriteria
IVAT4a (Intrathyroidal, Resectable), N0/N1, M0
IVBT4b (Gross Extrathyroidal Extension, Unresectable), Any N, M0
IVCAny T, Any N, M1

5. Diagnostic Workup

InvestigationPurpose
FNA Core BiopsyDiagnosis (Undifferentiated, Pleomorphic, Giant Cells)
IHCNegative: Thyroglobulin, TTF-1, Calcitonin; Positive: Vimentin, p53, Ki-67 (High)
CT Neck/ChestLocal Extent, Nodal Involvement, Lung Mets
MRI NeckSoft Tissue Invasion (Trachea, Oesophagus, Vessels, Nerves)
PET-CTDistant Staging (High FDG Avidity)
LaryngoscopyVocal Cord Function (Pre-Op)

6. Management Algorithm

```

ATC Diagnosed (FNA + IHC)

│

▼

ASSESS RESPECTABILITY + AIRWAY STATUS

│

├── AIRWAY COMPROMISE

│ → TRACHEOSTOMY (Urgent) / INTUBATION

│

├── POTENTIALLY RESECTABLE (T4a, No Major Vessel Encasement)

│ → TOTAL THYROIDECTOMY + CND + MODIFIED RADICAL ND (If Nodes)

│ → ADJUVANT RT (60-66 Gy) + CONCURRENT CHEMO (Doxorubicin/Cisplatin)

│

├── UNRESECTABLE LOCAL (T4b) / NODES / DISTANT METS

│ → CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION (Definitive)

│ │ RT: 60-66 Gy / 30-33 Fractions

│ │ Chemo: Doxorubicin 60mg/m² q3wk OR Cisplatin 40mg/m² Weekly

│ │

│ └── SYSTEMIC THERAPY (If Metastatic/Progression)

│ Doxorubicin (Single Agent / Combo)

│ Cisplatin (Single Agent / Combo)

│ Paclitaxel / Docetaxel (Weekly)

│ Lenvatinib / Sorafenib (TKI - Emerging)

│ Immunotherapy (Pembrolizumab - TMB-High/MSI-H)

```

7. Treatment Modalities

Surgery

IndicationProcedure
Resectable (T4a)Total Thyroidectomy + Central Neck Dissection + Modified Radical Neck Dissection (If Nodes)
Airway EmergencyTracheostomy (Preferred Over Intubation for Long-Term)
PalliativeDebulking for Airway/Oesophageal Compression

Radiation Therapy (RT)

SettingDose / Fractionation
Adjuvant (Post-Op)60-66 Gy / 30-33 Fractions (Concurrent Chemo)
Definitive (Unresectable)60-66 Gy / 30-33 Fractions (Concurrent Chemo)
Palliative30-40 Gy / 10-15 Fractions (Bleeding, Airway, Pain)

Chemotherapy

RegimenDose / ScheduleSetting
Doxorubicin60-75 mg/m² IV q3wkStandard Single Agent
Cisplatin40 mg/m² IV Weekly (Concurrent RT)Concurrent Chemoradiation
Paclitaxel80 mg/m² WeeklyAlternative/Tolerable
Docetaxel75 mg/m² q3wkAlternative
Cisplatin + DoxorubicinCisplatin 40mg/m² D1 + Doxorubicin 50mg/m² D1 q3wkCombination (Higher Toxicity)

Targeted Therapy / Immunotherapy

AgentIndication
LenvatinibProgressive, Metastatic (Off-Label/Emerging)
SorafenibProgressive, Metastatic
PembrolizumabTMB-High / MSI-H / dMMR (Subset of ATC)
Dabrafenib + TrametinibBRAF V600E Mutant (Off-Label)

8. Airway Management (Critical)

ScenarioAction
Impending ObstructionAwake Fibreoptic Intubation or Tracheostomy (Under Local)
Complete ObstructionEmergency Tracheostomy / Cricothyroidotomy
Post-OpTracheostomy Often Required (Swelling, Nerve Injury)

9. Prognosis & Survival

ParameterValue
Median Survival3-6 Months
1-Year Survival20-30%
5-Year Survival<5%
Better Prognosis<60 Years, Small Tumour (<5cm), Resectable, No Distant Mets
Worse Prognosis>70 Years, T4b, Distant Mets, High LDH, Leukocytosis

10. Palliative & End-of-Life Care

AspectManagement
PainOpioid Ladder; Radiotherapy (Bone Mets)
DyspnoeaOpioids, Benzodiazepines, Oxygen, Palliative RT
DysphagiaNG Tube / PEG; Palliative RT / Stent
BleedingPalliative RT; Tranexamic Acid; Embolisation
Tracheostomy CareHumidification, Suctioning, Speech Therapy

topics.title: Central Hypothyroidism
topics.html:
[!info]
Central Hypothyroidism = Thyroid Hormone Deficiency due to Pituitary (Secondary) or Hypothalamic (Tertiary) Failure. TSH is Low or Inappropriately Normal despite Low fT4. Most Common Cause: Pituitary Adenoma / Surgery / Radiotherapy.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define central hypothyroidism and distinguish Secondary vs Tertiary
  • [ ] Recognise the diagnostic hallmark: Low fT4 + Inappropriately Low/Normal TSH
  • [ ] List aetiologies (pituitary, hypothalamic, iatrogenic)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic algorithm (TSH + fT4 → MRI pituitary)
  • [ ] Prescribe levothyroxine replacement (initiate BEFORE glucocorticoid)

2. Definition & Classification

TypeLevel of DefectTSH PatternfT4Key Features
Secondary (Pituitary)Pituitary (TSH deficiency)Low / Inappropriately NormalLowOther pituitary deficits common
Tertiary (Hypothalamic)Hypothalamus (TRH deficiency)Low / Inappropriately NormalLowOften from Suprasellar Lesions / Radiation
Primary (for Contrast)ThyroidHighLowMost Common

Diagnostic Hallmark: Low fT4 + TSH NOT Elevated (TSH Low / Normal / Inappropriately Normal)

3. Aetiology

CategoryCauses
Pituitary AdenomaMacroadenoma (Compression), Surgery (TSS), Radiotherapy
Suprasellar TumoursCraniopharyngioma, Meningioma, Glioma
Infiltrative/InflammatorySarcoidosis, LCH, Haemochromatosis, TB, Lymphoma, Metastases
VascularPituitary Apoplexy, Sheehan Syndrome, Carotid Aneurysm
InflammatoryLymphocytic Hypophysitis (Postpartum), Autoimmune (APS)
IatrogenicSurgery (TSS), Radiotherapy (Delayed 5-10yr), Checkpoint Inhibitors
GeneticPROP1, POU1F1, LHX3, HESX1, SOX3, OTX2
TraumaBasal Skull Fracture, Diffuse Axonal Injury

4. Frequency of Hormone Loss (Order of Vulnerability)

OrderAxisHormonesMechanism
1st (Most Vulnerable)GHGH / IGF-1Somatotrophs most sensitive to compression/ischaemia
2ndGonadotrophsLH / FSHGonadotrophs sensitive to compression
3rdTSHTSH / fT4Thyrotrophs moderately resistant
4th (Least)ACTHACTH / CortisolCorticotrophs most resistant

Mnemonic: Go Go To Adrenal (GH → Gonad → TSH → ACTH)

5. Clinical Presentation

Central Hypothyroidism Features

FeatureDetails
GeneralFatigue, Weight Gain, Cold Intolerance, Constipation, Dry Skin
CardiacBradycardia, Hyperlipidaemia, Pericardial Effusion
NeuromuscularProximal Myopathy, Cramps, Carpal Tunnel
ReproductiveAmenorrhoea, Infertility, Low Libido
NeuropsychiatricDepression, Cognitive Slowing, Memory Impairment
Absence ofGoitre (Thyroid Atrophic), Hyperpigmentation

Clues to Aetiology

ClueSuggests
Bitemporal HemianopiaPituitary Macroadenoma / Craniopharyngioma
Headache + Visual LossSuprasellar Mass
DI (Polyuria/Polydipsia)Hypothalamic/Stalk Involvement
HyperprolactinaemiaStalk Effect (Disconnection) or Prolactinoma
Cranial Nerve PalsiesCavernous Sinus Invasion
Postpartum OnsetSheehan Syndrome / Lymphocytic Hypophysitis

6. Diagnosis — Diagnostic Algorithm

```

Clinical Suspicion (Hypothyroid Symptoms + No Goitre / Pituitary Signs)

│

▼

TSH + fT4 (Simultaneous)

│

├── fT4 LOW + TSH HIGH → PRIMARY HYPOTHYROIDISM (Hashimoto/Post-Surgical)

│

├── fT4 LOW + TSH LOW / NORMAL (INAPPROPRIATELY NORMAL)

│ │

│ ├── TSH LOW → SECONDARY (PITUITARY) / TERTIARY (HYPOTHALAMIC)

│ │

│ └── TSH NORMAL (INAPPROPRIATELY NORMAL) → SECONDARY / TERTIARY

│

└── fT4 NORMAL → NOT HYPOTHYROID

```

Confirmatory Tests

TestPurpose
fT4 + TSH (Paired)Gold Standard: Low fT4 + Inappropriately Low/Normal TSH = Central

| TRH Stimulation Test | TRH 200µg IV → TSH at 0, 20, 40, 60, 90, 120min

Central Hypo: Blunted/Delayed TSH Rise |

MRI Pituitary (3mm, Gadolinium)Gold Standard Imaging: Pituitary Stalk, Adenoma, Infiltration, Empty Sella
Full Pituitary PanelGH/IGF-1, LH/FSH, Prolactin, ACTH/Cortisol, TSH/fT4, Electrolytes

7. Secondary vs Tertiary Differentiation

FeatureSecondary (Pituitary)Tertiary (Hypothalamic)
TSH Response to TRHAbsent / BluntedDelayed / Exaggerated
TRH TestTSH No RiseDelayed/Prolonged Rise
Common CausesPituitary Adenoma, Surgery, RT, ApoplexyCraniopharyngioma, Hypothalamic Glioma, Sarcoid, Trauma
Associated DeficitsPanhypopituitarism PatternDI + Hypogonadism + Thermoregulation Issues

8. Iatrogenic Central Hypothyroidism

Post-Radiotherapy

TimelineDeficiency
6-12 monthsGH Deficiency (First)
2-5 yearsGonadotroph Deficiency
5-10 yearsTSH Deficiency
>10 yearsACTH Deficiency (Rare)

Surveillance: Annual Pituitary Hormones for 10+ Years Post-RT

Post-Surgical (TSS)

TimepointIncidence
ImmediateTransient DI (10-20%); New Hypopituitarism 5-10%
3-6 monthsGH > Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH

Checkpoint Inhibitor Hypophysitis

FeatureDetails
Anti-PD1/PD-L1/CTLA-4Grades 1-4; ACTH Deficiency Most Common
ManagementHigh-Dose Steroids (Pred 1-2mg/kg) + Hormone Replacement

9. Management

Levothyroxine Replacement

PrincipleDetails
Initiate BEFORE GlucocorticoidCritical: Thyroxine Increases Cortisol Clearance → Can Precipitate Adrenal Crisis
Starting Dose1.6 µg/kg/day (Young) → 25-50µg (Elderly/Cardiac)
TargetfT4 Upper Half of Normal (TSH Unreliable for Monitoring)
MonitoringfT4 q4-6wk until Stable; Then q6-12mo; TSH Unreliable

Replacement Priority Order

PriorityHormoneRationale
1stLevothyroxine (Thyroxine)Prevents Adrenal Crisis When Glucocorticoid Started
2ndGlucocorticoid (Hydrocortisone)Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
3rdSex SteroidsTestosterone / Oestradiol + Progestogen
4thGHIf Symptomatic GHD (ITT Peak <3 µg/L)

10. Monitoring

ParameterFrequency
fT4q4-6wk (Titration) → q6-12mo (Stable)
TSHUnreliable (Do Not Use for Dosing)
Other Axesq6mo (GH/IGF-1, LH/FSH, ACTH/Cortisol)
ImagingMRI Pituitary (Baseline, 6mo, 1yr, then Yearly ×5)

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Central Hypothyroidism HallmarkLow fT4 + TSH Low/Normal (Inappropriately Normal)
Primary vs CentralPrimary: TSH ↑↑ + fT4 ↓; Central: TSH Low/Normal + fT4 ↓
Secondary vs TertiarySecondary = Pituitary; Tertiary = Hypothalamic
TRH TestSecondary: No TSH Rise; Tertiary: Delayed/Exaggerated Rise
Hormone Loss OrderGH → Gonad → TSH → ACTH (Go Go To Adrenal)
Most Common CausePituitary Adenoma / Surgery / Radiotherapy
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis → Empty Sella; Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea
Levothyroxine TimingStart BEFORE Glucocorticoid (Prevent Adrenal Crisis)
MonitoringfT4 (Not TSH); Target fT4 Upper Normal
MRI IndicationAll Central Hypothyroidism (Exclude Mass)
TSH UnreliableDo Not Use TSH for Dose Adjustment in Central Hypo
CSF Leak in ESSRare; Defective Diaphragma Sellae

12. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Central Hypothyroidism))

Definition

Low fT4 + TSH Low/Normal (Inappropriately Normal)

Secondary (Pituitary)

Adenoma, Surgery, RT, Apoplexy, Sheehan

Tertiary (Hypothalamic)

Craniopharyngioma, Sarcoid, Trauma, Genetic

Diagnosis

fT4 Low + TSH Low/Normal → Central

MRI Pituitary (Gold Standard)

TRH Test: Secondary=No Rise; Tertiary=Delayed Rise

Hormone Loss Order

GH → Gonad → TSH → ACTH

"Go Go To Adrenal"

Replacement

Thyroxine FIRST (Before Glucocorticoid)

Monitor fT4 (Not TSH)

fT4 Upper Normal Target

Causes

Pituitary Adenoma (Most Common)

Surgery (TSS/Transcranial)

Radiotherapy (Delayed 5-10yr)

Sheehan (Postpartum Necrosis)

Craniopharyngioma (Suprasellar)

Lymphocytic Hypophysitis (Postpartum)

Checkpoint Inhibitors (Ai)

```

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Diagnostic HallmarkLow fT4 + TSH Low/Normal (Inappropriately Normal)
Primary vs CentralPrimary: TSH↑; Central: TSH Low/Normal
TSH in Central HypoLow or Inappropriately Normal (NOT Elevated)
Hormone Loss OrderGH → Gonad → TSH → ACTH
TRH TestSecondary: No Rise; Tertiary: Delayed Rise
Levothyroxine PriorityStart BEFORE Glucocorticoid (Prevent Adrenal Crisis)
MonitoringfT4 (Not TSH) — Target fT4 Upper Normal
Commonest CausePituitary Adenoma / Surgery / RT
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Necrosis → Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea
TSH UnreliableDo Not Use TSH for Dosing in Central Hypo
MRI IndicationAll Central Hypothyroidism (Exclude Mass)

14. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Thyroid Disorders|Thyroid Disorders]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]

See also: [[Hypothyroidism Overview]], [[Hypopituitarism]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Non-Functioning]], [[Sheehan Syndrome]], [[TSH/Thyroid Axis]]

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Central hypothyroidism =

A. Secondary (pituitary) or tertiary (hypothalamic) failure → low fT4 + inappropriately normal/low TSH

B. Primary thyroid failure

C. Thyroid hormone resistance

D. TSH-secreting adenoma

E. Factitious hypothyroidism

  1. Central hypothyroidism TSH pattern:

A. Low or inappropriately normal (NOT elevated) + low fT4

B. Elevated

C. Suppressed

D. Normal with high fT4

E. Pulsatile

  1. Commonest cause central hypo:

A. Pituitary macroadenoma / surgery / radiotherapy

B. Sheehan syndrome

C. Craniopharyngioma

D. Lymphocytic hypophysitis

E. Empty sella

  1. TRH test in central hypothyroidism:

A. Blunted/delayed TSH response

B. Exaggerated TSH response

C. Normal TSH response

D. Absent TSH

E. Prolonged peak

  1. Levothyroxine replacement in central hypo:

A. Target fT4 mid-normal; check cortisol first (co-existing ACTH deficiency)

B. Same as primary

C. Higher dose

D. Lower dose

E. T3 only

  1. Central vs primary hypo key difference:

A. Primary: TSH ↑↑; Central: TSH low/normal (inappropriately)

B. Primary: TSH low; Central: TSH high

C. Both TSH high

D. Both TSH low

E. No difference

  1. Sheehan syndrome:

A. Postpartum pituitary necrosis → panhypopituitarism; empty sella on MRI

B. Postpartum thyroiditis

C. Postpartum psychosis

D. Postpartum haemorrhage only

E. Postpartum cardiomyopathy

  1. Lymphocytic hypophysitis:

A. Autoimmune pituitary inflammation; peripartum; mass lesion on MRI

B. Bacterial pituitary infection

C. Pituitary adenoma

D. Craniopharyngioma

E. Empty sella

  1. Craniopharyngioma:

A. Suprasellar tumour; childhood/adolescent; visual field defects

B. Pituitary microadenoma

C. TSH-secreting adenoma

D. Empty sella

E. Rathke's cleft cyst

  1. Empty sella syndrome:

A. CSF herniation into sella; may cause central hypo; MRI: CSF in sella

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Craniopharyngioma

D. Normal variant only

E. Post-RAI change

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Post-pituitary surgery: fatigue, TSH 1.5, fT4 4. Diagnosis?

A. Central hypothyroidism

B. Primary hypothyroidism

C. Subclinical hypothyroidism

D. Euthyroid sick syndrome

E. Thyroid hormone resistance

  1. Same patient: check before starting levothyroxine?

A. Cortisol (co-existing ACTH deficiency → adrenal crisis risk)

B. Prolactin

C. GH

D. LH/FSH

E. TSH only

  1. 30yo woman: postpartum inability to breastfeed, fatigue, TSH 0.8, fT4 5, empty sella on MRI. Diagnosis?

A. Sheehan syndrome

B. Lymphocytic hypophysitis

C. Postpartum thyroiditis

D. Primary hypothyroidism

E. Euthyroid sick

  1. Central hypo on levothyroxine: TSH still low. Is this adequate?

A. Yes (TSH not used for monitoring central hypo); target fT4 mid-normal

B. No → increase dose

C. No → decrease dose

D. Switch to T3

E. Stop levothyroxine

  1. Craniopharyngioma child: visual field defects, polydipsia, TSH low, fT4 low. Next?

A. MRI brain → neurosurgery referral

B. Levothyroxine only

C. RAI

D. Carbimazole

E. Observation

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Central hypo definition

A: Low fT4 + inappropriately normal/low TSH (NOT elevated)

  • Q: TSH in central hypo

A: Low or inappropriately normal (NOT elevated)

  • Q: Commonest cause

A: Pituitary macroadenoma / surgery / radiotherapy

  • Q: Sheehan syndrome

A: Postpartum pituitary necrosis → panhypopituitarism; empty sella

  • Q: Lymphocytic hypophysitis

A: Autoimmune pituitary; peripartum; mass lesion on MRI

  • Q: Craniopharyngioma

A: Suprasellar tumour; childhood/adolescent; visual field defects

  • Q: Empty sella

A: CSF in sella; may cause central hypo; mostly asymptomatic

  • Q: TRH test

A: Central hypo: blunted/delayed TSH response

  • Q: Replacement

A: Target fT4 mid-normal; CHECK CORTISOL FIRST (ACTH deficiency)

  • Q: Monitoring

A: fT4 (not TSH); TSH remains low/suppressed on replacement

MCQs

  1. Primary: TSH ↑↑; Central: TSH low/normal (inappropriately) — Central vs primary hypo key difference:
  1. Postpartum pituitary necrosis → panhypopituitarism; empty sella on MRI — Sheehan syndrome:
  1. Autoimmune pituitary inflammation; peripartum; mass lesion on MRI — Lymphocytic hypophysitis:
  1. Suprasellar tumour; childhood/adolescent; visual field defects — Craniopharyngioma:
  1. CSF herniation into sella; may cause central hypo; MRI: CSF in sella — Empty sella syndrome:

SBAs

topics.title: Differentiated Thyroid Cancer Papillary Follicular
topics.html:
[!info]
Differentiated Thyroid Cancer (DTC) = Papillary (80-85%) + Follicular (10-15%). Excellent Prognosis (20-Year Survival >90%). ATA Risk Stratification (Low/Intermediate/High) Guides RAI and TSH Suppression.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply TNM staging (AJCC 8th) and ATA risk stratification
  • [ ] Indicate radioactive iodine (RAI) therapy based on risk
  • [ ] Apply TSH suppression targets by risk category
  • [ ] Outline follow-up protocol (Thyroglobulin, Ultrasound, WBS)

2. Classification & Epidemiology

TypeFrequencyKey Features
Papillary (PTC)80-85%Most Common; Lymphatic Spread; BRAF V600E (60%), RAS, RET/PTC Rearrangements
Follicular (FTC)10-15%Vascular Invasion; Haematogenous Spread (Bone, Lung); RAS, PAX8/PPARγ
Hürthle Cell3-5%Oncocytic Variant; More Aggressive; Often Classified as FTC Variant
Poorly Differentiated<5%High Mitotic Rate, Necrosis; Intermediate Behaviour

Demographics

FeatureDetails
IncidenceIncreasing (Better Detection); 3x Female > Male
Peak Age30-50 Years
Risk FactorsRadiation Exposure (Childhood), Iodine Deficiency, Genetic (Familial DTC 5%)

3. TNM Staging (AJCC 8th Edition) — Differentiated Thyroid Cancer

Age <55 Years (All Stage I/II)

StageCriteria
Stage IAny T, Any N, M0
Stage IIAny T, Any N, M1

Age ≥55 Years

StageTNM
IT1-T2N0/NXM0
IIT1-T2N1M0
T3aAny NM0
IIIT3bAny NM0
T4aAny NM0
IVAT4bAny NM0
IVBAny TAny NM1

T Category (Primary Tumour)

TCriteria
T1a≤1 cm (Intrathyroidal)
T1b>1-2 cm (Intrathyroidal)
T2>2-4 cm (Intrathyroidal)
T3a>4 cm (Intrathyroidal) OR Minimal Extrathyroidal Extension (Strap Muscles)
T3bGross Extrathyroidal Extension (Larynx, Trachea, Oesophagus, Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve)
T4aExtension Beyond Thyroid to Prevertebral Fascia, Carotid Artery, Mediastinal Vessels
T4bCarotid Artery Encasement, Prevertebral Fascia Invasion

N Category (Regional Nodes)

NCriteria
N0No Regional Nodes
N1aCentral Compartment (Level VI, VII: Prelaryngeal, Pretracheal, Paratracheal)
N1bLateral Compartment (Level I-V: Jugular, Scalene, Supraclavicular)

4. ATA Risk Stratification (Post-Surgery)

Risk CategoryCriteriaRecurrence Risk
LowNo Local/Gross Invasion, No Nodal Mets, No Vascular Invasion, <0.5cm PTC, No Aggressive Histology, Thyroglobulin Undetectable, No RAI Uptake Outside Bed<5%
IntermediateMicroscopic Invasion, N1 (≥5 Nodes), Vascular Invasion, 1-4cm PTC, Aggressive Histology (Tall Cell, Hobnail), Thyroglobulin Detectable, RAI Uptake Outside Bed5-20%
HighGross Extrathyroidal Extension, Incomplete Resection, Distant Mets, N1b (Lateral Nodes), Extensive Vascular Invasion, Thyroglobulin High, RAI Uptake Distant>20%

5. Management Algorithm

```

DTC Diagnosed (FNA/Histology)

│

▼

TOTAL THYROIDECTOMY ± CENTRAL NECK DISSECTION (Level VI)

│

├── T1a N0 (Low Risk) → Lobectomy May Suffice

└── T1b/T2 or N+ or High Risk → Total Thyroidectomy + CND

│

▼

POST-OP RISK STRATIFICATION (ATA)

│

├── LOW RISK

│ → RAI: Not Routine (Consider if Tg Detectable)

│ → TSH 0.5-2.0 mIU/L

│

├── INTERMEDIATE RISK

│ → RAI 30-100 mCi (Ablation)

│ → TSH 0.1-0.5 mIU/L

│

└── HIGH RISK

→ RAI 100-200 mCi (Ablation + Treatment)

→ TSH <0.1 mIU/L

```

6. Radioactive Iodine (RAI) Therapy

IndicationDosePreparation
Remnant Ablation (Low/Intermediate)30-100 mCiLow Iodine Diet 2 Weeks; TSH >30 (Thyroxine Withdrawal 4-6wk) OR rhTSH (Thyrogen)
Adjuvant Treatment (High Risk)100-200 mCiSame Preparation
Metastatic Disease150-250 mCi (Repeated q6-12mo)Same; May Need Hospitalisation

RAI Preparation

StepDetails
Low Iodine Diet2 Weeks Pre-RAI (<50 µg/day)
TSH StimulationThyroxine Withdrawal (LT4 Stop 4-6wk, LT3 Stop 2wk) → TSH >30 OR rhTSH (Thyrogen 0.9mg IM Day 1 & 2)
Contraception6 Months Post-RAI (Both Sexes)
Pregnancy TestMandatory Pre-RAI (Serum β-hCG)

7. TSH Suppression Targets

Risk CategoryTSH Target (mIU/L)Duration
Low0.5-2.0Lifelong (Adjust for Age/Comorbidities)
Intermediate0.1-0.55-10 Years, Then Reassess
High<0.110+ Years / Lifelong
Elderly/Osteoporosis/AF: Relax Targets (0.5-2.0 for Low, 0.5-1.0 for Intermediate)

8. Follow-Up & Surveillance

TimepointInvestigations
6-8 Weeks Post-RAITg, Anti-Tg, TSH, fT4, Neck US, Diagnostic WBS (If Post-Therapy)
6 MonthsTg, Anti-Tg, TSH, fT4, Neck US
12 MonthsTg, Anti-Tg, TSH, fT4, Neck US, Diagnostic WBS (If Intermediate/High)
Annual (Years 2-5)Tg, Anti-Tg, TSH, Neck US
Long-Term (>5yr)Tg, TSH, Neck US (Low Risk: q1-2yr); WBS if Tg Rising

Thyroglobulin (Tg) Monitoring

ScenarioAction
Tg Undetectable (<0.5-1 ng/mL) + TSH StimulatedExcellent Response → De-Escalate Surveillance
Tg Detectable (1-10 ng/mL)Indeterminate → Neck US, Consider Diagnostic WBS
Tg Rising / >10 ng/mLStructural Recurrence Likely → Diagnostic WBS, PET-CT, Cross-Sectional Imaging
Anti-Tg PositiveInterferes with Tg Assay → Use Trend, Not Absolute Value

9. Recurrent/Persistent Disease

SiteManagement
Central Neck NodesCompletion CND (If Missed) or RAI (If RAI-Avid)
Lateral Neck NodesModified Radical Neck Dissection (Levels II-V)
Distant Mets (Lung/Bone)RAI (If Avid) → TKI (Lenvatinib/Sorafenib) (If Refractory)
Non-RAI-AvidTKI (Lenvatinib/Sorafenib), Clinical Trials, EBRT (Bone)

10. Special Situations

Pregnancy

AspectManagement
RAIContraindicated
Surgery2nd Trimester Preferred (If Urgent)
TSH Target<2.5 mIU/L (1st Tri), <3.0 (2nd/3rd)
Tg MonitoringSafe (No RAI)

Pediatric

AspectManagement
More AggressiveHigher Nodal/Distant Mets Rate
SurgeryTotal Thyroidectomy + CND (Standard)
RAIMore Liberal Indications
Long-TermHigher Secondary Cancer Risk (RAI)

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
DTC TypesPapillary (80-85%) > Follicular (10-15%)
Papillary GeneticsBRAF V600E (60%), RET/PTC, RAS
Follicular GeneticsRAS, PAX8/PPARγ; Vascular Invasion
Staging Age Cutoff<55 = Stage I/II Only (No Stage III/IV)
ATA Low RiskNo Invasion, N0, No Vasc Inv, <0.5cm PTC, Tg Undetectable
ATA Low Risk RAINot Routine
TSH Suppression Low0.5-2.0 mIU/L
TSH Suppression High<0.1 mIU/L
RAI Dose Low/Int30-100 mCi; High 100-200 mCi
RAI PrepLow Iodine Diet 2wk + TSH >30 (Withdrawal or rhTSH)
Contraception Post-RAI6 Months
Tg MonitoringUndetectable + Stimulated = Excellent Response
Anti-Tg AntibodiesInterfere with Tg Assay → Use Trend
Recurrent NodesSurgery (CND/MRND) or RAI (If Avid)
RAI-Refractory MetsLenvatinib/Sorafenib (TKI)
Age <55 StagingAll M0 = Stage I; M1 = Stage II

12. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Differentiated Thyroid Cancer))

Types

Papillary (80-85%)

BRAF V600E, RET/PTC, RAS

Lymphatic Spread

Follicular (10-15%)

RAS, PAX8/PPARγ

Vascular Invasion

Staging (AJCC 8th)

<55y: Stage I/II Only

≥55y: T1-4, N1a/b, M1

ATA Risk

Low: No Invasion, N0, Tg Undetectable

Intermediate: Microinvasion, N1a, Vasc Inv

High: Gross ETE, N1b, Mets, Incomplete Resection

Surgery

Total Thyroidectomy + CND

Lobectomy if T1a N0 Low Risk

RAI

Low Risk: Not Routine

Intermediate: 30-100 mCi

High: 100-200 mCi

Prep: Low Iodine Diet + TSH >30 (Withdrawal/rhTSH)

TSH Suppression

Low: 0.5-2.0

Intermediate: 0.1-0.5

High: <0.1

Follow-up

Tg + Anti-Tg + Neck US

6mo, 1yr, then Annual

Tb Stimulated Tg if Low Risk

Recurrence

Central Nodes: CND or RAI

Lateral Nodes: MRND

Distant: RAI → TKI (Lenvatinib/Sorafenib) if Refractory

```

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Papillary vs FollicularPTC: Lymphatic, BRAF; FTC: Vascular, RAS
Staging Age<55: Only Stage I/II; ≥55: Full Staging
ATA Low RiskNo Invasion, N0, Tg Undetectable
ATA High RiskGross ETE, N1b, Mets, Incomplete Resection
RAI IndicationLow: Not Routine; Int: 30-100 mCi; High: 100-200 mCi
RAI PrepLow Iodine 2wk + TSH >30 (Withdrawal or rhTSH)
TSH Target Low0.5-2.0 mIU/L
TSH Target High<0.1 mIU/L
Contraception Post-RAI6 Months
Tg UndetectableExcellent Response
Anti-Tg AbInterferes with Tg → Use Trend
RAI RefractoryLenvatinib/Sorafenib (TKI)
Recurrent Central NodesCND or RAI
Recurrent Lateral NodesModified Radical Neck Dissection

14. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Thyroid Nodule Evaluation (TI-RADS)|Thyroid Nodule Evaluation (TI-RADS)]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Thyroid Nodule Evaluation (TI-RADS)]], [[Medullary Thyroid Cancer]], [[Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer]], [[Radioactive Iodine Therapy]], [[TSH Suppression]], [[Thyroid in Pregnancy]]

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Differentiated thyroid cancer types:

A. Papillary (PTC) and Follicular (FTC)

B. Medullary and Anaplastic

C. Hurthle cell only

D. Insular only

E. Poorly differentiated only

  1. PTC most common genetic alteration:

A. BRAF V600E (60%), RET/PTC rearrangements

B. RAS

C. PAX8-PPARG

D. TP53

E. TERT only

  1. FTC most common genetic alteration:

A. RAS mutations, PAX8-PPARG fusion

B. BRAF V600E

C. RET/PTC

D. TP53

E. TERT only

  1. PTC vs FTC spread:

A. PTC: lymphatic (cervical nodes); FTC: haematogenous (lung, bone)

B. Both lymphatic

C. Both haematogenous

D. PTC: haematogenous; FTC: lymphatic

E. Neither spreads

  1. DTC staging (AJCC 8th) - age cutoff:

A. 55 years (age <55: Stage I/II only; ≥55: I-IV)

B. 45 years

C. 65 years

D. No age cutoff

E. 60 years

  1. RAI remnant ablation indications:

A. Intermediate/high risk: >40yo, >4cm, gross ETE, mets; low risk: consider omitted

B. All DTC

C. Only PTC

D. Only FTC

E. Never needed

  1. Thyroglobulin (Tg) monitoring:

A. Post-TT + RAI: Tg <1 ng/mL (undetectable) = excellent response; rising Tg = recurrence

B. Tg not useful

C. Only for MTC

D. Only pre-op

E. Only for ATC

  1. TSH suppression targets:

A. Low risk: 0.5-2.0; Intermediate: 0.1-0.5; High risk: <0.1 mIU/L

B. All 0.4-4.0

C. All <0.1

D. All 2.5-5.0

E. Not needed

  1. DTC prognosis:

A. Excellent: 95% 10-yr survival (PTC); FTC slightly worse if angioinvasion

B. Poor: 50% 5-yr

C. Fatal in 1yr

D. Variable 10-20%

E. Depends on RAI only

  1. DTC recurrence risk stratification (ATA):

A. Low / Intermediate / High based on pathology, Tg, imaging

B. Only TNM

C. Only Tg

D. Only age

E. Only size

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 40yo woman: PTC 2cm, no ETE, no nodes. Post-TT: Tg 0.5, negative US. Risk?

A. Low risk (TSH suppression 0.5-2.0; consider no RAI)

B. High risk (RAI mandatory)

C. Intermediate (RAI 30mCi)

D. Very high (RAI 150mCi)

E. No follow-up needed

  1. 55yo man: FTC 4cm, capsular invasion, no nodes. Post-TT. Risk?

A. Intermediate (RAI 30-100mCi; TSH <0.1)

B. Low risk (no RAI)

C. High risk (RAI 150mCi)

D. Very low (observation)

E. Anaplastic

  1. DTC with rising Tg, negative US, positive I-131 scan in neck. Management?

A. Therapeutic RAI (150-200mCi) + re-imaging

B. Surgery

C. EBRT

D. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor

E. Observation

  1. DTC lung mets, Tg 50, RAI avid. Rx?

A. High-dose RAI (150-200mCi) + TSH suppression <0.1; repeat if needed

B. Surgery

C. EBRT only

D. Chemo

E. Observation

  1. DTC RAI-refractory lung mets, progress on TKI. Next?

A. Switch TKI (lenvatinib/sorafenib) or clinical trial

B. More RAI

C. Surgery

D. EBRT

E. Stop treatment

17. Flashcards

  • Q: DTC types

A: PTC (80-85%) + FTC (10-15%); papillary > follicular

  • Q: PTC genetics

A: BRAF V600E 60%, RET/PTC; lymph spread

  • Q: FTC genetics

A: RAS, PAX8-PPARG; haematogenous spread (angioinvasion key)

  • Q: DTC staging

A: Age 55 cutoff: <55 = Stage I/II only; ≥55 = I-IV

  • Q: RAI ablation

A: Intermediate/high risk → RAI; low risk → consider omitting

  • Q: Tg monitoring

A: Post-TT+RAI: Tg <1 = excellent; rising = recurrence

  • Q: TSH suppression

A: Low risk 0.5-2.0; Int 0.1-0.5; High <0.1 mIU/L

  • Q: Prognosis

A: Excellent 95% 10-yr (PTC); FTC worse if angioinvasion

  • Q: RAI-refractory

A: Tg rise + RAI-negative mets → TKI (lenvatinib/sorafenib)

  • Q: ATA risk stratification

A: Low / Intermediate / High → guides TSH target, RAI, follow-up

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Papillary (PTC) and Follicular (FTC) — Differentiated thyroid cancer types:
  1. BRAF V600E (60%), RET/PTC rearrangements — PTC most common genetic alteration:
  1. RAS mutations, PAX8-PPARG fusion — FTC most common genetic alteration:
  1. PTC: lymphatic (cervical nodes); FTC: haematogenous (lung, bone) — PTC vs FTC spread:
  1. 55 years (age <55: Stage I/II only; ≥55: I-IV) — DTC staging (AJCC 8th) - age cutoff:
  1. Intermediate/high risk: >40yo, >4cm, gross ETE, mets; low risk: consider omitted — RAI remnant ablation indications:
  1. Post-TT + RAI: Tg <1 ng/mL (undetectable) = excellent response; rising Tg = recurrence — Thyroglobulin (Tg) monitoring:
  1. Low risk: 0.5-2.0; Intermediate: 0.1-0.5; High risk: <0.1 mIU/L — TSH suppression targets:
  1. Excellent: 95% 10-yr survival (PTC); FTC slightly worse if angioinvasion — DTC prognosis:
  1. Low / Intermediate / High based on pathology, Tg, imaging — DTC recurrence risk stratification (ATA):

SBAs

  1. Low risk (TSH suppression 0.5-2.0; consider no RAI) — 40yo woman: PTC 2cm, no ETE, no nodes. Post-TT: Tg 0.5, negative US. Risk?
  1. Intermediate (RAI 30-100mCi; TSH <0.1) — 55yo man: FTC 4cm, capsular invasion, no nodes. Post-TT. Risk?
  1. Therapeutic RAI (150-200mCi) + re-imaging — DTC with rising Tg, negative US, positive I-131 scan in neck. Management?
  1. High-dose RAI (150-200mCi) + TSH suppression <0.1; repeat if needed — DTC lung mets, Tg 50, RAI avid. Rx?
  1. Switch TKI (lenvatinib/sorafenib) or clinical trial — DTC RAI-refractory lung mets, progress on TKI. Next?

topics.title: Drug Induced Thyroid Dysfunction
topics.html:
[!info]
Drug-induced thyroid dysfunction is common. Amiodarone (Type 1 vs Type 2), Lithium, and Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors are the major culprits. Differentiating iodine-induced (Type 1) from destructive (Type 2) amiodarone thyrotoxicosis is a classic viva topic.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify drugs causing thyroid dysfunction by mechanism
  • [ ] Differentiate amiodarone Type 1 vs Type 2 thyrotoxicosis
  • [ ] Manage lithium-associated thyroid dysfunction
  • [ ] Recognise and manage checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis
  • [ ] Apply appropriate treatment algorithms for each drug

2. Classification by Mechanism

MechanismDrugsThyroid Effect
Excess IodineAmiodarone, Iodinated Contrast, Iodine Supplements, KelpType 1 AIT (Jod-Basedow) / Hypothyroidism (Wolff-Chaikoff)
Direct CytotoxicityAmiodarone (Type 2), Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (Sunitinib, Sorafenib)Destructive Thyroiditis (Tyrotoxic → Hypothyroid)
Autoimmune InductionCheckpoint Inhibitors (Anti-PD1/PD-L1, CTLA-4), Interferon-α, AlemtuzumabAutoimmune Thyroiditis (Destructive → Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid)
Inhibition of Hormone Synthesis/ReleaseLithium, Iodide (High Dose), Sulfonamides, Para-aminosalicylic AcidHypothyroidism ± Goitre
Increased Metabolism/ClearancePhenytoin, Carbamazepine, Rifampicin, Phenobarbital↑ T4 Clearance → ↓ T4, ↑ TSH (Compensated)
TSH Receptor StimulationhCG (Molar Pregnancy, Choriocarcinoma), TSH-secreting AdenomaThyrotoxicosis

3. Amiodarone-Induced Thyroid Dysfunction (AIT)

Background

FeatureDetails
DrugAmiodarone (Class III Antiarrhythmic)
Iodine Content37% Iodine by Weight; 200mg Daily = ~75mg Iodine/Day (vs RDA 150µg) = 500x RDA
Half-life~50-100 Days (Long)
Incidence of AIT5-15% of Patients on Amiodarone
Time to OnsetVariable (Months to Years)

4. Amiodarone-Induced Thyrotoxicosis (AIT) — Type 1 vs Type 2

FeatureType 1 (Iodine Load / Jod-Basedow)Type 2 (Destructive Thyroiditis)
MechanismExcess Iodine Load → Substrate for Hormone Synthesis in Autonomously Functioning Nodules / Latent GravesDirect Cytotoxicity → Destructive Thyroiditis → Hormone Leak
Underlying ThyroidAbnormal (Nodular Goitre, Latent Graves, Iodine Deficiency Area)Normal Thyroid (No Pre-existing Disease)
PathophysiologyExcess Iodine Substrate → ↑ Hormone Synthesis in Autonomous TissueDirect Cytotoxicity → Follicular Destruction → Hormone Leak
Thyroid HistologyNormal / NodularLymphocytic Infiltration, Follicular Destruction, Giant Cells
RAI UptakeNormal / High (Iodine-Rich Gland Takes Up RAI)Very Low / Absent (Destructed Follicles)
Thyroid US / DopplerHypervascular (Colour Doppler)Hypoechoic, Heterogeneous, ↓ Vascularity
Colour Flow DopplerIncreased Vascularity ("Thyroid Inferno")Reduced / Absent Vascularity
s ThyroglobulinNormal / ↑Markedly Elevated (Leak)
IL-6, CRPNormalElevated (Inflammatory)
Response to ThionamidesGood (CBZ/PTU Effective)Poor (Thionamides Ineffective)
Response to SteroidsPoorExcellent (Pred 30-40mg/day)
DurationProlonged (Months-Years)Self-Limiting (3-6 Months)

5. AIT — Diagnostic Algorithm

```

Patient on Amiodarone → Thyrotoxicosis (TSH Suppressed, fT4/fT3 ↑)

│

▼

EXCLUDE OTHER CAUSES (Graves, Toxic Nodule, Thyroiditis)

│

▼

DIFFERENTIATE TYPE 1 vs TYPE 2

│

├── RAI UPTAKE (or 99mTc Pertechnetate Scan)

│ ├── HIGH / NORMAL → TYPE 1

│ └── LOW / ABSENT → TYPE 2

│

├── THYROID US + COLOUR DOPPLER

│ ├── HYPERVASCULAR ("Thyroid Inferno") → TYPE 1

│ └── HYPOECHOIC, HETEROGENEOUS, AVASCULAR → TYPE 2

│

├── SERUM THYROGLOBULIN

│ ├── NORMAL / MILD ↑ → TYPE 1

│ └── MARKEDLY ↑ → TYPE 2

│

├── CRP / ESR / IL-6

│ ├── NORMAL → TYPE 1

│ └── ELEVATED → TYPE 2

│

└── RESPONSE TO THERAPY (Diagnostic Trial)

├── THIONAMIDES (CBZ/PTU) WORK → TYPE 1

└── STEROIDS WORK → TYPE 2

```

6. Management of AIT

Type 1 (Iodine Load / Jod-Basedow)

TreatmentDetails
High-Dose ThionamidesCarbimazole 40-60mg/day or PTU 300-600mg/day (Higher Doses Needed)
Potassium Perchlorate200-500mg QDS (Blocks NIS → Iodide Uptake); Max 4-6 Weeks (Aplastic Anaemia Risk)
Potassium IodideNot Recommended (Worsens Iodine Load)
Beta-BlockersPropranolol 40-80mg QDS (Symptomatic Control)
DefinitiveRAI (After Thionamide Control) / Surgery (If Refractory)

Type 2 (Destructive Thyroiditis)

TreatmentDetails
CorticosteroidsPrednisolone 30-40mg/day (0.5-1mg/kg) → Taper over 4-8 Weeks
Beta-BlockersPropranolol 40-80mg QDS (Symptomatic)
ThionamidesIneffective (No Ongoing Synthesis)
NSAIDsFor Neck Pain (If Any)
Duration3-6 Months (Self-Limiting); Taper Steroids Over 4-8 Weeks

Mixed Type 1/2

ApproachDetails
Combined TherapyThionamide + Steroid (CBZ/PTU + Pred 20-30mg/day)
MonitorfT4, TSH, CRP, Thyroglobulin q2-4 Weeks

7. Amiodarone-Induced Hypothyroidism (AIH)

FeatureDetails
Incidence20-30% (More Common than AIT in Iodine-Sufficient Areas)
MechanismWolff-Chaikoff Effect (Excess Iodide → Transient Inhibition of Organification) → Failure to Escape → Hypothyroidism
Risk FactorsPre-existing Thyroid Autoimmunity (TPOAb+), Iodine Deficiency, Female Sex
OnsetMonths to Years After Starting Amiodarone
DiagnosisTSH ↑↑, fT4 ↓, TPOAb Often Positive
ManagementLevothyroxine (1.6 µg/kg/day); Amiodarone Can Usually Continue; Monitor TSH q3-6mo

8. Lithium-Induced Thyroid Dysfunction

Hypothyroidism (Common)

FeatureDetails
Incidence10-20% on Long-Term Lithium
MechanismInhibits Thyroid Hormone Release (Inhibits cAMP) + Goitrogen (Inhibits Iodide Organification)
Risk FactorsFemale, Pre-existing TPOAb+, High Lithium Levels, Long Duration
PresentationSubclinical → Overt Hypothyroidism; Goitre Common
MonitoringTSH q3-6mo; TPOAb at Baseline
TreatmentLevothyroxine if TSH >10 or Symptomatic; Lithium Can Continue
Stopping LithiumOften Reversible (TSH Normalises in 1-2mo)

Hyperthyroidism (Rare)

MechanismThyrotoxicosis Due to Lithium-Induced Graves (Rare) / Hashitoxicosis
ManagementThionamides if Graves; Monitor

9. Checkpoint Inhibitor Thyroiditis

Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors (ICIs)

Drug ClassExamplesThyroiditis Incidence
Anti-PD-1Pembrolizumab, Nivolumab5-10%
Anti-PD-L1Atezolizumab, Durvalumab, Avelumab3-8%
Anti-CTLA-4Ipilimumab2-5%
Combination (Ipi + Nivo)Ipilimumab + Nivolumab10-20%

Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetails
OnsetMedian 6-12 Weeks (Range 2-24 Weeks)
PatternDestructive Thyroiditis → Thyrotoxic Phase → Hypothyroid Phase
BiochemistryTSH Suppressed → ↑↑, fT4/fT3 ↑ then ↓, TPOAb Often Positive
TRAbUsually Negative
RAI UptakeLow (Destructive)

Grading & Management (ASCO/CTCAE)

GradeCriteriaManagement
Grade 1Asymptomatic; TSH <0.1; fT4 Normal/↑Continue ICI; Monitor TSH/fT4 q2-4wk
Grade 2Symptomatic Thyrotoxicosis; TSH <0.1; fT4 ↑Hold ICI; β-Blocker; Consider Pred 0.5-1mg/kg if Symptomatic
Grade 3Severe Symptoms; TSH <0.01; fT4 ↑↑; ↓ Weight/DecompensatedHold ICI; Pred 1-2mg/kg/day + β-Blocker; Resume ICI if Grade ≤1
Grade 4Life-Threatening (Thyroid Storm)Discontinue ICI; High-Dose Steroids; ICU Care

Hypothyroid Phase

Timing4-12 Weeks Post-Thyrotoxic Phase
Incidence~30-50% Develop Hypothyroidism
ManagementLevothyroxine (Full Replacement Dose) if TSH >10 or Symptomatic
ICI RechallengeIf Grade 1-2: Resume with Monitoring; Grade 3-4: Permanent Discontinuation

10. Other Notable Drugs

DrugThyroid EffectMechanismManagement
Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (Sunitinib, Sorafenib, Pazopanib, Lenvatinib)Hypothyroidism (20-40%)Vascular Injury → Thyroid IschaemiaLevothyroxine; Continue TKI
Interferon-αAutoimmune Thyroiditis (TPOAb+)Autoimmune InductionMonitor; Levothyroxine if Hypothyroid
AlemtuzumabAutoimmune Thyroid Disease (Graves > Hypothyroid)Immune ReconstitutionMonitor; Treat as Graves/Hypo
Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (Imatinib)Rare HypothyroidismUnknownMonitor
Interleukin-2thyroiditisImmune ActivationSteroids if Severe
High-Dose Iodide / ContrastWolff-Chaikoff → Transient Hypo; Jod-Basedow (If Autonomous)Iodine LoadMonitor; Thionamides if Thyrotoxic

11. Monitoring Recommendations

DrugBaselineMonitoring FrequencyKey Tests
AmiodaroneTSH, fT4, TPOAb, TgAbq3-6mo (q3mo if High Risk)TSH, fT4, fT3, TPOAb
LithiumTSH, TPOAb, Calciumq3-6moTSH, TPOAb, Lithium Level, Calcium
Checkpoint InhibitorsTSH, fT4, TPOAbq3-6wk during Tx; q3mo afterTSH, fT4, fT3, TPOAb, TRAb
TKIsTSH, fT4q4-8wk (First 6mo), then q3moTSH, fT4
Interferon-αTSH, TPOAbq3moTSH, fT4, TPOAb

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
AIT Type 1 vs 2Type 1 = Iodine Load → High RAI Uptake; Type 2 = Destructive → Low RAI Uptake
AIT Type 1 TreatmentHigh-Dose Thionamides (CBZ/PTU) + Perchlorate
AIT Type 2 TreatmentSteroids (Pred 30-40mg/day); Thionamides Ineffective
Mixed AITBoth Thionamides + Steroids
Amiodarone HypothyroidismMore Common than AIT in Iodine-Sufficient Areas; Levothyroxine; Amiodarone Can Continue
LithiumInhibits Hormone Release + Organification → Hypothyroidism + Goitre; TSH q6mo
Checkpoint Inhibitors5-20% Thyroiditis; Destructive Pattern (Thyrotoxic → Hypo); Steroids if Grade ≥2
ICI Thyroiditis PatternThyrotoxic (Low RAI) → Hypothyroid (30-50%); TPOAb Often +ve; TRAb Usually -ve
ICI GradingGrade 1: Monitor; Grade 2: Hold ICI + β-blocker ± Steroids; Grade 3: Hold ICI + High-Dose Steroids; Grade 4: Discontinue ICI
TKI Hypothyroidism20-40% Incidence; Levothyroxine; Continue TKI
Lithium Hypothyroidism10-20% Incidence; Goitre Common; Stop Lithium if Possible; Levothyroxine if Needed
AIT Type 1 vs 2 DifferentiationRAI Uptake (High vs Low); Doppler (Hypervascular vs Avascular); Steroids (Type 2 Responds)

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
AIT Type 1 vs 2Type 1 = Iodine Load (High RAI); Type 2 = Destructive (Low RAI) — Think "1 = High (Iodine), 2 = Low (Destruction)"
AIT Type 1 vs GravesBoth High RAI Uptake; Graves = TRAb+, Diffuse US; AIT Type 1 = TRAb-, Nodular/Goitre US
AIT Type 2 vs Silent ThyroiditisBoth Destructive (Low RAI); AIT = Amiodarone Context; Silent = Spontaneous, TPOAb+
Amiodarone Hypothyroidism vs AITHypothyroidism More Common in Iodine-Sufficient Areas; AIT More Common in Iodine-Deficient
Checkpoint Inhibitor vs GravesICI: TRAb Usually Negative, TPOAb Often +ve; Graves = TRAb Positive, Diffuse Uptake
Lithium vs Iodine HypothyroidismLithium = Inhibits Release + Organification; Iodine = Wolff-Chaikoff (Transient)

Mnemonic: AMIODARONE THYROID

  • AMIODARONE = AMIODARONE
  • MIXED TYPE 1/2 → COMBINED Rx (Thionamide + Steroid)
  • IODINE LOAD = TYPE 1 (High RAI, Jod-Basedow)
  • OVERLOAD IODINE → TYPE 1 (High RAI Uptake)
  • DEstructive = TYPE 2 (Low RAI, Steroids)
  • AMIODARONE HYPOTHYROIDISM = Common (Wolff-Chaikoff)
  • RESISTANCE TO THIONAMIDES = Type 2
  • AMIODARONE HYPOTHYROIDISM > AIT IN IODINE-SUFFICIENT

14. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Drug-Induced Thyroid Dysfunction))

Amiodarone

AIT Type 1 (Iodine Load)

High RAI Uptake

Thionamides + Perchlorate

AIT Type 2 (Destructive)

Low RAI Uptake

Steroids

Mixed Type 1/2

Thionamides + Steroids

Hypothyroidism (Common)

Wolff-Chaikoff Failure

Levothyroxine; Continue Amiodarone

Lithium

Hypothyroidism (Common)

Inhibits Release + Organification

Goitre Common

TSH q6mo; Levothyroxine if TSH >10

Checkpoint Inhibitors

Anti-PD1/PDL1/CTLA4

Destructive Thyroiditis

Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid

Grades 1-4 Management

Steroids Grade ≥2

TKIs

Hypothyroidism (20-40%)

Continue TKI; Levothyroxine

Other

Interferon-α: Autoimmune Thyroiditis

Alemtuzumab: Graves > Hypo

High-dose Iodide: Wolff-Chaikoff / Jod-Basedow

```

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Amiodarone AIT Type 1 vs 2RAI Uptake: Type 1 = High (Iodine Load); Type 2 = Low (Destructive)
AIT Type 1 RxHigh-Dose Thionamides + Potassium Perchlorate (Blocks Iodide Uptake)
AIT Type 2 RxSteroids (Pred 30-40mg/day); Thionamides Ineffective
Mixed AITThionamides + Steroids; Monitor fT4/CRP
Amiodarone HypothyroidismMore Common than AIT (Iodine-Sufficient Areas); Continue Amiodarone + Levothyroxine
Lithium Hypothyroidism10-20% Incidence; Inhibits Release + Organification; Goitre Common; TSH q6mo
Checkpoint Inhibitor Thyroiditis5-20% Incidence; Destructive Pattern (Thyrotoxic → Hypo); TRAb Usually Negative
ICI Thyroiditis GradingGrade 1: Monitor; Grade 2: Hold ICI + β-Blocker ± Steroids; Grade 3: Hold + High-Dose Steroids; Grade 4: Discontinue
ICI Thyroiditis PatternThyrotoxic (Low RAI) → Hypothyroid (30-50%); TPOAb Often +ve; TRAb Usually Negative
TKI Hypothyroidism20-40% Incidence; Continue TKI + Levothyroxine
AIT Type 1 vs 2RAI Uptake: Type 1 High (Iodine Load); Type 2 Low (Destructive)
Amiodarone + PerchloratePerchlorate Blocks NIS → Blocks Iodide Uptake; Use in Type 1 AIT
Lithium + ThyroidInhibits Hormone Release + Organification → Hypo + Goitre
AIT Type 1 vs GravesBoth High RAI Uptake; Graves = TRAb+, Diffuse US; AIT Type 1 = TRAb-, Nodular US

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Amiodarone causes thyrotoxicosis via:

A. Type 1: iodine load (excess substrate); Type 2: destructive thyroiditis

B. Only iodine load

C. Only destructive thyroiditis

D. Only TSH receptor stimulation

E. Only autoimmune

  1. Amiodarone type 1 thyrotoxicosis treated with:

A. Carbimazole +/- perchlorate

B. Steroids

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. PTU only

  1. Amiodarone type 2 thyrotoxicosis treated with:

A. Steroids; stop amiodarone

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Perchlorate

  1. Lithium causes:

A. Hypothyroidism (inhibits thyroid hormone release)

B. Hyperthyroidism

C. Thyroiditis

D. Thyroid cancer

E. Goitre only

  1. Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sunitinib etc) cause:

A. Hypothyroidism (thyroiditis/vascular effects)

B. Hyperthyroidism

C. Thyroid storm

D. Medullary cancer

E. No thyroid effect

  1. Checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1/PD-L1) cause:

A. Thyroiditis (painless) → thyrotoxic → hypothyroid

B. Only hyperthyroidism

C. Only hypothyroidism

D. TSHoma

E. Medullary cancer

  1. Anti-CTLA-4 (ipilimumab) causes:

A. Hypophysitis (ACTH/TSH deficiency)

B. Thyroiditis

C. Adrenalitis

D. Diabetes insipidus

E. Acromegaly

  1. Interferon-alpha causes:

A. Thyroiditis (both Graves-like and Hashimoto-like)

B. Only Graves

C. Only Hashimoto

D. Only cancer

E. No effect

  1. RAI uptake in amiodarone type 2:

A. Low (destructive thyroiditis)

B. Diffuse increased

C. Focal

D. Multifocal

E. Normal

  1. Amiodarose contains:

A. ~37% iodine by weight (high iodine load)

B. No iodine

C. Low iodine

D. Bromide

E. Chloride

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient on amiodarone 2yrs: TSH <0.01, fT4 35, fT3 10, TRAb -, low RAI uptake. Type?

A. Type 2 (destructive thyroiditis) → steroids; stop amiodarone

B. Type 1 → carbimazole

C. Graves → carbimazole

D. Subacute thyroiditis → NSAIDs

E. Toxic nodule → RAI

  1. Same patient but high RAI uptake, TRAb -. Type?

A. Type 1 (iodine load) → carbimazole +/- perchlorate

B. Type 2 → steroids

C. Graves → carbimazole

D. Toxic nodule → RAI

E. Factitious

  1. Patient on lithium 5yrs: TSH 15, fT4 6, goitre. Management?

A. Levothyroxine; continue lithium if essential; monitor TSH

B. Stop lithium

C. Carbimazole

D. RAI

E. Surgery

  1. Patient on pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1): TSH <0.01, fT4 32, painless. Course?

A. Thyroiditis: thyrotoxic → hypothyroid; β-blocker; hold ICI if severe

B. Graves → carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Permanent hyperthyroidism

  1. Patient on ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4): fatigue, hypotension, TSH low, fT4 low, cortisol low. Diagnosis?

A. Hypophysitis (ACTH/TSH deficiency) → high-dose steroids; hormone replacement

B. Primary AI

C. Thyroiditis

D. Diabetes insipidus

E. SIADH

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Amiodarone iodine

A: ~37% iodine by weight; 200mg = ~75mg iodine/day (vs RDA 150µg)

  • Q: Amiodarone Type 1

A: Iodine load → exacerbates autonomy; high RAI uptake; carbimazole +/- perchlorate

  • Q: Amiodarone Type 2

A: Destructive thyroiditis; low RAI uptake; steroids; stop amiodarone

  • Q: Lithium

A: Inhibits thyroid hormone release → hypothyroidism + goitre; monitor TSH

  • Q: TKIs (sunitinib)

A: Hypothyroidism (thyroiditis/vascular); monitor TSH

  • Q: Anti-PD-1/PD-L1

A: Painless thyroiditis → thyrotoxic→hypo; hold ICI if Grade 3-4

  • Q: Anti-CTLA-4

A: Hypophysitis (ACTH/TSH def); high-dose steroids; hormone replacement

  • Q: Interferon-alpha

A: Thyroiditis (Graves-like or Hashimoto-like); monitor TSH/TRAb/TPOAb

  • Q: Amiodarone monitoring

A: TSH/fT4 q6mo; baseline before start

  1. Hypophysitis (ACTH/TSH deficiency) — Anti-CTLA-4 (ipilimumab) causes:
  1. Thyroiditis (both Graves-like and Hashimoto-like) — Interferon-alpha causes:
  1. Low (destructive thyroiditis) — RAI uptake in amiodarone type 2:
  1. ~37% iodine by weight (high iodine load) — Amiodarose contains:

  1. Type 1 (iodine load) → carbimazole +/- perchlorate — Same patient but high RAI uptake, TRAb -. Type?
  1. Levothyroxine; continue lithium if essential; monitor TSH — Patient on lithium 5yrs: TSH 15, fT4 6, goitre. Management?

topics.title: Goitre Management
topics.html:
[!info]
Goitre = Thyroid Enlargement (WHO: >18mL Women, >25mL Men by US; or Visible/Palpable Neck Swelling). Aetiological Classification Essential (Diffuse vs Nodular, Toxic vs Non-Toxic). TSH First → Guides Further Workup.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify goitre (Diffuse/Nodular, Toxic/Non-Toxic, Endemic/Sporadic)
  • [ ] Apply investigation algorithm starting with TSH
  • [ ] Manage based on aetiology (Iodine, Autoimmune, Nodular, Malignancy)
  • [ ] Apply indications for surgery, radioiodine, and observation

2. Classification

ClassificationTypes
By MorphologyDiffuse (Whole Gland), Nodular (Single/Multinodular), Mixed
By FunctionToxic (Hyperthyroid), Non-Toxic (Euthyroid), Hypothyroid
By EpidemiologyEndemic (Iodine Deficiency, >10% Population), Sporadic

WHO Goitre Grading (Palpation)

GradeDefinition
Grade 0Not Palpable, Not Visible
Grade 1Palpable but Not Visible (Neck Extended)
Grade 2Visible (Neck Normal) + Palpable

3. Aetiological Classification

CategoryCauses
Iodine DeficiencyCommonest Worldwide (Endemic Goitre)
AutoimmuneHashimoto (Hypothyroid), Graves (Toxic)
NodularMultinodular Goitre (MNG), Toxic Nodule, Toxic MNG
PhysiologicalPuberty, Pregnancy (hCG, ↑ TBG)
Drug-InducedLithium, Amiodarone, Iodine Excess
CongenitalDyshormonogenesis (Dyshormonogenic CH)
MalignancyThyroid Cancer (Papillary, Follicular, Medullary, Anaplastic)
InfiltrativeSarcoidosis, Amyloid, Riedel Thyroiditis, Lymphoma
GeneticPendred Syndrome, TSHR Mutations, NIS Defects

4. Investigation Algorithm

```

GOITRE DETECTED (Clinical / US / Incidental)

│

▼

TSH + fT4 + fT3 (First-Line)

│

├── TSH LOW → Hyperthyroid Goitre

│ ├── TRAb Positive → GRAVES DISEASE

│ ├── TRAb Negative + Nodule → TOXIC NODULE / TOXIC MNG

│ │ (Technetium Scan: Hot vs Cold)

│ └── DRUG-INDUCED (Amiodarone, Lithium)

│

├── TSH HIGH → Hypothyroid Goitre

│ ├── TPOAb Positive → HASHIMOTO THYROIDITIS

│ ├── TPOAb Negative → IODINE DEFICIENCY / DYSHORMONOGENESIS

│ └── DRUG-INDUCED (Lithium, Amiodarone)

│

└── TSH NORMAL → Euthyroid Goitre

│

├── MULTINODULAR GOITRE (MNG) — Most Common

├── SOLITARY NODULE — TI-RADS Assessment

├── IODINE DEFICIENCY (Endemic Areas)

├── INFILTRATIVE (Sarcoid, Amyloid, Riedel, Lymphoma)

└── MALIGNANCY (If Suspicious Features)

```

5. Further Investigations (After TSH)

TestIndication
Thyroid Ultrasound (TI-RADS)All Nodular Goitres; Size, Echogenicity, Vascularity, Lymph Nodes
Thyroid Antibodies (TPOAb, TgAb, TRAb)Autoimmune Aetiology Suspected
Radioisotope Scan (Tc-99m)Toxic Nodular Goitre (Hot vs Cold Nodules)
FNA (Bethesda)Suspicious Nodules (TI-RADS 3-5)
CT/MRIRetrosternal Extension, Tracheal Compression, Invasive Features
Urinary IodinePopulation Assessment (Not Routine Individual)
CalcitoninIf Medullary Cancer Suspected (Family Hx, Nodule)

6. Euthyroid Multinodular Goitre (MNG) — Most Common

Management Options

OptionIndication
ObservationAsymptomatic, Benign US, No Compression; US q6-12mo, then Annually
Levothyroxine SuppressionNot Recommended Routinely (No Proven Size Reduction; Risk of Iatrogenic Thyrotoxicosis, Bone Loss)
Radioiodine (RAI)Large MNG, Cosmetic Concern, Elderly/Unfit for Surgery; Dose 150-300 MBq; ↓ Volume 30-50%
Surgery (Thyroidectomy)Compression (Trachea/Oesophagus), Retrosternal Extension, Cosmetic, Suspicion of Malignancy, Failure of RAI

Indications for Surgery in MNG

IndicationDetails
Compressive SymptomsDyspnoea, Dysphagia, Stridor, Orthopnoea
Retrosternal Extension>50% Gland Below Sternal Notch; CT for Assessment
Cosmetic ConcernVisible Neck Swelling (Patient Choice)
Suspicion of MalignancyBethesda V/VI, Rapid Growth, Fixation, Vocal Cord Palsy
Failure of RAIPersistent/Regrowing After RAI

7. Endemic Goitre (Iodine Deficiency)

AspectManagement
PopulationUniversal Salt Iodisation (USI) — Gold Standard Prevention
IndividualIodine Supplementation (KI 150-200 µg/day); Pregnancy/Lactation: 250 µg/day
Severe Iodine DeficiencyIodised Oil (Lipiodol) 1-2mL IM/Year (If Salt Iodisation Not Available)
MonitoringMedian Urinary Iodine Concentration (Target 100-199 µg/L)

8. Toxic Goitre Management

AetiologyKey Management
Graves DiseaseATD (Carbimazole/PTU) → RAI / Surgery (See Graves Note)
Toxic AdenomaRAI (1st Line); Surgery if Large/Compression/Refractory
Toxic MNGRAI (1st Line); Surgery if Large/Compression/Refractory
Amiodarone-InducedType 1 (Iodine Excess) → ATD + KPerchlorate; Type 2 (Destructive) → Steroids
Lithium-InducedStop Lithium if Possible; ATD if Persistent

9. Retrosternal / Substernal Goitre

FeatureManagement
Definition>50% Thyroid Volume Below Sternal Notch
ImagingCT Chest (Extent, Tracheal Deviation, Vascular Encroachment)
SymptomsDyspnoea, Dysphagia, Stridor, Superior Vena Cava Syndrome
SurgeryTotal/Subtotal Thyroidectomy via Cervical Approach (Usually); Sternotomy Rare
Pre-OpVocal Cord Assessment (Laryngoscopy); CT for Vascular/Tracheal Anatomy

10. Goitre & Pregnancy

AspectManagement
Physiological GoitreCommon (30-40%); hCG + ↑ TBG; Resolves Postpartum
Iodine Requirement250 µg/day (vs 150 µg Non-Pregnant)
New Goitre in PregnancyAssess TSH; FNA if Nodule >1cm Suspicious
Surgery2nd Trimester (If Urgent); Avoid 1st/3rd if Possible
RAIContraindicated

11. Riedel Thyroiditis (Fibrosing Variant)

FeatureManagement
PresentationHard, Fixed, "Woody" Thyroid; Invades Strap Muscles, Trachea, RLN
DiagnosisBiopsy (Dense Fibrosis, Lymphoplasmacytic Infiltrate); IgG4-Related Disease
ComplicationsTracheal Compression, RLN Palsy, Hypoparathyroidism
MedicalPrednisolone (1mg/kg → Taper); Tamoxifen; Rituximab (IgG4-RD)
SurgicalIsthmusectomy / Tracheostomy for Airway; Avoid Total Thyroidectomy (High RLN/Hypocalcaemia Risk)

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Goitre DefinitionThyroid Enlargement (>18mL F, >25mL M by US; or Visible/Palpable)
First InvestigationTSH (Guides Entire Workup)
TSH LowHyperthyroid Goitre (Graves, Toxic Nodule, Toxic MNG)
TSH HighHypothyroid Goitre (Hashimoto, Iodine Deficiency)
TSH NormalEuthyroid Goitre (MNG, Iodine Deficiency, Solitary Nodule)
MNG Most CommonEuthyroid MNG = Most Common Cause of Goitre
MNG Surgery IndicationsCompression, Retrosternal, Cosmetic, Suspicion Malignancy
Levothyroxine for MNGNot Routine (No Size Benefit; Thyrotoxicosis/Bone Loss Risk)
RAI for MNGLarge, Elderly, Unfit, Cosmetic (↓ Volume 30-50%)
Retrosternal Goitre>50% Below Sternum; CT Chest; Surgery via Cervical Approach
Riedel ThyroiditisHard/Fixed/Woody; IgG4-Related; Steroids/Tamoxifen; Avoid Total Thyroidectomy
Pregnancy + GoitrePhysiological (30-40%); Iodine 250µg/day; Surgery 2nd Trimester if Needed
Iodine DeficiencyCommonest Worldwide; Salt Iodisation (USI) Prevention
TSH FirstSingle Most Important Test in Goitre Workup

13. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Goitre Management))

Definition

Thyroid Enlargement

US: >18mL F, >25mL M

WHO Grade 0/1/2

Classification

Morphology: Diffuse / Nodular / Mixed

Function: Toxic / Non-Toxic / Hypothyroid

Epidemiology: Endemic / Sporadic

First Test

TSH (Guides Workup)

TSH Low (Hyper)

Graves (TRAb+)

Toxic Nodule/MNG (Scan: Hot)

Drug (Amiodarone, Lithium)

TSH High (Hypo)

Hashimoto (TPOAb+)

Iodine Deficiency

Drug (Lithium, Amiodarone)

TSH Normal (Euthyroid)

MNG (Most Common)

Iodine Deficiency

Solitary Nodule (TI-RADS)

Infiltrative (Sarcoid, Riedel, Lymphoma)

Malignancy

MNG Management

Observe (Asymptomatic, Benign)

RAI (Large, Elderly, Cosmetic)

Surgery (Compression, Retrosternal, Cosmetic, Malignancy Suspicion)

NO Routine LT4 Suppression

Endemic Goitre

Iodine Deficiency (Commonest)

USI (Salt Iodisation) Prevention

Supplementation (150-250µg/day)

Retrosternal

>50% Below Sternum

CT Chest (Extent, Vessels)

Surgery via Cervical Approach

Special

Pregnancy: Physiological 30-40%

Riedel: Hard/Fixed, IgG4, Steroids, Avoid TT

Pregnancy: Iodine 250µg/d

```

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Goitre DefinitionThyroid Enlargement (>18mL F, >25mL M US; Visible/Palpable)
First InvestigationTSH (Single Most Important Test)
TSH LowHyperthyroid Goitre → Graves, Toxic Nodule/MNG
TSH HighHypothyroid Goitre → Hashimoto, Iodine Deficiency
TSH NormalEuthyroid Goitre → MNG (Most Common), Iodine Deficiency
MNGMost Common Euthyroid Goitre; Observe if Asymptomatic/Benign
MNG SurgeryCompression, Retrosternal, Cosmetic, Malignancy Suspicion
LT4 SuppressionNot Routine for MNG (No Benefit; Risks)
RAI for MNGLarge, Elderly/Unfit, Cosmetic; ↓ Volume 30-50%
Retrosternal Goitre>50% Below Sternum; CT Chest; Cervical Approach Surgery
Riedel ThyroiditisHard/Fixed/Woody; IgG4-Related Disease; Steroids/Tamoxifen
Pregnancy GoitrePhysiological 30-40%; Iodine 250µg/day; Surgery 2nd Trimester
Iodine DeficiencyCommonest Worldwide; USI (Salt Iodisation) Prevention
TSH FirstSingle Most Important Test in Goitre Workup

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Goitre definition:

A. Thyroid enlargement (diffuse or nodular)

B. Thyroid cancer only

C. Thyroiditis only

D. Normal variant

E. Iodine deficiency only

  1. Commonest cause worldwide:

A. Iodine deficiency

B. Graves disease

C. Hashimoto's

D. Thyroid cancer

E. Drug-induced

  1. Simple diffuse goitre:

A. Iodine deficiency/mild TSH stimulation; diffuse enlargement

B. Nodular

C. Toxic

D. Malignant

E. Autoimmune

  1. Multinodular goitre (MNG):

A. Multiple nodules; may be toxic or non-toxic; long-standing

B. Single nodule

C. Diffuse

D. Cystic only

E. Inflammatory

  1. Toxic MNG:

A. Autonomous nodules; TSH suppressed; multifocal RAI uptake

B. Single hot nodule

C. Diffuse uptake

D. Low uptake

E. TRAb positive

  1. Retrosternal goitre:

A. Extends below thoracic inlet; may compress trachea/oesophagus

B. Always malignant

C. Always toxic

D. No surgery needed

E. Only in children

  1. Goitre management - asymptomatic euthyroid:

A. Observe if small; treat iodine deficiency; surgery if compressive/cosmetic

B. Immediate surgery

C. RAI always

D. Thyroxine suppression

E. Antithyroid drugs

  1. Thyroxine suppression therapy:

A. Not routinely recommended (small effect, side effects)

B. First-line for all goitres

C. Cures MNG

D. Prevents cancer

E. Preferred in elderly

  1. Surgery indications:

A. Compressive symptoms, suspicious nodules, cosmetic, retrosternal extension

B. All goitres

C. Only toxic

D. Only euthyroid

E. Never

  1. Goitre in pregnancy:

A. Physiological enlargement (HCG/estrogen); monitor TSH; iodine supplementation

B. RAI

C. Surgery

D. Carbimazole

E. Ignore

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 40yo woman: diffuse goitre, euthyroid, iodine deficient area. Management?

A. Iodine supplementation + monitor; surgery only if compressive

B. Levothyroxine suppression

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Carbimazole

  1. 60yo man: large MNG, tracheal deviation, dyspnoea. Management?

A. Total thyroidectomy (compressive symptoms)

B. Levothyroxine

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Carbimazole

  1. 55yo woman: MNG, TSH 0.01, multifocal uptake. Diagnosis?

A. Toxic MNG

B. Graves

C. Toxic adenoma

D. Thyroiditis

E. Factitious

  1. Retrosternal goitre on CT, asymptomatic. Management?

A. Surgery (risk of compression; malignancy cannot be excluded)

B. Observation

C. RAI

D. Levothyroxine

E. Fine needle aspiration only

  1. Pregnant woman: new goitre, TSH normal. Management?

A. Iodine supplementation; monitor TSH q4-6wk; reassure

B. Levothyroxine

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Carbimazole

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Goitre

A: Thyroid enlargement (diffuse/nodular); iodine deficiency = worldwide #1 cause

  • Q: Simple diffuse

A: Iodine deficiency/mild TSH stimulation; diffuse enlargement

  • Q: MNG

A: Multiple nodules; long-standing; may be toxic (autonomous)

  • Q: Toxic MNG

A: Autonomous nodules; TSH suppressed; multifocal RAI uptake

  • Q: Retrosternal

A: Extends below thoracic inlet; surgery indicated (compression/malignancy risk)

  • Q: Asymptomatic management

A: Iodine repletion; observe if small/euthyroid; surgery if compressive/cosmetic

  • Q: TSH suppression

A: Not routinely recommended (small effect, side effects)

  • Q: Surgery indications

A: Compressive symptoms, suspicious nodules, cosmetic, retrosternal

  • Q: Pregnancy

A: Physiological enlargement (HCG/estrogen); iodine supplementation; monitor TSH

  • Q: RAI

A: For toxic MNG; contraindicated in pregnancy/active TED

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Thyroid enlargement (diffuse or nodular) — Goitre definition:
  1. Iodine deficiency — Commonest cause worldwide:
  1. Iodine deficiency/mild TSH stimulation; diffuse enlargement — Simple diffuse goitre:
  1. Multiple nodules; may be toxic or non-toxic; long-standing — Multinodular goitre (MNG):
  1. Autonomous nodules; TSH suppressed; multifocal RAI uptake — Toxic MNG:
  1. Extends below thoracic inlet; may compress trachea/oesophagus — Retrosternal goitre:
  1. Observe if small; treat iodine deficiency; surgery if compressive/cosmetic — Goitre management - asymptomatic euthyroid:
  1. Not routinely recommended (small effect, side effects) — Thyroxine suppression therapy:
  1. Compressive symptoms, suspicious nodules, cosmetic, retrosternal extension — Surgery indications:
  1. Physiological enlargement (HCG/estrogen); monitor TSH; iodine supplementation — Goitre in pregnancy:

SBAs

  1. Iodine supplementation + monitor; surgery only if compressive — 40yo woman: diffuse goitre, euthyroid, iodine deficient area. Management?
  1. Total thyroidectomy (compressive symptoms) — 60yo man: large MNG, tracheal deviation, dyspnoea. Management?
  1. Toxic MNG — 55yo woman: MNG, TSH 0.01, multifocal uptake. Diagnosis?
  1. Surgery (risk of compression; malignancy cannot be excluded) — Retrosternal goitre on CT, asymptomatic. Management?
  1. Iodine supplementation; monitor TSH q4-6wk; reassure — Pregnant woman: new goitre, TSH normal. Management?

topics.title: Graves Disease
topics.html:
[!info]
Graves Disease = Autoimmune Hyperthyroidism driven by TSH Receptor Stimulating Antibodies (TRAb). Commonest cause of hyperthyroidism (70-80%). Triad: Hyperthyroidism + Ophthalmopathy + Dermopathy. Autoimmune, Female Predominance (5-10:1), Peak 30-50y.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain pathophysiology (TRAb, TSH-R stimulation)
  • [ ] Recognise clinical triad + associated features (ophthalmopathy, dermopathy, acropachy)
  • [ ] Apply investigation algorithm (TRAb, RAI uptake, US)
  • [ ] Compare management strategies (ATDs, RAI, Surgery)
  • [ ] Manage Graves in pregnancy and thyroid eye disease

2. Pathophysiology

ComponentDetails
AutoantibodyTRAb (TSH Receptor Antibodies) — Stimulating Type (Mimics TSH)
TargetTSH Receptor (TSH-R) on Thyroid Follicular Cells
MechanismConstitutive Activation → ↑ cAMP → ↑ T4/T3 Synthesis/Secretion + Thyroid Growth
Other AntibodiesTPOAb (80-90%), TgAb (50-70%) — Support Autoimmune Aetiology
GeneticsHLA-DR3 (Strongest Association); CTLA-4, PTPN22, FCRL3, CD40
TriggerStress, Smoking, Infection, Postpartum, Iodine Excess, Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors

3. Clinical Features

Thyrotoxicosis (General)

FeatureFrequency
Weight Loss + ↑ Appetite>90%
Heat Intolerance, Sweating>90%
Tachycardia, Palpitations>90%
Tremor, Anxiety, Irritability>80%
Diarrhoea, ↑ Bowel Frequency50-60%
Menstrual IrregularitiesCommon (Women)
Proximal Myopathy30-40%

Graves-Specific Features (The Triad)

FeatureFrequencyKey Details
Hyperthyroidism100%Biochemical Thyrotoxicosis
Ophthalmopathy25-50%Proptosis, Lid Lag, Diplopia, Optic Neuropathy
Dermopathy (Pretibial Myxoedema)1-5%Bilateral Pretibial, Non-pitting, Plaques/Nodules
Acropachy<1%Digital Clubbing, Periosteal Reaction, Fingertip Swelling

Ophthalmopathy (Graves Eye Disease) — Grading (EUGOGO)

GradeFeaturesManagement
MildMinor Lid Retraction/Lag, Mild Proptosis (<2mm), Mild Soft Tissue SignsObservation, Lubricants, Selenium 100µg BD
ModerateProptosis 2-4mm, Lid Swelling, Diplopia (Intermittent), Moderate Soft Tissue InvolvementSteroids (IV Methylprednisolone → Oral); Orbital RT if Steroid Fails
SevereProptosis >4mm, Sight-Threatening (Optic Neuropathy, Corneal Ulcer), Severe DiplopiaUrgent IV Methylprednisolone (500-1000mg × 3-6 doses) → Oral Taper + Orbital RT; Emergency Decompression if Optic Neuropathy

4. Pathophysiology — Key Points

AspectDetails
Primary AutoantibodyTRAb (TSH-R Stimulating Antibodies) — IgG1, Mimics TSH
MechanismTRAb → TSH-R Stimulations → ↑ cAMP → ↑ T4/T3 Synthesis + Growth
Associated AutoantibodiesTPOAb (80-90%), TgAb (50-70%) — Support Autoimmune Aetiology
HLA AssociationHLA-DR3 (Strongest); CTLA-4, PTPN22, FCRL3, CD40, IL2RA
Environmental TriggersSmoking (↑ Risk 2-3x, Worsens Eye Disease), Stress, Infection, Postpartum, Iodine Excess, Checkpoint Inhibitors

5. Clinical Assessment

Typical Presentation

SystemFeatures
GeneralWeight Loss, Heat Intolerance, Sweating, Tachycardia, Tremor, Anxiety
EyesLid Retraction, Lid Lag, Proptosis, Diplopia, Redness, Grittiness
SkinWarm, Moist, Fine Hair, Onycholysis (Plummer's Nails)
CardiacSinus Tachy, AF (10-15% Elderly), High Output Failure, Systolic Murmur
NeuromuscularTremor, Proximal Myopathy, Hyperreflexia
ReproductiveOligomenorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Infertility, Low Libido

6. Investigations

TestExpected in GravesInterpretation
TSHSuppressed (<0.01 mIU/L)Confirm Thyrotoxicosis
fT4 / fT3Elevated (fT3 > fT4 often)Confirm Thyrotoxicosis
TRAbPositive (>95%)Diagnostic of Graves
TPOAb / TgAbPositive (80-90% / 50-70%)Supports Autoimmune
RAI UptakeDiffuse ↑ (Homogeneous)Confirms Graves; Differentiates
Thyroid USDiffuse Enlargement, Hypervascular (Colour Doppler)Supports Graves
CT/MRI OrbitsIf Moderate-Severe OphthalmopathyMuscle Enlargement, Fat Expansion

Diagnosis of Graves: TSH Suppressed + fT4/fT3 ↑ + TRAb Positive = Diagnostic (No RAI Scan Needed if TRAb+)

7. Management — Algorithm

```

Graves Disease Diagnosed

│

├── PATIENT FACTORS

│ ├── Age <18 → Carbimazole (Long Course)

│ ├── Elderly / Cardiac Disease → RAI or Surgery

│ ├── Large Goitre (>80g) / Compressive → Surgery

│ ├── Active Ophthalmopathy → Avoid RAI; Surgery Preferred

│ ├── Pregnancy → PTU 1st Tri → Switch Carbimazole

│ └── Patient Preference / Access

│

├── 1ST LINE: ANTITHYROID DRUGS (ATDs)

│ Carbimazole 15-40mg/day (Divided BD/TDS)

│ Target: 12-18 Months Course

│ Remission Rate: 40-50%

│ Relapse → RAI or Surgery

│

├── DEFINITIVE THERAPY (If Relapse / Contraindication to ATDs)

│ ├── RAI (¹³¹I) — Contraindicated in Active Ophthalmopathy

│ │ Dose: 400-600 MBq (Calibrated)

│ │ Hypothyroidism >80% at 1yr

│ │

│ └── SURGERY (Total/Near-Total Thyroidectomy)

│ Large Goitre (>80g) / Compressive / RAI Contraindicated

│ Pre-op: Carbimazole → Euthyroid → Add Lugol's Iodine 5-7 Days

│

└── BLOCK & REPLACE (If Relapse on ATDs)

Carbimazole 20-40mg/day + Levothyroxine 100-150µg/day

```

8. Antithyroid Drugs (ATDs) — Comparison

ParameterCarbimazolePropylthiouracil (PTU)
MechanismInhibits TPO → Blocks Iodination + CouplingInhibits TPO + Peripheral T4→T3 Conversion
Dose (Induction)15-40mg/day (Divided BD/TDS)100-200mg TDS
Dose (Maintenance)5-15mg/day50-150mg/day
Onset3-6 Weeks3-6 Weeks
Half-life~6 Hours~1-2 Hours
Crosses PlacentaYes (Higher Teratogenicity — Aplasia Cutis)Yes (Lower Teratogenicity)
Preferred in Pregnancy2nd/3rd Trimester1st Trimester
Major Side EffectsAgranulocytosis (0.1-0.5%), Hepatitis, Rash, ArthralgiaHepatotoxicity (Rare, Severe), Agranulocytosis, Vasculitis
LactationCompatible (Low Milk Transfer)Compatible (Minimal Transfer)

9. Block & Replace Regimen

IndicationRegimen
Relapse on ATDsCarbimazole 20-40mg/day + Levothyroxine 100-150µg/day
Non-Adherence to TitrationFixed Dose ATD + Levothyroxine
AdvantageStable Thyroid Status; No Dose Adjustment
DisadvantageHigher ATD Dose → More Side Effects; No Physiological Feedback

10. Radioactive Iodine (RAI) — Definitive Therapy

AspectDetails
AgentIodine-131 (¹³¹I) — Beta Emitter (t½ 8 Days)
Dose400-600 MBq (Calibrated to Thyroid Uptake/Size)
Mechanismβ-Radiation → Thyroid Follicular Cell Destruction
Onset6-12 Weeks (Hypothyroidism)
Outcome>80% Permanent Hypothyroidism at 1 Year
ContraindicationsPregnancy, Breastfeeding, Active Ophthalmopathy
Relative ContraindicationsAge <15 (Relative), Active Ophthalmopathy (Relative), Inability to Follow Radiation Safety
PreparationStop ATDs 3-5 Days Prior; Low Iodine Diet 2 Weeks
Post-RAIHypothyroidism >80% at 1yr → Lifelong Levothyroxine
Cancer RiskSmall ↑ Solid Cancer Risk (Dose-Dependent); Not Leukaemia

11. Surgery (Thyroidectomy)

IndicationProcedure
Large Goitre / Compressive SymptomsTotal / Near-Total Thyroidectomy
RAI Contraindicated / FailedTotal / Near-Total Thyroidectomy
Suspicion of MalignancyTotal Thyroidectomy ± Central LND
Patient Preference / Rapid ControlTotal Thyroidectomy
Active OphthalmopathySurgery Preferred Over RAI

Pre-op Preparation:

  1. Achieve Euthyroidism with ATDs
  2. Lugol's Iodine 5-7 Days Pre-op (10-20 Drops TDS) → ↓ Vascularity
  3. Calcium/Vit D Baseline (Parathyroid Protection)

12. Graves Ophthalmopathy — Management Algorithm

```

Graves Ophthalmopathy (Confirmed)

│

├── MILD (EUGOGO)

│ Lubricants, Selenium 100µg BD, Smoking Cessation, Elevate Head

│ Monitor q3-6mo

│

├── MODERATE

│ IV Methylprednisolone 500mg Weekly ×6 → 250mg Weekly ×6

│ → Then Oral Prednisolone Taper (4-6 Months)

│ If Steroid-Resistant → Orbital Radiotherapy (20Gy/10fx)

│

├── SEVERE / SIGHT-THREATENING

│ URGENT IV Methylprednisolone 500-1000mg × 3-6 Doses

│ → Oral Prednisolone Taper (4-6mo)

│ Emergency Orbital Decompression if Optic Neuropathy

│ Orbital RT (20Gy/10fx) if Steroid-Resistant

│

└── REFRACTORY / RECURRENT

Rituximab (Anti-CD20) / Tocilizumab (IL-6R) / Teprotumumab (IGF-1R Ab)

```

Key Ophthalmopathy Points

FeatureDetails
Smoking↑ Risk 2-3x; Worsens Prognosis — Cessation Mandatory
Selenium100µg BD → Reduces Progression (Mild Disease)
IV Methylprednisolone500-1000mg Weekly × 6 → 250mg Weekly × 6 (Standard EUGOGO)
Orbital RT20Gy/10 Fractions; If Steroid-Resistant
Orbital DecompressionSight-Threatening; Post-Rehab (Squint/Ptosis Surgery Later)
TeprotumumabIGF-1R Antibody; FDA-Approved (Significant Proptosis Reduction)

13. Graves in Pregnancy

TrimesterManagement
Pre-conceptionAchieve Euthyroidism; TRAb <5 IU/L Ideal; Optimise ATD
1st TrimesterPTU 50-150mg/day (Lower Teratogenicity)
2nd/3rd TrimesterSwitch to Carbimazole (PTU Hepatotoxicity Risk)
ATD DoseLowest Effective Dose; Target fT4 Upper Normal Range
TRAb MonitoringEach Trimester; TRAb >5 IU/L → ↑ Neonatal Thyrotoxicosis Risk
Fetal SurveillanceTRAb >5 IU/L → Fetal US (Goitre, Tachycardia, Hydrops)
DeliveryVaginal Preferred; Neonate Monitored for Thyrotoxicosis (if TRAb High)
BreastfeedingCarbimazole/PTU Compatible (Low Milk Transfer)

14. Thyroid Storm (Graves) — Emergency

Criteria (Burch-Wartofsky)Points
Temperature (°C)37.2-37.7: 5; 37.8-38.3: 10; 38.4-38.8: 15; 38.9-39.4: 20; 39.5-39.9: 25; ≥40: 30
CNSAbsent: 0; Mild: 10; Moderate: 20; Severe (Seizure/Coma): 30
GIAbsent: 0; Moderate: 10; Severe: 20
CardiacHR 90-109: 5; 110-119: 10; 120-129: 15; ≥130: 20; AF: 10; HF: 10
PrecipitantAbsent: 0; Present: 10

Score ≥45 = Diagnostic; 25-44 = Impending; <25 = Unlikely

Emergency Protocol

StepDrugDose
1. ATDPTU 600mg Load → 200mg Q6H (Preferred)Blocks Peripheral T4→T3 Conversion
2. β-BlockerPropranolol 40-80mg Q6H (IV if Needed)Controls Adrenergic Symptoms
3. IodineLugol's Iodine 8 Drops Q6H (1hr After PTU)Blocks Hormone Release
4. SteroidsHydrocortisone 100mg IV q8hBlocks Peripheral Conversion
4. SupportiveFluids, Cooling, Antipyretics, Anticoagulation (AF), Inotropic Support

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Graves Prevalence70-80% of Hyperthyroidism; Female:Male = 5-10:1
TRAb>95% Sensitivity/Specificity; Diagnostic if Positive
TSH-R AntibodyStimulating Type (IgG1) Mimics TSH
Ophthalmopathy25-50%; Smoking Cessation Mandatory (2-3x Risk)
DermopathyPretibial Myxoedema (1-5%); Bilateral Pretibial Plaques
AcropachyDigital Clubbing + Periosteal Reaction (Rare <1%)
Carbimazole vs PTUCarbimazole 1st Line; PTU = 1st Trimester + Thyroid Storm
RAI ContraindicationActive Ophthalmopathy, Pregnancy, Breastfeeding
Block & ReplaceCarbimazole 20-40mg + Levothyroxine 100-150µg
PregnancyPTU 1st Tri → Switch Carbimazole 2nd Tri; Lowest Effective Dose
TRAb in Pregnancy>5 IU/L → ↑ Neonatal Thyrotoxicosis Risk; Fetal US if >5
Thyroid StormBurch-Wartofsky ≥45; PTU + Propranolol + Iodine + Steroids
Smoking2-3x Risk; Worsens Ophthalmopathy; Cessation Mandatory
Selenium100µg BD Reduces Progression (Mild Eye Disease)
TeprotumumabIGF-1R Antibody; Significant Proptosis Reduction

16. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Graves vs Toxic NoduleTRAb + Diffuse Uptake vs Focal Uptake (Hot Nodule)
RAI vs Surgery for EyesRAI Worsens Ophthalmopathy; Surgery Preferred if Active Eye Disease
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU = 1st Trimester + Storm; Carbimazole = Standard 1st Line
Block & ReplaceUsed for Relapse/Non-Adherence; Higher ATD Dose → More Side Effects
TRAb vs TPOAbTRAb = Pathogenic (Stimulates); TPOAb = Marker Only (Not Pathogenic)
Ophthalmopathy TimingCan Precede, Coincide, or Follow Thyrotoxicosis (Independent Course)
Carbimazole TeratogenicityAplasia Cutis, Choanal Atresia — Avoid 1st Trimester

17. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Graves Disease))

Pathophysiology

TRAb (TSH-R Stim Ab) → cAMP ↑ → T4/T3 ↑ + Growth

TPOAb 80-90%, TgAb 50-70%

HLA-DR3, CTLA-4, PTPN22

Clinical Triad

Hyperthyroidism

Ophthalmopathy (25-50%)

Dermopathy (1-5%)

Ophthalmopathy

Mild: Lubricants + Selenium

Moderate: IV Methylpred → Orbital RT

Severe: IV MPulse → Decompression

Smoking: 2-3x Risk

Management

Carbimazole 1st Line (15-40mg/day)

PTU: 1st Tri Pregnancy + Storm

RAI: Definitive (Contra: Eye Disease)

Surgery: Large Goitre/Compressive/Active Eye

Block & Replace: Relapse

Pregnancy

PTU 1st Tri → Carbimazole 2nd/3rd

TRAb >5 IU/L → Fetal Risk

Storm

BW Score ≥45

PTU + Propranolol + Iodine + Steroids

```

18. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Graves Prevalence70-80% of Hyperthyroidism; F:M = 5-10:1
TRAb>95% Sensitivity/Specificity; Diagnostic if Positive
TSH-R AntibodyStimulating Type (IgG1) Mimics TSH
Ophthalmopathy25-50%; Smoking Cessation Mandatory (2-3x Risk)
DermopathyPretibial Myxoedema (1-5%); Bilateral Pretibial Plaques
AcropachyDigital Clubbing + Periosteal Reaction (Rare <1%)
Carbimazole vs PTUCarbimazole 1st Line; PTU = 1st Trimester + Storm
RAI ContraindicationActive Ophthalmopathy, Pregnancy, Breastfeeding
Block & ReplaceCarbimazole 20-40mg + Levothyroxine 100-150µg
PregnancyPTU 1st Tri → Switch Carbimazole 2nd Tri; Lowest Effective Dose
TRAb in Pregnancy>5 IU/L → ↑ Neonatal Thyrotoxicosis Risk; Fetal US if >5
Thyroid StormBurch-Wartofsky ≥45; PTU + Propranolol + Iodine + Steroids
Smoking2-3x Risk; Worsens Ophthalmopathy; Cessation Mandatory
Selenium100µg BD Reduces Progression (Mild Eye Disease)
TeprotumumabIGF-1R Antibody; Significant Proptosis Reduction

19. MCQs (10)

  1. Pathogenesis of Graves disease:

A. TRAb (TSH receptor stimulating antibodies)

B. Anti-TPO antibodies destroying thyroid

C. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma

D. Autonomous toxic nodule

E. hCG excess

  1. Classic triad of Graves:

A. Hyperthyroidism + Ophthalmopathy + Pretibial myxoedema

B. Hyperthyroidism + Goitre + Thyrotoxicosis

C. Ophthalmopathy + Myxoedema + Acropachy

D. Hyperthyroidism + Acropachy + Myxoedema

E. Goitre + Ophthalmopathy + Acropachy

  1. TRAb positive in what % of Graves?

A. >95%

B. 70-80%

C. 50-60%

D. 30-40%

E. 100%

  1. RAI uptake in Graves:

A. Diffuse increased (homogeneous)

B. Focal hot nodule

C. Multifocal/patchy

D. Low/absent

E. Normal

  1. First-line treatment for 1st episode Graves:

A. Carbimazole 15-40mg/day for 12-18 months

B. RAI immediately

C. Surgery

D. PTU long-term

E. Propranolol only

  1. Remission rate after 12-18 months carbimazole:

A. ~40-50%

B. 80-90%

C. 10-20%

D. 60-70%

E. 20-30%

  1. RAI contraindicated in:

A. Active ophthalmopathy

B. Large goitre

C. Young age

D. Male sex

E. High TRAb

  1. Smoking effect on Graves:

A. 2-3x higher relapse risk; worsens ophthalmopathy

B. Protective

C. No effect

D. Only affects ophthalmopathy

E. Only affects relapse

  1. PTU used in 1st trimester because:

A. Lower teratogenicity (aplasia cutis risk with carbimazole)

B. More potent

C. Safer in breastfeeding

D. Does not cross placenta

E. Faster onset

  1. Definitive treatment after relapse:

A. RAI or Surgery

B. Continue carbimazole indefinitely

C. PTU long-term

D. Propranolol only

E. Observation

20. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 28yo woman: Graves, moderate, 1st episode. Plan?

A. Carbimazole 20mg daily 12-18 months → assess TRAb at 12mo

B. RAI immediately

C. Surgery

D. PTU 12-18 months

E. Propranolol alone

  1. 45yo man: Graves, large goitre, smoker, TRAb high. Best definitive?

A. Surgery (RAI worsens eye disease; smoker high relapse)

B. RAI

C. Carbimazole long-term

D. PTU

E. Observation

  1. 25yo woman: Graves, active ophthalmopathy. RAI?

A. Contraindicated (worsens eye disease) → Surgery or PTU+steroids

B. Safe with steroid cover

C. Preferred

D. Only option

E. Do after carbimazole

  1. Pregnant 10 weeks: Graves. ATD?

A. PTU 1st trimester → switch carbimazole 2nd/3rd

B. Carbimazole throughout

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Propranolol only

  1. Child 12yo: Graves. Management?

A. Carbimazole longer course; RAI if >10-15y

B. RAI immediately

C. Surgery

D. PTU

E. Observation

  1. Post-RAI hypothyroidism:

A. >80% at 1 year → lifelong levothyroxine

B. <10%

C. 30-40%

D. 50-60%

E. Never

  1. Carbimazole side effects:

A. Agranulocytosis (0.1-0.5%), hepatitis, rash, arthralgia

B. Only rash

C. Only hepatotoxicity

D. Only agranulocytosis

E. No significant side effects

  1. TRAb at 12mo predicts relapse:

A. High TRAb → >80% relapse

B. Low TRAb → high relapse

C. No correlation

D. Only TSH matters

E. Only fT4 matters

  1. Block-and-replace regimen:

A. Carbimazole 20-40mg + Levothyroxine 100-150µg

B. PTU + Iodine

C. RAI + Levothyroxine

D. Surgery + Levothyroxine

E. Propranolol + Carbimazole

  1. Pretibial myxoedema:

A. Graves-specific (1-5%); local TRAb effect on fibroblasts

B. All hyperthyroidism

C. Only in severe thyrotoxicosis

D. Post-RAI complication

E. Post-surgical

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Graves pathogenesis

A: TRAb (TSHR-stimulating IgG) → chronic TSHR activation → hyperthyroidism

  • Q: Classic triad

A: Hyperthyroidism + Ophthalmopathy + Pretibial myxoedema

  • Q: TRAb sensitivity

A: >95% in Graves

  • Q: RAI uptake

A: Diffuse homogeneous ↑ (vs toxic nodule = focal; thyroiditis = low)

  • Q: Carbimazole 1st line

A: 15-40mg/day 12-18mo; remission 40-50%

  • Q: RAI contraindicated

A: Pregnancy, breastfeeding, ACTIVE OPHTHALMOPATHY

  • Q: PTU in pregnancy

A: 1st trimester only (less teratogenic); switch carbimazole 2nd/3rd tri

  • Q: Smoking

A: 2-3x relapse risk; worsens ophthalmopathy

  • Q: Acropachy

A: Rare (clubbing, periosteal reaction); Graves-specific

  • Q: Post-RAI

A: >80% hypothyroid at 1yr → levothyroxine

  • Q: Block-and-replace

A: Carbimazole 20-40mg + LT4 100-150µg; if relapse/non-adherence

  • Q: Pretibial myxoedema

A: 1-5% Graves; TRAb → fibroblasts; local

  • Q: Carbimazole AE

A: Agranulocytosis 0.1-0.5%; hepatitis; rash; arthralgia

  • Q: TRAb at 12mo

A: High → >80% relapse
