topics.title: Cyclical Cushing
topics.html:
[!info]
Cyclical Cushing Syndrome = Intermittent Hypercortisolism with periods of normal cortisol secretion. Prevalence 5-15% of Cushing Syndrome. Diagnostic challenge: requires prolonged monitoring. Management same as sustained Cushing but timing of surgery critical.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define cyclical Cushing and its prevalence
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria using prolonged monitoring protocols
  • [ ] Differentiate from pseudo-cyclical patterns and pseudo-Cushing
  • [ ] Time surgical intervention appropriately
  • [ ] Manage medical therapy during off-cycles

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
DefinitionIntermittent Hypercortisolism with alternating periods of biochemical hypercortisolism and normocortisolism
Prevalence5-15% of Cushing Syndrome
Age & SexSimilar to Sustained Cushing (Female 3-5:1, Peak 20-50y)
AetiologyCushing Disease (Pituitary) ~70%; Adrenal Adenoma ~20%; Ectopic ACTH ~10%

3. Pathophysiology

MechanismDetails
Pulsatile ACTH SecretionCorticotroph Adenoma with Burst Firing Pattern → Intermittent ACTH Bursts
Variable Cortisol ClearanceAltered Hepatic Metabolism / CBG Fluctuations
Feedback Resistance FluctuationVariable Glucocorticoid Receptor Sensitivity
Tumour BiologyPulsatile Secretion from Adenoma (Intrinsic Pacemaker)
Feedback ThresholdVariable Set-Point of HPA Axis Negative Feedback

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureCyclical vs Sustained Cushing
SymptomsIntermittent — Waxing and Waning Over Days-Weeks
Weight GainFluctuating (Rapid Gain → Plateau/Loss)
Glucose ControlBrittle Glycaemic Control (Episodic Hyperglycaemia)
HypertensionLabile BP (Episodic Severe HTN)
MoodCyclical Mood Swings (Euphoria → Depression)
Skin ChangesStriae/Bruising Appear During Active Phase
Asymptomatic IntervalsComplete Resolution of Symptoms/Biochemistry Between Cycles

5. Diagnostic Criteria — Prolonged Monitoring

Diagnostic Standard

CriterionRequirement
Monitoring Duration≥2-4 Weeks (Usually 4 Weeks Daily Sampling)
Biochemical Criteria>3 Peaks of UFC >ULN Separated by Periods of Normal UFC
Cycle DurationTypically 3-10 Days (Variable: 1 Day to Several Weeks)
Nadir UFCNormal (<70-90 nmol/24h) During Off-Cycle

Monitoring Protocol

ScheduleTests
Daily24h UFC, Morning Cortisol, ACTH (If Possible)
Alternate DaysLate-Night Salivary Cortisol (LNSC)
WeeklySerum Cortisol (09:00), ACTH, Electrolytes
DurationMinimum 2 Weeks (Ideally 4 Weeks)

6. Diagnostic Criteria (Consensus)

CriterionThreshold
Peak UFC>ULN (Upper Limit of Normal)
Nadir UFC (Normal)
Number of Peaks≥3 Discrete Peaks
Inter-Peak IntervalNormal UFC Between Peaks
Cycle RegularityMay Be Regular (e.g., Every 3-7 Days) or Irregular

7. Differentiation — Cyclical vs Sustained vs Pseudo-Cushing

FeatureCyclical CushingSustained CushingPseudo-Cushing
UFC PatternPeaks >ULN + Normal NadirsPersistently >ULNVariable (Often Normal/Near-ULN)
Cycle DurationDays-Weeks (Regular/Irregular)ContinuousN/A
SymptomsWaxing-WaningPersistentOften Absent/Mild
LDDSTVariable (May Suppress During Nadir)Non-SuppressedMay Suppress
UFC BaselineAlways >ULNNormal/Near-ULN

8. Diagnostic Protocol

Standard Monitoring Protocol (4 Weeks)

DayTests
Daily (1-28)24h UFC, 09:00 Cortisol, ACTH (If Feasible)
Alternate DaysLate-Night Salivary Cortisol (LNSC)
WeeklySerum Electrolytes, Glucose, HbA1c, Lipids
As Needed1mg DST (During Suspected Nadir)

Interpretation Rules

PatternInterpretation
≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal UFC BetweenDiagnostic
<3 Peaks or No Normal NadirInconclusive → Extend Monitoring / Consider Pseudo-Cushing
Continuous >ULNSustained Cushing (Not Cyclical)
<2 PeaksInsufficient Data → Extend Monitoring

9. Aetiology of Cyclical Cushing

SourceFrequencyCycle Characteristics
Cushing Disease (Pituitary)~70%Often Regular (3-7 Days); Pituitary Adenoma Pulsatility
Adrenal Adenoma~20%Irregular; Adrenal Autonomous Cycling
Ectopic ACTH (Carcinoid)~10%Irregular; Tumour Burden Fluctuation
Adrenal CarcinomaRareVariable

10. Management — Timing is Critical

Surgical Timing

PrincipleDetails
Operate During Active PhaseBiochemical Confirmation During Peak → ↑ Surgical Success
Avoid Surgery During NadirRisk: False Negative Intra-Op Cortisol; Missed Tumour
Pre-Op ConfirmationConfirm Active Phase with UFC/LNSC Day Before Surgery

Surgical Outcomes

Phase at SurgeryRemission Rate
Active Phase (Peak)80-90% (Similar to Sustained)
Nadir Phase<30% (High False Negative Rate)

Medical Therapy During Nadir

SituationManagement
Waiting for PeakKetoconazole (200-400mg q6h) — Suppresses Peak Without Masking Nadir
Severe Symptoms During PeakMetyrapone (Bridge to Surgery)
Do NOTGive Steroids/Cortisol-Lowering During Nadir (Masks Cycle)

11. Diagnostic Challenges

Pseudo-Cyclical Patterns (False Positives)

PatternMechanismDifferentiation
Stress-InducedAcute Stress → Transient ↑ UFCNon-Recurrent, Resolves with Stress Removal
Drug-InducedOral Contraceptives, Oestrogen, MirtazapineDrug History; Repeats After Stopping
Lab ErrorCollection Errors, Improper TimingRepeat Sets; Standardised Protocol
Obese/DepressedPseudo-Cushing PatternResolves with Treatment of Primary Condition

Distinguishing Features

FeatureTrue CyclicalPseudo-Cyclical
RegularityOften Cyclic (e.g., Q3-7 Days)Irrandom/Non-Recurrent
DurationWeeks-MonthsDays (Acute Stressors)
TriggersSpontaneousIdentifiable (Stress, Drugs)
ResolutionSpontaneous CyclicResolves with Trigger Removal

12. Management Algorithm

```

Cyclical Cushing Suspected

│

▼

4-Week Monitoring (Daily UFC + Alternate LNSC)

│

├── ≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal Nadirs → CONFIRMED

│ │

│ ├── IDENTIFY SOURCE (ACTH, HDDST, MRI, CT)

│ │ ├── Pituitary → Cushing Disease

│ │ ├── Adrenal → Adrenal Adenoma/Carcinoma

│ │ └── Ectopic → Ectopic ACTH

│ │

│ └── SURGERY DURING ACTIVE PHASE (Confirmed Peak)

│

├── <3 Peaks / No Clear Nadir → EXTEND MONITORING (6-8 WEEKS)

│

└── CONTINUOUS ELEVATION → SUSTAINED CUSHING

```

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Cyclical Cushing Prevalence5-15% of Cushing Syndrome
Diagnostic Gold StandardDaily UFC × 2-4 Weeks
Diagnostic Criteria>3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal UFC Between Peaks
Cycle Duration3-10 Days (Typically 3-7 Days)
Most Common CauseCushing Disease (~70%)
Surgery TimingDuring Active Phase (Peak) — 80-90% Remission vs <30% if Nadir
Monitoring DurationMinimum 2 Weeks (Ideally 4 Weeks)
Diagnostic Criteria≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal UFC Between
Medical BridgeKetoconazole During Nadir (Suppresses Peak)
Surgery AvoidDuring Nadir (<30% Remission)
Pseudo-CyclicalStress, Drugs, Lab Error — Non-Recurrent, Resolves with Trigger Removal

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Cyclical vs SustainedCyclical = Fluctuating UFC (Peaks + Normal Nadir); Sustained = Persistently Elevated
Cyclical vs Pseudo-CushingTrue Cyclical = Recurrent Peaks + Normal Nadir; Pseudo = Stress/Drug-Induced, Non-Recurrent
Surgery TimingCritical: Operate at Peak (80-90% Cure); At Nadir = <30% Cure
Monitoring DurationMinimum 2 Weeks; Ideally 4 Weeks (Catch Irregular Cycles)
Medical BridgeKetoconazole During Nadir (Prevents Peak Overshoot) — Not During Peak

Mnemonic: CYCLICAL CUSHING

  • Cycles: 3-10 Days Typical
  • Yes to ≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN
  • Cortisol Normal Between Peaks
  • Length: Monitor 2-4 Weeks
  • Avoid Surgery at Nadir (<30% Cure)
  • Leak: Day 1 Cortisol <50 at Surgery = Good
  • Avoid Steroids During Nadir (Masks Cycle)
  • L percutaneous? No, Look for Ipss/HDDST for Source

15. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Cyclical Cushing))

Definition

Intermittent Hypercortisolism

Peaks + Normal Nadirs

5-15% of Cushing

Diagnosis

Daily UFC x 2-4 Weeks

≥3 Peaks >ULN + Normal Nadirs

Cycle: 3-10 Days (Regular/Irregular)

Aetiology

Pituitary (70%) - Regular

Adrenal (20%) - Irregular

Ectopic (10%) - Irregular

Management

Surgery at PEAK (80-90% Cure)

Avoid NADIR Surgery (<30%)

Monitor 2-4 Weeks Pre-Op

Ketoconazole Bridge at Nadir

Differential

Pseudo-Cushing: Non-Recurrent, Stress/Drugs

Sustained: Continuous >ULN

True Cyclical: Recurrent Peaks + Normal Nadirs

```

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Prevalence5-15% of Cushing Syndrome
Diagnostic StandardDaily UFC × 2-4 Weeks
Diagnostic Criteria≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal UFC Between
Cycle Duration3-10 Days (Typically 3-7 Days)
Surgery TimingAt Peak (80-90% Cure); Avoid Nadir (<30%)
Monitoring DurationMinimum 2 Weeks; Ideally 4 Weeks
Bridge TherapyKetoconazole at Nadir (Suppresses Peaks)
Pseudo-CyclicalNon-Recurrent; Stress/Drug-Induced
Key DifferentialSustained = Continuous >ULN; Cyclical = Peaks + Normal Nadir

  1. If single test during remission:

A. False negative (normal cortisol, ACTH, DST suppression)

B. Positive

C. Indeterminate

D. False positive

E. Always positive

  1. Treatment same as non-cyclical:

A. Yes (surgery for pituitary/adrenal/ectopic)

B. No - observation only

C. Only medical

D. Only radiotherapy

E. Only bilateral adrenalectomy

  1. Cyclical Cushing vs Pseudo-Cushing:

A. Cyclical: true cortisol excess periods; Pseudo-Cushing: stress-related

B. Same

C. Pseudo-Cushing has cycles

D. Cyclical is milder

E. Pseudo-Cushing always cyclical

  1. Diagnostic challenge:

A. May miss diagnosis if tested during remission

B. Easy to diagnose

C. Always obvious

D. Only needs one test

E. Never misses

  1. Recognition important for:

A. Avoiding false negative workup; timing of tests

B. Insurance

C. Research only

D. No clinical impact

E. Family screening

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient with intermittent Cushing features. Single UFC normal. Next?

A. Repeat UFC/salivary cortisol weekly x4-8 weeks

B. Diagnose pseudo-Cushing

C. Discharge

D. High-dose DST

E. IPSS

  1. Cyclical Cushing confirmed. Pituitary MRI shows 4mm adenoma. Treatment?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery (same as non-cyclical)

B. Observation

C. Ketoconazole only

D. Radiotherapy

E. Bilateral adrenalectomy

  1. Cyclical Cushing: how to time surgery?

A. During cortisol excess phase (easier localisation)

B. During remission

C. Any time

D. Only after 3 cycles

E. Never operate

  1. Cyclical ectopic ACTH:

A. Very rare; same diagnostic challenge

B. Common

C. Does not exist

D. Always SCLC

E. Always carcinoid

  1. Cyclical Cushing recurrence post-surgery:

A. May be harder to detect; need repeated testing

B. Never recurs

C. Always immediate

D. Only if Nelson syndrome

E. Only if radiotherapy

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Cyclical Cushing

A: Episodic cortisol excess with normal intervals (days-months)

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: Repeated 24h UFC / midnight salivary cortisol over weeks; single test may miss

  • Q: Can be

A: Pituitary (disease), adrenal, or ectopic

  • Q: HDDST/CRH/IPSS

A: May be falsely normal if tested in remission phase

  • Q: Treatment

A: Same as non-cyclical (surgery for source)

  • Q: Key pitfall

A: Testing during remission → false negative; need serial testing

  • Q: Pseudo-Cushing

A: Stress-related cortisol variability; not true cycles

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Periodic cortisol excess with normal intervals — Cyclical Cushing =
  1. Repeated testing (UFC, salivary cortisol) over weeks/months — Diagnosis requires:
  1. Pituitary adenoma with periodic ACTH secretion — Cyclical Cushing disease:
  1. Days to months — Cyclical Cushing frequency:
  1. Multiple 24h UFC / midnight salivary cortisol over time — Best diagnostic approach:
  1. False negative (normal cortisol, ACTH, DST suppression) — If single test during remission:
  1. Yes (surgery for pituitary/adrenal/ectopic) — Treatment same as non-cyclical:
  1. Cyclical: true cortisol excess periods; Pseudo-Cushing: stress-related — Cyclical Cushing vs Pseudo-Cushing:
  1. May miss diagnosis if tested during remission — Diagnostic challenge:
  1. Avoiding false negative workup; timing of tests — Recognition important for:

SBAs

  1. Transsphenoidal surgery (same as non-cyclical) — Cyclical Cushing confirmed. Pituitary MRI shows 4mm adenoma. Treatment?
  1. During cortisol excess phase (easier localisation) — Cyclical Cushing: how to time surgery?

  1. May be harder to detect; need repeated testing — Cyclical Cushing recurrence post-surgery:

topics.title: Ectopic Acth Syndrome
topics.html:
[!info]
Ectopic ACTH Syndrome = ACTH Secretion from Non-Pituitary Tumour. Accounts for 10-15% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing. SCLC (50%), Bronchial Carcinoid (20%), Pancreatic NET, Thymic Carcinoid. High ACTH, No HDDST Suppression, Localisation with CT/DOTATATE PET.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate ectopic ACTH from Cushing disease using HDDST, IPSS, CRH test
  • [ ] Identify common ectopic ACTH sources and their clinical features
  • [ ] Apply localisation strategies (CT, ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET, Bronchoscopy)
  • [ ] Outline medical management (ketoconazole, metyrapone, bilateral adrenalectomy)
  • [ ] Recognise cyclical ectopic ACTH and paraneoplastic features

2. Aetiology & Common Sources

SourceFrequencyACTH LevelClinical Features
Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC)50%Very High (>500 pg/mL)Rapid Onset, Severe Hypokalaemia, Hyperpigmentation, Weight Loss
Bronchial Carcinoid20-25%HighIndolent Course, Often Resectable, Atypical Carcinoid
Pancreatic NET10-15%HighMay Co-secrete Other Hormones (Insulin, Glucagon)
Thymic Carcinoid5-10%HighAssociated with MEN1
Phaeochromocytoma / Paraganglioma5%VariableParoxysmal Hypertension, Catecholamine Excess
Medullary Thyroid Cancer2-5%ElevatedDiarrhoea, Flushing, MEN2
Other (Ovarian, Gastric, Renal)RareVariableOccult Primary

3. Clinical Features — Ectopic ACTH vs Cushing Disease

FeatureEctopic ACTHCushing Disease
ACTH LevelVery High (Often >200 pg/mL, up to >1000)Normal / Mildly Elevated (10-50 pg/mL)
OnsetRapid (Weeks)Insidious (Months-Years)
HypokalaemiaSevere (<2.5 mmol/L, 80-90%)Mild/Moderate (10-20%)
Metabolic AlkalosisMarked (HCO₃⁻ >30)Mild
HyperpigmentationProminent (High ACTH → MSH Cross-Reactivity)Mild
Weight LossCommon (Cancer Cachexia)Weight Gain (Central Obesity)
HypertensionSevere, ResistantModerate
Glucose IntoleranceSevere (Insulin Resistance)Moderate
Mental StatusConfusion, Lethargy (Electrolyte Disturbance)Depression, Cognitive Changes

4. Diagnostic Differentiation

Key Investigations

TestEctopic ACTHCushing Disease
Plasma ACTH>100-200 pg/mL (Often >500)10-50 pg/mL
HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)No Suppression (<50%)>50% Suppression
CRH Test (100µg IV)No ACTH RiseACTH ↑ >50%
IPSSNo Central:Peripheral GradientCentral:Peripheral >2 (Basal) / >3 (Post-CRH)
MRI PituitaryNormal / IncidentalomaMicroadenoma (80-90%)
ImagingCT Chest/Abd/Pelvis + ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PETMRI Pituitary (Microadenoma)

5. Diagnostic Algorithm

```

Cushing Syndrome Confirmed + ACTH ≥10-20 pg/mL

│

└── HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)

│

├── NO SUPPRESSION (<50%) → ECTOPIC ACTH

│ │

│ ├── ACTH Very High (>200 pg/mL) → Strongly Suggests Ectopic

│ │

│ └── LOCALISATION WORKUP

│ ├── CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis (Contrast)

│ ├── ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Somatostatin Receptor Imaging)

│ ├── Bronchoscopy (If Lung Lesion)

│ └── Octreotide Scan (If DOTATATE Unavailable)

│

└── SUPPRESSION >50% → CUSHING DISEASE

MRI Pituitary → TSS

```

6. Localisation Strategy

Step 1: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis (Contrast)

TargetDetails
LungSCLC, Carcinoid, Metastases
MediastinumThymic Carcinoid, Lymph Nodes
Abdomen/PelvisPancreatic NET, Adrenal (Rule Out Adrenal Cushing), Phaeochromocytoma
ResolutionThin Slice (1-2mm), Arterial + Venous Phase

Step 2: Functional Imaging

ModalitySensitivitySpecificityIndication
⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT90-95% (SSTR2+)90%+First-Line (SCLC, Carcinoid, Pancreatic NET, Phaeo)
Octreotide Scan (Octreoscan)80-90%80%If DOTATATE Unavailable
FDG-PET/CTHigh (SCLC)Low (Net)SCLC Staging; Not for Well-Differentiated NET
Bronchoscopy——Central Bronchial Lesions (Carcinoid, SCLC)

7. Common Ectopic ACTH Sources

Tumour Type% of Ectopic ACTHACTH LevelKey Features
SCLC50%Very High (>500 pg/mL)Smoker, Weight Loss, Paraneoplastic (SIADH, LEMS)
Bronchial Carcinoid (Typical/Atypical)20-25%HighIndolent, Long Survival, Often Resectable
Pancreatic NET10-15%HighMay Co-secrete Insulin/Glucagon/PP
Thymic Carcinoid5-10%HighYoung, MEN1 Association
Phaeochromocytoma/Paraganglioma5%VariableParoxysmal HTN, Catecholamine Excess
Medullary Thyroid Cancer2-5%ElevatedDiarrhoea, Flushing, MEN2
Other (Ovarian, Gastric, Renal)<5%VariableOccult Primary

8. Medical Management

First-Line: Surgical Resection (If Localised/Resectable)

TumourApproach
Bronchial CarcinoidSurgical Resection (Lobectomy/Sleeve) — Curative if Localised
Thymic CarcinoidThymectomy ± Mediastinal Lymphadenectomy
Pancreatic NETDistal Pancreatectomy / Whipple (Per Location)
SCLCSurgery Rarely Curative (Usually Metastatic)

Medical Therapy (Unresectable/Metastatic/Pre-Op Bridge)

DrugDoseMechanismMonitoring
Ketoconazole200-400mg Q6H (Max 1200mg/day)CYP11B1 Inhibition (11β-Hydroxylase)LFTs q2wk, Adrenal Insufficiency Risk, QTc
Metyrapone250-750mg Q8H (Max 6g/day)11β-Hydroxylase InhibitionHypotension, Oedema, Drowsiness, GI
Osilodrostat2-30mg BD11β-Hydroxylase InhibitionECG (QT), Hypokalaemia, Liver Enzymes
EtoposideSCLC ProtocolChemotherapyCBC, LFTs, Neuropathy
Somatostatin AnaloguesOctreotide LAR 20-40mg IM q4wkSSTR2 Agonist (↓ ACTH Secretion)GI, Gallstones, Hyperglycaemia
Mifepristone300-1200mg/dayGlucocorticoid Receptor AntagonistEndometrial Changes, K+ Monitoring

9. Bilateral Adrenalectomy

Indications

IndicationDetails
Failed Medical TherapyUncontrolled Hypercortisolism Despite Maximal Medical Rx
Life-Threatening ComplicationsSevere Hypokalaemia, Resistant HTN, Psychosis, Cardiomyopathy
Unresectable Ectopic SourceMetastatic SCLC, Occult Primary
Patient PreferenceDefinitive Cure After Failed Options

Procedure

AspectDetails
ProcedureLaparoscopic Bilateral Adrenalectomy (Preferred)
Immediate Post-OpHydrocortisone 100mg IV q6h + Fludrocortisone 0.1mg OD
Fludrocortisone0.05-0.2mg OD (Life-Long Mineralocorticoid Replacement)
GlucocorticoidHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM (Life-Long)
Follow-upLifelong Endocrine Follow-up; Crisis Card; Medic Alert

10. Complications & Prognosis

ComplicationIncidenceManagement
Severe Hypokalaemia80-90%Aggressive K⁺ Replacement (IV KCl), Spironolactone/Amiloride
Metabolic AlkalosisCommonKCl, Acetazolamide (Rare)
Hyperglycaemia / Diabetes70-80%Insulin Sliding Scale / Basal-Bolus
Hypertension90%Multiple Agents (Including Spironolactone)
Osteoporosis50%Bisphosphonates, Calcium, Vitamin D
Psychiatric30-50%Antidepressants, Antipsychotics
Infection Risk↑ (Immunosuppression)Prophylactic TMP-SMX if Prolonged Steroids

Prognosis by Source

SourceMedian SurvivalCurative Potential
Bronchial Carcinoid10-20 YearsHigh (If Resected Early)
Pancreatic NET5-10 YearsModerate
Thymic Carcinoid5-10 YearsModerate (MEN1 Association)
SCLC6-12 MonthsPoor (Palliative)
PhaeochromocytomaVariableGood if Resected
Medullary Thyroid Cancer5-10 YearsRET Mutation Guides Surgery

11. Examination Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Ectopic ACTH %10-15% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing
ACTH Level>200 pg/mL (Often >500) vs Cushing Disease (10-50)
HDDSTNo Suppression (<50%) — Differentiates from Cushing Disease
SCLC50% of Ectopic ACTH; Very High ACTH, Severe Hypokalaemia
Bronchial Carcinoid20-25%; Indolent, Resectable, Better Prognosis
HDDSTNo Suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal; >50% Suppression = Cushing Disease
IPSSNo Central:Peripheral Gradient = Ectopic
LocalisationCT Chest/Abd → ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET (Best) → Bronchoscopy
Medical RxKetoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Etoposide (SCLC)
Bilateral AdrenalectomyLast Resort (Life-Long Gluco + Mineralocorticoid Replacement)
Cyclical Ectopic ACTHDaily UFC × 2-4 Weeks for Diagnosis

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Ectopic vs Cushing DiseaseEctopic: ACTH Very High, No HDDST Suppression; Cushing Disease: ACTH Normal/High, HDDST Suppresses
Ectopic vs Adrenal CushingEctopic: ACTH High; Adrenal: ACTH Suppressed
SCLC vs CarcinoidSCLC: Aggressive, High ACTH, Short Survival; Carcinoid: Indolent, Resectable
IPSS vs CRH TestIPSS = Gold Standard Localisation; CRH Test = Functional (ACTH Rise >50% = Pituitary)
Bilateral AdrenalectomyLast Resort; Life-Long Gluco + Mineralo Replacement; Medical Alert Bracelet
Ectopic CRHRare (<1%); CRH High, ACTH High; CRH Stimulation Test Positive

13. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Ectopic ACTH Syndrome))

Frequency

10-15% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing

Sources

SCLC (50%) - Aggressive, High ACTH

Bronchial Carcinoid (20-25%) - Indolent, Resectable

Pancreatic NET (10-15%)

Thymic Carcinoid (5-10%) - MEN1

Phaeo/Paraganglioma (5%)

MTC (2-5%) - MEN2

Diagnosis

HDDST No Suppression

IPSS No Gradient

MRI Pituitary Normal

Localisation

CT Chest/Abd/Pelvis

68Ga-DOTATATE PET (Gold Standard)

Bronchoscopy for Central Lesions

Management

Resectable → Surgery (Cure)

Unresectable → Ketoconazole/Metyrapone/Osilodrostat

Refractory → Bilateral Adrenalectomy

Complications

Hypokalaemia (80-90%)

Metabolic Alkalosis

Hyperglycaemia/Diabetes

Hypertension

Osteoporosis

Prognosis

Carcinoid: 10-20yr

SCLC: 6-12mo

```

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Ectopic ACTH %10-15% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing
ACTH Level>200 pg/mL (Often >500) vs Cushing Disease (10-50)
HDDST ResultNo Suppression (<50%) — Differentiates from Cushing Disease
Most Common SourceSCLC (50%)
Best Localisation⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Gold Standard)
SCLC FeaturesSmoker, Weight Loss, Severe Hypokalaemia, Paraneoplastic
Bronchial CarcinoidIndolent, Resectable, 10-20yr Survival
Medical TherapyKetoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Etoposide (SCLC)
Bilateral AdrenalectomyLast Resort (Life-Long Replacement)
Ectopic vs Cushing DiseaseACTH Very High, No HDDST Suppression, No IPSS Gradient
Adrenal vs EctopicAdrenal: ACTH Suppressed; Ectopic: ACTH High
IPSSNo Central:Peripheral Gradient = Ectopic

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]

16. MCQs (10)

A. >200 pg/mL (often >500)

B. Suppressed

  1. Carcinoid syndrome vs ectopic ACTH:

17. SBA Questions (10)

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Ectopic ACTH

topics.title: Phaeochromocytoma Paraganglioma
topics.html:
[!info]
Phaeochromocytoma = Catecholamine-Secreting Tumour of Adrenal Medulla. Paraganglioma = Extra-Adrenal (Sympathetic/Parasympathetic). "Rule of 10s" (Classic Teaching). Alpha-Blockade BEFORE Beta-Blockade — Mandatory Pre-Op Prep. Genetic Testing Mandatory (30-40% Hereditary).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation (paroxysmal hypertension, headaches, sweating, palpitations)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (plasma free metanephrines, 24h urine metanephrines)
  • [ ] Apply localisation imaging (CT/MRI, ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET, ¹²³I-MIBG)
  • [ ] Execute pre-operative alpha-blockade protocol (phenoxybenzamine)
  • [ ] Indicate genetic testing and recognise hereditary syndromes (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx)
  • [ ] Differentiate phaeochromocytoma vs paraganglioma

2. Epidemiology & "Rule of 10s" (Classic Teaching)

Rule of 10sDetail
10% BilateralAdrenal Phaeochromocytoma
10% MalignantPhaeochromocytoma; Paraganglioma Higher (30-50%)
10% Extra-AdrenalParaganglioma (Sympathetic Chain, Organ of Zuckerkandl)
10% FamilialActually 30-40% (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx, TMEM127, MAX)
10% Pediatric<18 Years
10% Extra-AbdominalParaganglioma (Head/Neck)
Modern View: "Rule of 10s" is an Approximation; Heritability 30-40% is Key Takeaway.

3. Clinical Presentation

Classic Triad (Episodic)

SymptomFrequencyMechanism
Headache90%Severe, Throbbing, Often Precipitated
Sweating70-80%Profuse, Generalised
Palpitations60-70%Tachycardia, Arrhythmias

Paroxysmal vs Sustained Hypertension

PatternFrequencyFeatures
Paroxysmal50-60%Episodic SBP >200, Duration Minutes-Hours
Sustained30-40%Persistent HTN, Paroxysms Superimposed
Normotensive5-10%Normotensive Between Spells

Additional Features

SystemFeatures
NeurologicalTremor, Anxiety, Panic Attacks, Tremor
GINausea, Vomiting, Abdominal Pain, Constipation
DermatologicalFlushing, Pallor (Post-Attack)
OphthalmicBlurred Vision, Hypertensive Retinopathy
MetabolicHypoglycaemia (Rare), Hyperglycaemia, Weight Loss

4. Diagnosis — Biochemical Confirmation

Gold Standard: Plasma Free Metanephrines

TestSensitivitySpecificityCut-off
Plasma Free Metanephrines99%89%Metanephrine ≥ Upper Limit OR Normetanephrine ≥ Upper Limit
24h Urine Metanephrines95%95%Metanephrine + Normetanephrine >ULN

Sample Collection Protocol (Critical)

RequirementDetails
Fasting≥8 Hours (Preferably Morning)
PositionSupine ≥30 Minutes Prior
Medications to AvoidL-DOPA, SSRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, Cocaine, Amphetamines, Sympathomimetics (≥7 Days)
Drugs Causing False PositivesL-DOPA, Metoclopramide, Antidepressants (TCA, SSRI), Cocaine, Amphetamines, Buspirone, Reserpine

24h Urine Metanephrines (Alternative)

Collection24h Urine + Acid Preservative (HCl)
AdvantagesLess Affected by Acute Stress; Integrates Over 24h
False NegativesIncomplete Collection, Acidification Failure

5. Localisation Imaging

First-Line: CT/MRI Adrenal

ModalityProtocol
CT (Adrenal Protocol)1mm Slices, Arterial + Venous Phase, Non-Contrast for HU
MRI (Adrenal Protocol)T1/T2, In/Out Phase (Chemical Shift), Post-Gadolinium

Functional Imaging (Gold Standard for Localisation)

ModalitySensitivitySpecificityIndication
⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT90-95%90%+First-Line (SSTR2+)
¹²³I-MIBG Scintigraphy80-90%80-90%If DOTATATE Unavailable; MIBG-Avid Tumours
¹⁸F-FDG PETHigh (Malignant)LowerSCLC, Poorly Differentiated NET
Octreoscan (¹¹¹In-Octreotide)80-85%80%If DOTATATE/MIBG Unavailable

6. Genetic Testing — Mandatory (30-40% Hereditary)

SyndromeGenePhaeo FeaturesOther Features
MEN2ARET (Codon 634)Phaeo 50% (Often Bilateral), MTC 95%, pHPT 20-30%
MEN2BRET M918T (95%)Phaeo 50%, MTC 100%Marfanoid, Mucosal Neuromas, Café-au-lait
VHLVHLPhaeo (Often Bilateral), RCC, Haemangioblastomas (CNS/Retina)
NF1NF1Phaeo (Unilateral), Café-au-lait Spots, Neurofibromas
SDHBSDHBPGL (Extra-Adrenal), High Malignancy (30-50%), GIST, RCC
SDHDSDHDHNPGL (Head/Neck PGL), Phaeo
SDHA/SDHC/SDHCSDHA/CVariable
TMEM127/MAXTMEM127/MAXPhaeo/PGL

Genetic Testing Indications

IndicationAction
All Phaeo/PGL PatientsMandatory (30-40% Hereditary)
Bilateral/MultifocalHigh Probability Genetic
Family HistoryStrong Indication
Young Age (<40)Higher Genetic Yield
Malignant PhaeoSDHB Suspicion High

7. Pre-Operative Preparation — Mandatory Protocol

Golden Rule: ALPHA-BLOCKADE BEFORE BETA-BLOCKADE

Alpha-Blockade Protocol (Phenoxybenzamine)

StepDoseTarget
Starting10mg BD (Oral)—
TitrationIncrease by 10mg BD every 2-3 DaysSBP <130/80 Supine, <120/70 Standing
Maintenance20-80mg/day (Divided BD/TDS)HR 60-80 bpm
Duration≥2 Weeks (Ideally 3-4 Weeks)Before Surgery

Beta-Blockade (ONLY After Alpha-Blockade Established)

AgentDoseTiming
Propranolol20-40mg TDSAFTER Alpha-Blockade Established
Atenolol/Metoprolol50-100mg OD/BDAfter Alpha-Blockade Adequate

Volume Expansion

InterventionDetails
IV Fluids2-3L 0.9% NaCl/Day (Start with Alpha-Blockade)
GoalCVP 8-12 mmHg, UOP >0.5 mL/kg/h
MonitorNa+, K+, Glucose, CVP, UOP q1-2h

8. Paraganglioma (Extra-Adrenal) — Key Differences

FeaturePhaeochromocytoma (Adrenal)Paraganglioma (Extra-Adrenal)
LocationAdrenal MedullaSympathetic Chain (Organ of Zuckerkandl — Most Common)
Malignancy Rate~10%30-50% (SDHB Highest)
Malignancy DefinitionMetastases (Non-Chromaffin Tissue)Metastases (Bone, Liver, Lung, Lymph Nodes)
GeneticsMEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHxSDHB (High Malignancy), SDHD (HNPGL)
ImagingAdrenal CT/MRIWhole Body (Neck to Pelvis)

Head & Neck Paraganglioma (HNPGL)

FeatureDetails
LocationCarotid Body, Jugulotympanic, Vagal, Laryngeal
GeneSDHD (Maternal Imprinting — Paternal Transmission), SDHC, SDHAF2
MalignancyRare (<5%)
SymptomsMass Effect (Cranial Nerve Palsies, Tinnitus, Dysphagia)
TreatmentSurgery ± Radiotherapy (Stereotactic)

9. Pre-Operative Optimisation Checklist

ParameterTargetTimeline
Alpha-BlockadePhenoxybenzamine 20-80mg/day≥2 Weeks (Ideally 3-4 Weeks)
BP ControlSupine <130/80; Standing <120/70Achieved Before Surgery
Heart Rate60-80 bpm (Add Beta-Blocker After Alpha)After Alpha-Blockade
Volume StatusEuvolaemic (CVP 8-12, UOP >0.5 mL/kg/h)At Surgery
GlucoseNormoglycaemia (Insulin Sliding Scale if Diabetic)Peri-operative
ElectrolytesK+ 3.5-5.0 (Correct Hypokalaemia Pre-Op)Pre-Op

10. Surgical Management

Laparoscopic Adrenalectomy — Gold Standard

ApproachIndication
Laparoscopic TransabdominalStandard (<8-10cm, No Invasion)
Posterior Retroperitoneoscopic (PRA)Small Tumours, Prior Abdominal Surgery
Open Adrenalectomy>10cm, Suspected Carcinoma, Invasion, Prior Surgery

Intraoperative Management

ChallengeManagement
Tumour ManipulationCatecholamine Surge → Deepen Anaesthesia, Esmolol/Nitroprusside
Venous SamplingAdrenal Vein Sampling (If Bilateral/Uncertain Laterality)
Bilateral PhaeoStaged (6-8 Weeks Apart) or Simultaneous (Experienced Centre)

Post-Operative Care

ComplicationMonitoring
HypotensionVasopressors (Norepinephrine), Fluid Resuscitation
HypoglycaemiaGlucose Monitoring q1-2h (First 24h)
Adrenal InsufficiencyHydrocortisone 50-100mg IV q6h (If Bilateral/Contra-lateral Suppressed)

11. Malignancy & Follow-up

Malignancy Criteria (No Histological Benign/Malignant)

CriterionDefinition
MetastasesNon-Chromaffin Tissue (Bone, Liver, Lung, Lymph Nodes)
Local InvasionAdjacent Organ Invasion
RecurrenceLocal/Distant Recurrence Post-Resection

SDHB Mutation Carriers — High Malignancy Risk

FeatureDetails
PGL (Extra-Adrenal)Most Common in SDHB Carriers
Malignancy Rate30-50% (Highest of All SDHx)
SurveillanceAnnual ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT + Biochemical

12. Follow-up & Surveillance

TimepointBiochemicalImaging
Post-Op 2 WeeksMetanephrines—
3 MonthsMetanephrinesCT/MRI if Residual Concern
6 MonthsMetanephrinesMRI/CT (If Residual)
Annual (×5yrs)MetanephrinesMRI/CT Alternating
Lifelong (If Hereditary)Annual MetanephrinesMRI/CT q1-2yr

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Phaeo vs ParagangliomaPhaeo = Adrenal; PGL = Extra-Adrenal (Sympathetic Chain/Organ of Zuckerkandl)
MalignancyMetastases to Non-Chromaffin Tissue = Malignant (Not Size/Histology)
Alpha vs Beta BlockadeAlpha FIRST (Phentolamine/Phenoxybenzamine); Beta ONLY After Alpha
Beta-Blocker FirstDANGEROUS → Unopposed Alpha → Hypertensive Crisis
SDHB vs SDHDSDHB = Malignant PGL (Abdomen/Pelvis); SDHD = HNPGL (Head/Neck)
MEN2A vs MEN2BMEN2A: MTC + Phaeo + pHPT; MEN2B: MTC + Phaeo + Marfanoid/Neuromas
MIBG vs DOTATATEDOTATATE Superior Sensitivity (90-95% vs 80-90%); MIBG for MIBG-Avid Tumours
Genetic TestingMandatory for ALL (30-40% Hereditary) — Not Just Syndromic

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Phaeochromocytoma

A: Adrenal medulla catecholamine-secreting tumour

  • Q: Paraganglioma

A: Extra-adrenal (sympathetic chain) catecholamine tumour

  • Q: Rule of 10s

A: 10% bilateral, 10% malignant, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% familial, 10% childhood

  • Q: Screening

A: Plasma free metanephrines (or 24h urinary) - highest sensitivity

  • Q: Pre-op

A: Alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) ≥2 weeks → THEN beta-blockade

  • Q: Alpha-blocker

A: Phenoxybenzamine (irreversible, non-selective) 10-20mg BD-TDS

  • Q: Beta-blockade

A: ONLY after alpha-blockade (unopposed alpha → hypertensive crisis)

  • Q: Genetics

A: MEN2 (RET), VHL, NF1, SDHx; SDHB → highest malignant risk

  • Q: Crisis Rx

A: Phentolamine 1-5mg IV → fluids → beta-blockade after alpha

  • Q: Malignant

A: Defined by metastases (not local invasion); SDHB high risk

  • Q: MEN2A

A: RET; MTC + phaeo + hyperparathyroidism; phaeo often bilateral

  • Q: MEN2B

A: RET; MTC + phaeo + marfanoid + mucosal neuromas; no hyperparathyroidism

  • Q: VHL

A: VHL gene; phaeo + renal Ca + cerebellar haemangioblastoma + pancreatic cysts

  • Q: NF1

A: NF1 gene; phaeo (unilateral) + neurofibromas + café-au-lait

topics.title: Primary Aldosteronism Conn'S Syndrome
topics.html:
[!info]
Primary Aldosteronism (Conn's Syndrome) = Autonomous Aldosterone Secretion from Adrenal Cortex. Commonest Cause of Secondary Hypertension (5-10% of HTN). Surgical Cure Possible (Unilateral Adenoma). Key: ARR Screening → Confirmatory Tests → AVS for Lateralisation → Surgery (Unilateral) vs Medical (Bilateral).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Screen for primary aldosteronism using ARR
  • [ ] Apply confirmatory tests (saline infusion, fludrocortisone suppression, captopril challenge)
  • [ ] Interpret adrenal venous sampling (AVS) for lateralisation
  • [ ] Differentiate unilateral adenoma vs bilateral hyperplasia
  • [ ] Apply surgical vs medical management algorithms

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
DefinitionAutonomous Aldosterone Production by Adrenal Cortex → Sodium Retention, K+ Loss, Hypertension
Prevalence5-10% of Hypertension; Up to 20% in Resistant HTN
DemographicsAge 30-50; Female > Male (2:1)
Commonest SubtypeUnilateral Aldosterone-Producing Adenoma (APA) (~60%)
Second CommonestBilateral Adrenal Hyperplasia (BAH) (~40%)
RareAdrenal Carcinoma (<1%), Familial Hyperaldosteronism (FH-I, II, III)

3. Pathophysiology & Clinical Features

Pathophysiology

MechanismConsequence
Aldosterone → ENaC Activation (Collecting Duct)Na+ Reabsorption → Volume Expansion → Hypertension
Aldosterone → ROMK ActivationK+ Excretion → Hypokalaemia
Aldosterone → H+ ExcretionMetabolic Alkalosis
Volume ExpansionSuppressed Renin (Feedback)

Clinical Features

FeatureFrequencyDetails
Hypertension>95%Often Resistant (≥3 Drugs); Early Morning Surge
Hypokalaemia60-80%Often Asymptomatic; Muscle Weakness, Cramps, Polyuria, Polydipsia
Metabolic AlkalosisCommonHCO₃⁻ ↑ (H+ Excretion in Exchange for K+ Reabsorption)
No OedemaAbsentAtrial Natriuretic Peptide (ANP) Escape → Natriuresis
SymptomsFatigue, Muscle Weakness, Nocturia, Polyuria, Headaches

Key Clue: Hypertension + Hypokalaemia (Spontaneous or Diuretic-Induced) → Screen for PA.

4. Screening — Aldosterone/Renin Ratio (ARR)

Screening Indications

IndicationPopulation
Resistant HTN≥3 Drugs Including Diuretic
HypokalaemiaSpontaneous or Diuretic-Induced
Adrenal IncidentalomaMandatory Workup
Hypertension + HypokalaemiaSpontaneous or Diuretic-Induced
Family HistoryEarly-Onset HTN, Stroke <40y
Adrenal IncidentalomaMandatory Workup

ARR Calculation & Interpretation

ParameterFormula / Units
ARRAldosterone (pmol/L) / Renin (mIU/L or pg/mL)
UnitsAldosterone: pmol/L; Renin: mIU/L (Direct) or pg/mL (Activity)
Cut-off (pmol/L : mIU/L)ARR >70 (or >30 + Aldosterone >400 pmol/L)
Cut-off (pmol/L : pg/mL)ARR >1000 (or >150 : >200 pg/mL)

Important: ARR is a Screening Test — False Positives/Negatives Occur; Confirmatory Testing Required.

5. ARR — Pitfalls & Preparation

Pre-Test Conditions (Critical for Reliability)

RequirementTarget
Potassium>4.0 mmol/L (Correct Hypokalaemia First)
Sodium IntakeNormal/High Salt (Not Low Salt)
Medications to STOP (4-6 Weeks)Spironolactone, Eplerenone, Amiloride, Triamterene (MRAs)
Medications to STOP (2 Weeks)ACEi, ARB, Diuretics, Beta-Blockers, CCBs, NSAIDs
Medications ALLOWEDAmlodipine, Doxazosin, Prazosin, Hydralazine, Moxonidine, Clonidine
PostureSeated ≥30 min (or Supine 30min)
Time09:00-10:00 AM (After 30min Seated)

6. Confirmatory Tests (After Positive ARR)

TestProtocolPositive Criteria
Saline Infusion Test (SIT)2L 0.9% NaCl IV over 4h → AldosteroneAldosterone >140 pmol/L (5 ng/dL) = Positive
Fludrocortisone Suppression Test (FST)Fludrocortisone 0.1mg q6h × 4d + High Salt DietAldosterone >140 pmol/L = Positive
Captopril Challenge Test (CCT)Captopril 50mg PO → Aldosterone at 0, 60, 90, 120minAldosterone >140 pmol/L or ARR >70 Post-Captopril
Oral Sodium Loading6g NaCl/day × 3d → 24h Urine Aldosterone + Na+Urine Aldo >33 nmol/day (12 µg/day) + Na+ >200 mmol/day

SIT Preferred (Simplest, Safest); FST = Gold Standard (Complex); CCT = Alternative.

7. Subtype Differentiation — Adrenal Venous Sampling (AVS)

Indications for AVS

IndicationDetails
Confirmed PAAll Patients Willing/Eligible for Surgery
Age <40Some Guidelines: AVS Optional if Clear Unilateral Lesion on CT
Age >40Mandatory (CT Alone Unreliable for Lateralisation)

AVS Procedure

StepDetails
PreparationStop MRAs 4-6wk; Continue Other BH Meds; Cosyntropin 250µg IV Bolus → 50µg/hr Infusion (Optional, Improves Selectivity)
CatheterisationBilateral Adrenal Veins + IVC (Peripheral)
SamplingAldosterone + Cortisol from Each Adrenal Vein + IVC
TimingSteady-State (After 20-30min Cosyntropin Infusion)

Interpretation — Selectivity & Lateralisation Indices

IndexFormulaSuccess Criteria
Selectivity Index (SI)Adrenal Vein Cortisol / IVC CortisolSI ≥2-3 (With Cosyntropin) ≥3-5 (Without)
Lateralisation Index (LI)High-Side Aldosterone/Cortisol ÷ Low-Side Aldosterone/CortisolLI ≥4 (With Cosyntropin); ≥2-3 (Without)
Contralateral SuppressionLow-Side Aldosterone/Cortisol ≤ Peripheral IVC Aldosterone/CortisolSupports Unilateral Disease

8. Interpretation of AVS Results

ResultInterpretationManagement
SI ≥2-3 (Both Sides) + LI ≥4Unilateral (Adenoma)Adrenalectomy (Laparoscopic)
SI ≥2-3 (Both Sides) + LI <4Bilateral (BAH / Hyperplasia)Medical Therapy (Spironolactone / Eplerenone)
SI <2-3 (One/Both Sides)Non-Selective / Failed CannulationRepeat AVS or CT-Guided Decision
Contralateral SuppressionLow-Side A/C ≤ Peripheral IVC A/CSupports Unilateral Disease

9. CT Imaging vs AVS

ModalitySensitivity for LateralisationRole
Adrenal CT50-60% (Poor)Initial Anatomy; AVS Gold Standard
AVS90-95%Gold Standard for Lateralisation

Key: CT Alone Unreliable for Lateralisation (30-40% Discordance with AVS).

10. Subtype Classification

SubtypeFrequencyPathologyTreatment
Aldosterone-Producing Adenoma (APA)~60%Unilateral Adenoma (KCNJ5, CACNA1D, ATP1A1 Mutations)Adrenalectomy (Laparoscopic)
Bilateral Adrenal Hyperplasia (BAH)~40%Bilateral Micronodular/Macronodular HyperplasiaMedical (MR Antagonist)
Unilateral HyperplasiaRareUnilateral Diffuse HyperplasiaAdrenalectomy
Adrenal Carcinoma<1%Large, Heterogeneous, InvasionRadical Adrenalectomy (+ Mitotane)
Familial HyperaldosteronismRareFH-I (GRA), FH-II (APA FHx), FH-III (KCNJ5 Germline)Per Subtype

11. Management

Unilateral Adenoma (APA) — Surgical

StepProcedure
Pre-OpSpironolactone 200-400mg/day (Control BP/K+) → Stop 4-6wk Pre-Op
SurgeryLaparoscopic Adrenalectomy (Gold Standard)
Post-OpMonitor K+ (Rebound Hyperkalaemia Common); Wean MR Antagonist
Cure Rate80-95% Normotension; 95% K+ Normalisation

Bilateral Hyperplasia (BAH) — Medical

DrugDoseNotes
Spironolactone25-100mg BD (Max 400mg/day)First-Line; Anti-Androgen (Gynaecomastia)
Eplerenone50-100mg BDMore Selective; No Gynaecomastia; Costlier
Amiloride5-20mg BDENaC Blocker; Add-On
Triamterene50-100mg BDAlternative

Amiloride / Triamterene (Add-On)

DrugDoseUse
Amiloride5-20mg BDENaC Blocker; K+-Sparing
Triamterene50-100mg BDAlternative to Spironolactone

12. Metabolic Alkalosis & Hypokalaemia Management

ProblemCorrection
HypokalaemiaKCl 40-80mmol/day Oral; IV if Severe (<3.0)
Metabolic AlkalosisCorrect K+ First; Acetazolamide (Rare)
HypertensionSpironolactone/Eplerenone + CCB/ACEi/ARB; Avoid Thiazides (Worsen K+)

13. Special Populations

Pregnancy

AspectManagement
DiagnosisARR Unreliable (Renin/Aldo Physiologically ↑); Use Saline Infusion Test (If Needed)
TreatmentMethyldopa / Labetalol / Nifedipine (Avoid MR Antagonists)
PostpartumRe-evaluate ARR at 6-12 Weeks Postpartum

Familial Hyperaldosteronism

TypeGeneFeaturesTreatment
FH-I (GRA)CYP11B1/CYP11B2 ChimericDexamethasone-Suppressible; Autosomal DominantLow-Dose Dexamethasone
FH-IIUnknown (Chromosome 7)APA-like; Autosomal DominantAdrenalectomy / MR Antagonist
FH-IIIKCNJ5 (Germline)Severe Early-Onset; Adrenal HyperplasiaAdrenalectomy

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
PA Prevalence5-10% of HTN; Up to 20% Resistant HTN
ScreeningARR (Aldosterone/Renin Ratio) — ARR >70 (pmol/mIU) or >1000 (pmol/pg)
Pre-Test ConditionsK+ >4.0, Off MRAs 4-6wk, Off ACEi/ARB/Diuretics 2wk
Positive ARR>70 (pmol/mIU) or >1000 (pmol/pg); Confirm with Confirmatory Test
Confirmatory TestsSIT (>140 pmol/L), FST, CCT — SIT Simplest
AVS IndicationConfirmed PA + Surgery Candidate; Age <40 Optional if CT Clear
AVS LateralisationLI ≥4 (Cosyntropin) = Unilateral; <4 = Bilateral
Adrenal Adenoma (APA)Surgery (Laparoscopic Adrenalectomy) → 80-95% Cure
Bilateral HyperplasiaMedical (Spironolactone/Eplerenone)
Spironolactone Dose25-100mg BD (Max 400mg); Monitor K+, Gynaecomastia
Eplerenone50-100mg BD; No Gynaecomastia; Costlier
Post-Adrenalectomy K+Rebound Hyperkalaemia Common → Monitor
FH-I (GRA)CYP11B1/B2 Chimera; Dexamethasone Suppressible
FH-IIAPA Familial; No Dexamethasone Suppression
FH-IIIKCNJ5 Germline; Severe Early-Onset; Adrenalectomy

15. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
ARR Unitspmol/L ÷ mIU/L = >70 OR pg/mL ÷ pg/mL = >1000 — Know Your Lab Units!
ARR False PositiveHypokalaemia, ACEi/ARB/Diuretics, Low Salt Diet
ARR ConfirmatoryPositive ARR ≠ PA — Must Confirm (SIT, FST, CCT)
CT vs AVSCT Unreliable for Lateralisation (30-40% Discordance) → AVS Gold Standard
APA vs BAHAPA = Unilateral Adenoma (Surgery); BAH = Bilateral Hyperplasia (Medical)
Spironolactone vs EplerenoneSpiro = Cheaper, Gynaecomastia; Eplerenone = Selective, No Gynaecomastia
Post-Adrenalectomy K+Rebound Hyperkalaemia Common → Monitor, Adjust MR Antagonist
FH-I (GRA)Dexamethasone Suppressible — CYP11B1/B2 Chimeric Gene
FH-IIFamilial APA; No Dexamethasone Suppression
FH-IIIKCNJ5 Germline; Severe Infantile; Adrenalectomy

16. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Primary Aldosteronism))

Epidemiology

5-10% HTN; 20% Resistant HTN

Female > Male (2:1); Age 30-50

Screening

ARR = Aldo (pmol/L) / Renin (mIU/L)

>70 (or >1000 pg/mL) = Positive

Pre-test: K+ >4.0; Off MRAs 4-6wk; Off ACEi/ARB/Diuretic 2wk

Confirmatory

SIT: 2L Saline 4h → Aldo >140 pmol/L

FST: Fludro 0.1mg q6h x4d + Salt → Aldo >140

CCT: Captopril 50mg → Aldo >140

AVS (Gold Standard)

Selectivity Index: Adrenal Cortisol/IVC ≥2-3

Lateralisation Index: High/Low A/C ≥4 (Cosyn) / ≥2-3 (No)

Contralateral Suppression

Subtypes

APA (60%): Unilateral Adenoma → Surgery

BAH (40%): Bilateral Hyperplasia → Medical (Spiro/Eplerenone)

FH-I (GRA): Dexamethasone Suppressible

FH-II: Familial APA

FH-III: KCNJ5 Germline (Severe Infantile)

Management

APA → Lap Adrenalectomy (Cure 80-95%)

BAH → Spiro/Eplerenone

Post-Op: Rebound HyperK+, Monitor

FH Types

FH-I: CYP11B1/B2 Chimera → Dex Supp

FH-II: Familial APA

FH-III: KCNJ5 Mut → Severe Infantile

```

17. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
PA Prevalence5-10% HTN; 20% Resistant HTN
ARR Cut-off>70 (pmol/mIU) or >1000 (pg/mL ÷ pg/mL)
Pre-Test StopMRAs 4-6wk; ACEi/ARB/Diuretics 2wk; K+ >4.0
ConfirmatorySIT: Aldo >140 pmol/L post-2L Saline; FST Gold Standard
AVS IndicationConfirmed PA + Surgery Candidate; Age <40 Optional if CT Clear
AVS LateralisationLI ≥4 (Cosyn) / ≥2-3 (No Cosyn) = Unilateral
APA vs BAHAPA = Surgery (Curative); BAH = Medical (Spiro/Eplerenone)
Post-Adrenalectomy K+Rebound Hyperkalaemia Common
FH-I (GRA)Dexamethasone Suppressible (CYP11B1/B2 Chimera)
FH-IIFamilial APA; No Dex Suppression
FH-IIIKCNJ5 Germline; Severe Infantile; Adrenalectomy
Spironolactone25-100mg BD (Max 400mg); Gynaecomastia Risk
Eplerenone50-100mg BD; No Gynaecomastia; Costlier

topics.title: Fhh Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia
topics.html:
[!info]
Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia (FHH) = Autosomal Dominant Disorder due to Inactivating CaSR Mutations. Benign, Asymptomatic Hypercalcaemia with Relative Hypocalciuria. Mimics Primary Hyperparathyroidism but No Surgery Needed. CaSR Loss-of-Function → Right-Shifted Calcium Set-Point.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate FHH from Primary Hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) using urinary calcium excretion
  • [ ] Apply the Calcium/Creatinine clearance ratio (Ca/Cr <0.01) as key discriminator
  • [ ] Recognise genetic basis (CaSR inactivating mutations, Autosomal Dominant)
  • [ ] Avoid unnecessary parathyroid surgery in FHH
  • [ ] Recognise neonatal severe hyperparathyroidism (NSHPT) as homozygous/compound heterozygous form

2. Pathophysiology & Genetics

FeatureDetails
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant (Penetrance ~100%)
GeneCaSR (Calcium-Sensing Receptor) — Chromosome 3q13-q21
Mutation TypeInactivating (Loss-of-Function) — Heterozygous
MechanismRight-Shifted Calcium Set-Point → Higher Serum Calcium Needed to Suppress PTH
PenetranceHigh (>90%)
Homozygous/Compound HeterozygousNeonatal Severe Hyperparathyroidism (NSHPT) — Severe, Life-Threatening

3. Pathophysiology

Normal PhysiologyFHH (CaSR Mutation)
CaSR Activation by Ca²⁺ → ↓ PTH SecretionImpaired Ca²⁺ Sensing → Right-Shifted Set-Point
Normal SET-POINT (~1.1-1.3 mmol/L Ionised Ca²⁺)Right-Shifted SET-POINT (~1.3-1.5 mmol/L Ionised Ca²⁺)
Normal Urinary Ca²⁺ Excretion (Fractional Excretion ~1-2%)Reduced Renal Ca²⁺ Excretion (FE_Ca <1%)

Key Physiological Consequences

ParameterNormalFHH
Serum CalciumNormalMildly Elevated (2.7-3.0 mmol/L)
PTHNormalInappropriately Normal/Mildly Elevated
Urinary Calcium ExcretionNormal (FE_Ca 1-2%)Markedly Reduced (FE_Ca <1%)
PTH LevelNormalInappropriately Normal/Mildly Elevated
PhosphateNormalNormal/Mildly Low
MagnesiumNormalMildly Elevated (Relative Hypomagnesiuria)

4. Diagnostic Criteria — FHH vs PHPT

The Gold Standard Discriminator: Urinary Calcium Excretion

TestPHPTFHH
24h Urinary Calcium>7.5 mmol/24h (300 mg/day)<2.5 mmol/24h (<100 mg/day)
Fasting Urine Calcium/Creatinine Ratio (Ca/Cr)>0.02 (mmol/mmol)<0.01 (mmol/mmol)
Calcium/Creatinine Clearance Ratio (CCCR)>0.02<0.01
CCCR = (Urine Ca × Plasma Creatinine) / (Plasma Ca × Urine Creatinine)
CCCR <0.01 = FHH; CCCR >0.02 = PHPT

Key Biochemical Differences

ParameterPHPTFHH
Serum CalciumHigh (2.8-3.5 mmol/L)Mildly High (2.7-3.0 mmol/L)
PTHHigh / Inappropriately NormalNormal / Mildly Elevated
24h Urine CalciumHigh (>7.5 mmol/24h)Low (<2.5 mmol/24h)
Urine Ca/Cr Ratio>0.02<0.01
CCCR>0.02<0.01
PhosphateLowNormal / Mildly Low
MagnesiumNormal/LowMildly Elevated
Family HistoryUsually SporadicStrong (AD, Multiple Affected)

5. Diagnostic Algorithm

```

Hypercalcaemia + Inappropriately Normal/High PTH

│

▼

24h Urine Calcium OR Fasting Ca/Cr Ratio

│

├── Ca/Cr Ratio <0.01 (mmol/mmol) OR 24h Urine Ca <2.5 mmol/24h

│ → FHH LIKELY → Genetic Testing (CaSR) → NO SURGERY

│

└── Ca/Cr Ratio >0.02 OR 24h Urine Ca >7.5 mmol/24h

→ PHPT LIKELY → Localisation → Surgery

```

6. Genetic Testing

GeneMutation TypeInheritancePhenotype
CaSR (Chromosome 3q13)Inactivating (Loss-of-Function)Autosomal DominantFHH Type 1 (Typical)
GNA11InactivatingADFHH Type 2 (Rare)
AP2S1 (AP2σ)InactivatingADFHH Type 3 (Rare)
GenotypePhenotype
Heterozygous (1 Mutant Allele)FHH Type 1 (Typical, Benign)
Homozygous / Compound HeterozygousNeonatal Severe Hyperparathyroidism (NSHPT) — Lethal if Untreated

7. NSHPT (Neonatal Severe Hyperparathyroidism)

FeatureDetails
GenotypeHomozygous / Compound Heterozygous CaSR Mutations
PresentationSevere Hypercalcaemia (Ca²⁺ >3.5 mmol/L) in Neonate
ComplicationsSevere Hypercalcaemia → Dehydration, Renal Failure, Pancreatitis, Demineralisation, Fractures
PTHVery High (>1000 pg/mL)
TreatmentUrgent Parathyroidectomy (Subtotal/Total) — Life-Saving
GeneticsAutosomal Recessive (Homozygous/Compound Heterozygous)

8. Differential Diagnosis: FHH vs PHPT

ParameterPHPTFHH
Serum CalciumHigh (2.8-3.5 mmol/L)Mildly High (2.7-3.0 mmol/L)
PTHHigh / Inappropriately NormalNormal / Mildly Elevated
24h Urine Calcium>7.5 mmol/24h (300 mg/day)<2.5 mmol/24h (<100 mg/day)
Ca/Cr Ratio (Fasting)>0.02<0.01
CCCR>0.02<0.01
PhosphateLowNormal / Slightly Low
MagnesiumNormal/LowMildly Elevated
Family HistoryRareStrong (AD, Multiple Affected)
Surgical IndicationYES (Curative)NO (Benign)
GeneticsSporadic (Usually)CaSR Mutation (AD)

9. Diagnostic Algorithm

```

Hypercalcaemia + Inappropriately Normal/High PTH

│

▼

Fasting Urine Calcium/Creatinine Ratio (or 24h Urine Ca)

│

├── Ca/Cr <0.01 OR 24h Urine Ca <2.5 mmol/24h

│ → FHH LIKELY

│ → Genetic Testing (CaSR) → Confirm

│ → NO SURGERY (Benign Condition)

│

└── Ca/Cr >0.02 OR 24h Urine Ca >7.5 mmol/24h

→ PHPT LIKELY

→ Localisation → Surgery

```

10. Special Variants

FHH Type 2 (GNA11 Mutation)

FeatureDetails
GeneGNA11 (G-Protein α11 Subunit)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant
PhenotypeSimilar to FHH Type 1 (CaSR)

FHH Type 3 (AP2S1 Mutation)

FeatureDetails
GeneAP2S1 (Adaptor Protein 2 Sigma Subunit)
PhenotypeMore Severe Hypercalcaemia; Earlier Onset

11. Management & Counselling

AspectFHH Management
SurgeryContraindicated (No Benefit; Benign Condition)
MonitoringAnnual Serum Calcium, PTH, Renal Function
Genetic CounsellingAutosomal Dominant — 50% Offspring Risk; Offer Predictive Testing
PregnancyNormal Course; Mild Hypercalcaemia Usually Well-Tolerated
Hypercalcaemic CrisisRare; Treat as Primary Hyperparathyroidism if Severe

Family Screening

RelativeAction
First-Degree RelativesScreen Serum Calcium + PTH
Genetic TestingOffer CaSR Sequencing (If Mutation Known in Proband)
PrenatalOffer CVS/Amniocentesis if Known Familial Mutation

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
FHH vs PHPT DiscriminatorUrine Calcium/Creatinine Ratio <0.01 (FHH) vs >0.02 (PHPT)
FHH HallmarkHypocalciuria (Urine Ca <2.5 mmol/24h; Ca/Cr <0.01)
PTH in FHHNormal or Mildly Elevated (Inappropriately Normal for Calcium)
Serum CalciumMildly Elevated (2.7-3.0 mmol/L)
PhosphateNormal / Mildly Low
MagnesiumMildly Elevated (Relative Hypomagnesiuria)
GeneticsCaSR Inactivating Mutation (AD); Heterozygous = FHH; Homozygous = NSHPT
SurgeryContraindicated (Benign; No Benefit)
NSHPTHomozygous CaSR Mutation; Severe Neonatal Hypercalcaemia; Emergency PTX
Genetic CounsellingAD Inheritance → 50% Offspring Risk; Offer Predictive Testing
PregnancyUsually Uneventful; Monitor Calcium

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
FHH vs PHPTUrine Ca/Cr <0.01 = FHH; >0.02 = PHPT — Gold Standard Discriminator
FHH vs PHPT CalciumFHH: Mild (2.7-3.0); PHPT: Higher (2.8-3.5)
FHH vs PHPT PTHFHH: Normal/Mild ↑; PHPT: High/Inappropriately Normal
Calcium ExcretionFHH: Low (FE_Ca <1%); PHPT: High (FE_Ca >2%)
CCCR Formula(Urine Ca × Plasma Creatinine) / (Plasma Ca × Urine Creatinine)
CCCR Cut-off<0.01 = FHH; >0.02 = PHPT
Family HistoryFHH = Strong AD Family History; PHPT = Usually Sporadic
NSHPTHomozygous CaSR Mutation; Severe Neonatal Hypercalcaemia; Emergency PTX

Mnemonic: FHH vs PHPT — "CALCIUM EXCRETION"

  • FHH = Calcium Low Creatinine Ratio (<0.01)
  • PHPT = Calcium High Creatinine Ratio (>0.02)

Mnemonic: FHH FEATURES

  • Family History (AD)
  • Hypocalciuria (Ca/Cr <0.01)
  • Hypercalcaemia Mild

Mnemonic: FHH vs PHPT — "FHH = Low Urine Calcium"

  • PHPT = Prolific Hypercalciuria (>7.5 mmol/24h)
  • FHH = Frugal Hypocalciuria (<2.5 mmol/24h)

14. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia))

Genetics

CaSR Inactivating Mutation (AD)

GNA11, AP2S1 (Rare Types 2,3)

Heterozygous = FHH (Benign)

Homozygous = NSHPT (Lethal)

Pathophysiology

Right-Shifted CaSR Set-Point

Higher Ca Threshold for PTH Suppression

↓ Renal Ca Excretion (FE_Ca <1%)

Diagnostics

Ca/Cr <0.01 (mmol/mmol)

24h Urine Ca <2.5 mmol

CCCR <0.01

PTH Normal/Mildly ↑

Differential

PHPT: Ca/Cr >0.02, Urine Ca >7.5mmol/24h

FHH: Ca/Cr <0.01, Urine Ca <2.5mmol/24h

Management

NO Surgery (Benign)

Genetic Counselling (AD 50%)

Annual Monitoring

NSHPT

Homozygous CaSR Mutation

Severe Neonatal Hypercalcaemia

Emergency Parathyroidectomy

```

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point

FHH HallmarkHypocalciuria (Urine Ca <2.5 mmol/24h; Ca/Cr <0.01)
PTH in FHHNormal / Mildly Elevated (Inappropriately Normal for Calcium)
Serum CalciumMildly Elevated (2.7-3.0 mmol/L)
PhosphateNormal / Mildly Low
MagnesiumMildly Elevated (Relative Hypomagnesiuria)
GeneticsCaSR Inactivating Mutation (AD); Heterozygous = FHH; Homozygous = NSHPT
SurgeryContraindicated (Benign Condition; No Benefit)

Genetic CounsellingAD Inheritance → 50% Offspring Risk

topics.title: Hypercalcaemia Of Malignancy
topics.html:
[!info]
Hypercalcaemia of Malignancy (HCM) = Most Common Cause of Hypercalcaemia in Hospitalised Patients. PTH Suppressed (vs Primary HPT where PTH High). PTHrP-Mediated (80%) Most Common; Osteolytic (20%); Vitamin D-Mediated (Rare). Medical Emergency — Requires Urgent Hydration + Bisphosphonates/Denosumab.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate hypercalcaemia of malignancy from primary hyperparathyroidism
  • [ ] Classify mechanisms (PTHrP, Osteolytic, 1,25-(OH)₂D-Mediated)
  • [ ] Apply emergency management (Hydration → Bisphosphonates/Denosumab)
  • [ ] Recognise tumour types associated with each mechanism
  • [ ] Apply monitoring and long-term bone protection

2. Epidemiology & Aetiology

StatisticValue
Incidence20-30% of Cancer Patients Develop Hypercalcaemia
Commonest CauseHCM = #1 Cause of Hypercalcaemia in Hospitalised Patients
Mortality50% at 30 Days (Reflects Advanced Disease)
Median Survival30-60 Days After Hypercalcaemia Onset
Commonest MechanismPTHrP-Mediated (80%)

3. Mechanisms of Hypercalcaemia of Malignancy

MechanismFrequencyPathophysiologyTypical Tumours
1. PTHrP (Humoral)80%PTHrP (PTH-related Protein) → Binds PTH1R → ↑ Bone Resorption, ↑ Renal Ca²⁺ Reabsorption, ↑ Phosphate ExcretionSCLC, Squamous Cell (Lung, H&N, Cervix), Renal Cell, Breast, Ovarian, Bladder, Pancreatic
2. Osteolytic (Local Osteolysis)20%Local Cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, RANKL) → Local Osteoclast ActivationBreast, Myeloma, Prostate, Lung (Adenocarcinoma), Thyroid
3. 1,25-(OH)₂D-Mediated (Vitamin D)<5%Ectopic 1α-Hydroxylase → ↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D → ↑ Intestinal Ca²⁺ AbsorptionLymphoma (Hodgkin/NHL), Granulomatous (Sarcoid, TB)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetails
OnsetAcute/Subacute (Days-Weeks) vs Chronic in Primary HPT
SeverityOften Severe (Ca²⁺ >3.0 mmol/L; Often >3.5)
SymptomsAcute: Nausea, Vomiting, Confusion, Polyuria, Polydipsia, Constipation, Lethargy, Coma
Underlying CancerOften Known (Metastatic); Occasionally Paraneoplastic Presenting Feature
RenalPolyuria (Nephrogenic DI), Dehydration, AKI (Pre-renal + Tubular Toxicity)
CardiacShortened QT, Arrhythmias, Bradycardia, Heart Block
NeurologicalConfusion, Lethargy, Coma, Seizures

5. Biochemical Differentiation — HCM vs Primary HPT

ParameterHypercalcaemia of MalignancyPrimary Hyperparathyroidism
PTH (Intact)Suppressed (<2 pmol/L; Often <1 pmol/L)Elevated / Inappropriately Normal
PTHrPElevated (80% of Cases)Negative
CalciumOften >3.0 mmol/L (Severe)Mild-Moderate (2.7-3.0)
PhosphateLow (PTHrP → Phosphaturia)Low/Normal
Alkaline PhosphataseOften High (Bone Turnover)Normal/Mildly Elevated
25-OH Vitamin DNormal/LowLow/Normal
1,25-(OH)₂DLow/NormalNormal/High
PTHrPPositive (80%)Negative

6. PTHrP — Key Mediator (Humoral Hypercalcaemia of Malignancy - HHM)

FeatureDetails
StructurePTH-related Protein (139-173 AA); N-Terminal Homology with PTH (1-13 AA)
ReceptorPTH1R (Same as PTH Receptor) — Binds N-Terminus
ActionsIdentical to PTH: ↑ Bone Resorption, ↑ Renal Ca²⁺ Reabsorption, ↑ Phosphate Excretion
AssayPTHrP Assay (Immunoradiometric/ELISA) — Mid-Region / C-Terminal
SpecificityMid-Region PTHrP (Cross-Reacts Less); C-Terminal Less Specific

7. Acute Management (Medical Emergency)

Step 1: IV Hydration (First-Line)

InterventionDetails
Fluid0.9% NaCl 1-2L over 1-2h (Then 200-300 mL/hr)
GoalUrine Output >150 mL/hr (Flush Calcium + Improve GFR)
TargetEuvolaemia → CVP 8-12 cm H₂O
CautionCardiac Failure → Caution (Monitor JVP, CVP, Lungs)

Step 2: Loop Diuretic (After Hydration)

DrugDoseCaution
Furosemide20-40mg IV (After Hydration)Only After Adequate Hydration; Monitor K⁺, Mg²⁺

Step 3: Bisphosphonates (First-Line Anti-Resorptive)

DrugDoseAdministrationOnsetDuration
Zoledronic Acid4mg IV (15min Infusion)First-Line (Potent, Long-Lasting)24-48h3-4 Weeks
Pamidronate60-90mg IV (2-4h Infusion)Alternative24-48h2-3 Weeks
Ibandronate4-6mg IVAlternative24-48h2-4 Weeks

Renal Impairment Adjustments

eGFRZoledronic AcidPamidronate
≥604mg (Standard)90mg
30-594mg (Caution)60mg
<30Avoid / 3mg (Caution)30mg (Caution)

8. Denosumab (Alternative/Refractory)

FeatureDetails
MechanismAnti-RANKL Monoclonal Antibody → Inhibits Osteoclast Formation/Activity
Dose120mg SC (Monthly)
IndicationRefractory to Bisphosphonates / Renal Impairment (eGFR <30)
AdvantageNo Renal Adjustment Needed; Faster Onset; Longer Duration
MonitoringHypocalcaemia Risk (Supplement Ca²⁺/Vit D)
Duration~4 Weeks (Monthly Dosing)

Comparison: Bisphosphonate vs Denosumab

FeatureZoledronic AcidDenosumab
Renal ImpairmentDose Adjust/Avoid (eGFR <30)No Adjustment Needed
Onset24-48hFaster (24h)
Duration3-4 Weeks4 Weeks
Hypocalcaemia RiskModerateHigher (Monitor Closely)
Osteonecrosis JawRareRare (Similar)
Renal FailureAvoid/AdjustPreferred

9. Other Therapies

AgentIndicationDose
CalcitoninRapid Onset (2-4h); Short Duration (2-3 Days)4-8 IU/kg IM/SC q6-12h
Gallium NitrateRefractory100-200mg/m²/day IV (5d)
Plicamycin (Mithramycin)Historical/Refractory25µg/kg/day IV (4-7 Days)
DenosumabRefractory / Renal Impairment120mg SC Monthly
SteroidsHaematological Malignancy (Lymphoma, Myeloma)Prednisolone 40-60mg/day
DialysisSevere Renal Failure + Life-Threatening HypercalcaemiaEmergency HD

10. Acute Hypercalcaemia — Emergency Protocol

```

Hypercalcaemia (Ca²⁺ >3.0 mmol/L or Symptomatic)

│

▼

  1. IV 0.9% NaCl 1-2L STAT (Over 1-2h) → Target UOP >150 mL/hr

│

▼

  1. Loop Diuretic (AFTER Hydration) → Furosemide 20-40mg IV

│

▼

  1. BISPHOSPHONATE: Zoledronic Acid 4mg IV (15min Infusion)

│

▼

  1. IF REFRACTORY / RENAL IMPAIRMENT → Denosumab 120mg SC

│

▼

  1. SUPPORTIVE
  • Hydration Maintenance (2-3L/day)
  • Monitor Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺, Phosphate, Renal Function q6-12h
  • Cardiac Monitoring (Shortened QT, Arrhythmias)
  • Treat Underlying Malignancy

```

11. Monitoring & Follow-up

ParameterFrequencyTarget
Serum Calcium (Corrected)q6-12h (Acute); Daily (Subacute)<2.6 mmol/L
Ionised Calciumq6-12h (Acute)<1.3 mmol/L
PhosphateDaily (Hypophosphataemia Risk)>0.8 mmol/L
MagnesiumDaily>0.7 mmol/L
Renal Functionq6-12hMonitor AKI
PTHrPBaseline; Post-TreatmentMonitor Response
Urine OutputHourly (Acute)>100-150 mL/hr

12. Long-Term Bone Protection (After Acute Control)

AgentIndicationDose
Zoledronic AcidBone Metastases / Myeloma4mg IV q3-4mo
DenosumabBone Metastases (Solid Tumours) / Myeloma120mg SC Monthly
PamidronateAlternative90mg IV q4-6wk

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Commonest Cause Hospital HypercalcaemiaMalignancy (20-30% Cancer Patients)
Mechanism 80%PTHrP (Humoral) — Binds PTH1R → Mimics PTH
PTH in HCMSuppressed (<1 pmol/L) — Differentiates from Primary HPT
PTHrPPTH-related Protein; N-Terminal Homology with PTH; Binds PTH1R
Osteolytic (20%)Local Cytokines (IL-6, TNF, RANKL) → Local Bone Destruction (Myeloma, Breast)
Vitamin D MediatedLymphoma, Sarcoid, TB → Ectopic 1α-Hydroxylase → ↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D
Emergency RxHydration → Zoledronic Acid 4mg IV (Denosumab if Refractory/Renal Fail)
Zoledronic Acid Dose4mg IV (15min) — Adjust for Renal Impairment
Denosumab120mg SC Monthly; Preferred if eGFR <30; No Renal Adjustment
CalcitoninRapid Onset (2-4h); Short Duration (2-3d); Adjunct Only
Differentiation PHPT vs HCMPHPT: PTH High; HCM: PTH Suppressed + PTHrP Positive
Bisphosphonate Renal DoseZoledronic: eGFR≥60: 4mg; 30-59: 4mg; <30: Avoid/3mg
Hydration FirstAlways Hydrate First Before Bisphosphonate/Diuretic
Malignancy TypesSCLC, SQCC, Breast, Renal, Myeloma, Lymphoma (Vit D)
PTHrP AssayMid-Region Preferred; Confirms HHM
Bisphosphonate OnsetZoledronic 24-48h; Pamidronate 24-48h
Hypercalcaemia EmergencyHydration → Bisphosphonate → Denosumab (Refractory/Renal)

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HCM vs Primary HPTHCM: PTH Suppressed, PTHrP+; Primary HPT: PTH High
Osteolytic vs HumoralHumoral = PTHrP (Systemic); Osteolytic = Local Cytokines (Local Bone Destruction)
Vitamin D MediatedLymphoma, Sarcoid, TB → 1α-Hydroxylase → ↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D
Zoledronic vs DenosumabDenosumab: No Renal Adjustment; Faster; Preferred in Renal Failure
PTHrP vs PTHPTHrP Shares N-Terminal Homology with PTH (1-13 AA); Binds Same Receptor (PTH1R)
CalcitoninRapid but Weak/Transient; Adjunct Only
Osteolytic HCMMyeloma, Breast, Prostate → Local Bone Destruction (RANKL/IL-6)
1,25-(OH)₂D MediatedLymphoma, Sarcoidosis, TB → Ectopic 1α-Hydroxylase

Mnemonic: HYPERCALCAEMIA OF MALIGNANCY

  • Hypercalcaemia Malignancy → PTH Suppressed
  • Ectopic PTHrP (80%) — Humoral
  • Osteolytic (20%) — Local Cytokines
  • Vitamin D Mediated (Lymphoma) — Ectopic 1α-OHase

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Commonest Cause Hospital HypercalcaemiaMalignancy (20-30% of Hospitalised Cancer Patients)
Mechanism 80%PTHrP (Humoral) — Binds PTH1R → Mimics PTH
PTH in MalignancySuppressed (<1 pmol/L)
PTHrPPTH-related Protein; N-Terminal Homology with PTH; Binds PTH1R
Osteolytic HCMLocal Bone Destruction (Myeloma, Breast, Prostate) → Cytokines (RANKL, IL-6)
Vitamin D MediatedLymphoma, Sarcoidosis, TB → 1α-Hydroxylase → ↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D
Emergency TreatmentHydration → Zoledronic Acid 4mg IV (Denosumab if Refractory/Renal)
Zoledronic Acid4mg IV 15min; Renal Adjust (eGFR<30: Avoid/3mg)
Denosumab120mg SC Monthly; No Renal Adjustment; Preferred if eGFR<30
CalcitoninRapid (2-4h) but Weak/Short; Adjunct Only
PTH in HCMSuppressed (<1 pmol/L) — Key Differentiator from PHPT
PTHrPBinds PTH1R; 1-13 AA Homology with PTH
Bisphosphonate Renal AdjustZoledronic: eGFR<30 Avoid/3mg; Pamidronate eGFR<30: 30mg
Hydration FirstALWAYS Hydrate Before Bisphosphonate/Diuretic
Malignancy TypesSCLC, SCC, Breast, Renal, Myeloma, Lymphoma

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

17. MCQs (10)

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 60yo man: metastatic squamous lung Ca, Ca 3.5, PTH <5, PTHrP high. Treatment?

  1. Same patient: Ca 3.8, renal impairment eGFR 25. Bisphosphonate?

  1. Myeloma: Ca 3.2, lytic lesions, PTH suppressed. Mechanism?

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Malignancy hyperCa mechanisms

A: PTHrP (80% humoral); osteolytic mets (local cytokines); Vit D-mediated (lymphoma/sarcoid)

20. Answer Key with Explanations

topics.title: Hypocalcaemia
topics.html:
[!info]
Hypocalcaemia = Serum Corrected Calcium <2.15 mmol/L (<8.6 mg/dL) or Ionised Calcium <1.1 mmol/L. Common Electrolyte Emergency. Causes: Hypoparathyroidism, Vitamin D Deficiency, Renal Failure, Acute Pancreatitis, Massive Transfusion, Drugs. Acute Severe = Tetany/Seizures → IV Calcium Gluconate STAT.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define hypocalcaemia and classify by severity/acuity
  • [ ] Differentiate true hypocalcaemia from factitious (albumin effect)
  • [ ] Apply differential diagnosis algorithm (PTH, Vitamin D, Phosphate, Magnesium)
  • [ ] Execute acute management of hypocalcaemic tetany (IV Calcium Gluconate)
  • [ ] Prescribe chronic replacement therapy (Calcium + Active Vitamin D)
  • [ ] Recognise special causes (Hungry Bone Syndrome, Massive Transfusion, Pancreatitis)

2. Definitions & Diagnostic Criteria

ParameterNormal RangeHypocalcaemia Threshold
Corrected Serum Calcium2.15-2.55 mmol/L (8.6-10.2 mg/dL)<2.15 mmol/L (<8.6 mg/dL)
Ionised Calcium1.15-1.30 mmol/L (4.5-5.2 mg/dL)<1.10 mmol/L (<4.4 mg/dL)
Correction Formula: Corrected Ca = Measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin g/L) [or ×0.8 per 1g/dL Albumin]

Severity Classification

SeverityCorrected Ca (mmol/L)Ionised Ca (mmol/L)Clinical Features
Mild1.9-2.151.0-1.1Asymptomatic / Mild Paraesthesia
Moderate1.5-1.90.8-1.0Paraesthesia, Tetany (Trousseau/Chvostek), Muscle Cramps
Severe<1.5<0.8Tetany (Carpopedal Spasm, Laryngospasm), Seizures, QT Prolongation, Arrhythmias

3. Factitious vs True Hypocalcaemia

ScenarioTotal CalciumAlbuminIonised CalciumInterpretation
True HypocalcaemiaLowNormal/LowLowTrue Hypocalcaemia
FactitiousLowLow (Hypoalbuminaemia)NormalFactitious — Correct Calcium Formula
Transfusion/CitrateLowNormalLowCitrate Chelation (Massive Transfusion)
EDTA ContaminationLow (Spurious)NormalNormalLab Artefact

Corrected Calcium Formula:

  • mmol/L: Corrected Ca = Measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin g/L)
  • mg/dL: Corrected Ca = Measured Ca + 0.8 × (4.0 - Albumin g/dL)

4. Aetiological Classification

A. Hypoparathyroidism (Low PTH)

CauseMechanism
Post-Surgical (Thyroidectomy/Parathyroidectomy)Commonest (75%) — Transient vs Permanent
AutoimmuneIsolated / APS-1 (AIRE Mutation)
GeneticDiGeorge (22q11.2), CASR Activating Mutation, GCM2, PTH Gene
InfiltrativeHaemochromatosis, Wilson's, Metastases, Sarcoidosis
RadiationNeck Irradiation, Radioiodine Ablation

B. Vitamin D Deficiency / Resistance

CauseMechanism
Nutritional DeficiencyInadequate Intake/Sunlight; Malabsorption
Renal Failure↓ 1α-Hydroxylase → ↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D
Hepatic Failure↓ 25-Hydroxylation
Drug-InducedPhenytoin, Carbamazepine, Rifampicin, Isoniazid (Enzyme Induction)
Vitamin D Resistance (VDDR)VDR Mutation (Type II) / 1α-Hydroxylase Defect (Type I)

C. Renal Failure

MechanismDetails
CKD↓ 1α-Hydroxylase → ↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D; Hyperphosphataemia; ↓ Ca²⁺ Absorption
DialysisAluminium-Related Bone Disease (Historical); Hungry Bone Post-Tx

D. Acute Pancreatitis

MechanismDetails
SaponificationFree Fatty Acids + Ca²⁺ → Calcium Soaps (Precipitate)
Lipase↑ Vascular Permeability → Calcium Leak into Peritoneal Fluid
MagnesiumOften Co-existing Hypomagnesaemia

E. Acute Critical Illness / Massive Transfusion

CauseMechanism
Massive TransfusionCitrate Chelates Ca²⁺ (1 Unit Blood = 3g Citrate ≈ 45 mmol Ca²⁺)
Sepsis/Septic ShockVasodilatation, Capillary Leak, Citrated Blood Products
Acute PancreatitisSaponification + Lipase-Mediated Vascular Leak

E. Drug-Induced

DrugMechanism
Loop Diuretics↑ Ca²⁺ Excretion
Cisplatin/CarboplatinRenal Tubular Toxicity → Ca²⁺ Wasting
CisplatinHypomagnesaemia → Functional Hypoparathyroidism
Bisphosphonates/DenosumabOsteoclast Inhibition → Hypocalcaemia (Post-Dose)
Cisplatin/CisplatinRenal Tubular Ca²⁺ Wasting
CinacalcetCaSR Agonist → ↓ PTH → Hypocalcaemia
Bisphosphonates (IV)Acute Transient Hypocalcaemia (24-72h Post-Dose)

F. Other

CauseMechanism
Hungry Bone SyndromePost-Parathyroidectomy/Thyroidectomy → Rapid Bone Mineralisation
Osteoblastic MetastasesProstate, Breast → "Calcium Sink"
RhabdomyolysisCalcium Sequestration in Damaged Muscle
Tumour Lysis SyndromePhosphate Release → Ca²⁺ Precipitation

5. Clinical Presentation

Acute Hypocalcaemic Tetany (Emergency)

Sign/SymptomDescription
Perioral ParaesthesiaNumbness/Tingling Lips, Fingers, Toes
Carpopedal SpasmTrousseau's Sign (BP Cuff Inflated >SBP → Carpal Spasm)
Chvostek's SignFacial Twitch on Facial Nerve Tap (Anterior to Ear)
LaryngospasmStridor, Airway Obstruction — Emergency
Generalised SeizuresTonic-Clonic
CardiacProlonged QT → Torsades de Pointes, VF
NeuropsychiatricAnxiety, Confusion, Memory Impairment

Chronic Hypocalcaemia

FeatureDetails
Basal Ganglia CalcificationCT Brain (Fahr's Syndrome)
CataractsPosterior Subcapsular
DentalEnamel Hypoplasia, Caries
NeuropsychiatricDepression, Anxiety, Dementia, Parkinsonism
Skin/Hair/NailsDry Skin, Coarse Hair, Brittle Nails
CataractsPosterior Subcapsular

6. Aetiological Diagnosis — Algorithm

```

Hypocalcaemia (Corrected Ca <2.15 / Ionised Ca <1.10)

│

▼

MEASURE: PTH, PHOSPHATE, MAGNESIUM, ALBUMIN, CREATININE, 25-OH VIT D

│

├── PTH LOW / UNDETECTABLE → HYPOPARATHYROIDISM

│ ├── Post-Surgical → Transient vs Permanent

│ ├── Autoimmune (APS-1) → Screen Adrenal/Thyroid

│ ├── Genetic (DiGeorge, CASR Activating, GCM2)

│ └── Mg Deficiency → Functional Hypopara

│

├── PTH HIGH → VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY / RENAL FAILURE / PSEUDOHYPOPARATHYROIDISM

│ ├── PHOSPHATE LOW + 25-OH D LOW → VIT D DEFICIENCY

│ ├── PHOSPHATE HIGH + CREATININE HIGH → RENAL FAILURE

│ └── PTH HIGH + CALCIUM LOW + PHOSPHATE HIGH → PSEUDOHYPOPARATHYROIDISM

│

└── PTH NORMAL/LOW + Mg LOW → MAGNESIUM DEFICIENCY

(Functional Hypoparathyroidism)

```

7. Specific Aetiologies — Key Features

CauseCalciumPhosphatePTHKey Feature
HypoparathyroidismLowHighLow/UndetectablePost-Surgical, Autoimmune, Genetic
Vitamin D DeficiencyLowLowHighLow 25-OH D; High ALP
Renal FailureLowHighHigh (Secondary)High Creatinine
PseudohypoparathyroidismLowHighHighPTH Resistance; PHP1a = AHO
Vitamin D DeficiencyLowLow/NormalHighLow 25-OH D; High ALP
Acute PancreatitisLowNormal/HighNormal/HighHigh Amylase/Lipase; Saponification
Massive TransfusionLowNormal/HighNormalCitrate Chelation
RhabdomyolysisLowHighNormalHigh CK, Myoglobinuria
Tumour LysisLowVery HighNormalHyperphosphataemia Dominant
Mg DeficiencyLowLow/NormalInappropriately LowCorrect Mg First

8. Acute Management — Hypocalcaemic Tetany (Emergency)

Immediate IV Calcium (Life-Saving)

DrugDoseAdministration
10% Calcium Gluconate10-20 mL (1-2g) IVSlow IV Over 10 Min (Cardiac Monitor)
RepeatIf Persistent TetanyEvery 10-20 Min Until Controlled

Follow-Up Infusion

InfusionRate
10% Calcium Gluconate1-2 g/hr (10-20 mL/hr) in 5% Dextrose / 0.9% Saline
TargetIonised Ca²⁺ >1.1 mmol/L; Symptom Relief

Adjuncts

DrugDoseIndication
Magnesium Sulphate2-4g IV (If Hypomagnesaemia)Mg <0.7 mmol/L
Vitamin D (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol)1-2 µg IV/POAccelerate Ca²⁺ Absorption
Oral Calcium1-2g TDSAfter IV Stabilisation

9. Chronic Management

Calcium Supplementation

PreparationElemental Ca²⁺Dose
Calcium Carbonate40% (400mg/tablet)1-2g TDS with Meals
Calcium Citrate21%1-2g TDS (Better if Achlorhydria)
TargetTotal Elemental Ca²⁺ 1.5-2g/dayDivided Doses

Active Vitamin D Analogues (Essential for Absorption)

AgentDoseAdvantage
Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)0.25-1 µg/dayRapid Onset; No Renal Activation Needed
Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃)0.25-0.5 µg/dayActive Form; Short Half-Life

topics.title: Hypocalcaemic Tetany
topics.html:
[!info]
Hypocalcaemic Tetany = Acute Neuromuscular Excitability due to Ionised Hypocalcaemia. Medical Emergency Requiring Immediate IV Calcium Gluconate. Key Differential: Hypoparathyroidism, Vitamin D Deficiency, Alkalosis, Magnesium Deficiency.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical features of hypocalcaemic tetany (Trousseau's, Chvostek's signs)
  • [ ] Execute immediate IV calcium gluconate administration
  • [ ] Differentiate causes (hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, alkalosis, magnesium deficiency)
  • [ ] Apply chronic replacement strategies (calcium + active vitamin D analogues)
  • [ ] Identify special causes (hungry bone syndrome, massive transfusion, pancreatitis)

2. Definitions & Diagnostic Criteria

ParameterNormalHypocalcaemiaSevere (Tetany)
Corrected Serum Calcium2.15-2.55 mmol/L (8.6-10.2 mg/dL)<2.15 mmol/L (<8.6 mg/dL)<1.75 mmol/L (<7.0 mg/dL)
Ionised Calcium1.15-1.30 mmol/L (4.5-5.2 mg/dL)<1.10 mmol/L<1.0 mmol/L
Correction Formula: Corrected Ca = Measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin g/L) [mmol/L]

3. Aetiology & Differential Diagnosis

CategoryCauseKey Features
HypoparathyroidismPost-Thyroidectomy (75%), Autoimmune (APS-1), DiGeorge (22q11.2), Mg DeficiencyLow PTH, High Phosphate
Vitamin D DeficiencyHigh PTH (Secondary Hyperparathyroidism)Low 25-OH D, High ALP, Low Phosphate
Renal FailureHigh Phosphate, High PTH, Low 1,25-(OH)₂Duraemia
Acute PancreatitisSaponification (Ca²⁺ + FFA → Calcium Soaps)High Amylase/Lipase, Abdominal Pain
AlkalosisRespiratory (Hyperventilation) / Metabolic (Vomiting)↑ Protein Binding → ↓ Ionised Ca²⁺
Massive TransfusionCitrate Chelation (1 Unit = ~3g Citrate = 45 mmol Ca²⁺)Ionised Ca²⁺ Drop
RhabdomyolysisCa²⁺ Sequestration in Damaged MuscleHigh CK, Myoglobinuria
Tumour Lysis SyndromePhosphate Release → Ca²⁺ PrecipitationHigh Phosphate, Uric Acid, K⁺
Hungry Bone SyndromePost-PTX/Thyroidectomy; Rapid Bone MineralisationSevere HypoCa²⁺, HypoPhos, HypoMg
Mg²⁺ DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism (Mg Needed for PTH Secretion/Action)Hypokalaemia, Hypocalcaemia Resistant to Ca²⁺

4. Clinical Presentation

Acute Tetany (Emergency)

SignDescriptionElicitation
Trousseau's SignCarpal Spasm (Adduction Thumb, Flexion Wrist/MCPs, Extension Fingers)BP Cuff Inflated >SBP for 3 Min
Chvostek's SignFacial Muscle Twitch (Lips, Nose, Eye)Percussion Over Facial Nerve (Anterior to Ear)
LaryngospasmStridor, Airway ObstructionEmergency — Intubation May Be Needed
Generalised SeizuresTonic-Clonic—
CardiacProlonged QT → Torsades de Pointes, VFECG Monitoring
NeuropsychiatricAnxiety, Confusion, Irritability, Depression—

Chronic Hypocalcaemia

FeatureDetails
Basal Ganglia CalcificationCT Brain (Fahr's Syndrome)
CataractsPosterior Subcapsular
DentalEnamel Hypoplasia, Caries
NeuropsychiatricDepression, Anxiety, Dementia, Parkinsonism
Skin/Hair/NailsDry Skin, Coarse Hair, Brittle Nails
CataractsPosterior Subcapsular

5. Aetiology & Differential Diagnosis

CausePTHPhosphateMagnesiumKey Feature
HypoparathyroidismLowHighNormalPost-Surgical (75%), Autoimmune, Genetic
Vitamin D DeficiencyHigh (2° HPT)LowNormalLow 25-OH D, High ALP
Renal FailureHigh (2° HPT)HighNormalHigh Creatinine
PseudohypoparathyroidismHigh (Resistance)HighNormalPHP1a = AHO (Short Stature, Obesity)
Vitamin D DeficiencyHigh (2° HPT)Low/NormalNormalLow 25-OH D
Acute PancreatitisNormal/HighHighNormalHigh Amylase/Lipase
AlkalosisNormal/HighNormalNormalRespiratory (>7.45) / Metabolic
HypomagnesaemiaLow/NormalLow/NormalLowCorrect Mg First

6. Acute Management — Hypocalcaemic Tetany (Emergency)

Immediate IV Calcium (Life-Saving)

DrugDoseAdministration
10% Calcium Gluconate10-20 mL (1-2g) IVSlow IV Over 10 Min (Cardiac Monitor)
RepeatIf Persistent TetanyEvery 10-20 Min Until Controlled

Follow-Up Infusion

InfusionRate
10% Calcium Gluconate1-2 g/hr (10-20 mL/hr) in 5% Dextrose / 0.9% Saline
TargetIonised Ca²⁺ >1.1 mmol/L; Symptom Relief

Adjuncts

DrugDoseIndication
Magnesium Sulphate2-4g IVMg <0.7 mmol/L
Vitamin D (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol)1-2 µg IV/POAccelerate Ca²⁺ Absorption
Oral CalciumCalcium Carbonate 1-2g TDSAfter IV Stabilisation

7. Chronic Management

Calcium Supplementation

PreparationElemental Ca²⁺Dose
Calcium Carbonate40% (400mg/tablet)1.5-2g TDS with Meals
Calcium Citrate21%1-2g TDS (Better Absorption if Achlorhydria)
TargetTotal Elemental Ca²⁺ 1.5-2g/dayDivided Doses

Active Vitamin D Analogues (Essential for Absorption)

AgentDoseAdvantage
Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)0.25-1 µg/dayRapid Onset; No Renal Activation Needed
Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃)0.25-0.5 µg/dayActive Form; Short Half-Life
Dose TitrationTarget: Ionised Ca²⁺ 1.1-1.3 mmol/LAdjust q2-4wk

Magnesium

IndicationDose
HypomagnesaemiaMgSO₄ 2-4g IV (Acute); Oral Mg 300-600mg/day (Chronic)

Thiazide Diuretic (Adjunct)

RoleDose
Reduce Urinary Ca²⁺ ExcretionHydrochlorothiazide 25mg OD (If Hypercalciuria)

8. Monitoring & Targets (Chronic)

ParameterTargetFrequency
Serum Calcium (Corrected)2.2-2.5 mmol/L (Slightly Low Normal)Monthly (Stable)
Ionised Calcium1.1-1.3 mmol/Lq1-2wk (Titration)
Phosphate<1.5 mmol/LMonthly
24h Urine Calcium<7.5 mmol/day (300mg)3-6 Monthly
Renal FunctioneGFR, Creatinine6-Monthly
25-OH Vitamin D>50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL)Annually
Bone DensityDEXA (Spine/Hip)q2-3 Years

9. Special Situations

Hypocalcaemic Tetany (Emergency)

StepAction
1. IV Calcium Gluconate 10%10-20 mL (1-2g) IV Over 10 Min (Cardiac Monitor)
2. RepeatEvery 10-20 Min Until Tetany Controlled
3. Infusion10% Calcium Gluconate 100mL (10g) in 500mL 5% Dextrose @ 50-100mL/hr
4. MagnesiumIf Mg <0.7 → 2-4g MgSO₄ IV
5. Vitamin DAlfacalcidol/Calcitriol 1-2µg IV/PO

Hungry Bone Syndrome (Post-Parathyroidectomy)

FeatureManagement
Severe HypocalcaemiaIV Calcium Gluconate Infusion (1-2g/hr)
DurationDays-Weeks (Until Bone Remineralisation)
PhosphateHypophosphataemia → Oral Phosphate Supplements
MagnesiumOften Co-existing Hypomagnesaemia

Magnesium Deficiency

FeatureManagement
Functional HypoparathyroidismCorrect Mg First
Prophylactic100mg/day Oral Mg (If on Loop Diuretics/PPI)
AcuteIV MgSO₄ 2-4g (Repeat if Needed)

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)

AspectManagement
Phosphate BindersCalcium-Based (If Ca Normal) / Non-Ca (Sevelamer/Lanthanum) if Hypercalcaemia
Vitamin DAlfacalcidol/Calcitriol (Active Analogues)
CinacalcetIf PTH ↑↑ Despite Binders
