18. MCQs (10)

  1. Vitamin D deficiency definition:

A. 25-OH D <30 nmol/L (12 ng/mL); insufficiency 30-50 nmol/L

B. <50

C. <75

D. <100

E. <20

  1. Vitamin D sources:

A. Sunlight (UVB) -> skin 7-dehydrocholesterol -> Vit D3; diet (oily fish, fortified) minor

B. Diet only

C. Sunlight only

D. Supplements only

E. Endogenous synthesis only

  1. Vit D metabolism:

A. Skin (D3) -> liver 25-hydroxylase -> 25-OH D -> kidney 1a-hydroxylase -> 1,25(OH)2D (active)

B. Kidney only

C. Liver only

D. Intestine only

E. Adrenal only

  1. Vit D deficiency causes:

A. Rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults), secondary HPT, muscle weakness, falls

B. Osteoporosis only

C. Hypercalcaemia

D. Renal stones

E. No symptoms

  1. Vit D deficiency treatment:

A. Colecalciferol (D3) 20,000-40,000 IU weekly x8-12wk -> maintenance 800-2000 IU/day

B. Ergocalciferol single dose

C. Calcitriol first line

D. IV Vit D

E. Sunlight only

  1. Vit D toxicity:

A. Hypercalcaemia, hypercalciuria, renal stones, nephrocalcinosis; 25-OH D >250 nmol/L

B. Hypocalcaemia

C. Hyponatraemia

D. Hypertension

E. Weight loss

  1. Vit D in CKD:

A. Use active Vit D (calcitriol/paricalcitol) - kidney cannot 1a-hydroxylate

B. Colecalciferol sufficient

C. Ergocalciferol preferred

D. No Vit D needed

E. Toxic in CKD

  1. Vit D in obesity:

A. Sequestration in adipose tissue -> higher doses needed (2-3x)

B. Lower doses

C. Normal doses

D. Contraindicated

E. No adjustment

  1. Vit D and falls:

A. Deficiency -> proximal myopathy -> falls; supplementation d falls in elderly

B. No effect on falls

C. Increases falls

D. Only in osteoporosis

E. Only in children

  1. Rickets vs osteomalacia:

A. Rickets: children (growth plate); osteomalacia: adults (mineralisation defect)

B. Same

C. Rickets: adults

D. Osteomalacia: children

E. No difference

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Elderly woman: falls, proximal weakness, 25-OH D 18 nmol/L. Treatment?

A. Colecalciferol 20,000 IU weekly x8wk -> maintenance 1000 IU/day

B. Calcitriol

C. Ergocalciferol 50,000 IU monthly

D. IV Vit D

E. Sunlight only

  1. Same patient: after loading, maintenance dose?

A. 800-2000 IU/day (1000-2000 IU/day); monitor 25-OH D at 3-6mo

B. 400 IU/day

C. 50,000 IU monthly

D. No maintenance

E. Calcitriol

  1. CKD stage 4: 25-OH D 25, PTH 400. Vit D therapy?

A. Calcitriol/paricalcitol (active Vit D); kidney cannot 1a-hydroxylate

B. Colecalciferol high dose

C. Ergocalciferol

D. No Vit D

E. IV Vit D

  1. Vit D toxicity: 25-OH D 300, Ca 3.0. Management?

A. Stop Vit D; IV hydration; furosemide; avoid Ca; monitor Ca

B. Continue Vit D

C. Increase Ca

D. Cinacalcet

E. Calcitriol

  1. Obesity (BMI 40): 25-OH D 20. Loading dose?

A. 2-3x standard dose (60,000-120,000 IU weekly) due to sequestration

B. Standard dose

C. Half dose

D. No loading

E. Calcitriol

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Vit D deficiency

A: 25-OH D <30 nmol/L; insufficiency 30-50 nmol/L

  • Q: Vit D synthesis

A: Skin UVB -> D3 -> liver 25-OH D -> kidney 1a-OH -> 1,25(OH)2D (active)

  • Q: Deficiency causes

A: Rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults), secondary HPT, myopathy, falls

  • Q: Treatment

A: Colecalciferol (D3) 20-40k IU weekly x8-12wk -> maintenance 800-2000 IU/day

  • Q: Toxicity

A: Hypercalcaemia, hypercalciuria, stones; 25-OH D >250 nmol/L

  • Q: CKD Vit D

A: Use ACTIVE Vit D (calcitriol/paricalcitol); kidney cannot 1a-hydroxylate

  • Q: Obesity

A: Sequestration in fat -> 2-3x higher doses

  • Q: Falls

A: Deficiency -> proximal myopathy -> falls; D supplementation d falls in elderly

  • Q: Rickets vs osteomalacia

A: Rickets: children (growth plate); osteomalacia: adults (mineralisation defect)

  • Q: Vit D forms

A: D3 (colecalciferol) preferred over D2 (ergocalciferol); D3 more potent

21. Answer Key with Explanations

  1. Deficiency -> proximal myopathy -> falls; supplementation d falls in elderly — Vit D and falls:

topics.title: Acromegaly
topics.html:
[!info]
Acromegaly = Chronic GH Excess (Usually Pituitary Macroadenoma). Insidious Onset → Coarse Features, Acral Enlargement, Organomegaly. IGF-1 = Screening Test; OGTT GH Suppression = Gold Standard Diagnosis. Transsphenoidal Surgery = First-Line Treatment.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical features (acral enlargement, coarse features, organomegaly)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (IGF-1, OGTT GH suppression)
  • [ ] Interpret pituitary MRI and plan surgical approach
  • [ ] Outline medical management (somatostatin analogues, pegvisomant, dopamine agonists)
  • [ ] Manage complications (cardiomyopathy, sleep apnoea, glucose intolerance, colonic polyps)

2. Epidemiology & Aetiology

FeatureDetails
Incidence3-4 per million/year
Prevalence40-70 per million
Peak Age40-50 Years
SexEqual (M:F = 1:1)
AetiologyPituitary GH-Secreting Adenoma (>95%) — Macroadenoma >10mm (80-90%)
Rare CausesEctopic GHRH (Pancreatic NET, Bronchial Carcinoid), Ectopic GH (Rare)
GeneticsAIP (Young-Onset, Familial), GPR101, GNAS (McCune-Albright), MEN1

3. Clinical Features

Acral & Facial Changes

FeatureDescription
Acral EnlargementHands/Feet Enlargement → Ring/Shoe Size Increase, Spade-Like Hands
FacialCoarse Features, Prognathism, Macroglossia, Wide Spacing Teeth, Prominent Supraorbital Ridges
SkinThickened, Oily, Hyperhidrosis, Skin Tags, Acanthosis Nigricans
Soft TissueSoft Tissue Swelling, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome, Arthralgias

Systemic Manifestations

SystemComplications
CardiovascularCardiomyopathy (LVH, Diastolic Dysfunction), Hypertension, Sleep Apnoea (Obstructive), Arrhythmias
RespiratoryObstructive Sleep Apnoea (50-80%), Upper Airway Obstruction
MetabolicImpaired Glucose Tolerance / Diabetes (30-50%), Insulin Resistance, Dyslipidaemia
GastrointestinalColonic Polyps (↑ Risk 2-3x, Adenomatous), Diverticulosis
MusculoskeletalArthralgias, Joint Swelling, Osteoarthritis, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
ReproductiveHypogonadism (↓ LH/FSH), Menstrual Irregularities, Infertility, Galactorrhoea (If Co-Secreting PRL)

4. Diagnosis — Biochemical & Imaging

Screening & Confirmation

TestNormalAcromegaly
IGF-1 (Age-Adjusted)Normal↑↑ (2-3x ULN) — Best Screening Test
OGTT (75g Glucose)GH Suppressed <1 µg/L at 60-120minGH NOT Suppressed ≥1 µg/L (Gold Standard)
Random GH<0.4 µg/L (Nadir)>1 µg/L (If IGF-1 Elevated)
MRI PituitaryNormalMacroadenoma >10mm (80-90%)

Diagnostic Criteria

CriterionThreshold
IGF-1Age-Adjusted >ULN (Usually 2-3x ULN)
OGTT GHNot Suppressed <1 µg/L at 60-120min (Gold Standard)
Random GH>1 µg/L (If IGF-1 Elevated)
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma >10mm (80-90%)

Differential Diagnosis of Elevated IGF-1

ConditionIGF-1OGTT GH SuppressionOther Features
Acromegaly↑↑Not Suppressed (≥1 µg/L)Pituitary Macroadenoma
Pubertal Gigantism↑↑Not SuppressedOpen Epiphyses, Age <18
Pregnancy↑ (Placental GH)N/APhysiological
Hepatic Failure↓ (Impaired Synthesis)N/ALiver Disease
Renal Failure↑ (↓ Clearance)Variable↑ Creatinine
Malnutrition/Anorexia↓↓Paradoxical ↑ GHLow BMI, Starvation

5. Pituitary MRI — Imaging Protocol

ParameterSpecification
Slice Thickness3mm (No Gap)
SequencesT1 Pre/Post-Gadolinium (Coronal, Sagittal), T2
Dynamic ContrastT1 Post-Gad at 30s, 60s, 90s (Microadenoma Delayed Enhancement)
Field of ViewSella + Suprasellar Region + Optic Chiasm

Adenoma Classification

TypeSizeFrequency
Microadenoma<10mm10-20%
Macroadenoma≥10mm80-90%
Giant>40mmRare

6. Complications — High Yield for Exams

SystemComplicationScreening
CardiovascularCardiomyopathy (LVH, Diastolic Dysfunction), Hypertension (30-50%), Sleep Apnoea (50-80%)Echo, Sleep Study, BP Monitoring
RespiratoryObstructive Sleep Apnoea (50-80%)Sleep Study (Polysomnography)
MetabolicImpaired Glucose Tolerance / Diabetes (30-50%), DyslipidaemiaHbA1c, Fasting Glucose, Lipids
GIColonic Polyps (↑ 2-3x Risk), DiverticulosisColonoscopy at Diagnosis, q3-5yr
JointsArthralgias, Osteoarthritis, Carpal Tunnel SyndromeClinical Exam
ReproductiveHypogonadism (↓ LH/FSH), Menstrual Irregularities, InfertilityLH, FSH, Testosterone/Oestradiol, Prolactin
Neoplastic RiskThyroid Nodules (↑), Colonic Polyps (↑ 2-3x)Thyroid US, Colonoscopy

7. Management Algorithm

```

Acromegaly Diagnosed (↑ IGF-1 + GH Not Suppressed on OGTT)

│

▼

MRI PITUITARY (3mm Slices) → Macroadenoma >10mm (90%)

│

▼

TRANSSEPHENOIDAL SURGERY (TSS) — 1st LINE

│

├── CURE (Post-Op IGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L)

│ → SURVEILLANCE (Annual IGF-1 + OGTT)

│

└── RESIDUAL DISEASE (IGF-1 Elevated / GH Not Suppressed)

│

├── RE-OPERATION (If Accessible Residual)

└── MEDICAL THERAPY

├── SOMATOSTATIN ANALOGUES (1st Line Medical)

│ Octreotide LAR 20-40mg IM q4wk

│ Lanreotide Autogel 60-120mg SC q4wk

│ Pasireotide LAR 40-60mg IM q4wk (2nd Line)

│

├── PEGVISOMANT (GH Receptor Antagonist)

│ 10-30mg SC Daily (If SST Refractory)

│

├── DOPAMINE AGONISTS (Adjunct)

│ Cabergoline 0.5-3mg/week

│

└── RADIOTHERAPY (Stereotactic / Fractionated)

→ If Surgery + Medical Fail

```

8. Medical Therapy — Agents & Dosing

Somatostatin Analogues (1st Line Medical)

AgentDoseRouteFrequencyKey Features
Octreotide LAR20-40mgIMq4 WeeksStandard 1st Line
Lanreotide Autogel60-120mgSCq4 WeeksAlternative; Self-Administration
Pasireotide LAR40-60mgIMq4 Weeks2nd Line; ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk

Pegvisomant (GH Receptor Antagonist) — 2nd/3rd Line

FeatureDetails
MechanismGH Receptor Antagonist — Blocks GH Action (↓ IGF-1)
Dose10-30mg SC Daily (Titrate to IGF-1 Normalisation)
IndicationSST Refractory / Intolerant / Post-RT
AdvantageNormalises IGF-1 Without ↓ GH (GH ↑↑ on Pegvisomant)
MonitoringIGF-1 Normalisation (GH Levels Rise — Do Not Use for Monitoring)

Dopamine Agonists (Adjunct)

AgentDoseRole
Cabergoline0.5-3mg/weekAdjunct; Mild GH Suppression; Low Cost

9. Treatment Goals & Cure Criteria

Biochemical Cure (Consensus)

ParameterTarget
IGF-1Normal (Age-Adjusted)
OGTT GH<1 µg/L at 60-120min
Random GH<0.4 µg/L (Nadir)

Surgical Cure Rates

Tumour TypeCure Rate (Biochemical Remission)
Microadenoma (<10mm)80-90%
Macroadenoma (>10mm)50-60%
Giant (>40mm)<30%

10. Comorbidities & Surveillance

Surveillance Protocol

ParameterFrequency
IGF-13-6 Monthly (Active); Annually (Remission)
OGTT GH6Monthly (Active); Annually (Remission)
MRI Pituitary3mo, 1yr, then Annually ×5yr
Visual Fields6Monthly (If Chiasmal Compression)
Cardiac EchoBaseline, Then q1-2yr (Cardiomyopathy)
Sleep StudyBaseline + q1-2yr (Sleep Apnoea)
ColonoscopyAt Diagnosis → q3-5yr (↑ Colonic Polyps 2-3x)
Glucose/HbA1c6-Monthly (Diabetes Risk)
LipidsAnnual
Thyroid USq1-2yr (↑ Thyroid Nodules)

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Acromegaly ScreeningIGF-1 (Age-Adjusted) — Single Best Test
Acromegaly ConfirmationOGTT: GH Not Suppressed <1 µg/L (Gold Standard)
GH on OGTTNormal: <1 µg/L at 60-120min; Acromegaly: Not Suppressed
IGF-1 in Acromegaly2-3x ULN (Age-Adjusted)
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma >10mm in 90%
1st Line TreatmentTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
Surgical Cure RateMicro: 80-90%; Macro: 50-60%
Remission CriteriaIGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
Medical 1st LineSomatostatin Analogues (Octreotide LAR / Lanreotide)
PegvisomantGH Receptor Antagonist; Normalises IGF-1; GH ↑ on Treatment
ComplicationsCardiomyopathy, Sleep Apnoea, Diabetes, Colonic Polyps (↑2-3x)
Pasireotide2nd Line SST; ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk
PegvisomantGH Receptor Antagonist; GH ↑ During Treatment; IGF-1 Normalises
Remission CriteriaIGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
Recurrence10-20% at 10 Years; Annual Surveillance

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
IGF-1 vs GH for DiagnosisIGF-1 = Screening (Stable); OGTT GH = Confirmation (Dynamic)
GH RandomUseless (Pulsatile); Use IGF-1 or OGTT
Gigantism vs AcromegalyGigantism = Pre-Epiphyseal Fusion; Acromegaly = Post-Fusion
Pegvisomant EffectGH ↑↑ on Treatment (Displaces GH from Receptor); IGF-1 Normalises
Pasireotide vs OctreotidePasireotide = SSTR1/2/3/5 (Broader); ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk
Surgery Cure RateMicro 80-90%; Macro 50-60%
Remission DefinitionIGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
Sleep Apnoea50-80% in Acromegaly; Screen with Sleep Study
Colonic Polyps2-3x Increased Risk; Colonoscopy at Diagnosis
Pegvisomant MonitoringMonitor IGF-1 (NOT GH — GH Rises on Treatment)

13. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Acromegaly))

Treatment

Medical: SST Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide)

Radiotherapy: If Surgery/Med Fail

Surgery

TSS (Endoscopic Preferred)
```

PegvisomantGH Receptor Antagonist; GH ↑, IGF-1 Normalises

ComplicationsCardiomyopathy, Sleep Apnoea, DM, Colonic Polyps (2-3x)
SurveillanceAnnual IGF-1 + OGTT; MRI 5yr; Colonoscopy q3-5yr
Pegvisomant MonitoringMonitor IGF-1 (NOT GH — GH Rises)

topics.title: Diabetes Insipidus
topics.html:
[!info]
Diabetes Insipidus (DI) = Polyuria + Polydipsia due to ADH Deficiency (Central) or Renal Resistance (Nephrogenic). Central DI = ADH Deficiency; Nephrogenic DI = Renal Resistance. Water Deprivation Test + Desmopressin Challenge = Gold Standard Diagnostic Test.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate Central DI, Nephrogenic DI, and Primary Polydipsia
  • [ ] Perform and interpret Water Deprivation Test + Desmopressin Challenge
  • [ ] Apply desmopressin therapy for Central DI
  • [ ] Manage Nephrogenic DI (Thiazides, Amiloride, Low Salt Diet)
  • [ ] Recognise Gestational DI and its management

2. Overview & Classification

TypePathophysiologyADH LevelUrine OsmolalityDesmopressin Response
Central DIADH Deficiency (Pituitary/Stalk/Hypothalamic Lesion)LowLow (Dilute)Responds ↑ Urine Osm >50%
Nephrogenic DIRenal Resistance to ADH (V2 Receptor/AQP2 Defect)High/NormalLow (Dilute)No Response
Primary PolydipsiaExcessive Water Intake (Psychogenic/Dipsogenic)SuppressedLow (Concentrates on Deprivation)Concentrates on Deprivation
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase Degrades ADHLow/NormalDiluteResponds to Desmopressin

3. Aetiology

Central DI (CDI)

CauseFrequencyDetails
Idiopathic~30%Autoimmune (Lymphocytic Infundibuloneurohypophysitis)
Pituitary Surgery / Trauma~20%Post-TSS, Head Trauma, Basal Skull Fracture
Infiltration~15%Sarcoidosis, Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis, Metastases, Haemochromatosis
GeneticRareAVP Gene Mutations (Autosomal Dominant)
Pituitary Surgery/Apoplexy~10%Post-TSS, Pituitary Apoplexy
Craniopharyngioma~5%Suprasellar Tumor Compressing Stalk

Nephrogenic DI (NDI)

CauseMechanismKey Features
LithiumMost Common Acquired↓ AQP2 Expression, ↓ cAMP; Dose/Duration Dependent
DrugsDemeclocycline, Amphotericin B, Foscarnet, Ofloxacin, IfosfamideV2 Receptor / AQP2 Inhibition
ElectrolytesHypercalcaemia, HypokalaemiaTubular Damage
Obstructive UropathyTubulointerstitial Damage
GeneticAVPR2 (X-Linked Recessive), AQP2 (AR)Congenital NDI

Primary Polydipsia (Psychogenic)

FeatureDetails
AetiologyPsychogenic (Schizophrenia), Habitual, Dipsogenic
MechanismExcess Water Intake → Suppresses ADH → Dilute Urine
Key FeatureUrine Concentrates During Deprivation (Unlike DI)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureCentral DINephrogenic DIPrimary Polydipsia
Polyuria>3-4 L/day>3-4 L/day>3-4 L/day
PolydipsiaProfound Thirst, NocturiaProfound Thirst, NocturiaCompulsive Drinking
Urine OsmolalityLow (<300 mOsm/kg)Low (<300 mOsm/kg)Low/Variable (Concentrates on Deprivation)
Serum OsmolalityHigh (>295 mOsm/kg)HighNormal/ Low
Serum SodiumHigh (>145 mmol/L)HighNormal/Low
ADH LevelLow/UndetectableHighSuppressed
NocturiaSevereSevereSevere

5. Diagnostic Algorithm — Water Deprivation Test (WDT)

Protocol

PhaseProcedureStop Criteria
1. Fluid DeprivationNPO + Fluid Restriction; Weight, Serum/Urine Osmolality q1-2hWeight Loss >5%; Urine Osm > Plasma Osm; Haemodynamic Instability
2. Desmopressin ChallengeDDAVP 2µg SC/IV (or 10-40µg Intranasal)Measure Urine Osm at 60min

Interpretation Matrix

ResultCentral DINephrogenic DIPrimary Polydipsia
Baseline Urine OsmLow (<300)Low (<300)Low/Variable
After DeprivationLow (<300)Low (<300)Concentrates (>600)
Post-DDAVP (60min)↑ >50%No Response (<10%)N/A (Already Concentrated)
Plasma ADHLowHighLow (Suppressed)

Stop Criteria (Safety)

CriterionAction
Weight Loss >5%STOP
Urine Osm > Plasma OsmSTOP
Haemodynamic Instability (SBP<90, HR>120)STOP
Time >8 HoursSTOP

6. Desmopressin (DDAVP) Therapy

Central DI

RouteDoseFrequency
Intranasal10-40µgq12-24h
Oral (Tablet)0.1-0.4mgq12-24h
Subcutaneous / IV2-4µgq12-24h
Dose TitrationTarget: Urine Output 1.5-2.5L/day, No Nocturia, Normal Na⁺

Nephrogenic DI

TreatmentDoseMechanism
Low Salt / Low Protein Diet—Reduces Solute Load
Thiazide DiureticHydrochlorothiazide 25-50mg BDInduces Mild Volume Depletion → ↑ Proximal Reabsorption
Amiloride5-10mg BDENaC Blocker; K⁺-Sparing
NSAIDs (Indomethacin)25-50mg TDS↑ Prostaglandin Inhibition → ↓ PGE2 → ↑ ADH Sensitivity
DesmopressinPartial Response (If Partial NDI)Limited Role

7. Gestational DI

FeatureDetails
CausePlacental Vasopressinase Degrades ADH
Onset2nd/3rd Trimester
ResolutionPostpartum (4-6 Weeks)
TreatmentDesmopressin (Safe in Pregnancy); Monitor Fluid Balance
RecurrenceHigh in Subsequent Pregnancies

8. Differential Diagnosis

FeatureCentral DINephrogenic DIPrimary Polydipsia
Serum Osmolality>295 mOsm/kg>295 mOsm/kgNormal/Low
Urine Osmolality<300 mOsm/kg<300 mOsm/kgConcentrates on Deprivation
Urine Osm Post-DDAVP↑ >50%No ChangeN/A
Plasma ADHLowHighSuppressed
Desmopressin TestPositive ResponseNo ResponseConcentrates on Deprivation

9. Management

Central DI

AspectDetails
Desmopressin (DDAVP)Intranasal 10-40µg q12-24h; Oral 0.1-0.4mg q12-24h; SC/IV 2-4µg q24h
Dose TitrationStart Low; Titrate to Urine Output 1.5-2.5L/day; Nocturia ≤1
Fluid IntakeNo Restriction (Ad Libitum)
Adverse EffectsHyponatraemia (Water Retention) — Monitor Na⁺

Nephrogenic DI

TherapyDoseRole
DietLow Salt, Low ProteinReduce Solute Load
ThiazideHCTZ 25-50mg BDInduces Mild Volume Depletion → ↑ Proximal Reabsorption
Amiloride5-10mg BDENaC Blocker; K⁺-Sparing
NSAIDsIndomethacin 25-50mg TDS↓ Prostaglandin → ↑ ADH Sensitivity
DesmopressinPartial Response OnlyIf Partial NDI

10. Complications & Monitoring

ComplicationMonitoring
HyponatraemiaSerum Na⁺ q6-12h (If on Desmopressin)
Water IntoxicationFluid Restriction if Severe; Stop Desmopressin
HypernatraemiaIf Desmopressin Missed/Dose Inadequate
Breakthrough PolyuriaDose Escalation / Split Dosing

11. Special: Gestational DI

FeatureManagement
Onset2nd/3rd Trimester (Placental Vasopressinase)
TreatmentDesmopressin (Safe in Pregnancy)
DeliveryMonitor Fluid Balance; Desmopressin Peripartum
PostpartumResolves 4-6 Weeks Postpartum
RecurrenceHigh Recurrence in Subsequent Pregnancies

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
DI vs SIADHDI = Polyuria + Dilute Urine + High Serum Osm; SIADH = Hyponatraemia + Concentrated Urine
Central vs Nephrogenic DICentral: ADH Low, Responds to DDAVP; Nephrogenic: ADH High, No DDAVP Response
WDT Stop CriteriaWeight Loss >5% OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm OR Haemodynamic Instability
Central DI TreatmentDesmopressin (DDAVP) — Intranasal/Oral/SC/IV
Nephrogenic DI TreatmentThiazide + Amiloride + Low Salt Diet (Desmopressine Ineffective)
LithiumMost Common Cause of Acquired NDI (↓ AQP2, ↓ cAMP)
Primary PolydipsiaUrine Concentrates on Deprivation; DDAVP Not Needed
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase; Desmopressin Safe; Resolves Postpartum
WDT Stop CriteriaWeight Loss >5%, Urine Osm > Plasma Osm, Haemodynamic Instability
Desmopressin HyponatraemiaMonitor Na⁺; Limit Fluids if on DDAVP
Lithium NDI↓ AQP2 Expression → Stop Lithium if Possible
Central vs Nephrogenic DIDDAVP Response: Central = ↑ Urine Osm >50%; Nephrogenic = No Response

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Central vs Nephrogenic DICentral = ADH Deficiency; Nephrogenic = Renal Resistance to ADH
DDAVP ResponseCentral = Works; Nephrogenic = Fails
Primary Polydipsia vs DIPolydipsia: Urine Concentrates on Deprivation; DI = Fails to Concentrate
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase → ADH Degradation; Resolves Postpartum
Lithium NDI↓ AQP2 Expression; ↓ cAMP; Most Common Acquired NDI
Desmopressin vs VasopressinDDAVP = Pure V2 Agonist (No Pressor Effect); Vasopressin = V1+V2
Desmopressin in EnuresisIntranasal/Oral at Bedtime; No Fluid Restriction Needed
WDT StopWeight Loss >5% OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm

14. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Diabetes Insipidus))

Types

Central DI (ADH Deficiency)

Pituitary Surgery/Trauma

Infiltration (Sarcoid, LCH, Mets)

Genetic (AVP Mutation)

Nephrogenic DI (Renal Resistance)

Lithium (Most Common Acquired)

Drugs (Demeclocycline, Amphotericin)

Genetic (AVPR2, AQP2)

Electrolytes (HyperCa, HypoK)

Primary Polydipsia

Psychogenic / Habitual

Urine Concentrates on Deprivation

Gestational DI

Placental Vasopressinase

Desmopressin Safe

Diagnosis

Water Deprivation Test (Gold Standard)

Deprivation → DDAVP Challenge

Central: Urine Osm ↑ >50% post DDAVP

Nephrogenic: No Response

Polydipsia: Concentrates on Deprivation

Treatment

Central: Desmopressin (DDAVP)

Nephrogenic: Thiazide + Amiloride + Low Salt

Gestational: DDAVP (Safe)

WDT Protocol

Deprivation until >5% weight loss or Uosm > Posm

DDAVP 2µg SC/IV → Urine Osm at 60min

Central: ↑>50%; Nephrogenic: No Response

```

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Central vs Nephrogenic DICentral: ADH Low → DDAVP Response; Nephrogenic: ADH High → DDAVP No Response
WDT Gold StandardDeprivation → DDAVP Challenge
Central DIDDAVP → Urine Osm ↑ >50%
Nephrogenic DIDDAVP → No Response
Primary PolydipsiaUrine Concentrates on Deprivation (No DDAVP Needed)
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase → ADH Degradation → Desmopressin Safe
Lithium NDIMost Common Acquired NDI (↓ AQP2, ↓ cAMP)
DDAVP in Central DIIntranasal/Oral/SC/IV; Monitor Na+ (Hyponatraemia Risk)
NDI ManagementThiazide + Amiloride + Low Salt Diet; Desmopressine Not Effective
WDT Stop CriteriaWeight Loss >5% OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Posterior Pituitary- ADH-Vasopressin|Posterior Pituitary- ADH-Vasopressin]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Diabetes insipidus =

A. ADH deficiency (central) or renal resistance (nephrogenic); polyuria/polydipsia

B. ADH excess

C. Aldosterone deficiency

D. Cortisol deficiency

E. Thyroid hormone excess

  1. Central DI causes:

A. Pituitary surgery, trauma, tumours, infiltrative, idiopathic

B. Renal disease

C. Lithium

D. Hypercalcaemia

E. Hypokalaemia

  1. Nephrogenic DI causes:

A. Lithium, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, renal disease, genetic (AQP2)

B. Pituitary surgery

C. Trauma

D. Tumours

E. Idiopathic

  1. Water deprivation test - central DI:

A. No urine concentration -> DDAVP -> urine osmolality rises >50%

B. No response to DDAVP

C. Normal concentration

D. Urine osmolality >800

E. Serum Na+ normal

  1. Water deprivation test - nephrogenic DI:

A. No urine concentration -> DDAVP -> NO response

B. Concentrates with DDAVP

C. Normal concentration

D. Urine osmolality >800

E. Serum Na+ normal

  1. Central DI treatment:

A. Desmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal/IV; monitor sodium

B. Fluid restriction

C. Thiazide

D. NSAIDs

E. Fludrocortisone

  1. Nephrogenic DI treatment:

A. Thiazide + low salt + NSAID (indomethacin); DDAVP ineffective

B. Desmopressin

C. Fluid restriction only

D. Furosemide

E. Spironolactone

  1. Gestational DI:

A. Placental vasopressinase degrades ADH; treat with desmopressin

B. Central DI

C. Nephrogenic DI

D. Primary polydipsia

E. SIADH

  1. Primary polydipsia:

A. Water deprivation -> partial concentration; DDAVP -> further concentration

B. Central DI

C. Nephrogenic DI

D. SIADH

E. No concentration

  1. Desmopressin dosing:

A. Central DI: 100-400mcg oral q12-24h; 1-4mcg IV/SC q8-12h; titrate to urine osmolality

B. Fixed 1mg daily

C. Only IV

D. Only nasal

E. Weight-based only

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 30yo man: polyuria 6L/day, polydipsia, serum Na+ 150, urine osmolality 100. After water dep: urine osmolality 150. DDAVP -> 600. Diagnosis?

A. Central DI

B. Nephrogenic DI

C. Primary polydipsia

D. SIADH

E. Gestational DI

  1. Same patient: treatment?

A. Desmopressin 100-200mcg oral/nasal q12h; monitor sodium

B. Fluid restriction

C. Thiazide

D. NSAIDs

E. Furosemide

  1. Patient on lithium: polyuria, Na+ 148, urine osmolality 150. Water dep no concentration. DDAVP no response. Diagnosis?

A. Nephrogenic DI (lithium)

B. Central DI

C. Primary polydipsia

D. SIADH

E. Gestational DI

  1. Same patient: treatment?

A. Thiazide + amiloride + low salt + NSAID; stop lithium if possible

B. Desmopressin

C. Fluid restriction

D. Furosemide

E. Spironolactone

  1. Pregnant woman: polyuria, Na+ 150. Water dep no concentration. DDAVP -> concentration. Diagnosis?

A. Gestational DI

B. Central DI

C. Nephrogenic DI

D. Primary polydipsia

E. SIADH

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Central DI

A: ADH deficiency; water dep no concentrate -> DDAVP -> urine concentrates

  • Q: Nephrogenic DI

A: Renal resistance to ADH; no concentration even with DDAVP

  • Q: Primary polydipsia

A: Water dep -> some concentration; DDAVP -> further concentration

  • Q: Water deprivation test

A: Central DI: DDAVP works; Nephrogenic: DDAVP fails; Polydipsia: some concentration

  • Q: Desmopressin

A: Central DI: 100-400mcg q12-24h oral/nasal; 1-4mcg q8-12h IV/SC

  • Q: Nephrogenic DI Rx

A: Thiazide + low salt + NSAID (indomethacin); stop offending drug

  • Q: Gestational DI

A: Placental vasopressinase; treat with desmopressin

  • Q: Primary polydipsia

A: Water dep partial concentration; DDAVP further concentrates

  • Q: Lithium DI

A: Nephrogenic; thiazide + amiloride + low salt + NSAID

  • Q: SIADH vs DI

A: SIADH: euvolaemic hyponatraemia, Uosm >100, UNa+ >20; DI: hypernatraemia, polyuria

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. ADH deficiency (central) or renal resistance (nephrogenic); polyuria/polydipsia — Diabetes insipidus =
  1. Pituitary surgery, trauma, tumours, infiltrative, idiopathic — Central DI causes:
  1. Lithium, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, renal disease, genetic (AQP2) — Nephrogenic DI causes:
  1. No urine concentration -> DDAVP -> urine osmolality rises >50% — Water deprivation test - central DI:
  1. No urine concentration -> DDAVP -> NO response — Water deprivation test - nephrogenic DI:
  1. Desmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal/IV; monitor sodium — Central DI treatment:
  1. Thiazide + low salt + NSAID (indomethacin); DDAVP ineffective — Nephrogenic DI treatment:
  1. Placental vasopressinase degrades ADH; treat with desmopressin — Gestational DI:
  1. Water deprivation -> partial concentration; DDAVP -> further concentration — Primary polydipsia:
  1. Central DI: 100-400mcg oral q12-24h; 1-4mcg IV/SC q8-12h; titrate to urine osmolality — Desmopressin dosing:

SBAs

  1. Central DI — 30yo man: polyuria 6L/day, polydipsia, serum Na+ 150, urine osmolality 100. After water dep: urine osmolality 150. DDAVP -> 600. Diagnosis?
  1. Desmopressin 100-200mcg oral/nasal q12h; monitor sodium — Same patient: treatment?
  1. Nephrogenic DI (lithium) — Patient on lithium: polyuria, Na+ 148, urine osmolality 150. Water dep no concentration. DDAVP no response. Diagnosis?
  1. Thiazide + amiloride + low salt + NSAID; stop lithium if possible — Same patient: treatment?
  1. Gestational DI — Pregnant woman: polyuria, Na+ 150. Water dep no concentration. DDAVP -> concentration. Diagnosis?

topics.title: Gonadotroph Adenoma
topics.html:
[!info]
Gonadotroph Adenoma = Pituitary Adenoma Secreting FSH and/or LH (and α-Subunit). Usually Clinically Silent / Non-Functioning. Most Present as Macroadenomas with Mass Effect. No Classical Hormone Excess Syndrome (Unlike Prolactinoma/Acromegaly/Cushing).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise the clinical presentation (mass effect rather than hormone excess)
  • [ ] Interpret hormonal profile (FSH/LH/α-subunit elevation without clinical syndrome)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic workup (MRI, hormonal panel, visual fields)
  • [ ] Apply management algorithm (observation vs surgery)
  • [ ] Differentiate from other pituitary adenomas

2. Pathophysiology & Classification

FeatureDetails
Cell OriginGonadotrophs (FSH/LH Producing Cells) — ~10-15% of Anterior Pituitary
Hormones SecretedFSH, LH, α-Subunit (Common), β-Subunits (FSH-β, LH-β)
Clinical SyndromeUsually NONE ("Silent" / Non-Functioning)
Size at PresentationMacroadenoma (>10mm) in >80% — Presents Late Due to Lack of Hormone Excess

Hormone Profile

HormoneTypical Pattern
FSH↑↑ (Often Disproportionately High vs LH)
LH↑ or Normal
α-SubunitConsistently Elevated (Most Sensitive Marker)
Free α-SubunitMarkedly Elevated (Specific Marker)
Testosterone/OestradiolLow/Normal (Due to ↓ Pulsatile LH) — Not Elevated

3. Clinical Presentation — Mass Effect Dominates

PresentationFrequencyDetails
Incidentaloma20-30%Found on MRI for Headache/Trauma
Visual Field Defects50-70%Bi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
Headache40-60%Frontal/Retro-orbital
Hypopituitarism40-50%GH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH Order
Cranial Nerve Palsies10-20%CN III/IV/VI (Cavernous Sinus Invasion)
Hormone Excess SymptomsAbsentNo Classical Syndrome (No Hyperprolactinaemia, Acromegaly, Cushing)

4. Biochemical Profile

Hormonal Pattern

HormoneTypical FindingSignificance
FSH↑↑ (Often Disproportionately > LH)Gonadotroph Hyperplasia/Neoplasia
LH↑ or NormalOften Less Than FSH
α-Subunit↑↑ (Markedly Elevated)Most Sensitive Marker
Free α-SubunitMarkedly ElevatedSpecific for Gonadotroph Adenoma
LH/FSH Ratio<1 (FSH Predominates)Opposite to PCOS / Menopause
Testosterone / OestradiolLow / NormalDysregulation; Not Elevated
ProlactinNormal / Mildly ↑ (Stalk Effect)Stalk Compression (Disinhibition)

5. Diagnosis — Workup Algorithm

```

Pituitary Incidentaloma / Mass Effect Symptoms

│

▼

HORMONAL PANEL: FSH, LH, α-Subunit, Free α-Subunit, Testosterone/Oestradiol

│

├── FSH ↑↑, α-Subunit ↑↑, LH Normal/↑, Testosterone/Oestradiol Low/Normal

│ → GONADOTROPH ADENOMA (Silent)

│

├── Prolactin ↑↑ → PROLACTINOMA

├── GH/IGF-1 ↑ → ACROMEGALY

├── ACTH/Cortisol ↑ → CUSHING DISEASE

├── TSH ↑ + fT4/fT3 ↑ → TSH-oma

└── All Normal / Mild α-Subunit ↑ → NON-FUNCTIONING / NULL CELL ADENOMA

```

Key Diagnostic Features

FeatureGonadotroph AdenomaNon-Functioning (Null Cell)
FSH/LH↑↑ / ↑ (FSH > LH)Normal / Slightly ↑
α-SubunitMarkedly ElevatedNormal / Mildly ↑
Free α-SubunitMarkedly ↑Normal
Sex SteroidsLow / Normal (Not Elevated)Low (Due to Compression)
Clinical SyndromeNone (Silent)None (Silent)

6. Imaging & Localisation

MRI Pituitary (3mm, Gadolinium)

FeatureFindings
SizeMacroadenoma >10mm (>80%)
EnhancementHomogeneous / Heterogeneous
ExtensionSuprasellar (Chiasm), Cavernous Sinus (Knosp Grade)
SignalT1 Hypointense, T2 Variable

Cavernous Sinus Invasion (Knosp Classification)

GradeDescriptionSurgical Resectability
Grade 0Tumour ≤ Intercarotid LineComplete Resection Likely
Grade 1> Intercarotid, ≤ Tangent to Lateral ICAComplete Possible
Grade 2> Lateral ICA, ≤ Tangent to Medial ICAPartial Resection
Grade 3> Medial ICA, ≤ Tangent to MCCSubtotal
Grade 4> Tangent to MCCGross Total Unlikely

7. Management Algorithm

```

Gonadotroph Adenoma Confirmed (Hormones + MRI)

│

├── MICROADENOMA (<10mm) + ASYMPTOMATIC

│ → OBSERVATION

│ MRI 6-12mo → Yearly ×2 → Then 2-5yrly

│ Hormonal Surveillance Annual

│

├── MACROADENOMA (≥10mm) + ASYMPTOMATIC

│ → SURGERY RECOMMENDED (High Risk Future Mass Effect)

│ Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)

│

├── VISUAL FIELD DEFECTS (Any Size)

│ → URGENT TRANSSEPHENOIDAL SURGERY (TSS)

│

├── HYPOPITUITARISM (Symptomatic)

│ → SURGERY (TSS) + Hormone Replacement

│

└── RECURRENT / RESIDUAL POST-SURGERY

├── ASYMPTOMATIC RESIDUAL (<1cm) → Observation

├── SYMPTOMATIC / GROWING → Re-operation / Radiotherapy

└── NOT SURGICAL CANDIDATE → Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)

```

8. Surgical Management

Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)

AspectDetails
ApproachEndoscopic Endonasal (Preferred) / Microscopic Sub-labial
GoalGross Total Resection (GTR) if Possible; Decompression if Cavernous Sinus Invasion
Success RatesGTR 70-80% (Knosp 0-1); Decompression 90-95%
ComplicationsCSF Leak (2-5%), DI (10-20% Transient), New Hypopituitarism (5-10%), Epistaxis

Cavernous Sinus Invasion (Knosp)

GradeDescriptionResection
Grade 0≤ Intercarotid LineComplete Likely
Grade 1> Intercarotid, ≤ Tangent Lateral ICAComplete Possible
Grade 2> Lateral ICA, ≤ Medial ICASubtotal Expected
Grade 3> Medial ICA, ≤ MCCSubtotal
Grade 4> MCCDecompression Only

9. Post-Operative Management

TimepointAssessments
Day 1-2Hormones (FSH, LH, α-Subunit, Testosterone/Oestradiol), Cortisol, Electrolytes, Visual Fields
6 WeeksFull Hormone Panel, Visual Fields
3 MonthsFull Hormones, MRI, Visual Fields
6 MonthsHormones, MRI (If Residual), Visual Fields
Annual ×5yrHormones, MRI, Visual Fields
Long-term2-3 Yearly MRI + Hormones + Visual Fields

Post-Op Hormone Replacement

DeficiencyReplacement
ACTH/CortisolHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
TSH/fT4Levothyroxine 1.6µg/kg/day
GonadotrophsTestosterone / HRT (If Hypogonadal)
GHRH-GH if Confirmed GHD (ITT Peak <3 µg/L)
ProlactinNot Replaced (Except Lactation)

10. Recurrence & Surveillance

OutcomeRate
Recurrence (GTR)10-15% at 10 Years
Recurrence (Subtotal)30-50% at 5 Years
New Hypopituitarism10-20% (Post-Surgery)

Surveillance Protocol

TimepointInvestigations
Pre-OpFull Hormones, MRI 3mm, Visual Fields
Day 1-2 Post-OpHormones, Electrolytes, Visual Fields
6 WeeksFull Hormones, Visual Fields
3 MonthsHormones, MRI, Visual Fields
Annual (×5yr)Hormones, MRI, Visual Fields
Long-Termq2-3yr MRI + Hormones + Visual Fields

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Gonadotroph AdenomaSilent (No Hormone Excess Syndrome); FSH > LH; α-Subunit ↑↑
α-SubunitMarkedly Elevated (Most Sensitive Marker); Free α-Subunit Best
FSH vs LHFSH > LH (Disproportionate) — Opposite to PCOS
Sex SteroidsLow/Normal (Not Elevated) — Key Differentiator
Clinical PresentationMass Effect Only (Visual Fields, Headache, Hypopituitarism)
Incidentaloma Rate20-30% of Pituitary Adenomas
Size at PresentationMacroadenoma >10mm in >80% (Due to Silent Nature)
Surgery IndicationMacroadenoma + Asymptomatic → Surgery Recommended
Knosp ClassificationGrade 0-1 = GTR Likely; Grade 3-4 = Subtotal/Decompression
IOPTH EquivalentNo Standard IOPTH for Gonadotroph (Unlike Cushing)
Recurrence10-15% (GTR); 30-50% (Subtotal) → Adjuvant RT

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Gonadotroph vs Non-FunctioningGonadotroph = FSH/LH/α-Subunit ↑ (Biochemically Active); Non-Func = All Hormones Normal
Gonadotroph vs ProlactinomaProlactinoma = PRL ↑↑ + Galactorrhoea/Amenorrhoea; Gonadotroph = FSH/LH/α-Subunit ↑, PRL Normal
Gonadotroph vs TSH-omaTSH-oma: TSH ↑ + fT4/fT3 ↑; Gonadotroph: TSH Normal
Gonadotroph vs Null CellGonadotroph = α-Subunit/FSH/LH ↑; Null Cell = All Hormones Normal
FSH > LHCharacteristic of Gonadotroph Adenoma (vs PCOS LH>FSH)
α-SubunitBest Marker for Gonadotroph Adenoma (More Sensitive than FSH/LH)
Free α-SubunitMore Specific than Total α-Subunit
SurgeryRecommended for All Macroadenomas (Even Asymptomatic)
Knosp ≥3Subtotal Resection Expected; Adjuvant RT Considered

13. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Gonadotroph Adenoma))

Definition

FSH/LH/α-Subunit Secreting

Usually Silent (No Hormone Syndrome)

Hormones

FSH ↑↑ (Disproportionate)

LH ↑/Normal

α-Subunit ↑↑ (Marker)

Free α-Subunit ↑↑

Sex Steroids: Low/Normal

Presentation

Mass Effect: Visual Fields, Headache, Hypopituitarism

NO Hormone Excess Syndrome

Incidentaloma (20-30%)

Diagnosis

FSH↑ + LH↑/N + α-Subunit↑↑ + Steroid Low/Normal

MRI: Macroadenoma >10mm (80%)

α-Subunit ↑↑ = Best Marker

Management

Micro + Asymptomatic → Observe

Macro + Asymptomatic → Surgery

Visual Fields / Hypopituitarism → Urgent Surgery

TSS (Endoscopic) Preferred

Knosp Grade Guides Resectability

Surgery

TSS (Endoscopic Preferred)

GTR 70-80% (Knosp 0-1)

Complications: CSF Leak, DI, New Hypopit

Surveillance

Hormones + MRI + Visual Fields

Annual ×5yr → 2-3yrly

Recurrence: GTR 10-15%, Subtotal 30-50%

```

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Gonadotroph AdenomaSilent Adenoma; FSH > LH; α-Subunit Markedly Elevated
Clinical PresentationMass Effect Only (Visual Fields, Headache, Hypopituitarism)
No Hormone Excess SyndromeUnlike Prolactinoma/Acromegaly/Cushing
FSH > LHCharacteristic (Opposite to PCOS where LH>FSH)
α-SubunitMost Sensitive Marker (Free α-Subunit Best)
Size at PresentationMacroadenoma >10mm in >80% (Silent → Late Detection)
Surgery IndicationAll Macroadenomas (Even Asymptomatic) → TSS
Knosp Grading0-1 = GTR Likely; 2 = Partial; 3-4 = Decompression Only
IOPTHNot Standard for Gonadotroph (Unlike Cushing)
Recurrence10-15% (GTR); 30-50% (Subtotal)
Free α-SubunitMore Sensitive/Specific than Total α-Subunit

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Pituitary Adenomas- Functioning|Pituitary Adenomas- Functioning]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Non-Functioning]], [[FSH-LH-Gonadal Axis]], [[Pituitary Apoplexy]], [[Hypopituitarism]], [[Reproductive Endocrinology]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Gonadotroph adenomas are typically:

A. Non-functioning (clinically silent) or minimally functioning; FSH/LH subunits elevated but no clinical syndrome

B. Functioning with hypergonadism

C. Functioning with hypogonadism

D. Prolactin-secreting

E. ACTH-secreting

  1. Most common hormone immunohistochemically detected in gonadotroph adenomas:

A. FSH > LH (FSHβ subunit)

B. LH > FSH

C. TSH

D. GH

E. ACTH

  1. Clinical presentation of gonadotroph adenoma:

A. Mass effect (headache, visual field defects, hypopituitarism) — NOT hormonal syndrome

B. Precocious puberty

C. Ovarian hyperstimulation

D. Testicular enlargement

E. Gynaecomastia

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma in pre-pubertal child may cause:

A. Precocious puberty (rare; LH/FSH secretion)

B. Delayed puberty

C. No effect

D. Growth failure

E. Diabetes insipidus

  1. Post-menopausal women: gonadotroph adenoma may present with:

A. Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (high FSH → multiple follicles)

B. Breast cancer

C. Endometrial cancer

D. Osteoporosis

E. Thrombosis

  1. Immunohistochemistry for gonadotroph adenoma:

A. FSHβ, LHβ, α-subunit, GATA3, SF1, PITX1

B. PRL, GH, ACTH, TSH

C. Only α-subunit

D. Only LHβ

E. Only FSHβ

  1. Transsphenoidal surgery for gonadotroph adenoma:

A. Indicated for mass effect; gross total resection ~70-80%; recurrence 10-20%

B. Never indicated

C. Only for hormonal syndrome

D. Radiotherapy first

E. Medical therapy first

  1. Post-op surveillance for gonadotroph adenoma:

A. MRI 3mo, 1yr then annually ×5yr; visual fields if chiasmal compression

B. No surveillance needed

C. Only hormone levels

D. PET-CT annually

E. Only if symptomatic

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma vs non-functioning pituitary adenoma:

A. Gonadotroph = IHC positive for FSH/LH subunits; NFPA = negative for all anterior pituitary hormones

B. Same thing

C. Gonadotroph secretes prolactin

D. NFPA secretes GH

E. No difference

  1. Medical treatment for gonadotroph adenoma:

A. No effective medical therapy (dopamine agonists, somatostatin analogues ineffective)

B. Cabergoline

C. Octreotide

D. Pegvisomant

E. Temozolomide

17. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 55yo man: bitemporal hemianopia, headache. MRI: 2.5cm pituitary macroadenoma, suprasellar extension. IHC: FSHβ+, LHβ+, α-subunit+. Diagnosis?

A. Gonadotroph adenoma

B. Non-functioning pituitary adenoma

C. Prolactinoma

D. Somatotroph adenoma

E. Corticotroph adenoma

  1. Same patient: post-transsphenoidal surgery, residual tumour on 3mo MRI. Management?

A. Surveillance MRI 6-12mo; radiotherapy if progression; no medical therapy

B. Cabergoline

C. Octreotide

D. Temozolomide

E. Re-operation immediately

  1. 8yo girl: breast development, vaginal bleeding. MRI: 1.8cm pituitary tumour. IHC: LHβ+, FSHβ+. Diagnosis?

A. Gonadotroph adenoma causing precocious puberty

B. Hypothalamic hamartoma

C. Ovarian tumour

D. Adrenal tumour

E. McCune-Albright

  1. 65yo woman: incidentally found 8mm pituitary microadenoma. IHC: FSHβ+, LHβ+. Asymptomatic, normal visual fields. Management?

A. Conservative: MRI 6-12mo then annually; no surgery unless growth/mass effect

B. Transsphenoidal surgery

C. Radiotherapy

D. Cabergoline

E. Octreotide

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma recurrence post-surgery: typical timing:

A. 5-10 years; surveillance MRI annually ×5yr then q2-3yr

B. 6 months

C. 1 year

D. 20 years

E. Never recurs

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Gonadotroph adenoma

A: Pituitary adenoma expressing FSH/LH/α-subunit; usually clinically NON-functioning

  • Q: Clinical presentation

A: Mass effect: headache, visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia), hypopituitarism

  • Q: IHC markers

A: FSHβ, LHβ, α-subunit, GATA3, SF1, PITX1

  • Q: Prevalence

A: ~15-20% of pituitary adenomas (2nd after NFPA); but many are clinically silent

  • Q: Pre-pubertal

A: Rare: precocious puberty (LH/FSH secretion)

  • Q: Post-menopausal

A: Rare: ovarian hyperstimulation (high FSH)

  • Q: Surgery

A: TSS for mass effect; GTR 70-80%; recurrence 10-20% at 10yr

  • Q: Medical therapy

A: NONE effective (DA, SST, pegvisomant all ineffective)

  • Q: Radiotherapy

A: If residual/progressive post-surgery; stereotactic or fractionated

  • Q: Surveillance

A: MRI 3mo, 1yr, then annually ×5yr; visual fields if chiasmal compression

  • Q: vs NFPA

A: Gonadotroph: IHC+ for gonadotropin subunits; NFPA: IHC- for all anterior hormones

  • Q: Silent gonadotroph

A: Most gonadotroph adenomas are 'silent' — no clinical hormonal syndrome

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Non-functioning (clinically silent) or minimally functioning; FSH/LH subunits elevated but no clinical syndrome — Gonadotroph adenomas are typically:
  1. FSH > LH (FSHβ subunit) — Most common hormone immunohistochemically detected in gonadotroph adenomas:
  1. Mass effect (headache, visual field defects, hypopituitarism) — NOT hormonal syndrome — Clinical presentation of gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. Precocious puberty (rare; LH/FSH secretion) — Gonadotroph adenoma in pre-pubertal child may cause:
  1. Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (high FSH → multiple follicles) — Post-menopausal women: gonadotroph adenoma may present with:
  1. FSHβ, LHβ, α-subunit, GATA3, SF1, PITX1 — Immunohistochemistry for gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. Indicated for mass effect; gross total resection ~70-80%; recurrence 10-20% — Transsphenoidal surgery for gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. MRI 3mo, 1yr then annually ×5yr; visual fields if chiasmal compression — Post-op surveillance for gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. Gonadotroph = IHC positive for FSH/LH subunits; NFPA = negative for all anterior pituitary hormones — Gonadotroph adenoma vs non-functioning pituitary adenoma:
  1. No effective medical therapy (dopamine agonists, somatostatin analogues ineffective) — Medical treatment for gonadotroph adenoma:

SBAs

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma — 55yo man: bitemporal hemianopia, headache. MRI: 2.5cm pituitary macroadenoma, suprasellar extension. IHC: FSHβ+, LHβ+, α-subunit+. Diagnosis?
  1. Surveillance MRI 6-12mo; radiotherapy if progression; no medical therapy — Same patient: post-transsphenoidal surgery, residual tumour on 3mo MRI. Management?
  1. Gonadotroph adenoma causing precocious puberty — 8yo girl: breast development, vaginal bleeding. MRI: 1.8cm pituitary tumour. IHC: LHβ+, FSHβ+. Diagnosis?

topics.title: Prolactinoma
topics.html:
[!info]
Prolactinoma = Prolactin-Secreting Pituitary Adenoma. Commonest Functioning Pituitary Adenoma (40-50%). Microprolactinoma (<10mm) 60%; Macroprolactinoma (≥10mm) 40%. Cabergoline = 1st Line Treatment. Macroprolactinoma in Pregnancy = Continue Cabergoline.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Diagnose prolactinoma using prolactin levels and MRI
  • [ ] Differentiate true prolactinoma from macroprolactinaemia and stalk effect
  • [ ] Apply cabergoline dosing and titration protocols
  • [ ] Manage prolactinoma in pregnancy (micro vs macro)
  • [ ] Apply withdrawal criteria and monitor for recurrence

2. Classification & Epidemiology

TypeSizeProlactin LevelPrevalence
Microprolactinoma<10mm500-5000 mIU/L~60%
Macroprolactinoma≥10mm>5000 mIU/L (Often >10,000)~40%
Giant>40mmVery HighRare
FeatureDetails
IncidenceMost Common Functioning Pituitary Adenoma (40-50%)
Sex RatioFemale > Male (5:1)
Peak Age20-40 Years
GeneticsMEN1 (Rare), AIP (Young-Onset Familial), GPR101

3. Clinical Presentation

FeatureMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
Prolactin Level500-5000 mIU/L>5000 mIU/L (Often >10,000)
PresentationGalactorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Infertility, Low LibidoVisual Field Defects (Bi-temporal Hemianopia), Headache, Hypopituitarism
Visual FieldsNormalBi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
HeadacheRareCommon

Other Clinical Features

SystemFeatures
ReproductiveGalactorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Oligomenorrhoea, Infertility, Low Libido, Erectile Dysfunction
BoneOsteoporosis (Oestrogen/Testosterone Deficiency)
MetabolicWeight Gain, Insulin Resistance

4. Differential Diagnosis of Hyperprolactinaemia

```

Prolactin >500 mIU/L

│

▼

EXCLUDE PHYSIOLOGICAL / PHARMACOLOGICAL CAUSES:

│

├── DRUGS: Antipsychotics (Risperidone > Olanzapine > Quetiapine), Metoclopramide, Antidepressants, OCP, Verapamil, Opioids

├── HYPOTHYROIDISM (TRH-driven) → Check TSH

├── RENAL FAILURE (Reduced Clearance)

├── PREGNANCY

├── NIPPLE STIMULATION / STRESS (Recent)

└── MACROPROLACTINAEMIA

│

▼

REPEAT PROLACTIN (Fasting, Non-stressed, Avoid Nipple Stimulation)

│

├── NORMAL → Transient/Physiological

│

└── PERSISTENTLY ELEVATED

│

▼

PEG PRECIPITATION TEST → MACROPROLACTINAEMIA?

│

├── YES → MACROPROLACTINAEMIA (Benign, No Treatment)

│

└── NO → TRUE HYPERPROLACTINAEMIA

│

▼

MRI PITUITARY (3mm Slices, Gadolinium)

│

├── NO TUMOUR + Prolactin <1000 → IDIOPATHIC / STALK EFFECT

├── MICROPROLACTINOMA (<10mm) → CABERGOLINE

├── MACROPROLACTINOMA (≥10mm) → CABERGOLINE / SURGERY

└── NO TUMOUR + Prolactin >1000 → STALK EFFECT / ECTOPIC (Rare)

```

5. Macroprolactinaemia

FeatureDetails
DefinitionProlactin Complexed with IgG Autoantibody (PRL-IgG Complex)
CharacteristicsHigh Molecular Weight (>150 kDa); Biologically Inactive
Prevalence10-25% of Hyperprolactinaemia
DiagnosisPEG (Polyethylene Glycol) Precipitation → Recovery <60% = Macroprolactinaemia
Clinical SignificanceBenign; No Pituitary Tumour; No Treatment Needed
PitfallCan Coexist with True Hyperprolactinaemia → Measure Monomeric PRL Post-PEG

6. Prolactinoma — Management

Microprolactinoma (<10mm)

AspectProtocol
1st LineCabergoline 0.25mg BD → Titrate Weekly by 0.25mg BD
Target Dose0.5-1mg BD (Max 2mg BD)
GoalProlactin Normalisation + Tumour Shrinkage (>50% Volume Reduction)
MonitoringProlactin q4-8w During Titration; MRI at 6-12mo if Macro Features
WithdrawalAfter 2-3 Years Normal Prolactin + No Tumour on MRI → Taper Over 3-6 Months

Macroprolactinoma (≥10mm)

AspectProtocol
1st LineCabergoline 0.5mg BD → Titrate to 1-2mg BD (Max 3mg BD)
TargetProlactin Normalisation + >50% Volume Reduction
Surgery IndicationsCabergoline Resistant/Intolerant; Apoplexy; Chiasmal Compression with Visual Loss Despite Medical Rx
Visual FieldsMandatory (Bi-temporal Hemianopia = Urgent Decompression)

Cabergoline Dosing Schedule

WeekMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
1-20.25mg BD0.5mg BD
3-40.5mg BD1mg BD
5-81mg BD1.5-2mg BD
Max2mg BD3mg BD (Rarely >2mg)

7. Cabergoline — Key Pharmacology

PropertyDetails
MechanismD2 Dopamine Receptor Agonist → Inhibits Prolactin Secretion + Shrinks Tumour
Half-Life63-69 Hours (Long; Allows BD/Weekly Dosing)
OnsetProlactin Falls Within Hours; Tumour Shrinkage Over Weeks-Months
Side EffectsNausea (40%), Postural Hypotension, Nasal Congestion, Impulse Control Disorders (Rare), Valvulopathy (High Dose, Chronic)
MonitoringEchocardiogram If Dose >2mg/day or Duration >5 Years; Prolactin q3-6mo

8. Withdrawal / Discontinuation Criteria

CriteriaRequired
Duration≥2-3 Years on Stable Dose
BiochemicalProlactin Normalised on Stable Dose for ≥2 Years
RadiologicalNo Tumour Visible on MRI (or Microadenoma Resolved)
ProcessTaper Cabergoline Over 3-6 Months; Recheck Prolactin q3mo; Restart if Recurrence

9. Pregnancy & Prolactinoma

ScenarioMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
Pre-ConceptionStop Cabergoline if Prolactin Normal + MRI StableContinue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%)
During PregnancyStop Cabergoline (Low Growth Risk)Continue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%)
MonitoringProlactin q Trimester; Visual Fields q Trimester (If Macro)Prolactin q Trimester; Visual Fields q Trimester; MRI if Symptoms
DeliveryVaginal PreferredC-Section if Chiasmal Compression Risk
PostpartumBreastfeeding → Prolactin ↑; Cabergoline Suppresses LactationBreastfeeding → Cabergoline Suppresses Lactation

Key Point: Microprolactinoma → Stop Cabergoline; Macroprolactinoma → Continue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%).

10. Stalk Effect / Disconnection Hyperprolactinaemia

FeatureDetails
MechanismPituitary Stalk Compression → ↓ Dopamine Delivery to Lactotrophs → Disinhibition
Prolactin LevelUsually <2000 mIU/L (Rarely >3000)
MRINon-Functioning Macroadenoma Compressing Stalk / Suprasellar Mass
ManagementTreat Underlying Mass (Surgery if Macroadenoma); Prolactin Normalises Post-Decompression
Key DifferentiatorVisual Field Defects Common; Other Pituitary Deficits Often Coexist

11. Drug-Induced Hyperprolactinaemia

Drug ClassTypical Prolactin LevelManagement
Antipsychotics (Typical > Atypical)Risperidone > Olanzapine > Quetiapine > Clozapine > Aripiprazole (Low)Switch to Lower-Risk Agent (Aripiprazole); If Essential → Monitor
Metoclopramide / DomperidoneD2 Antagonism (GI Motility)Switch if Possible; Reduce Dose
Antidepressants (SSRIs, TCAs)Serotonergic ModulationSwitch if Possible
Opioidsμ-Receptor → ↓ DopamineReduce Dose / Switch
Verapamil / Alpha-MethyldopaCentral D2 EffectSwitch if Possible
OCPOestrogen → Lactotroph HyperplasiaSwitch to POP / Non-Hormonal

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Prolactinoma

A: Prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma (~40% functioning adenomas)

  • Q: Prolactin levels

A: Micro: 100-2000; Macro: >2000 (often >5000)

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: Prolactin >2000 + pituitary adenoma on MRI

  • Q: Cabergoline

A: 0.25mg BD titrate; normalise PRL; shrink 80-90%

  • Q: Surgery

A: If visual loss / resistant / pregnant intolerant

  • Q: Pregnancy

A: Stop cabergoline; monitor PRL/visual fields; restart postpartum if macro

  • Q: Dopamine agonist resistance

A: PRL not normalising on max dose; switch bromocriptine / surgery / RT

  • Q: Macroprolactin

A: High MW; biologically inactive; PEG precipitation to exclude

  • Q: Stalk effect

A: PRL <100 mIU/L; dopamine dishibition; NOT prolactinoma

  • Q: Bromocriptine

A: Ergot derivative; more SE; 2nd line if cabergoline fails

topics.title: Siadh
topics.html:
[!info]
SIADH = Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH Secretion. Commonest Cause of Euvolaemic Hyponatraemia. Inappropriate ADH Secretion → Water Retention → Dilutional Hyponatraemia. Diagnosis = Exclusion (Schwartz-Bartter Criteria). Treatment: Fluid Restriction → Demeclocycline → Tolvaptan (V2 Antagonist).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply Schwartz-Bartter diagnostic criteria for SIADH
  • [ ] Differentiate SIADH from Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) and Hypovolaemic Hyponatraemia
  • [ ] Apply stepwise management: Fluid Restriction → Demeclocycline → Tolvaptan
  • [ ] Manage severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with hypertonic saline
  • [ ] Identify and treat underlying causes (drugs, malignancy, CNS, pulmonary)

2. Diagnostic Criteria (Schwartz-Bartter)

CriterionRequirement
1. HyponatraemiaSerum Na+ <135 mmol/L
2. Hypo-osmolalitySerum Osm <275 mOsm/kg
3. Inappropriately Concentrated UrineUrine Osm >100 mOsm/kg (Usually >200)
4. EuvolaemiaNormal Volume Status (No Oedema, Normal JVP, Normal BP)
5. Elevated Urine SodiumUrine Na+ >20 mmol/L (Euvolaemic)
6. Normal Thyroid/Adrenal/RenalTSH Normal, Cortisol Normal, Creatinine Normal
7. No DiureticsExclude Recent Diuretic Use (Within 1-2 Weeks)

All 6 Criteria Must Be Met for Diagnosis.

3. Aetiology — SIADH Causes

CategoryCauses
Drugs (Commonest)SSRIs, SNRIs, Tricyclics, Carbamazepine, Oxcarbazepine, Chlorpropamide, Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine, MDMA
MalignancySmall Cell Lung Cancer (Most Common), Pancreatic, Thymoma, Lymphoma, Head & Neck
CNS DisordersStroke, Haemorrhage, Meningitis, Encephalitis, Guillain-Barré, Tumours, Trauma, Neurosurgery
PulmonaryPneumonia, TB, Lung Abscess, Positive Pressure Ventilation
EndocrineHypothyroidism, Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's)
Post-OperativePost-Neurosurgery, Post-Abdominal Surgery
OtherPorphyria, Acute Intermittent Porphyria, Nausea/Vomiting

4. Diagnostic Approach

Stepwise Diagnosis

```

Hyponatraemia (Serum Na+ <135 mmol/L)

│

├── ASSESS VOLUME STATUS

│ ├── HYPOVOLAEMIC → Hypovolaemic Hyponatraemia

│ │ (Diuretics, GI Losses, Cerebral Salt Wasting)

│ │

│ ├── HYPERVOLAEMIC → Hypervolaemic Hyponatraemia

│ │ (HF, Cirrhosis, Nephrotic Syndrome)

│ │

└── EUVOLAEMIC → SIADH (Most Common)

│

├── EXCLUDE: Hypothyroidism, Adrenal Insufficiency, Renal Failure

├── EXCLUDE: Diuretic Use (Recent)

├── APPLY SCHWARTZ-BARTTER CRITERIA (All 6 Must Be Met)

└── CONFIRM SIADH

```

Diagnostic Criteria (Schwartz-Bartter) — All 6 Must Be Met

#CriterionRequirement
1HyponatraemiaSerum Na+ <135 mmol/L
2Hypo-osmolalitySerum Osm <275 mOsm/kg
3Inappropriately Concentrated UrineUrine Osm >100 mOsm/kg (Usually >200)
4EuvolaemiaNormal JVP, No Oedema, Normal BP
5Elevated Urine Na+Urine Na+ >20 mmol/L (Euvolaemic)
6Normal Endocrine/RenalTSH Normal, Cortisol Normal, Creatinine Normal; No Diuretics

5. Differential Diagnosis — Euvolaemic Hyponatraemia

ConditionSerum Na+Urine OsmUrine Na+Volume StatusKey Differentiator
SIADHLow>100>20EuvolaemicInappropriate ADH
Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW)Low>100>20 (Often Higher)HypovolaemicHypovolaemia + High Urine Na+
HypothyroidismLowVariableVariableEuvolaemicTSH ↑, Low fT4
Adrenal InsufficiencyLowHighHighHypovolaemicCortisol Low, ACTH ↑, Hyperpigmentation
Glucocorticoid DeficiencyLow>100>20HypovolaemicCortisol Low
Reset OsmostatStable Low (125-130)Inappropriately High>20EuvolaemicStable Na+ 125-130
Diuretic UseLowVariable>20Euvo/HypovolaemicRecent Diuretic History
Beer PotomaniaLowLowLowEuvolaemicLow Solute Intake History

6. SIADH vs Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) — Critical Distinction

FeatureSIADHCerebral Salt Wasting (CSW)
Volume StatusEuvolaemicHypovolaemic
Urine Na+>20 mmol/L>20 (Often Higher)
Urine Osm>100>100 (Often Higher)
Serum Na+LowLow
JVPNormalLow
Skin TurgorNormalDry
Postural BPNormalPostural Drop >20 mmHg
Urine OutputLow/NormalHigh (Polyuria)
Uric AcidLow/NormalHigh
ManagementFluid RestrictionSaline Resuscitation (+ Fludrocortisone)
PathophysiologyADH Excess → Water RetentionRenal Salt Wasting → Volume Depletion → ADH Release
Key: Volume Status is the Key Discriminator — SIADH = Euvolaemic; CSW = Hypovolaemic.

7. Management Algorithm

Stepwise Management (Euvoleaemic Hyponatraemia = SIADH)

```

SIADH Confirmed (All 6 Criteria Met)

│

▼

IDENTIFY & TREAT UNDERLYING CAUSE

│

├── Drug-Induced → Stop/Reduce Offending Drug

├── Malignancy → Treat Cancer

├── CNS/Pulmonary → Treat Underlying

│

▼

STEP 1: FLUID RESTRICTION (<1 L/day)

│

├── Effective → Goal Na+ Rise 0.5-1 mmol/L/day; Target Na+ >130

├── IF Na+ Rise <0.5 mmol/L/day OR Severe Symptoms → STEP 2

│

▼

STEP 2: DEMECLOCYCLINE 600-1200mg/day (600-1200mg/day in Divided Doses)

│ MoA: Induces Nephrogenic DI (ADH Resistance)

│ Side Effects: Photosensitivity, Nephrotoxicity, GI

│ Monitor: Renal Function, Photosensitivity

│

▼

STEP 3: TOLVAPTAN (V2 Receptor Antagonist) — Severe/Refractory

│ Dose: 15mg OD PO → Titrate to 30, 45, 60mg OD

│ Target: Na+ Rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h (Max 8-10 mmol/L/24h)

│ Monitoring: Na+ q4-6h, Liver Function (Hepatotoxicity Risk)

│ Max Duration: 30 Days (US FDA); Longer in Some Countries

│

▼

STEP 4: HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NaCl) — SEVERE SYMPTOMATIC HYPONATRAEMIA

│ Na+ <120 mmol/L OR Seizures/Coma

│ 3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (Target ↑Na+ 4-6 mmol/L/24h)

│ Monitor: Na+ q1-2h, Fluid Balance, Neurological Status

```

8. Severe Symptomatic Hyponatraemia — Emergency Protocol

SeveritySerum Na+Neurological StatusImmediate Treatment
Mild130-134Asymptomatic/MildFluid Restriction
Moderate125-129Nausea, Headache, Mild ConfusionFluid Restriction ± Demeclocycline
Severe<125Seizures, Coma, Severe Confusion3% Hypertonic Saline 100-150mL over 1-2h
Life-Threatening<115Coma, Seizures, Respiratory Arrest3% Saline 100-150mL over 1h + ICU

Hypertonic Saline Protocol

ParameterTarget
Na+ Rise Rate4-6 mmol/L in First 24h (Max 8 mmol/L/24h)
Max Correction8-10 mmol/L in 24h (Avoid Central Pontine Myelinolysis)
Infusion3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (Repeat if Needed)
MonitorNa+ q1-2h, Neuro Checks, Fluid Balance, Urine Output

9. Specific Causes & Management

Drug-Induced SIADH

Drug ClassExamplesManagement
AntidepressantsSSRIs (Sertraline, Fluoxetine), TCAs, MAOIsStop/Reduce; Switch to Alternative
Carbamazepine / OxcarbazepineCommon CauseSwitch to Alternative AED
Chemotherapy (Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine)ChemoSupportive; Demeclocycline if Needed
Opioids / MDMAAcuteStop Drug; Supportive

Malignancy-Related SIADH

TumourFrequencyManagement
Small Cell Lung CancerMost CommonTreat Cancer; Demeclocycline / Tolvaptan
Pancreatic / Thymoma / LymphomaLess CommonTreat Cancer

SIADH in CNS Disorders

ConditionManagement
Stroke / HaemorrhageSupportive; Fluid Restrict; Demeclocycline if Persistent
Meningitis / EncephalitisTreat Infection; Manage SIADH
NeurosurgeryPost-Op SIADH Common; Transient Usually
TraumaMonitor; Fluid Restrict; Demeclocycline if Persistent

10. Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) — Detailed

Diagnostic Criteria

FeatureRequirement
CNS DiseasePresent (SAH, TBI, Neurosurgery, Tumour)
HyponatraemiaSerum Na+ <135
Volume StatusHypovolaemic (Low JVP, Dry Mucosa, Postural Hypotension)
Urine Na+>20 mmol/L (Often >100)
Urine Osm>100 mOsm/kg (Often >300)
Urine OutputHigh (Polyuria)

Management of CSW

StepAction
1. Volume ResuscitationIV 0.9% NaCl 1-2L Bolus → Then Maintenance
2. Fludrocortisone0.05-0.2mg OD (Mineralocorticoid Replacement)
3. Salt SupplementationNaCl Tablets 1-2g TDS
4. MonitorNa+, K+, Volume Status, UOP

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
SIADH = Euvolaemic HyponatraemiaMost Common Cause of Hyponatraemia in Hospital
Schwartz-Bartter Criteria6 Criteria — All Must Be Met
SIADH vs CSWVolume Status = Key Discriminator (Euvolaemic vs Hypovolaemic)
CSWHypovolaemic + High Urine Na+ + High Urine Osm → Saline + Fludrocortisone
First-Line SIADHFluid Restriction <1L/day
Second-LineDemeclocycline 600-1200mg/day (Induces Nephrogenic DI)
Third-LineTolvaptan 15-60mg OD (V2 Antagonist); Max 30 Days (US)
Severe Hyponatraemia3% NaCl 100-150mL/1-2h (Target ↑ Na 4-6 mmol/L/24h)
Correction Rate4-6 mmol/L/24h Max (Avoid Central Pontine Myelinolysis)
CSW vs SIADHCSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic
CSW ManagementIV Saline + Fludrocortisone + Salt Tablets
TolvaptanV2 Antagonist; Max 30 Days (US); Monitor LFTs
DemeclocyclineInduces Nephrogenic DI; Photosensitivity, Nephrotoxicity
Rapid Correction>8-10 mmol/L/24h → Central Pontine Myelinolysis (Osmotic Demyelination)

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
SIADH vs CSWVolume Status is Key: SIADH = Euvolaemic; CSW = Hypovolaemic
Urine Na+ in BothBoth Have High Urine Na+ (>20); Volume Status Differentiates
CSW ManagementSaline + Fludrocortisone (Not Fluid Restriction!)
Rapid Correction>8-10 mmol/L/24h → Central Pontine Myelinolysis (Osmotic Demyelination)
DemeclocyclineInduces Nephrogenic DI; Photosensitivity / Nephrotoxicity
TolvaptanV2 Antagonist; Max 30 Days (US); Hepatotoxicity Risk
Fludrocortisone in CSWMineralocorticoid Replacement → Salt Retention
Reset OsmostatStable Mild Hyponatraemia (125-130); No Treatment Needed
Beer PotomaniaLow Solute Intake → Low Urine Osm; Fluid Restriction Works
Tea & Toast DietLow Solute → Hyponatraemia; Improve Nutrition

13. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((SIADH))

Criteria (Schwartz-Bartter)

  1. Hyponatraemia (<135)
  2. Hypo-osmolality (<275)
  3. Urine Osm >100
  4. Euvolaemic
  5. Urine Na+ >20
  6. Normal Thyroid/Adrenal/Renal

Aetiology

Drugs: SSRIs, Carbamazepine, Cyclophosphamide

Malignancy: SCLC (Most Common)

CNS: Stroke, Meningitis, Trauma

Pulmonary: Pneumonia, TB

Endocrine: Hypothyroidism, Adrenal Insufficiency

Differential

SIADH: Euvolaemic

CSW: Hypovolaemic (Hypovolaemic + High Urine Na+)

Hypovolaemic: Urine Na+ <20

Hypervolaemic: Oedema + Hyponatraemia

Management

  1. Fluid Restriction <1L/day
  2. Demeclocycline 600-1200mg/day
  3. Tolvaptan 15-60mg OD (V2 Antagonist)
  4. Hypertonic Saline (Severe)

CSW

Hypovolaemic + High Urine Na+

Rx: Saline + Fludrocortisone + Salt

Severe Hyponatraemia

3% NaCl 100-150mL/1-2h

Target ↑Na 4-6 mmol/L/24h

Max 8-10 mmol/L/24h

```

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
SIADH DefinitionEuvolaemic Hyponatraemia + Inappropriate ADH
Schwartz-Bartter 6 CriteriaHyponatraemia, Hypo-osmolality, Urine Osm >100, Euvolaemia, Urine Na+ >20, Normal Endocrine/Renal
SIADH vs CSWVolume Status = Key (Euvolaemic vs Hypovolaemic)
CSW ManagementSaline + Fludrocortisone + Salt (Opposite of SIADH)
First-Line SIADHFluid Restriction <1L/day
DemeclocyclineInduces Nephrogenic DI; 600-1200mg/day; Photosensitivity Risk
TolvaptanV2 Antagonist; 15-60mg OD; Hepatotoxicity Risk; Max 30 Days (US)
Severe Hyponatraemia3% NaCl 100-150mL/1-2h; Target ↑Na 4-6 mmol/L/24h
Rapid Correction LimitMax 8-10 mmol/L/24h (Avoid CPM)
CSW TreatmentVolume Resuscitation + Fludrocortisone (Not Fluid Restrict)
CSW vs SIADHVolume Status: CSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic
Rapid Correction Risk>10 mmol/L/24h → Central Pontine Myelinolysis
Drug CausesSSRIs, Carbamazepine, Cyclophosphamide, MDMA
Malignancy SIADHSCLC (Most Common)
CSW AetiologySAH, TBI, Neurosurgery, CNS Tumours
Tolvaptan MonitoringLFTs (Hepatotoxicity); Na+ q4-6h

16. MCQs (10)

  1. SIADH diagnostic criteria (Schwartz-Bartter):

A. Hyponatraemia + hypo-osmolality + urine osm >100 + urine Na>20 + euvolaemia + normal thyroid/adrenal/renal

B. High urine output

C. Hypervolaemia

D. High serum osm

E. Low urine sodium

  1. SIADH urine osmolality is typically:

A. >100 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated relative to serum)

B. <100 mOsm/kg

C. Equal to serum

D. >500 mOsm/kg always

E. Variable

  1. Most common cause of SIADH:

A. Drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, PPIs, opioids)

B. Malignancy

C. CNS disease

D. Pulmonary disease

E. Post-op

  1. Malignancy-associated SIADH most common:

A. Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) — ADH ectopic production

B. Breast cancer

C. Lymphoma

D. Pancreatic cancer

E. Prostate cancer

  1. SIADH management step 1:

A. Fluid restriction <1-1.5 L/day

B. 3% saline

C. Tolvaptan

D. Demeclocycline

E. Furosemide

  1. Demeclocycline for SIADH:

A. Inhibits ADH action on V2 receptor → nephrogenic DI-like state; onset 3-5 days

B. V2 agonist

C. V1a antagonist

D. Increases ADH

E. Loop diuretic

  1. Tolvaptan (vaptan) in SIADH:

A. V2 antagonist → aquaresis (water excretion without Na+ loss); hepatotoxicity risk

B. V2 agonist

C. V1a agonist

D. Increases ADH

E. Causes hyponatraemia

  1. Acute severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures/coma) in SIADH:

A. 3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (target Na+ rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h, max 8 in 24h)

B. Fluid restriction

C. Tolvaptan

D. Demeclocycline

E. Furosemide alone

  1. SIADH vs Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW):

A. SIADH = euvolaemic; CSW = hypovolaemic + polyuria + high urine Na+

B. Both euvolaemic

C. Both hypovolaemic

D. SIADH = polyuria

E. CSW = low urine Na+

  1. SIADH monitoring on fluid restriction:

A. Daily weights, serum Na+ q1-2d, fluid balance, urine output, urine Na+/osm

B. Weekly Na+ only

C. No monitoring

D. Only weight

E. Only urine output

17. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 70yo woman on sertraline: confused, Na+ 118, euvolaemic, serum osm 250, urine osm 380, urine Na+ 55, normal TSH/cortisol/creatinine. Management?

A. Fluid restriction <1L + stop sertraline; if Na+<120/seizures → 3% saline 100-150mL

B. 3% saline rapid correction

C. Tolvaptan immediately

D. Demeclocycline

E. Furosemide + saline

  1. 60yo man with SCLC: Na+ 122, asymptomatic. Urine osm 450, urine Na+ 60, euvolaemic. Step 1?

A. Fluid restriction 800-1000 mL/day; treat underlying malignancy

B. Tolvaptan

C. Demeclocycline

D. 3% saline

E. Continue fluids

  1. Same patient on fluid restriction: Na+ 120 after 48h. Next?

A. Tolvaptan 15mg OD (titrate to 30-60mg); monitor Na+ q4-6h, LFTs

B. Demeclocycline

C. 3% saline

D. Increase fluid restriction

E. Furosemide

  1. Post-op day 2: hip fracture repair, Na+ 125, oliguric, urine osm 500, urine Na+ 80, JVP low, postural drop. Diagnosis?

A. Cerebral Salt Wasting (hypovolaemic) — NOT SIADH

B. SIADH

C. Reset osmostat

D. Diuretic effect

E. Adrenal insufficiency

  1. Tolvaptan contraindications:

A. Anuria, hypovolaemia, pregnancy, concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors

B. Hypertension

C. Diabetes

D. CKD

E. Liver disease only

18. Flashcards

  • Q: SIADH criteria

A: Hyponatraemia + hypo-osmolality + urine osm >100 + urine Na>20 + euvolaemia + normal TSH/cortisol/renal

  • Q: Pathophysiology

A: Inappropriate ADH → water retention → dilutional hyponatraemia + natriuresis

  • Q: Causes

A: Drugs (SSRIs #1), SCLC, CNS (stroke, TBI), pulmonary (pneumonia), post-op, endocrine (hypothyroid, AI)

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: Exclusion: euvolaemic hyponatraemia + concentrated urine + high urine Na+

  • Q: Fluid restriction

A: 1st line: <1-1.5 L/day; target Na+ rise 1-2 mmol/L/day

  • Q: Demeclocycline

A: Tetracycline; V2 inhibition → nephrogenic DI; 150mg QDS; onset 3-5d; photosensitivity, nephrotoxicity

  • Q: Tolvaptan

A: V2 antagonist; aquaresis; 15-60mg OD; monitor Na+ q4-6h, LFTs; hepatotoxicity; max 30d US

  • Q: Acute severe

A: Seizures/coma: 3% NaCl 100-150mL/1-2h; target ↑4-6 mmol/L/24h (max 8); avoid overcorrection

  • Q: CSW vs SIADH

A: CSW: hypovolaemic + polyuria + high urine Na+ (post-neurosurg/SAH/TBI); Rx: IV saline + fludrocortisone

  • Q: Reset osmostat

A: SIADH variant: regulates at lower set-point; stable mild hyponatraemia; no treatment needed

  • Q: Drug SIADH

A: SSRIs/SNRIs most common; carbamazepine, PPIs, opioids, cyclophosphamide

  • Q: Malignancy

A: SCLC (ADH ectopic); monitor Na+ during chemo; treat cancer = treat SIADH

  • Q: Monitoring

A: Daily weights, serum Na+ q1-2d, fluid balance, urine output, urine Na+/osm

  1. 3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (target Na+ rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h, max 8 in 24h) — Acute severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures/coma) in SIADH:
  1. SIADH = euvolaemic; CSW = hypovolaemic + polyuria + high urine Na+ — SIADH vs Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW):
  1. Daily weights, serum Na+ q1-2d, fluid balance, urine output, urine Na+/osm — SIADH monitoring on fluid restriction:

topics.title: Tsh Secreting Adenoma
topics.html:
[!info]
TSH-oma = TSH-Secreting Pituitary Adenoma. Rare (1-2% of Pituitary Adenomas). Causes Central Hyperthyroidism: fT4/fT3 ↑ with Inappropriately Normal/High TSH. TRH Test: Paradoxical TSH Rise. Surgery = 1st Line.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation of central hyperthyroidism (thyrotoxicosis with non-suppressed TSH)
  • [ ] Differentiate TSH-oma from Graves disease and thyroid hormone resistance (RTH)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (TSH inappropriately normal/high with elevated fT4/fT3)
  • [ ] Apply TRH stimulation test and MRI pituitary for diagnosis
  • [ ] Outline management (surgery 1st line, somatostatin analogues, radiotherapy)

2. Epidemiology & Pathophysiology

FeatureDetails
Frequency1-2% of Pituitary Adenomas
DemographicsAdults (Peak 40-50y); No Sex Predilection
Cell OriginThyrotroph Adenoma (TSH-Secreting)
GeneticsGNAS (Activating), GNA11, USP8, ATP1A1; MEN1 (Rare)
SecretionTSH + α-Subunit (Often Co-Secretion)
Feedback ResistanceImpaired Negative Feedback — TSH Fails to Suppress with High fT4/fT3

3. Clinical Presentation

FeatureTSH-omaGraves Disease
Thyrotoxic SymptomsWeight Loss, Tachycardia, Tremor, Heat IntoleranceIdentical
GoitrePresent (Usually)Present
OphthalmopathyAbsent (Rare)Present (25-50%)
Dermopathy/AcropachyAbsentMay Be Present
TSHInappropriately Normal/HighSuppressed (<0.01 mIU/L)
TRAbNegativePositive (>95%)
α-SubunitElevated (Co-Secreted)Normal
TRH TestParadoxical TSH RiseBlunted (No Rise)

4. Biochemical Profile

ParameterTSH-omaGraves DiseaseRTH (THRβ Mutation)
TSHNormal / High (Inappropriately Normal)Suppressed (<0.01)Normal / High
fT4 / fT3ElevatedElevatedElevated
TRAbNegativePositive (>95%)Negative
α-SubunitElevated (Co-Secreted)NormalNormal
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 (Often >1.0)<1.0Variable
TRH Test (100µg IV)Paradoxical TSH Rise (>2mIU/L or >50% ↑)Blunted (No Rise)Exaggerated (Central Resistance)
RAI UptakeDiffuse ↑Diffuse ↑Normal/↑
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma (Usually >10mm)NormalNormal

5. Diagnostic Algorithm

```

Thyrotoxicosis (fT4/fT3 ↑ + Symptoms)

│

▼

TSH LEVEL

│

├── TSH SUPPRESSED (<0.01) → GRAVES DISEASE (TRAb+)

│

└── TSH NORMAL / HIGH → CENTRAL HYPERTHYROIDISM

│

├── TRAb POSITIVE → GRAVES (Rare Co-Existence)

│

├── TRAb NEGATIVE + TSH NORMAL/HIGH

│ │

│ ├── TRH TEST: PARADOXICAL TSH RISE → TSH-OMA

│ │

│ └── MRI PITUITARY → Macroadenoma (>10mm Usually)

│

└── THRA MUTATION (RTH) → TSH Normal/High + fT4/fT3 ↑ + TRAb Negative + FHx

```

6. Diagnostic Criteria (Consensus)

CriterionTSH-oma
TSHInappropriately Normal/High (≥1.0 mIU/L) with fT4/fT3 ↑
fT4/fT3Elevated
TRAbNegative
α-SubunitElevated (Often >TSH)
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 (Often >>1.0)
TRH TestParadoxical TSH Rise (>2 mIU/L or >50% Rise)
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma (>10mm) Usually

7. TRH Stimulation Test

ProtocolDetails
DoseTRH 100-200µg IV Bolus
SamplingTSH at 0, 20, 40, 60, 90, 120min
Normal ResponseTSH Peak 5-15 mIU/L at 20-30min; Return to Baseline by 60-120min
TSH-oma ResponseParadoxical Rise → TSH ↑↑ (Often >50% Rise or >2 mIU/L Above Baseline)
GravesBlunted/No Rise (TSH Suppressed)
RTHExaggerated Rise (Central Resistance)

8. Diagnosis & Localisation

MRI Pituitary (Gold Standard)

FeatureFinding
LesionMacroadenoma (>10mm) in >90%
EnhancementHypointense on T1 Post-Gad (Delayed Enhancement vs Normal Pituitary)
ExtensionSuprasellar Extension, Cavernous Sinus Invasion (Knosp Grading)

α-Subunit

FeatureSignificance
ElevatedCo-Secreted with TSH by Thyrotroph Adenoma
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 (Often >>1.0) — Highly Suggestive

9. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionTSHfT4/fT3TRAbα-SubunitTRH Test
TSH-omaNormal/HighHighNegativeHighParadoxical Rise
GravesSuppressed (<0.01)HighPositiveNormalBlunted
RTH (THRβ)Normal/HighHighNegativeNormalExaggerated Rise
T3 ThyrotoxicosisSuppressedfT3↑, fT4 NormalNegativeNormalBlunted

10. Management

First-Line: Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)

AspectDetails
IndicationAll TSH-omas (Micro/Macro)
ApproachEndoscopic Endonasal TSS (Preferred)
GoalGross Total Resection (GTR)
Cure CriteriaTSH Normalisation + fT4/fT3 Normalisation

Surgery Outcomes

Tumour SizeGTR RateRemission Rate
Microadenoma (<10mm)80-90%70-80%
Macroadenoma (>10mm)50-70%40-60%

Pre-Op Medical Preparation

AgentDosePurpose
Somatostatin Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide)Standard DosesPre-Op ↓ TSH → ↓ Vascularity; ↑ Resection Rate
Beta-BlockersPropranolol 40-80mg QDSSymptomatic Control (Tachycardia)
Antithyroid DrugsNOT Routine (May Worsen Goitre)

11. Medical Therapy (If Surgery Not Feasible / Residual Disease)

Somatostatin Analogues (1st Line Medical)

AgentDoseRegimen
Octreotide LAR20-40mg IMq4 Weeks
Lanreotide Autogel60-120mg SCq4 Weeks
Pasireotide LAR40-60mg IMq4 Weeks (2nd Line; ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk)
EffectDetails
TSH Suppression50-70% Normalise TSH/fT4
Tumour Shrinkage30-50% Volume Reduction
α-SubunitNormalises with TSH

Dopamine Agonists (Adjunct)

DrugDoseEfficacy
Cabergoline0.5-2mg BDModest TSH Suppression (30-40%)
Bromocriptine2.5-15mg/dayLess Effective

12. Radiotherapy

IndicationModalityDose
Residual/Recurrent Post-TSSStereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)15-25 Gy (Single Fraction)
Unresectable / RecurrentFractionated RT45-50 Gy / 25-28 Fractions
Latency5-10 Years for Biochemical Control

13. Post-Operative Follow-up

TimepointInvestigations
Day 1TSH, fT4, fT3, α-Subunit
Week 1-2TSH, fT4, fT3, α-Subunit
6 WeeksTSH, fT4, fT3, α-Subunit, MRI
6 MonthsFull Panel + MRI
Annual (Lifelong)TSH, fT4, fT3, α-Subunit, MRI (Yearly ×5yr, then 2-3yrly)

Remission Criteria

ParameterTarget
TSHNormalised (Within Reference Range)
fT4/fT3Normal
α-SubunitNormalised

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TSH-oma Prevalence1-2% of Pituitary Adenomas
Key Biochemical FeatureTSH Inappropriately Normal/High with fT4/fT3 ↑
TRAbNegative (Differentiates from Graves)
α-SubunitMarkedly Elevated (Co-Secreted); α/TSH Ratio >1
TRH TestParadoxical TSH Rise (>50% Rise or >2mIU/L Rise)
TSH-oma vs GravesTSH-oma: TSH Normal/High + TRAb (-); Graves: TSH Suppressed + TRAb (+)
TSH-oma vs RTHTSH-oma: Pituitary Adenoma on MRI; RTH: THRβ Mutation, No Pituitary Mass
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 Suggests TSH-oma
MRIMacroadenoma >10mm (Usually); Hypointense on T1 Post-Gad
1st Line TreatmentTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
Medical TherapySomatostatin Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide) 1st Line; Cabergoline Adjunct
Radiotherapy2nd Line (Residual/Recurrent); Delayed Effect (5-10yr)
TRH TestParadoxical TSH Rise = TSH-oma; Blunted = Graves; Exaggerated = RTH
α-SubunitCo-Secreted with TSH; ↑ in TSH-oma
TSH-oma vs RTHRTH: THRβ Mutation, Generalised Resistance, Goitre, Tachycardia

15. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
TSH-oma vs GravesTSH-oma: TSH Normal/High + TRAb(-); Graves: TSH Suppressed + TRAb+
TSH-oma vs RTHTSH-oma: Pituitary Adenoma; RTH: THRβ Mutation, No Pituitary Mass
TRH TestTSH-oma: ↑ TSH (Paradoxical); Graves: Blunted; RTH: Exaggerated
α-Subunit Ratioα/TSH >1.0 = TSH-oma; <1.0 = Normal/Other
α-Subunit SourceCo-Secreted with TSH by Thyrotroph Adenoma
Medical RxSomatostatin Analogues 1st Line (Octreotide/Lanreotide)
CabergolineModest TSH Suppression (30-40%); Adjunct
RadiotherapyDelayed Effect (5-10yr); For Residual/Recurrent

19. MCQs (10)

  1. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (TSHoma) prevalence:

A. ~1-2% of pituitary adenomas; rare

B. 10-15%

C. 30-40%

D. 50%

E. Most common

  1. TSHoma biochemical hallmark:

A. Inappropriately normal/elevated TSH with elevated fT4/fT3 (loss of negative feedback)

B. Low TSH + high fT4

C. High TSH + low fT4

D. Normal TSH + normal fT4

E. Undetectable TSH

  1. Alpha-subunit in TSHoma:

A. Elevated; α-subunit:TSH molar ratio >1 (normal <1)

B. Low

C. Normal

D. Only TSH elevated

E. Only α-subunit elevated

  1. TSHoma TRH test:

A. Blunted/absent TSH response to TRH (autonomous secretion)

B. Exaggerated response

C. Normal response

D. Prolactin rise

E. GH rise

  1. TSHoma T3 suppression test:

A. Failure of TSH suppression with T3 (autonomous)

B. Normal suppression

C. Paradoxical rise

D. No test available

E. Only for acromegaly

  1. TSHoma imaging:

A. MRI pituitary: macroadenoma >10mm (80%); may have cystic component

B. Normal pituitary

C. Microadenoma only

D. No imaging needed

E. CT only

  1. TSHoma treatment 1st line:

A. Transsphenoidal surgery (TSS); gross total resection 60-70%

B. Somatostatin analogues

C. Thionamides

D. Radioactive iodine

E. Observation

  1. Pre-op preparation for TSHoma surgery:

A. Euthyroid state: thionamides (carbimazole) ± beta-blocker ± potassium iodide (short-term)

B. SST analogues only

C. RAI

D. No preparation

E. Steroids only

  1. Somatostatin analogues for TSHoma:

A. Octreotide/lanreotide: ↓ TSH, ↓ thyroid hormones; tumour shrinkage in 30-50%

B. No effect

C. Worsens hyperthyroidism

D. Only for acromegaly

E. Only for carcinoid

  1. TSHoma vs Resistance to Thyroid Hormone (RTH):

A. TSHoma: pituitary tumour, α-subunit↑, α:TSH>1, TRH blunted; RTH: genetic (THRB), goitre, tachycardia, normal pituitary

B. Same

C. RTH has pituitary tumour

D. TSHoma genetic

E. No difference

20. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 45yo woman: weight loss, tremor, tachycardia, fT4 35, fT3 12, TSH 2.5 (inappropriately normal). MRI: 1.2cm pituitary macroadenoma. α-subunit:TSH ratio 2.5. Diagnosis?

A. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (TSHoma)

B. Graves disease

C. Thyroid hormone resistance (RTH)

D. T3 toxicosis

E. Factitious thyrotoxicosis

  1. Same patient: pre-op preparation for TSS?

A. Carbimazole 20-40mg/day + propranolol until euthyroid; short-term KI (7-10d) pre-op

B. RAI

C. Octreotide only

D. No preparation

E. Thyroidectomy first

  1. TSHoma post-TSS: residual tumour, TSH 5, fT4 28. Next?

A. Somatostatin analogue (octreotide LAR/lanreotide) → consider re-op or RT if progressive

B. Carbimazole long-term

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor

  1. TSHoma vs RTH: key discriminating test:

A. α-subunit:TSH molar ratio (>1 in TSHoma, <1 in RTH); TRH test (blunted in TSHoma); pituitary MRI

B. TSH level only

C. fT4 only

D. Family history only

E. Genetic testing only

  1. TSHoma genetic association:

A. GNAS mutations (rare); MEN1 (rare); mostly sporadic

B. RET

C. MEN1 always

D. VHL

E. SDHx

21. Flashcards

  • Q: TSHoma prevalence

A: 1-2% of pituitary adenomas; rare

  • Q: Biochemical hallmark

A: Inappropriately normal/↑ TSH with ↑ fT4/fT3 (lost negative feedback)

  • Q: Alpha-subunit

A: Elevated; α:TSH molar ratio >1 (normal <1)

  • Q: TRH test

A: Blunted/absent TSH response (autonomous secretion)

  • Q: T3 suppression

A: Failure of TSH suppression with T3

  • Q: Imaging

A: MRI: macroadenoma >10mm (80%); may be cystic

  • Q: Surgery 1st line

A: TSS; GTR 60-70%; remission: normal TSH + normal fT4

  • Q: Pre-op prep

A: Euthyroid: carbimazole + propranolol ± KI (7-10d)

  • Q: SST analogues

A: Octreotide/lanreotide: ↓ TSH, ↓ thyroid hormones; shrinkage 30-50%

  • Q: RTH vs TSHoma

A: RTH: THRB mutation, goitre, tachycardia, normal pituitary, α:TSH<1, TRH normal

  • Q: Genetics

A: Mostly sporadic; rare GNAS/MEN1

  • Q: Surveillance

A: TSH, fT4, α-subunit q6-12mo; MRI annually ×5yr

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. ~1-2% of pituitary adenomas; rare — TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (TSHoma) prevalence:
  1. Inappropriately normal/elevated TSH with elevated fT4/fT3 (loss of negative feedback) — TSHoma biochemical hallmark:

  1. Transsphenoidal surgery (TSS); gross total resection 60-70% — TSHoma treatment 1st line:
  1. Euthyroid state: thionamides (carbimazole) ± beta-blocker ± potassium iodide (short-term) — Pre-op preparation for TSHoma surgery:
