  1. TSHoma: pituitary tumour, α-subunit↑, α:TSH>1, TRH blunted; RTH: genetic (THRB), goitre, tachycardia, normal pituitary — TSHoma vs Resistance to Thyroid Hormone (RTH):

SBAs

  1. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (TSHoma) — 45yo woman: weight loss, tremor, tachycardia, fT4 35, fT3 12, TSH 2.5 (inappropriately normal). MRI: 1.2cm pituitary macroadenoma. α-subunit:TSH ratio 2.5. Diagnosis?
  1. Carbimazole 20-40mg/day + propranolol until euthyroid; short-term KI (7-10d) pre-op — Same patient: pre-op preparation for TSS?
  1. Somatostatin analogue (octreotide LAR/lanreotide) → consider re-op or RT if progressive — TSHoma post-TSS: residual tumour, TSH 5, fT4 28. Next?
  1. α-subunit:TSH molar ratio (>1 in TSHoma, <1 in RTH); TRH test (blunted in TSHoma); pituitary MRI — TSHoma vs RTH: key discriminating test:
  1. GNAS mutations (rare); MEN1 (rare); mostly sporadic — TSHoma genetic association:

topics.title: Contraception
topics.html:
[!info]
Contraception = Prevention of Pregnancy. UKMEC (Medical Eligibility Criteria) guides safe prescribing. LARC (Long-Acting Reversible Contraception) = First-Line for Most Women. Tiered Efficacy: LARC > COCP/POP > Barrier > Natural Methods.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply UKMEC categories for contraceptive eligibility
  • [ ] Compare efficacy, mechanisms, and side effects of all methods
  • [ ] Select appropriate method based on patient factors (age, BMI, medical history, preferences)
  • [ ] Manage emergency contraception
  • [ ] Counsel on non-contraceptive benefits and side effects

2. Contraceptive Efficacy — Tiered by Typical Use Failure Rate

TierMethodTypical Failure Rate (1st Year)Perfect Use Failure Rate
Tier 1 (LARC)Implant (ENG)0.05%0.05%
IUS (LNG 52mg)0.2%0.2%
Copper IUD0.8%0.6%
Injectable (DMPA)3-6%0.2%
Tier 2COCP7-9%0.3%
POP (Desogestrel)7-9%0.3%
Patch / Ring7-9%0.3%
Tier 3Male Condom13-18%2%
Female Condom21%5%
Diaphragm/Cap17%4%
Tier 4Fertility Awareness12-24%1-5%
Withdrawal20-22%4%
Spermicides21-28%18%
LARC = First-Line for Most Women (Highest Efficacy, Long Duration, User-Independent)

3. UKMEC (Medical Eligibility Criteria) — Key Categories

CategoryDefinitionClinical Action
UKMEC 1No RestrictionUse Without Restriction
UKMEC 2Advantages > RisksGenerally Use
UKMEC 3Risks > AdvantagesGenerally Avoid (Unless Other Methods Unacceptable)
UKMEC 4Unacceptable RiskDo Not Use

Key UKMEC 4 Contraindications (Selected)

ConditionCOCPPOPImplantIUS/IUDInjectable
Breast Cancer (Current)44444
VTE (Current/History)42223
Stroke / TIA42223
Ischaemic Heart Disease42223
Uncontrolled HTN (≥160/100)42223
Migraine with Aura42222
Smoker ≥35, ≥15/day42222
Major Surgery (Immobility)41112
Postpartum <21 days (Non-Breastfeeding)41111
Postpartum <21 days (Breastfeeding)41111
Postpartum 21-42 days (Breastfeeding)31111
Liver Tumour43333
Acute Liver Disease42223

4. Contraceptive Methods — Quick Reference

Long-Acting Reversible Contraception (LARC)

MethodMechanismDurationPearl Index (Typical)Key Features
Copper IUDCu²⁺ Toxic to Sperm/Ova; Endometrial Inflammation5-10 Years0.6-0.8%Non-Hormonal; Heavy Periods; Emergency Contraception
LNG-IUS (52mg)LNG Release → Endometrial Suppression5 Years0.2%↓ Menstrual Bleeding; Local Progestogen; LNG 20µg/day
LNG-IUS (19.5mg/13.5mg)Lower Dose3-5 Years0.2%Smaller Frame; Less Bleeding
Implant (ENG 68mg)ENG Release → Oestrogen Suppression3 Years0.05%Subdermal; Predictable Bleeding
DMPA (Injectable)MPA → Ovulation Suppression; Endometrial Thinning12-13 Weeks3-6%Weight Gain; Delayed Fertility Return (9-10mo)

5. Combined Oral Contraceptive Pill (COCP)

AspectDetails
CompositionEE 20-35µg + Progestogen (Levonorgestrel, Norethisterone, Desogestrel, Gestodene, Drospirenone, Dienogest)
Regimen21 Days On / 7 Days Off (Withdrawal Bleed)
Extended RegimensTricycling (63/7), Continuous (No Break)
MechanismSuppress Ovulation (E2 → ↓ FSH/LH); Endometrial Thinning; Cervical Mucus Thickening
Failure Rate7-9% (Typical); 0.3% (Perfect)

COCP — Non-Contraceptive Benefits

BenefitEvidence
Menstrual RegulationRegular, Lighter, Less Painful Periods
Acne / HirsutismDrospirenone/Dienogest/Cyproterone (Anti-Androgenic)
Endometrial Cancer↓ 50% (Long-Term)
Ovarian Cancer↓ 40-50% (Long-Term)
Colorectal Cancer↓ 20%
PID↓ Risk
Functional Ovarian Cysts↓ Incidence

COCP — Risks (UKMEC 3/4)

RiskMagnitudeMitigation
VTE3-6x (Baseline 5-10/100,000 → 20-40/100,000)Transdermal Preferred if Risk Factors
Stroke1.5-2x (Ischaemic)Avoid if Migraine with Aura
MISlight ↑Avoid if CVD
Breast CancerSlight ↑ (Current/Recent Use)↓ After Stopping
Cervical CancerSlight ↑ (HPV Co-Factor)HPV Vaccination
Benefits vs RisksNet Benefit for Most Women <35, Non-Smoker

COCP Contraindications (UKMEC 4)

ConditionRationale
Current/Recent Breast CancerOestrogen-Dependent Tumour
VTE (Current/History)↑ Thrombotic Risk
Stroke / TIA / MIThrombotic Risk
Uncontrolled HTN (≥160/100)↑ Stroke/MI Risk
Migraine with Aura↑ Stroke Risk (2-4x)
Smoker ≥35y, >15/daySynergistic VTE Risk
Major Surgery (Immobility)Post-Op VTE Risk
Major Surgery (Planned)Stop 4 Weeks Pre-Op
Liver Tumour / Severe Liver DiseaseMetabolism Impaired

6. Progestogen-Only Pill (POP)

FeatureDetails
TypesDesogestrel 75µg (Newer, Ovulation Inhibition); Traditional (Norethisterone 350µg, Levonorgestrel 30µg) — Cervical Mucus Effect
MechanismDesogestrel: Ovulation Inhibition (97%); Traditional: Cervical Mucus + Endometrial Thinning
RegimenStrict Timing (Same Time Daily; Desogestrel 12h Window)
Bleeding PatternUnpredictable (Amenorrhoea, Spotting, Prolonged Bleeding)
UKMEC 4Breast Cancer, Undiagnosed Bleeding, Severe Liver Disease

7. Emergency Contraception (EC)

MethodWindowEfficacyDetails
Cu-IUD≤5 Days (120h)>99% (Most Effective)First-Line; Also Ongoing Contraception
Ulipristal Acetate (UPA 30mg)≤120h (5 Days)~98% (More Effective Than LNG)Prescription; Delay Ovulation
Levonorgestrel (LNG 1.5mg)≤72h (3 Days)~95% (Within 24h)OTC; Less Effective After 72h

EC Decision Algorithm

```

Unprotected Intercourse / Contraceptive Failure

│

▼

Time Since Intercourse

│

├── ≤120h (5 Days) → Cu-IUD (1st Line) → If Unsuitable/Unavailable → UPA 30mg

│

└── ≤72h (3 Days) → LNG 1.5mg (If UPA Unavailable/Contraindicated)

│

▼

Post-EC Care

│

├── Pregnancy Test if Next Period Delayed >7 Days

├── Start/Resume Regular Contraception Immediately

├── Cu-IUD → Ongoing Contraception

└── STI Screen if Indicated

```

8. Male Contraception

MethodMechanismEfficacyStatus
Condom (Male)Barrier82-98% (Typical)Only User-Controlled Male Method
VasectomyVas Deferens Occlusion>99% (Permanent)Surgical; Reversal Possible (50-70% Success)
HormonalTestosterone + Progestogen (Gel/Injectable)Trials OngoingNot Yet Licensed (Phase 3)

9. Quick Reference — Method Selection

Patient ProfileFirst-Line ChoiceAlternative
Nulliparous, No Risk FactorsImplant or IUSCu-IUD
Postpartum (Breastfeeding)Implant or POP or Cu-IUD (After 4wks)POP
Postpartum (Non-Breastfeeding)Implant, Cu-IUD, DMPAPOP
Post-AbortionAll Methods (Including IUD Immediate)—
Postpartum <21 Days (Non-BF)Cu-IUD, Implant, DMPA, POPCOCP (UKMEC 4)
Postpartum <21 Days (BF)Implant, POP, Cu-IUDDMPA (UKMEC 2)
Obesity (BMI ≥30)Implant, Cu-IUD, IUSPOP, DMPA
AdolescentImplant, Cu-IUD, IUSPOP, Condoms
HIV (On ART)Most Methods OK; Check Interactions (Rifampicin, EFV)POP if EFV
Breast CancerCu-IUD, POP, LNG-IUS (Caution)
VTE HistoryCu-IUD, POP, LNG-IUS (Caution)COCP, DMPA (UKMEC 3-4)
Smoker >35, >15/dayCu-IUD, POP, Implant, LNG-IUSCOCP (UKMEC 4)

10. Contraception After Pregnancy Events

EventTimingMethods
Delivery (Non-BF)ImmediateAll Methods (COPC UKMEC 4 <21d)
Delivery (Breastfeeding)Immediate (POP, Implant, Cu-IUD)DMPA (21-42d UKMEC 2)
Delivery <21d (BF)DMPA (UKMEC 2)
Delivery 21-42d (BF)DMPA (UKMEC 2)
Post-AbortionImmediateAll Methods (Including IUD)
Post-EctopicAfter hCG <5All Methods
Post-MiscarriageImmediateAll Methods

11. Follow-Up & Counselling

Initiation Counselling

TopicKey Points
MechanismHow It Works
EffectivenessPearl Index (Typical vs Perfect)
Side EffectsCommon (Bleeding Changes, Weight, Mood, Headache)
Warning SignsWhen to Seek Help (Severe Pain, Heavy Bleeding, Signs of VTE)
Follow-Up3-6 Month Review (Bleeding Pattern, Side Effects, Satisfaction)
STI ProtectionCondoms Only (Dual Protection)

Common Side Effects & Management

Side EffectMethodManagement
Irregular BleedingPOP, Implant, IUS, DMPAReassurance; Mefenamic Acid (IUS); Switch if Persistent >6mo
Weight GainDMPA (Mean +2-3kg)Counselling; Switch if >5kg
Mood ChangesCOCP, POP, DMPASwitch Method; SSRI if Depressive
AcneCOCP (Anti-Androgenic Progestogen)Switch Progestogen
AmenorrhoeaIUS, Implant, DMPAReassurance (Physiological)

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
LARC = First-LineImplant, IUS, Cu-IUD; Highest Efficacy, Lowest User-Dependence
COCP Failure Rate7-9% Typical; 0.3% Perfect Use
Cu-IUDMost Effective EC (≤5 Days); 10-Year Duration
UPA (UPA 30mg)120h Window (vs LNG 72h); More Effective
LNG-EC1.5mg Single Dose; ≤72h (Best ≤24h)
Cu-IUD Post-AbortionImmediate Insertion Safe & Effective
DMPADelayed Fertility Return (Median 9-10 Months)
UKMEC 4 COCPBreast Ca, VTE, Stroke, MI, Uncontrolled HTN, Migraine with Aura, Smoker ≥35 >15/day
UKMEC 4 POPBreast Cancer, Unexplained Bleeding, Severe Liver Disease
DMPA UKMECBreast Cancer (4), Active VTE (3), Stroke/MI (3), Severe Liver (3)
POP TimingStrict (Desogestrel 12h Window; Traditional 3h Window)
Cu-IUDEmergency Contraception ≤5 Days; 10-Year Duration
LNG-IUS52mg (5yr), 19.5mg (3-5yr); ↓ Menstrual Bleeding; Local Progestogen
Implant68mg ENG; 3 Years; 0.05% Failure
Vasectomy>99% Effective; Permanent; Reversal 50-70%
COCP VTE Risk3-6x Baseline; Transdermal ↓ Risk
COPC Migraine AuraUKMEC 4 (Contraindicated)
Postpartum ECCu-IUD (1st Line), UPA 30mg (120h), LNG-EC (72h)
UKMEC Categories1=No Restriction, 2=Advantages>Risks, 3=Risks>Advantages, 4=Unacceptable

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
LARC vs Non-LARCLARC = Implant, IUS, Cu-IUD; Set-and-Forget; Non-LARC = User-Dependent
COCP vs POPCOCP = Oestrogen + Progestogen (Suppress Ovulation); POP = Progestogen Only (Cervical Mucus/Endometrium)
Emergency ContraceptionCu-IUD = Most Effective (≤5 Days); UPA = 120h; LNG = 72h
DMPA DelayFertility Return 9-10 Months (Not Immediate)
IUD vs IUSCu-IUD = Copper; LNG-IUS = Levonorgestrel-Releasing
POP TimingDesogestrel: 12h Window; Traditional POP: 3h Window
UKMEC Categories1=Use, 2=Use, 3=Avoid, 4=Do Not Use
EC WindowCu-IUD ≤5 Days; UPA ≤5 Days; LNG ≤72h (Best ≤24h)
DMPA DelayReturn to Fertility 9-10 Months (Plan Ahead)
Breastfeeding + COCPSuppresses Lactation; Avoid <6 Weeks
Vasectomy>99% Effective; Reversal 50-70% (Not Guaranteed)

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Most Effective ContraceptionLARC (Implant, IUS, Cu-IUD) — Failure Rate <1%
Cu-IUDEmergency Contraception ≤5 Days; 10-Year Duration
UPA (EllaOne)120h Window; More Effective Than LNG-EC
LNG-EC72h Window (Best ≤24h); Less Effective Than UPA
Cu-IUD Post-AbortionImmediate Insertion Safe & Effective
DMPADelayed Fertility Return (9-10 Months Median)
COCP UKMEC 4Breast Ca, VTE, Stroke, MI, Uncontrolled HTN, Migraine with Aura, Smoker ≥35 >15/day
POP UKMEC 4Breast Cancer, Unexplained Bleeding, Severe Liver Disease
DMPA UKMECVTE/Stroke/MI (3), Breast Ca (4)
POP TimingDesogestrel 12h Window; Traditional 3h
Cu-IUDEC ≤5 Days; 10-Year Contraception
LNG-IUS52mg (5yr), 19.5mg (3-5yr); ↓ Bleeding; Local Effect
ImplantENG 68mg; 3 Years; 0.05% Failure Rate
Vasectomy>99% Effective; Reversal 50-70%; Not Immediate
COCP VTE Risk3-6x Baseline; Transdermal ↓ Risk
Migraine + Aura + COCPUKMEC 4 (Contraindicated)
Postpartum ECCu-IUD (1st), UPA (2nd), LNG (3rd)
UKMEC1=Use, 2=Use, 3=Avoid, 4=Contraindicated

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Reproductive Endocrinology|Reproductive Endocrinology]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Female Hypogonadism]], [[PCOS]], [[Menopause & HRT]], [[Infertility]], [[Female Hypogonadism]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Combined OCP mechanism:

A. Oestrogen + progestin; inhibits ovulation; thickens cervical mucus; thins endometrium

B. Progestin only

C. Oestrogen only

D. Barrier only

E. Spermicide only

  1. COCP effectiveness:

A. Perfect use: >99%; Typical use: ~91%

B. 100%

C. 50%

D. 80%

E. 70%

  1. COCP contraindications:

A. VTE risk (personal/family), migraine with aura, smoking >35yo, hypertension, liver disease, breast cancer

B. Age <18

C. Nulliparity

D. Smoking <10/day

E. BMI <30

  1. Progestin-only pill:

A. Desogestrel 75mcg daily; inhibits ovulation; safe in breastfeeding; no VTE risk

B. Contains oestrogen

C. Less effective

D. Daily at same time not required

E. Contraindicated in breastfeeding

  1. IUD copper:

A. T-shaped copper device; >99% effective; 5-10yr duration; non-hormonal; increases menstrual bleeding

B. Hormonal

C. Less effective

D. Lasts 2yr

E. Contraindicated in nulliparity

  1. IUD hormonal (LNG-IUS):

A. Levonorgestrel 20mcg/day; >99%; 5-8yr; reduces bleeding; treats menorrhagia

B. Copper

C. Less effective

D. Lasts 2yr

E. Increases bleeding

  1. Implant (etonogestrel):

A. Subdermal rod; 3yr; >99%; inhibits ovulation; may cause irregular bleeding

B. IUD

C. Oral pill

D. Injection

E. Vaginal ring

  1. Depot medroxyprogesterone (DMPA):

A. IM 150mg q12wk; inhibits ovulation; weight gain; bone density loss; delayed return fertility

B. Oral

C. IUD

D. Implant

E. Vaginal ring

  1. Emergency contraception - levonorgestrel:

A. 1.5mg within 72h post-coitus; inhibits/delays ovulation; not abortifacient

B. Within 24h only

C. After 120h

D. Abortifacient

E. Requires prescription

  1. Emergency contraception - ulipristal:

A. 30mg within 120h; more effective than LNG; inhibits ovulation; not in breastfeeding

B. Within 72h only

C. Less effective than LNG

D. Abortifacient

E. OTC

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 30yo woman: migraine with aura, wants contraception. Best option?

A. Progestin-only pill or LNG-IUS or copper IUD (no oestrogen)

B. COCP

C. DMPA

D. Vaginal ring

E. Diaphragm

  1. 25yo woman: missed 2 COCP pills in week 1. Advice?

A. Take 2 pills today + 2 tomorrow + condom x7d

B. Skip missed

C. Stop pack

D. Emergency contraception only

E. Continue as normal

  1. 40yo smoker: wants COCP. Risk?

A. VTE risk increased; avoid COCP; offer progestin-only or IUD

B. Safe

C. Reduce cigarettes

D. Low-dose COCP OK

E. Patch preferred

  1. Breastfeeding mother: 6wks pp, wants contraception. Best?

A. Progestin-only pill or LNG-IUS or copper IUD or implant

B. COCP

C. DMPA

D. Vaginal ring

E. Emergency contraception

  1. Woman: copper IUD for emergency contraception. Timeframe?

A. Within 120h (5 days) post-coitus; >99% effective; can remain for ongoing contraception

B. Within 72h only

C. Within 24h

D. After 5 days

E. Not effective

18. Flashcards

  • Q: COCP

A: Oestrogen + progestin; inhibits ovulation; >99% perfect use; contraindicated: VTE, migraine aura, smoking>35

  • Q: POP

A: Desogestrel 75mcg; inhibits ovulation; safe breastfeeding; no VTE risk

  • Q: LNG-IUS

A: Levonorgestrel 20mcg/day; 5-8yr; reduces bleeding; treats menorrhagia

  • Q: Copper IUD

A: Non-hormonal; >99%; 5-10yr; increases bleeding

  • Q: Implant

A: Etonogestrel rod; 3yr; >99%; may cause irregular bleeding

  • Q: DMPA

A: 150mg IM q12wk; weight gain; bone loss; delayed fertility return

  • Q: Emergency contraception

A: LNG 1.5mg 72h; Ulipristal 30mg 120h; not abortifacient

  • Q: Ulipristal

A: 30mg 120h; more effective than LNG; not in breastfeeding

  • Q: Copper IUD EC

A: Within 120h; >99%; ongoing contraception

  • Q: Contraindications COCP

A: VTE, migraine aura, smoking>35, HTN, liver disease, breast cancer

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Oestrogen + progestin; inhibits ovulation; thickens cervical mucus; thins endometrium — Combined OCP mechanism:

  1. T-shaped copper device; >99% effective; 5-10yr duration; non-hormonal; increases menstrual bleeding — IUD copper:
  1. Levonorgestrel 20mcg/day; >99%; 5-8yr; reduces bleeding; treats menorrhagia — IUD hormonal (LNG-IUS):
  1. Subdermal rod; 3yr; >99%; inhibits ovulation; may cause irregular bleeding — Implant (etonogestrel):
  1. IM 150mg q12wk; inhibits ovulation; weight gain; bone density loss; delayed return fertility — Depot medroxyprogesterone (DMPA):
  1. 1.5mg within 72h post-coitus; inhibits/delays ovulation; not abortifacient — Emergency contraception - levonorgestrel:
  1. 30mg within 120h; more effective than LNG; inhibits ovulation; not in breastfeeding — Emergency contraception - ulipristal:

SBAs

topics.title: Female Hypogonadism
topics.html:
[!info]
Female Hypogonadism = Ovarian Failure or Insufficient Gonadotropin Drive. Presents as Primary Amenorrhoea (No Menarche by 15) or Secondary Amenorrhoea (Absence of Menses ≥3-6 Months). Primary (Hypergonadotropic) = Ovarian Failure. Secondary (Hypogonadotropic) = Pituitary/Hypothalamic Failure.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate primary vs secondary hypogonadism using FSH/LH
  • [ ] Diagnose and manage Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI)
  • [ ] Manage hypothalamic amenorrhoea and PCOS overlap
  • [ ] Apply HRT in POI and menopause
  • [ ] Address fertility concerns and contraception

2. Classification & Aetiology

TypeFSH/LHOestradiolCommon Causes
Primary (Hypergonadotropic)↑↑LowTurner Syndrome (45,X), Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI), Chemo/Radiotherapy, Autoimmune, Surgery (Oophorectomy), Galactosaemia, Infections (Mumps)
Secondary (Hypogonadotropic)↓/NormalLowHypothalamic Amenorrhoea (Weight Loss, Stress, Exercise), Pituitary Adenoma, Hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann Syndrome, Sheehan Syndrome, Craniopharyngioma

3. Clinical Presentation

FeaturePrimary (POI/Turner)Secondary (Hypothalamic/Pituitary)
Primary AmenorrhoeaTurner Syndrome, Gonadal DysgenesisKallmann Syndrome, CNS Tumours
Secondary AmenorrhoeaPOI (Autoimmune, Chemo, Idiopathic)Weight Loss, Stress, Exercise, Prolactinoma, Sheehan, Craniopharyngioma
InfertilityBoth TypesBoth Types
Oestrogen Deficiency SignsHot Flushes, Vaginal Dryness, Osteoporosis, Low LibidoSame
Additional FeaturesTurner: Short Stature, Webbed Neck, CoarctationProlactinoma: Galactorrhoea; Kallmann: Anosmia

4. Primary Hypogonadism — Key Entities

Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI)

FeatureDetails
DefinitionOvarian Failure <40 Years (FSH >40 IU/L on 2 Occasions 4-6 Weeks Apart)
Incidence1% by Age 40; 0.1% by Age 30
AetiologyIdiopathic (90%); Autoimmune (Adrenal, Thyroid), Genetic (FMR1 Premutation, BMP15), Chemo/Radiotherapy, Surgery, Infections (Mumps), Galactosaemia
AutoimmuneAdrenal (Addison's) 10-20%, Thyroid (Hashimoto), Coeliac, T1DM, Pernicious Anaemia
GeneticFMR1 Premutation (5-10%), BMP15, FOXL2; Turner (45,X) / Mosaic
DiagnosisFSH >40 IU/L on 2 Occasions 4-6 Weeks Apart + Amenorrhoea >4 Months + Age <40

Turner Syndrome (45,X / Mosaic)

FeatureDetails
Karyotype45,X (50%); Mosaic 45,X/46,XX (15-20%)
PhenotypeShort Stature, Webbed Neck, Low Hairline, Low-Set Ears, Shield Chest, Cubitus Valgus
CardiacCoarctation of Aorta (10-20%), Bicuspid Aortic Valve
RenalHorseshoe Kidney (30%), Collecting System Anomalies
EndocrineStreak Ovaries → Primary Amenorrhoea, Infertility
ScreeningEchocardiogram, Renal US, Audiology, Ophthalmology, BP Monitoring

5. Secondary Hypogonadism — Key Entities

Hypothalamic Amenorrhoea (Functional)

CauseMechanismFeatures
Weight Loss / Anorexia↓ Leptin → ↓ GnRH Pulse GeneratorLow BMI (<18.5), Fear of Weight Gain
Excessive Exercise↓ Leptin, ↑ Endorphins → ↓ GnRH Pulse FrequencyAthletes, Dancers, Low Body Fat
Psychological Stress↑ CRH, ↑ Cortisol → ↓ GnRHStress, Life Events
Pituitary AdenomaProlactinoma (Most Common)Galactorrhoea, Headache, Visual Field Defects
CraniopharyngiomaSuprasellar MassVisual Field Defects, Hormone Deficiencies
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary NecrosisAgalactorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Loss of Pubic/Axillary Hair
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic HypogonadismANOS1/KAL1 Gene

Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) — See Separate Note

FeaturePCOS
Diagnosis2/3 Rotterdam: Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCO Morphology
EndocrineLH:FSH >2:1, Hyperandrogenism, Insulin Resistance
ManagementLifestyle, COCP, Metformin, Letrozole (Ovulation Induction)

6. Investigation Algorithm

```

Amenorrhoea (Primary or Secondary)

│

▼

PREGNANCY TEST (β-hCG) → Exclude Pregnancy

│

▼

FSH, LH, OESTRADIOL, PROLACTIN, TSH

│

├── FSH/LH HIGH + OESTRADIOL LOW → PRIMARY HYPOGONADISM

│ ├── TURNER (45,X) → Karyotype, Cardiac/Renal US

│ ├── POI (FSH >40 IU/L x2) → Autoimmune Screen, FMR1, Bone Density

│ └── POST-SURGICAL / CHEMO / RADIO → History

│

├── FSH/LH LOW/NORMAL + OESTRADIOL LOW → SECONDARY

│ ├── PROLACTIN HIGH → PROLACTINOMA (MRI Pituitary)

│ ├── LOW BMI / EXERCISE / STRESS → FUNCTIONAL HYPOTHALAMIC AMENORRHOEA

│ ├── PCOS (LH:FSH >2:1, ANDROGENS ↑) → ROTTERDAM CRITERIA

│ ├── SHEEHAN SYNDROME (POSTPARTUM) → EMPTY SELLA MRI

│ └── CRANIOPHARYNGIOMA / TUMOUR → MRI BRAIN

│

└── ANDROGENS HIGH + OLIGO/ANOVULATION → PCOS (See PCOS Note)

```

7. Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI) — Management

Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT) — Until Age 50

ComponentRegimen
OestrogenTransdermal 50-100µg/day (Patch/Gel) OR Oral 1-2mg
ProgestogenCyclical (Medroxyprogesterone 10mg Days 1-12) or Continuous (If Hysterectomy)
DurationUntil Natural Menopause Age (~50-51 Years)
Bone ProtectionBisphosphonate if T-score ≤-2.5; Calcium/Vit D
CardiovascularMonitor BP, Lipids; Transdermal Preferred (↓ VTE Risk)

Fertility in POI

OptionDetails
Spontaneous Pregnancy5-10% (Intermittent Ovarian Activity)
Donor Oocyte IVFGold Standard (Success 40-50%)
Own Oocyte IVFRarely Successful (Low AMH, High FSH)
Fertility PreservationOocyte/Embryo Cryopreservation Before Chemo/Radiotherapy

Autoimmune Screening (POI)

TestFrequency
21-Hydroxylase AbAdrenal (Addison's) — 10-20%
TPOAb / TgAbThyroid (Hashimoto) — 20-30%
Coeliac (TTG-IgA)5-10%
Pernicious AnaemiaB12, IF-Ab — 5-10%

8. Turner Syndrome Management

SystemSurveillance / Intervention
CardiacEchocardiogram at Diagnosis; Annual BP; MRI Aorta if Dilated
RenalRenal US at Diagnosis; Hypertension Screening
GrowthGH Therapy (0.035 mg/kg/day) — Start Early (Age 4-6)
Puberty InductionOestrogen (Low Dose → Adult Dose over 2-3 Years)
FertilityDonor Oocyte IVF (Own Oocytes Rarely Viable)
Bone HealthCalcium/Vit D; DEXA q2-3yr; Bisphosphonate if T-score ≤-2.5
ThyroidAnnual TSH (Autoimmune Thyroiditis Common)
Audiology/OphthalmologyBaseline + q2-3yr

9. Hypothalamic Amenorrhoea Management

CauseManagement
Weight Loss / AnorexiaWeight Restoration (BMI >18.5); Multidisciplinary (Dietitian, Psych)
Excessive ExerciseReduce Intensity/Volume; ↑ Energy Availability (>45 kcal/kg FFM/day)
StressCBT, Stress Management, ↓ Training Load
ProlactinomaCabergoline (0.25mg BD → Titrate); Surgery if Macro/Visual Defects
Kallmann SyndromehCG + rFSH for Fertility; Testosterone/Oestradiol Replacement

10. Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI) — Long-Term Risks

ComplicationScreening / Prevention
OsteoporosisDEXA at Diagnosis; Repeat q2-3yr; Bisphosphonate if T-score ≤-2.5
CardiovascularEarly Menopause → ↑ CVD Risk; HRT Until Age 50
Cognitive DeclineEarly Menopause → ↑ Dementia Risk; HRT Neuroprotective?
Autoimmune RiskAnnual TSH, 21-OH Ab, Coeliac Screen
Fertility5-10% Spontaneous Pregnancy; Donor Oocyte IVF Main Option

11. Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT) in POI / Turner

RegimenDetails
OestrogenTransdermal 50-100µg/day (Patch/Gel) — Preferred (↓ VTE Risk)
ProgestogenCyclical (Medroxyprogesterone 10mg Days 1-12) or Continuous (If Hysterectomy)
DurationUntil Age 50-51 (Natural Menopause Age)
Non-HormonalIf HRT Contraindicated: Calcium/Vit D, Bisphosphonates, Lifestyle

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Primary Amenorrhoea CausesTurner (45,X), Müllerian Agenesis, Androgen Insensitivity, Kallmann
Secondary Amenorrhoea Investigationβ-hCG → FSH/LH/Oestradiol/Prolactin/TSH
POI DefinitionFSH >40 IU/L on 2 Occasions (4-6 Weeks Apart) + Age <40 + Amenorrhoea >4 Months
POI AetiologyIdiopathic (90%), Autoimmune (Adrenal/Thyroid), FMR1 Premutation, Chemo/Radiotherapy
POI HRTUntil Age 50; Transdermal Oestrogen + Cyclical Progestogen
Turner Syndrome45,X; Short Stature, Webbed Neck, Coarctation, Streak Ovaries; GH Therapy; Coarctation Repair
Hypothalamic AmenorrhoeaFunctional (Weight Loss, Exercise, Stress) → Weight Gain, ↓ Exercise
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism; ANOS1/KAL1
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis; Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Hair Loss; Empty Sella
POI HRT DurationUntil Age 50 (Natural Menopause Age)
Turner CardiacCoarctation of Aorta (10-20%), Bicuspid Aortic Valve
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism; ANOS1/KAL1 Gene
POI Autoimmune Screen21-OH Ab (Adrenal), TPOAb (Thyroid), TTG-IgA (Coeliac)
Fertility in POI5-10% Spontaneous; Donor Oocyte IVF → Main Option
Turner RenalHorseshoe Kidney (30%); Renal US at Diagnosis

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Primary vs SecondaryPrimary = FSH/LH ↑↑; Secondary = FSH/LH Low/Normal
POI vs Early MenopausePOI = <40 Years + FSH >40; Early Menopause = 40-45 Years
Turner vs NoonanTurner: 45,X, Webbed Neck, Coarctation; Noonan: Normal Karyotype, Pyloric Stenosis, Pulmonary Stenosis
POI vs Early MenopausePOI = Before 40; Early Menopause = 40-45
Functional vs OrganicFunctional = Weight Loss/Exercise/Stress; Organic = Prolactinoma, Craniopharyngioma, Kallmann
Kallmann vs Isolated HHKallmann = Anosmia + HH; Isolated HH = No Anosmia
POI HRT DurationUntil Age 50 (Natural Menopause Age)
Turner FertilityDonor Oocyte IVF (Own Oocytes Non-Viable)
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis → Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Empty Sella
Turner RenalHorseshoe Kidney (30%); Renal US at Diagnosis

18. SBA Questions (10)

C. PCOS

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Primary ovarian failure

A: Low oestradiol + HIGH FSH/LH (e.g. Turner, POI, chemo, surgery)

  • Q: Secondary (hypothalamic) failure

A: Low oestradiol + LOW/normal LH/FSH (stress, weight loss, exercise, tumour)

  • Q: POI

A: <40yr; FSH>40 x2; HRT until 50; 5-10% spontaneous pregnancy; donor oocyte IVF

  • Q: Turner syndrome

A: 45,X; streak ovaries; primary amenorrhoea; short stature; coarctation

  • Q: Kallmann syndrome

A: Congenital GnRH deficiency + anosmia; KAL1 (X-linked) or autosomal

  • Q: Hypothalamic amenorrhoea

A: Stress/weight loss/exercise -> suppressed GnRH -> low LH/FSH; reversible

  • Q: POI management

A: HRT until 50; fertility referral; genetic counselling; 5-10% spontaneous pregnancy

  • Q: Hypothalamic amenorrhoea Rx

A: Address cause (weight/exercise); HRT if persistent; fertility: gonadotrophins

  • Q: PCOS vs POI

A: PCOS: high androgens, PCO, high LH:FSH; POI: high FSH, low oestradiol

  • Q: Contraception in POI

A: Not needed if confirmed POI; but 5-10% spontaneous pregnancy possible

  1. Address cause (weight gain, reduce exercise, stress reduction); HRT if persistent; fertility: gonadotrophins — Hypothalamic amenorrhoea treatment:

topics.title: Infertility
topics.html:
[!info]
Infertility = Failure to Conceive After 12 Months of Regular Unprotected Intercourse (6 Months if Female >35). Prevalence: 1 in 7 Couples. Male Factor (30-40%), Female Factor (30-40%), Combined (20-30%), Unexplained (10-20%).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply the infertility investigation algorithm (male + female)
  • [ ] Select appropriate ovulation induction agent (letrozole vs clomifene vs gonadotrophins)
  • [ ] Indicate IVF/ICSI and interpret success rates
  • [ ] Manage male factor infertility (lifestyle, antioxidants, surgical, ICSI)
  • [ ] Counsel on age-related fertility decline and fertility preservation

2. Definitions & Epidemiology

DefinitionCriteria
InfertilityFailure to Conceive After 12 Months Regular Unprotected Intercourse (6 Months if Female ≥35)
Primary InfertilityNever Conceived
Secondary InfertilityPrevious Conception (Regardless of Outcome)
Prevalence1 in 7 Couples (15%)
DistributionMale 30-40%, Female 30-40%, Combined 20-30%, Unexplained 10-20%

Age-Related Decline

Female AgeMonthly FecundabilityCumulative 12-Month Pregnancy Rate
<3020-25%85-90%
30-3415-20%75-80%
35-3710-15%60-70%
38-405-10%40-50%
41-42<5%20-30%
>42<5%<10%

3. Investigation Algorithm

Initial Assessment (Both Partners)

PartnerTests
FemaleTSH, Prolactin, FSH/LH (Day 2-4), Oestradiol (Day 2-4), AMH, Pelvic US (AFC, Uterus), HSG/HyCoSy (Tubal Patency), Cervical Smear/HPV, Rubella/Varicella Immunity, Chlamydia/Gonorrhoea Screen
MaleSemen Analysis (WHO 2021 Criteria: Volume ≥1.5mL, Conc ≥15M/mL, Progressive Motility ≥32%, Normal Morphology ≥4%), Hormones (Testosterone, LH, FSH, Prolactin) if Abnormal Semen

Timing of Investigation

ScenarioTiming
Female <35After 12 Months Unprotected Intercourse
Female 35-39After 6 Months
Female ≥40Immediate
Known Cause (e.g., Amenorrhoea, Azospermia)Immediate

4. Female Factor Investigation

Ovulation Assessment

MethodDetails
Progesterone (Day 21)>30 nmol/L = Ovulation Confirmed
Urinary LH KitsLH Surge Detection (Home)
Basal Body TemperatureBiphasic Shift (Post-Ovulatory Rise)
Ultrasound (Follicular Tracking)Follicle Growth → Rupture (Gold Standard)

Tubal Patency

TestIndicationPros/Cons
HSG (Hysterosalpingography)First-LineX-Ray; Radiation; Therapeutic (Oil Contrast)
HyCoSyNo Radiation; Office-BasedOperator-Dependent; False Positives
Laparoscopy + DyeGold StandardInvasive; Diagnostic + Therapeutic

Ovarian Reserve

TestInterpretation
AMHBest Marker; <1.0 ng/mL = Low; >3.5 = High (PCOS)
AFC (Antral Follicle Count)<5-7 = Low; >15 = High (PCOS)
FSH (Day 2-4)>10 IU/L = Diminished Reserve; >20 = Severely Diminished

5. Male Factor Investigation

Semen Analysis (WHO 2021 Criteria)

ParameterLower Reference Limit (5th Percentile)
Volume≥1.5 mL
Concentration≥15 million/mL
Total Motility≥40% (Progressive ≥32%)
Progressive Motility≥32%
Normal Morphology≥4% (Strict Kruger Criteria)
Vitality≥58% Live
pH≥7.2

Additional Male Tests

TestIndication
Hormones (T, LH, FSH, Prolactin)Abnormal Semen Analysis
Scrotal USVaricocele, Testicular Mass, Obstruction
GeneticKaryotype (Klinefelter), Y-Microdeletion (If Severe Oligo/Azoospermia), CFTR (CAVD)
DNA FragmentationRecurrent Miscarriage, Failed IVF, Unexplained Infertility

6. Female Factor Management

Anovulation (PCOS / Hypothalamic Amenorrhoea)

AgentDoseProtocolPregnancy Rate
Letrozole2.5-5mg Days 2-61st Line (PCOS)22-25%/Cycle
Clomifene50-150mg Days 2-62nd Line15-20%/Cycle
Gonadotrophins (rFSH/hMG)75-225 IU/day3rd Line / PCOS Resistant20-25%/Cycle
Ovulation TriggerhCG 5000-10000 IU (Lead Follicle ≥18mm)

Tubal Factor

PathologyManagement
Proximal BlockageTubal Cannulation (HSG-Guided)
Distal Blockage / HydrosalpinxSalpingectomy → IVF (Hydrosalpinx ↓ Implantation 50%)
Periadnexal AdhesionsLaparoscopic Adhesiolysis → IVF if Severe

Endometriosis

StageManagement
Minimal/Mild (I-II)Expectant / Laparoscopic Ablation → Natural / IUI
Moderate/Severe (III/IV)Surgery (Excision) → IVF (If Not Conceiving 6-12mo Post-Op)

Uterine Factors

PathologyManagement
Fibroids (Submucosal/Intramural >4cm)Hysteroscopic/Myomectomy → IVF
PolypsHysteroscopic Polypectomy
AdenomyosisMedical (GnRH Agonist) → IVF
Septate UterusHysteroscopic Septoplasty
Asherman SyndromeHysteroscopic Adhesiolysis + Oestrogen + IUCD (Prevent Re-Adhesion)

7. Male Factor Management

Lifestyle & Medical

InterventionEvidence
Weight Loss (BMI >25)↑ Sperm Count/Motility
Antioxidants (CoQ10, Vit C/E, Zinc, Se, L-Carnitine)Meta-Analysis: ↑ Sperm Parameters
Smoking Cessation↑ Count, Motility, Morphology
Alcohol Reduction<14 Units/Week
Heat AvoidanceHot Tubs, Tight Underwear, Laptop on Lap

Surgical

ConditionProcedure
Varicocele (Grade 2-3)Microsurgical Varicocelectomy → ↑ Pregnancy Rate (If Clinical + Abnormal SA)
Obstructive AzoospermiaVasovasostomy / Vasoepididymostomy
Ejaculatory Duct ObstructionTURED (Transurethral Resection)

Assisted Reproduction (Male Factor)

ScenarioTechnique
Mild Oligo/AsthenozoospermiaIUI (If TMSC >5 Million)
Severe Oligo/Astheno/TeratozoospermiaICSI
Non-Obstructive Azoospermia (NOA)Micro-TESE → ICSI
Obstructive Azoospermia (OA)TESE/PESA + ICSI
Genetic RiskKaryotype + Y-Microdeletion Before ICSI

8. Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART)

IVF vs ICSI Indications

TechniqueIndication
IVFTubal Factor, Unexplained, Mild Male Factor, Endometriosis (Mild), Ovulation Induction Failure
ICSISevere Male Factor (Oligo/Astheno/Teratozoospermia), Failed Fertilisation IVF, Frozen Sperm, Surgical Sperm Retrieval, PGD/PGS

Ovarian Stimulation Protocols (IVF)

ProtocolIndicationKey Features
Long Agonist (GnRH-a)Standard (Good Responders)Down-Regulation → Stimulation
Antagonist (GnRH-ant)Preferred (OHSS Risk ↓, Shorter)Flexible Start (Day 6 Stim)
Short AgonistPoor RespondersFlare Effect
Mild/Minimal StimulationPoor Responders / OHSS RiskLow Dose Gonadotrophins + Antagonist

Trigger & Timing

TriggerDoseTiming
hCG5000-10,000 IU36h Post-Trigger = Oocyte Retrieval
GnRH Agonist Trigger0.2mg Triptorelin / 0.2mg LeuprorelinOHSS Prevention (No LH Surge)
Dual TriggerhCG 1000-2000 IU + GnRH-a 0.2mgPoor Responders / OHSS Risk

9. IVF/ICSI Outcomes & Complications

Success Rates (HFEA/ESHRE Data)

Female AgeLive Birth Rate per Embryo Transfer
<3530-35%
35-3720-25%
38-3915-20%
40-4210-15%
>42<5%

OHSS (Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome)

GradeCriteriaManagement
MildBloating, Mild Abdominal PainOutpatient, Oral Fluids
ModerateAscites, Nausea/VomitingAdmission, IV Fluids, Monitor
SevereTense Ascites, Haemoconcentration, Oliguria, DVT RiskICU, Paracentesis, Albumin, Anticoagulation, Cabergoline (Dopamine Agonist)

Prevention: GnRH Agonist Trigger, Coasting, Cabergoline, Albumin, Freeze-All Strategy.

10. Special Scenarios

Poor Ovarian Response (POR) — Bologna Criteria

Criteria (≥2 of 3)
Age ≥40 / AMH <1.1 ng/mL / AFC <5
Previous Poor Response≤3 Oocytes with Standard Stimulation
StrategyProtocol
Mild StimulationLow-Dose Gonadotrophins + Antagonist
Agonist FlareGnRH-a Flare Protocol
DuoStimDual Stimulation (Follicular + Luteal Phase)
AccumulationFreeze All Embryos Across Cycles

Poor Responder Management

StrategyOutcome
Mild StimulationLower Dose Gonadotrophins + Antagonist
GnRH Agonist FlareShort Protocol
DuoStimDual Stimulation (Follicular + Luteal)
Embryo AccumulationFreeze All Across Cycles

Fertility Preservation

IndicationMethod
Cancer (Chemo/Radiotherapy)Oocyte/Embryo Cryopreservation (Oocyte Vitrification)
Gender AffirmationOocyte/Sperm Cryopreservation Pre-Hormones
Elective (Social)Oocyte Cryopreservation (Age <35 Optimal)

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Infertility Definition12 Months (6mo if Female ≥35)
Male Factor30-40%; Semen Analysis WHO 2021 (Conc ≥15M, Motility ≥40%/32% Prog, Morph ≥4%)
Female AgeCritical Determinant; Fecundability ↓ After 35; Investigate at 6mo if ≥35
AMHBest Ovarian Reserve Marker (<1 ng/mL = Low)
AFC<5-7 = Low; >15 = High (PCOS)
HSG vs HyCoSyHSG = Radiation; HyCoSy = No Radiation, Office-Based
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole 1st Line (↑ Live Birth, ↓ Multiples, ↓ OHSS)
Letrozole Dose2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (Letrozole Preferred Over Clomifene)
Clomifene Dose50-150mg Days 2-6; Max 6 Cycles
hCG Trigger5000-10000 IU; When Lead Follicle ≥18mm
ICSI IndicationSevere Male Factor, Failed IVF Fertilisation, Frozen Sperm, PGD
OHSS PreventionGnRH Agonist Trigger; Coasting; Cabergoline; Freeze-All
Embryo TransferDay 3 (Cleavage) vs Day 5 (Blastocyst) — Blastocyst Preferred
Embryo FreezingVitrification > Slow Freezing; Freeze-All Strategy (OHSS Prevention)
PGD/PGSGenetic Diagnosis (Monogenic / Aneuploidy)
Male Factor ICSISevere Oligo/Astheno/Terato; Failed IVF Fertilisation; TESE/MESA Sperm
VaricoceleMicrosurgical Varicocelectomy (Grade 2-3 + Abnormal SA)
AzoospermiaOA → TESE/PESA + ICSI; NOA → Micro-TESE + ICSI
Age EffectFemale Age = Single Most Important Factor (↓ Fecundity After 35)
Cumulative LBRCumulative LBR >50% After 3 Cycles (Age <35)
OHSSMild/Moderate/Severe; Prevention: GnRH-a Trigger, Coasting, Cabergoline, Freeze-All
POI & Fertility5-10% Spontaneous; Donor Oocyte IVF Main Option
EndometriosisStage I-II: Expectant/IUI; III-IV: Surgery → IVF

12. Mind Map

```mermaid
mindmap

35-37: 20-25%

38-39: 15-20%

40-42: 10-15%

>42: <5%

Complications

OHSS: Mild/Mod/Severe; Prevention: GnRH-a Trigger, Freeze-All

Ectopic: 2-5%

Multiple: SET Policy

```

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Infertility Definition12 Months (6mo if Female ≥35)
AMHBest Ovarian Reserve Marker (<1 ng/mL = Low)
AFC<5-7 = Low; >15 = High (PCOS)
Female AgeMost Critical Factor; 6mo Investigation if ≥35
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole 1st Line (↑ Live Birth, ↓ Multiples, ↓ OHSS)
Letrozole Dose2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (1st Line)
Clomifene Dose50-150mg Days 2-6; Max 6 Cycles
ICSI IndicationsSevere Male Factor, Failed IVF Fertilisation, PGD, Frozen Sperm
Male Factor Semen ParametersConc ≥15M, Motility ≥40% (Prog ≥32%), Morph ≥4%
Varicocele RepairMicrosurgical (Grade 2-3 + Abnormal SA)
AzoospermiaOA → TESE/PESA + ICSI; NOA → Micro-TESE + ICSI
OHSS PreventionGnRH-a Trigger, Coasting, Cabergoline, Freeze-All
TriggerhCG 5000-10000 IU (Follicle ≥18mm)
Female AgeMost Important Prognostic Factor
Cumulative LBR>50% After 3 Cycles (Age <35)
VaricoceleMicro-Varicocelectomy if Grade 2-3 + Abnormal SA
DNA FragmentationRecurrent Loss / Failed IVF → Test SCSA/TUNEL
Embryo TransferBlastocyst (Day 5) Preferred Over Cleavage (Day 3)
Freeze-All StrategyOHSS Prevention; ↑ Endometrial Receptivity
PGD/PGSMonogenic / Aneuploidy Screening
OHSS GradesMild/Moderate/Severe; Prevention Key

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Infertility definition:

C. 24 months any age

D. 3 months

E. Immediate

  1. Infertility causes - male:

C. Only age

E. Only genetic

D. Metformin

E. Surgery

B. Clomiphene

C. IUI

D. Medication only

E. No treatment

  1. IUI indications:

A. Unexplained infertility, mild male factor, cervical factor, ovulation induction with donor sperm

  1. IVF indications:

A. High oestradiol, >15 follicles, ascites, haemoconcentration, renal impairment; prevent with GnRH antagonist/agonist trigger

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Infertility

A: 12mo unprotected intercourse (6mo if >35yo)

  • Q: Male causes

A: Oligo/astheno/terato/azoospermia, varicocele, genetic, ejaculatory

  • Q: Male workup

  • Q: Clomiphene

  • Q: IUI

A: Own gametes failed; genetic disorders; advanced age; POI/azoospermia

A: Female fertility declines >35; sharp drop >40; male gradual decline >40

topics.title: Male Hypogonadism
topics.html:
[!info]
Male Hypogonadism = Testosterone Deficiency with Impaired Spermatogenesis. Primary (Hypergonadotropic) = Testicular Failure. Secondary (Hypogonadotropic) = Pituitary/Hypothalamic Failure. Late-Onset (Age-Related) Increasingly Recognised.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate primary vs secondary hypogonadism using LH/FSH
  • [ ] Diagnose using testosterone, LH, FSH, and clinical features
  • [ ] Prescribe testosterone replacement therapy (formulations, monitoring)
  • [ ] Address fertility preservation (hCG, rhFSH)
  • [ ] Manage special situations (obesity, elderly, cardiovascular disease)

2. Classification & Aetiology

TypeLH/FSHTestosteroneCommon Causes
Primary (Hypergonadotropic)↑↑LowKlinefelter (47,XXY), Mumps Orchitis, Chemo/Radiotherapy, Autoimmune, Trauma, Torsion, Genetic (SRY, NR5A1), HIV
Secondary (Hypogonadotropic)↓/NormalLowPituitary Adenoma, Hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann Syndrome, Functional (Obesity, Stress, Exercise), Chronic Disease, Drugs (Opioids, Glucocorticoids)
Mixed / FunctionalVariableLowAgeing, Obesity, Alcohol, Liver Disease, Chronic Illness

3. Clinical Presentation

DomainFeatures
SexualLow Libido, Erectile Dysfunction, Infertility, Reduced Ejaculate Volume
PhysicalReduced Beard/Body Hair, Gynaecomastia, Reduced Muscle Mass, Increased Visceral Fat, Decreased Bone Density
GeneralFatigue, Low Mood, Poor Concentration, Hot Flushes, Sleep Disturbance, Anaemia
MetabolicInsulin Resistance, ↑ Visceral Fat, Dyslipidaemia, Metabolic Syndrome

4. Diagnosis — Biochemical Differentiation

Key Investigations

TestPrimary HypogonadismSecondary HypogonadismNormal
Total TestosteroneLow (<12 nmol/L / 350 ng/dL)LowNormal
Free TestosteroneLowLowNormal
LH↑↑Low / Inappropriately NormalNormal
FSH↑↑Low / Inappropriately NormalNormal
SHBGNormal/HighNormal/LowNormal

Diagnostic Criteria

ParameterThreshold
Total Testosterone<12 nmol/L (350 ng/dL) on 2 Occasions (Morning, Fasting)
Free Testosterone<225 pmol/L (65 pg/mL) (If SHBG Abnormal)
LH/FSHInterpret with Testosterone (See Table Above)

5. Investigations — First-Line

TestPurpose
Total Testosterone (09:00, Fasting)×2 Occasions (Confirm Low)
LH, FSHDifferentiate Primary vs Secondary
ProlactinExclude Hyperprolactinaemia (Prolactinoma)
SHBGCalculate Free Testosterone
TSH, fT4Exclude Thyroid Disease
FBC, U&E, LFT, Glucose, LipidsGeneral Health
Bone Density (DEXA)If Osteoporosis Risk
Semen AnalysisIf Fertility Concern

6. Aetiology — Primary vs Secondary

Primary Hypogonadism (Testicular Failure)

CauseKey Features
Klinefelter Syndrome (47,XXY)Commonest Genetic; Tall, Small Firm Testes, Gynaecomastia, Azoospermia
Mumps OrchitisPost-Pubertal; Unilateral/Bilateral; ↑ FSH > LH
Chemotherapy / RadiotherapyAlkylating Agents (Cyclophosphamide), Pelvic RT
AutoimmunePolyglandular Syndromes (APS-1, APS-2), Anti-Testicular Antibodies
GeneticSRY, NR5A1, DAX1, ATRX Mutations
ToxinsAlcohol, Heavy Metals, Pesticides
Trauma / TorsionBilateral Trauma / Untreated Torsion

Secondary Hypogonadism (Pituitary/Hypothalamic)

CauseKey Features
Pituitary AdenomaMacroadenoma; Hyperprolactinaemia; Mass Effect
HyperprolactinaemiaProlactinoma + Stalk Effect
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism; ANOS1/KAL1 Gene
Functional (Weight/Stress/Exercise)Obesity, Anorexia, Excessive Exercise, Chronic Illness
Drug-InducedOpioids, Glucocorticoids, GnRH Analogues, Chemotherapy
Pituitary AdenomaMacroadenoma (Compression); Hyperprolactinaemia
CraniopharyngiomaSuprasellar; Visual Field Defects; Panhypopituitarism

7. Investigation Algorithm

```

Low Testosterone (<12 nmol/L) on 2 Occasions

│

▼

MEASURE LH & FSH

│

├── LH/FSH HIGH → PRIMARY (TESTICULAR FAILURE)

│ Karyotype (Klinefelter), Mumps History, Chemo/Radiotherapy Hx

│ Genetic Testing if Young / Family History

│

└── LH/FSH LOW / NORMAL → SECONDARY (PITUITARY / HYPOTHALAMIC)

│

├── PROLACTIN ↑ → PROLACTINOMA / DRUGS (Antipsychotics, Metoclopramide)

├── MRI PITUITARY → ADENOMA / CRANIOPHARYNGIOMA / EMPTY SELLA

├── MRI BRAIN (Hypothalamus) → KALLMANN / TUMOUR / INFILTRATION

└── FUNCTIONAL → OBESITY / STRESS / EXERCISE / DRUGS

```

8. Testosterone Replacement Therapy (TRT)

Indications

CriteriaDetails
SymptomaticLow Libido, ED, Fatigue, Low Mood, Reduced Muscle Mass
BiochemicalTotal T <12 nmol/L (350 ng/dL) on 2 Occasions + Symptoms
Fertility DesireContraindicated if Active Fertility Desired (Use hCG/rFSH Instead)

Contraindications (Absolute)

ConditionReason
Prostate CancerAndrogen-Dependent
Breast CancerRare in Men
Severe BPH (IPSS >19)Risk of Urinary Retention
Uncontrolled Heart FailureFluid Retention Risk
Polycythaemia (Hct >54%)Thrombosis Risk
Active Desire for FertilityTRT Suppresses Spermatogenesis

Relative Caution

ConditionManagement
Age >65Start Low Dose; Monitor PSA, Hct, Lipids
Sleep ApnoeaMonitor (May Worsen)
Cardiovascular DiseaseIndividualised (Recent Trials: Neutral)
PolycythaemiaMonitor Hct; Venesection if >54%

9. Testosterone Formulations

FormulationDoseFrequencyAdvantagesDisadvantages
Transdermal Gel5g (50mg) DailyFirst-Line (Physiological, Steady Levels)Skin Transfer Risk, Daily Application
IM Testosterone Undecanoate (Nebido)1000mg (4mL)q10-14 WeeksLong-Acting, No Peaks/TroughsRequires IM Injection; Delayed Onset
IM Testosterone Enantate250mgq3-4 WeeksCheapPeaks/Troughs; Injection Site Pain
Testosterone Propionate25-50mgq2-3 DaysShort-ActingFrequent Injections
Oral Testosterone Undecanoate40-80mg BIDWith MealsNo InjectionsVariable Absorption, Hepatotoxicity Risk
Subcutaneous Pellets600-1200mgEvery 4-6 MonthsLong-LastingSurgical Insertion; Expulsion Risk

10. Monitoring on TRT

ParameterBaseline3 Months6 MonthsAnnually
Total Testosterone✅✅ (Target: Mid-Normal Range)✅✅
Haematocrit (Hct)✅✅✅✅ (Target <54%)
PSA✅—✅ (If >40yr)✅ (Annual)
Liver Function✅—✅✅
Lipid Profile✅—✅✅
Bone Density (DEXA)——✅q2-3yr
Semen AnalysisIf Fertility Desired———

11. Fertility Preservation / Induction

GoalTreatmentProtocol
Fertility Preservation (Pre-TRT)Sperm CryopreservationBefore Starting TRT
Fertility Induction (Secondary Hypogonadism)hCG (Mimics LH)1500-5000 IU 2-3x/week; Add rFSH if No Response
+ Recombinant FSH75-150 IU 3x/week (If No Response to hCG Alone)
Fertility with TRTContraindicated (Suppresses Spermatogenesis)Use hCG/rFSH Instead

12. Special Populations

Late-Onset Hypogonadism (LOH / "Andropause")

FeatureDetails
DefinitionAge-Related Testosterone Decline + Symptoms
DiagnosisT <12 nmol/L + Symptoms + Exclude Other Causes
TRT BenefitModest (Libido, Mood, Body Composition); Not Universally Recommended
Risks↑ Hct, ↑ PSA, CV Events (Controversial)

Obesity-Related Hypogonadism

FeatureManagement
MechanismAromatase ↑ → Oestrogen ↑ → LH/FSH Suppression; Inflammatory Cytokines
First-LineWeight Loss (5-10%) → Restores Testosterone in 50%
TRTOnly if Persistent Symptoms After Weight Loss

Advanced Age (>75)

ConsiderationApproach
FrailtyLower Starting Dose (Gel 25mg/day)
Cognitive ImpairmentCaution (Testosterone May Improve Mood/Cognition)
Cardiovascular RiskIndividualised Decision

13. Monitoring on TRT

ParameterBaseline3 Months6 Months12 MonthsAnnually
Total Testosterone✅✅✅✅✅
Free Testosterone✅✅✅✅
Haematocrit (Hct)✅✅✅✅✅ (Target <54%)
PSA✅ (Baseline)—✅✅✅ (Annual >40yr)
Liver Function✅—✅✅✅
Lipids✅—✅✅✅
HbA1c / Glucose✅—✅✅✅
Bone Density (DEXA)If Indicated——✅q2-3yr

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Low Testosterone Diagnosis<12 nmol/L (350 ng/dL) on 2 Occasions (Morning, Fasting)
Primary vs SecondaryPrimary: LH/FSH ↑↑; Secondary: LH/FSH ↓/Normal
Klinefelter Syndrome47,XXY; Small Firm Testes; Gynaecomastia; Azoospermia; Most Common Genetic Cause
TRT ContraindicationsProstate Cancer, Breast Cancer, Severe BPH, Polycythaemia (Hct>54%), Active Fertility Desire
TRT FormulationsGel (Daily), IM Undecanoate q10-14wk, Enantate q3-4wk, Oral TU
TRT MonitoringTestosterone, Hct (Target <54%), PSA, LFTs, Lipids
Fertility on TRTContraindicated (Suppresses Spermatogenesis); Use hCG ± rFSH
hCG for Fertility1500-5000 IU 2-3x/week; Mimics LH; Add rFSH if No Response
Late-Onset HypogonadismWeight Loss First (5-10% → ↑ Testosterone); TRT if Persistent Symptoms
Klinefelter Syndrome47,XXY; Small Firm Testes; Gynaecomastia; Azoospermia; High FSH/LH
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism; ANOS1/Kal1 Gene
Monitoring on TRTTestosterone, Hct <54%, PSA, LFTs, Lipids
Testosterone Threshold<12 nmol/L (350 ng/dL) = Low

15. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Primary vs SecondaryPrimary = Testicular Failure → LH/FSH ↑↑; Secondary = Pituitary Failure → LH/FSH ↓/Normal
Total vs Free TestosteroneFree T Better if SHBG Abnormal (Obesity, Ageing, Liver Disease)
TRT & FertilityTRT Suppresses Spermatogenesis; Use hCG/rFSH if Fertility Desired
hCG vs rFSHhCG = LH Mimic; rFSH = Direct FSH Action; Combo if hCG Alone Fails
Klinefelter47,XXY; Small Firm Testes, Gynaecomastia, Azoospermia; High FSH/LH
Testosterone Threshold<12 nmol/L (350 ng/dL) = Low
PSA on TRTMonitor Annually (>40yr); Stop if PSA ↑ >1.5x Baseline or >4 ng/mL
PolycythaemiaCommon Side Effect; Hct >54% → Venesection / Dose Reduction / Stop
hCG vs TestosteronehCG Preserves Fertility; Testosterone Suppresses Spermatogenesis
Testosterone GelTransfer Risk to Partner/Children; Wash Hands, Cover Area

16. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Male Hypogonadism))

Classification

Primary (Hypergonadotropic): LH/FSH ↑↑, Testosterone ↓

Secondary (Hypogonadotropic): LH/FSH ↓/Normal, Testosterone ↓

Causes - Primary

Klinefelter (47,XXY)

Mumps Orchitis

Chemo/Radiotherapy

Genetic (SRY, NR5A1)

Causes - Secondary

Pituitary Adenoma / Prolactinoma

Kallmann Syndrome (Anosmia)

Functional (Obesity, Stress, Drugs)

Chronic Disease (Liver, Kidney, HIV)

Diagnosis

T <12 nmol/L (x2) + Symptoms

LH/FSH Differentiate Primary vs Secondary

Prolactin / MRI Pituitary if Secondary

Management

TRT if Symptomatic + T<12

Contraindicated: Prostate Ca, Fertility Desire, Hct>54%

Formulations: Gel, IM Undecanoate, Enantate, Oral TU

Contraception: hCG/rFSH (Not Testosterone)

Monitoring

T, Free T, Hct (<54%), PSA, LFT, Lipids

Bone Density (DEXA q2-3yr)

Special

Kallmann: Anosmia + HH (KAL1 Gene)

```

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Primary hypogonadism

  • Q: Klinefelter syndrome

A: 47,XXY; small testes, gynaecomastia, tall, low T, HIGH LH/FSH, infertility

A: Congenital GnRH deficiency + anosmia; KAL1 (X-linked) or autosomal

A: Undecanoate 1000mg IM q10-14wk; or gel 50mg daily; monitor haematocrit/PSA

  • Q: Klinefelter fertility

A: Azoospermia common; micro-TESE + ICSI (40-50% retrieval)

  • Q: Kallmann fertility

A: hCG + hMG or recombinant FSH/LH -> spermatogenesis (6-12mo)

  • Q: Testosterone monitoring

A: Testosterone, PSA, haematocrit, lipids q3-6mo -> 6-12mo

  • Q: Anabolic steroid use

A: Causes secondary hypogonadism (low T, low LH/FSH); reversible on cessation

  • Q: Male infertility causes

A: Primary testicular (varicocele, chemo, genetic); secondary (pituitary); idiopathic

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Testes failure: low testosterone, HIGH LH/FSH (Klinefelter, mumps, chemo, trauma) — Male hypogonadism - primary:
  1. Pituitary/hypothalamic failure: low testosterone, LOW/normal LH/FSH — Male hypogonadism - secondary:
  1. 47,XXY; small firm testes, gynaecomastia, tall, low testosterone, HIGH LH/FSH, infertility — Klinefelter syndrome:
  1. Congenital GnRH deficiency + anosmia; X-linked (KAL1) or autosomal; low LH/FSH, low testosterone — Kallmann syndrome:
  1. Low libido, ED, infertility, gynaecomastia, muscle loss, osteoporosis, fatigue, mood changes — Male hypogonadism symptoms:
  1. Testosterone undecanoate 1000mg IM q10-14wk; steady levels; monitor haematocrit — Testosterone replacement - injections:
  1. Gel 50mg daily; stable levels; skin transfer risk; monitor PSA/haematocrit — Testosterone replacement - transdermal:
  1. Testosterone, PSA, haematocrit, lipids, LFTs q3-6mo initially; then 6-12mo — Testosterone monitoring:
  1. Low testosterone -> impaired spermatogenesis; treat with hCG/hMG or IVF/ICSI if primary — Male infertility - hypogonadism:
  1. Azoospermia common; micro-TESE + ICSI possible (40-50% sperm retrieval) — Klinefelter fertility:

SBAs

  1. Primary hypogonadism (testicular failure) — 30yo man: low libido, ED, low testosterone 8, LH 20, FSH 25. Diagnosis?
  1. Klinefelter syndrome (primary hypogonadism) — Same patient: karyotype 47,XXY. Diagnosis?
  1. Secondary hypogonadism (pituitary adenoma) — 35yo man: low testosterone 6, LH 2, FSH 1, MRI: 8mm pituitary microadenoma. Diagnosis?
  1. Testosterone undecanoate 1000mg IM q10-14wk; monitor haematocrit/PSA — Klinefelter: testosterone replacement?
  1. hCG + hMG or recombinant FSH/LH -> spermatogenesis; takes 6-12mo — Kallmann syndrome: fertility treatment?

topics.title: Menopause And Hrt
topics.html:
[!info]
Menopause = Permanent Cessation of Menses for ≥12 Months (Mean Age 51 Years). Perimenopause = Transition Period (Irregular Cycles, Vasomotor Symptoms). HRT = Most Effective Treatment for Vasomotor Symptoms & Bone Protection. Individualised Risk-Benefit Assessment is Key.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Diagnose menopause and perimenopause (clinical vs biochemical)
  • [ ] Apply individualised HRT prescribing (indications, contraindications, route, duration)
  • [ ] Differentiate oestrogen-only vs combined HRT regimens
  • [ ] Counsel on risks/benefits (WHI, MWS data) and non-hormonal alternatives
  • [ ] Manage special populations (premature menopause, breast cancer survivors, cardiovascular disease)

2. Definitions & Staging

StageDefinitionTypical Age
PremenopauseRegular Cycles, Normal Ovarian Function<45 Years
PerimenopauseIrregular Cycles (Cycle Length Variability >7 Days) + Vasomotor Symptoms45-55 Years (Mean 47)
Menopause12 Consecutive Months Amenorrhoea (Retrospective Diagnosis)Mean 51 Years (Range 45-55)
Postmenopause>12 Months Post Final Menstrual Period>51 Years
Premature Menopause<40 Years<40 Years (See POI Note)
Early Menopause40-45 Years40-45 Years
Surgical MenopauseBilateral Oophorectomy (Abrupt Hormone Loss)Any Age

STRAW+10 Staging

StageCriteriaHormonal Profile
-3 to -2ReproductiveRegular Cycles, Normal FSH
-1Early PerimenopauseVariable Cycle Length (>7 Days Variability), ↑ FSH
0Late Perimenopause≥60 Days Amenorrhoea, ↑↑ FSH, ↓ Inhibin B
+1a/bEarly Postmenopause (1-3yr / 3-8yr)↑↑ FSH, ↓↓ Oestradiol
+2Late PostmenopauseStable ↑ FSH, Low Oestradiol

3. Clinical Features

Vasomotor Symptoms (VMS) — Hallmark

SymptomFrequencyDescription
Hot Flushes75-85%Sudden Heat, Flushing, Sweating, Palpitations
Night Sweats60-80%Sleep Disruption, Fatigue
Urogenital (GSM)50-70%Vaginal Dryness, Dyspareunia, Urinary Frequency/UTI
Psychological40-60%Mood Swings, Anxiety, Depression, Irritability, Brain Fog
Sleep40-60%Insomnia, Night Sweats, Fragmented Sleep
Musculoskeletal50-60%Joint Pain, Muscle Stiffness, Frozen Shoulder
Sexual30-50%Low Libido, Dyspareunia (GSM)

4. Diagnosis

MethodCriteria
Clinical≥12 Months Amenorrhoea (Age >45) = Clinical Diagnosis
Biochemical (If <45 or Uncertain)FSH >30 IU/L on 2 Occasions (4-6 Weeks Apart) + Low Oestradiol
PerimenopauseClinical (Irregular Cycles + VMS) — Biochemical Not Required
Routine FSH Not Needed >45 Years with Typical Presentation.

5. Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT) — Indications & Contraindications

Indications for HRT

IndicationStrength
Vasomotor SymptomsPrimary Indication (Gold Standard)
Genitourinary Syndrome (GSM)Local Oestrogen if Systemic Contraindicated
Bone ProtectionIf High Fracture Risk (T-Score ≤-2.5, Prior Fracture)
Premature Menopause (<45)Strong Indication (Until Age 50-51)
Quality of LifeSignificant Symptom Burden Affecting Function

Absolute Contraindications

ConditionDetails
Breast Cancer (Current/Recent)Absolute
Endometrial Cancer (Active)Absolute
VTE (Active/History Unprovoked)Absolute
Stroke / TIA / MI (Active/Recent)Absolute
Unexplained Vaginal BleedingInvestigate First
Active Liver DiseaseSevere Impairment
Porphyria Cutanea TardaRare

Relative Contraindications (Individualise)

ConditionConsideration
Age >60 / >10 Years Post-Menopause↑ VTE/Stroke Risk (WHI Data)
Controlled HypertensionMonitor BP; Transdermal Preferred
Migraine with AuraTransdermal Preferred (↓ Stroke Risk)
Gallbladder DiseaseOral ↑ Risk; Transdermal Preferred
Fibroids / EndometriosisMay Worsen; Monitor
Family History Breast CancerIndividualised; Transdermal Preferred

6. HRT Regimens — Types & Routes

Oestrogen-Only (Hysterectomised Women)

RouteDoseAdvantages
OralOestradiol 1-2mg DailySimple, Cheap
Transdermal Patch25-100µg/24h (Weekly/Biweekly)↓ VTE Risk, Steady Levels
Gel/Spray0.5-1.5mg/dayFlexible Dosing, Low VTE Risk
Implant50-100mg (6-Monthly)Long-Lasting, No Daily Adherence
Vaginal (Local Only)Pessary/Ring/CreamGSM Only (No Systemic Effect)

Combined HRT (Oestrogen + Progestogen) — Intact Uterus

RegimenOestrogenProgestogenSchedule
Sequential (Cyclical)Continuous10-14 Days/MonthWithdrawal Bleed
Continuous CombinedContinuousContinuousNo Bleed (After 1yr Post-Menopause)
Long-CycleContinuousQuarterly (3-Monthly)Reduced Bleeding Frequency

Progestogen Options (Intact Uterus)

AgentDose (Sequential)Dose (Continuous)Notes
Medroxyprogesterone Acetate (MPA)10mg Days 1-122.5-5mg DailyCheap; Androgenic
Dydrogesterone10mg Days 1-125-10mg DailyNeutral Metabolic Profile
Micronised Progesterone200mg Days 15-26100mg DailyBest Safety Profile (↓ Breast Cancer Risk)
Levonorgestrel IUS (52mg)—20µg/day (Intrauterine)Best Endometrial Protection; ↓ Systemic SE

7. Tibolone (Alternative)

FeatureDetails
MechanismSERM + Weak Oestrogenic/Androgenic/Progestogenic Activity
Dose2.5mg OD
IndicationsVasomotor Symptoms, Osteoporosis Prevention, Libido
ContraindicationsBreast Cancer, VTE, Stroke, Undiagnosed Vaginal Bleeding
AdvantageNo Withdrawal Bleed; ↑ Libido
Caution↑ Stroke Risk (>60y), ↑ Breast Cancer Risk (Long-Term)

8. Non-Hormonal Alternatives

AgentIndicationDoseEvidence
CBTVMS, Mood, Sleep6-8 SessionsStrong (NICE 1st Line)
SSRIs/SNRIs (Paroxetine, Venlafaxine, Escitalopram)VMSLow DoseModerate
Gabapentin300-900mg/dayVMS (Breast Cancer Survivors)Moderate
Clonidine50-75µg BDVMSWeak
Oxybutynin5mg BDVMS (Off-Label)Weak
Fezolinetant (NK3 Antagonist)45mg ODNew (2023); VMSStrong (New)
Phytoestrogens (Soy, Red Clover)Food/SupplementsVariableWeak/Moderate
Black CohoshHerbalVariableWeak/Inconsistent

9. HRT — Risks vs Benefits (WHI / MWS Evidence)

OutcomeCombined HRT (oestrogen + progestogen)Oestrogen-Only (Hysterectomy)
Cardiovascular↑ CHD (Small ↑ if >10yr Post-Menopause); No ↑ if <10yrNeutral / ↓ CHD if <10yr Post-Menopause
Stroke↑ Risk (All Ages)↑ Risk (All Ages)
VTE↑↑ Risk (2-3x)↑ Risk (1.5x)
Breast Cancer↑ Risk (Combined > Oestrogen-Only)↑ Risk (Long-Term >10yr)
Endometrial CancerProtected (Progestogen)↑↑ Risk (If No Progestogen)
Ovarian CancerSlight ↑Slight ↑
Colorectal Cancer↓ Risk↓ Risk
Hip Fracture↓↓ Risk↓↓ Risk
Mortality (All-Cause)Neutral / Slight ↓ (If Started <60)Neutral / Slight ↓
Key: "Window of Opportunity" — Start HRT <10yr Post-Menopause / Age <60 → Net Benefit (↓ CHD, ↓ Mortality). >10yr Post-Menopause / Age >60 → Net Harm (↑ CHD, Stroke, VTE).

10. Duration & Monitoring

AspectRecommendation
DurationShortest Effective Duration; Individualised; Annual Review
Typical Duration2-5 Years for VMS; Longer if Osteoporosis Prevention
ReviewAnnual (Symptoms, Bleeding, Breast Exam, BP, BP, Weight, Breast Exam)
StoppingGradual Taper (3-6 Months) to Avoid Rebound Symptoms
Vaginal OestrogenLong-Term Safe (Local Only) — Continue Indefinitely for GSM

Monitoring on HRT

ParameterFrequency
Blood PressureEvery Visit
Breast ExamAnnual (+ Mammogram per National Screening)
Blood PressureEach Visit
Weight / BMIAnnual
Lipids / GlucoseAnnual
LFTsIf Oral HRT / Baseline
MammogramPer National Screening Programme
Bone Density (DEXA)If Indicated (Baseline, then q2-3yr)

11. Special Populations

Premature Menopause (<40) / POI

AspectRecommendation
HRT IndicationStrong — Until Age 50-51 (Natural Menopause Age)
DoseHigher Oestrogen Dose (Estradiol 100-200µg/day Patch)
Bone ProtectionCritical (Peak Bone Mass Not Achieved)
FertilityDonor Oocyte IVF (If Desired); HRT Not Contraceptive

Breast Cancer Survivors

AspectRecommendation
Systemic HRTContraindicated (ER+ Tumours)
Vaginal OestrogenLow-Dose (Pessary/Ring/Cream) — Safe (Minimal Absorption)
Non-HormonalCBT, SSRIs, Gabapentin, Fezolinetant, Vaginal Moisturisers/Lubricants

Cardiovascular Disease

ScenarioRecommendation
Established CVDTransdermal Oestrogen Preferred; Avoid Oral
Stroke/TIAAvoid Systemic HRT; Local Vaginal Only
HypertensionControl BP First; Transdermal Preferred

12. Non-Hormonal Management of VMS

AgentDoseEvidence
CBT6-8 SessionsStrong (NICE 1st Line)
SSRIs/SNRIs (Paroxetine 7.5-25mg, Venlafaxine 37.5-75mg)VMS Reduction 50-60%
Fezolinetant (NK3 Antagonist)45mg ODNew (2023); Strong Evidence
Gabapentin300-900mg/dayModerate
Clonidine50-75µg BDWeak
Oxybutynin5mg BDWeak (Off-Label)
Phytoestrogens (Soy, Red Clover)50-100mg IsoflavonesWeak/Moderate
Black CohoshVariableInconsistent Evidence

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Menopause Definition12 Months Amenorrhoea (Mean Age 51)
Perimenopause DiagnosisClinical (Irregular Cycles + VMS); FSH Not Routine
HRT IndicationVMS (1st Line), GSM (Local Oestrogen), Bone (If High Risk)
HRT ContraindicationsBreast Ca, VTE, Stroke, MI, Unexplained Bleeding, Active Liver Disease
RouteTransdermal → ↓ VTE, ↓ Stroke, ↓ Gallbladder
ProgestogenMicronised Progesterone (Best Breast Safety); LNG-IUS (Best Endometrial Protection)
Tibolone2.5mg OD; ↑ Libido; No Withdrawal Bleed; ↑ Stroke Risk >60
WHI Key Finding<10yr Post-Menopause / Age <60 → Net Benefit; >10yr / >60 → Net Harm
Window of OpportunityStart <10yr Post-Menopause / Age <60 for CVD Benefit
Breast Cancer RiskCombined HRT > Oestrogen-Only > None; Duration-Dependent
VTE RiskOral > Transdermal; Highest 1st Year
Premature MenopauseHRT Until Age 50-51 (Mandatory for Bone/Heart/Brain)
Breast Cancer SurvivorsSystemic HRT Contraindicated; Vaginal Oestrogen Safe
Tibolone2.5mg OD; No Withdrawal Bleed; ↑ Stroke Risk >60
Non-Hormonal 1st LineCBT (NICE); SSRIs/SNRIs/Gabapentin/Fezolinetant
Vaginal OestrogenSafe in Breast Cancer Survivors; Long-Term Use OK

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HRT vs ERTHRT = Oestrogen + Progestogen (Intact Uterus); ERT = Oestrogen Only (Hysterectomy)
Sequential vs ContinuousSequential = Cyclical Progestogen (Withdrawal Bleed); Continuous = No Bleed (Post-Menopause >1yr)
Transdermal vs OralTransdermal = ↓ VTE, ↓ Stroke, ↓ Gallbladder, ↓ Triglycerides
Tibolone vs HRTTibolone = SERM + Weak Oestrogen/Androgen/Progestogen; No Bleed; ↑ Stroke >60
Window of Opportunity<10yr Post-Menopause / Age <60 → Net CVD Benefit; >10yr / >60 → Net Harm
Vaginal Oestrogen in Breast CancerSafe (Low Systemic Absorption); Systemic HRT Contraindicated
Progestogen TypesMicronised Progesterone = Best Breast Safety; LNG-IUS = Best Endometrial Protection
DurationShortest Effective; Annual Review; Taper Gradually (3-6mo)
Local OestrogenSafe Long-Term; No Progestogen Needed; First-Line for GSM

15. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Menopause & HRT))

Definitions

Menopause: 12mo Amenorrhoea (Mean 51)

Perimenopause: Irregular Cycles + VMS

Premature: <40y (POI)

Surgical: Bilateral Oophorectomy

Diagnosis

Clinical: 12mo Amenorrhoea (>45y)

Biochemical: FSH>30 x2 (if <45y)

VMS

Hot Flushes (75-85%)

Night Sweats, Sleep Disturbance

GSM: Vaginal Dryness, Dyspareunia

HRT Indications

VMS (1st Line), GSM, Bone (High Risk), Premature Menopause

Contraindications

Absolute: Breast Ca, VTE, Stroke, MI, Bleeding, Liver

Relative: Age>60, HTN, Migraine+Aura, Gallbladder, Family Hx Breast Ca

Routes

Transdermal (Best Safety): Patch, Gel, Spray

Oral: Simple, ↑ VTE/Stroke

Vaginal: Local Only (GSM)

Progestogens

Micronised Progesterone (Best Breast)

Dydrogesterone, LNG-IUS (Best Endometrial Protection)

Risks/Benefits

<10yr Post-Menopause: Net Benefit (↓CHD, ↓Fracture, ↓Mortality)

>10yr: Net Harm (↑VTE, ↑Stroke, ↑Breast Ca)

Window of Opportunity: <10yr Post-Menopause / Age <60

Non-Hormonal

CBT (1st Line NICE), SSRIs, Fezolinetant, Gabapentin, Clonidine

Vaginal Oestrogen: Safe in Breast Ca Survivors

Special

POI: HRT Until Age 50-51 (Mandatory)

Breast Ca Survivors: No Systemic HRT; Vaginal Oestrogen OK

CVD: Transdermal Preferred; Avoid Oral

```

TopicKey Point

VTE RiskOral > Transdermal; Highest 1st Year
Premature MenopauseHRT Until Age 50-51 (Mandatory for Bone/Heart/Brain)
Breast Cancer SurvivorsSystemic HRT Contraindicated; Vaginal Oestrogen Safe
Tibolone2.5mg OD; No Bleed; ↑ Libido; ↑ Stroke Risk >60
Non-Hormonal 1st LineCBT (NICE); SSRIs/SNRIs/Fezolinetant/Gabapentin

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

21. Answer Key with Explanations

topics.title: Pcos
topics.html:
[!info]
PCOS = Most Common Endocrine Disorder in Women of Reproductive Age (10-15%). Rotterdam Criteria (2/3): Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism + Polycystic Ovaries. Insulin Resistance = Core Pathophysiology. Long-term Risks: T2DM, CVD, Endometrial Cancer, Infertility.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply Rotterdam diagnostic criteria (2/3) and phenotypic classification
  • [ ] Differentiate PCOS from other hyperandrogenic states
  • [ ] Assess metabolic risk and screen for long-term complications
  • [ ] Apply management algorithms (lifestyle, COCP, metformin, letrozole, IVF)
  • [ ] Counsel on long-term health risks (T2DM, CVD, endometrial cancer)

2. Diagnostic Criteria — Rotterdam (2/3 Required)

CriterionDefinition
1. Oligo/Anovulation<8 Cycles/Year or Cycle Length >35 Days
2. HyperandrogenismClinical (Hirsutism mFG >8, Acne, Alopecia) OR Biochemical (Total Testosterone >2.5-3 nmol/L, FAI >5)
3. Polycystic Ovarian Morphology (PCOM)≥20 Follicles (2-9mm) Per Ovary OR Ovarian Volume >10 mL (US)

Diagnosis = 2 of 3 Criteria (After Excluding Other Aetiologies)

Phenotypic Classification (4 Phenotypes)

PhenotypeCriteriaMetabolic Risk
A (Classic/Full)Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCOMHighest
B (Classic Non-PCO)Oligo/Anovulation + HyperandrogenismHigh
C (Ovulatory PCOS)Hyperandrogenism + PCOMModerate
D (Non-Hyperandrogenic)Oligo/Anovulation + PCOMLowest

3. Differential Diagnosis — Exclusion of Mimics

ConditionKey Differentiators
NCCAH (21-OH Deficiency)↑ 17-OHP (Basal >200, Stim >1000 ng/dL); Genetic Testing (CYP21A2)
Cushing SyndromeCortisol Excess (Dex Suppression Test); Striae, Moon Face, HTN
Androgen-Secreting TumourRapid Onset, Very High Testosterone (>5 nmol/L); DHEAS ↑; Imaging
Thyroid DysfunctionTSH Abnormal; Prolactin Normal
HyperprolactinaemiaProlactin ↑; Galactorrhoea; MRI Pituitary
Drug-InducedAnabolic Steroids, Danazol, Valproate, CYP21A2 Inhibitors
AcromegalyGH/IGF-1 ↑; OGTT GH Not Suppressed
Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia17-OHP ↑; 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency

4. Clinical Features & Phenotypes

FeatureFrequencyDetails
Oligomenorrhoea/Amenorrhoea70-80%<8 Cycles/Year or >35 Day Cycles
Hirsutism (mFG >8)60-70%Ferriman-Gallwey Score; Chin, Upper Lip, Chest, Abdomen, Thighs
Acne40-50%Jawline, Jaw, Back
Alopecia10-20%Female Pattern (Ludwig)
Acanthosis Nigricans30-40%Neck, Axillae, Groins (Insulin Resistance Marker)
Obesity40-80% (Central)BMI >30 in 40-50%; Waist >88cm
Infertility70-80%Anovulation; ↑ Miscarriage Risk
MetabolicInsulin Resistance (70-80%); Dyslipidaemia; ↑ T2DM Risk

5. Diagnostic Algorithm

```

Clinical Suspicion (Oligomenorrhoea + Hyperandrogenism)

│

▼

EXCLUDE: Pregnancy, Thyroid, Prolactin, Cushing, NCCAH, Androgen-Secreting Tumour

│

▼

APPLY ROTTERDAM CRITERIA (2/3)

│

├── Oligo/Anovulation (<8 cycles/yr or >35d cycle)

├── Hyperandrogenism (Clinical mFG>8 OR Biochemical T >2.5-3 nmol/L)

└── PCOM on US (≥20 Follicles 2-9mm OR Volume >10mL)

│

└── 2/3 CRITERIA MET → DIAGNOSE PCOS

│

▼

PHENOTYPING (A/B/C/D) → Guides Metabolic Risk & Management

```

6. Investigations

Hormonal Profile

TestTimingExpected in PCOS
Total TestosteroneDay 2-4 (or Random)>2.5-3 nmol/L (or FAI >5)
Free Androgen Index (FAI)(Total T × 100) / SHBG>5
SHBGLow (Insulin ↓ SHBG Synthesis)
LH/FSHDay 2-4LH:FSH >2:1 (Classic but Not Diagnostic)
17-OHPDay 2-4Exclude NCCAH (Basal <200 ng/dL; Stim >1000 ng/dL)
TSH, ProlactinExclude Thyroid / Prolactinoma
17-OHP (Basal)<200 ng/dL (Exclude NCCAH)
DHEASMildly ↑ OftenExclude Adrenal Tumour if Markedly ↑

Metabolic Workup

TestTarget / Threshold
Fasting Glucose / HbA1c<5.6 mmol/L / <5.7%
OGTT (75g)120min <7.8 mmol/L (Normal); 7.8-11.0 = IGT; ≥11.1 = DM
Fasting Insulin / HOMA-IRHOMA-IR >2.5 = Insulin Resistance
Lipid ProfileTG >1.7, HDL <1.3 (Women)
Liver FunctionNAFLD Common (ALT ↑)

Pelvic Ultrasound

FindingPCOM Criteria
Follicle Count≥20 Follicles (2-9mm) Per Ovary
Ovarian Volume>10 mL
StromaIncreased Echogenicity

7. Long-Term Health Risks

ComplicationRelative RiskScreening
Type 2 Diabetes4-7xOGTT q1-3yr (Age >30 / BMI >25 / Prior GDM)
Cardiovascular Disease2-3xBP, Lipids, HbA1c Annually
Endometrial Cancer3-4x (Unopposed Oestrogen)Ultrasound if Abnormal Bleeding; Progestogen Challenge
Obstructive Sleep Apnoea5-10xSTOP-BANG; Sleep Study if Symptomatic
Mood Disorders2-3x (Depression/Anxiety)PHQ-9/GAD-7 Screening
NAFLD2-3xLFTs, Ultrasound Liver

8. Management — Phenotype-Guided

Universal First-Line: Lifestyle Modification

TargetIntervention
Weight Loss5-10% Body Weight → Restores Ovulation in 50-60%
DietLow GI, High Fibre, Moderate Protein, Mediterranean
Exercise150 min/week Moderate + Resistance 2x/week
BehaviouralCBT, Self-Monitoring, Stress Management

Pharmacotherapy — By Goal

GoalFirst-LineSecond-LineThird-Line
Menstrual RegulationCOCP (Low-Dose, EE 20-30µg + Anti-Androgen Progestogen)Cyclical Progestogen (MPA 10mg Days 1-12)—
Hirsutism/AcneCOCP (Cyproterone/Drospirenone)Anti-Androgens (Spironolactone 50-100mg, Finasteride, Flutamide)Laser/Electrolysis
Ovulation InductionLetrozole 2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (1st Line)Clomifene 50-150mg Days 2-6Gonadotrophins (rFSH/hMG) → IVF
Metabolic/IRMetformin 500mg BD → 1g BD (If BMI ≥25 / IGT)Pioglitazone (If Metformin Intolerant)GLP-1 RA (Semaglutide/Tirzepatide) if Obese
Weight Loss (BMI ≥25)Lifestyle 5-10% Weight LossGLP-1 RA (Semaglutide 2.4mg / Tirzepatide 15mg)Bariatric Surgery (BMI ≥35/40)

9. Ovulation Induction — Algorithm

```

Anovulatory PCOS Desiring Pregnancy

│

▼

PRE-CONCEPTION: Folic Acid 5mg/day; Rubella/Varicella Immune; TSH/TSH/HbA1c

│

└── LIFESTYLE OPTIMISATION (3-6 Months)

│

├────────────────┬─────────────────────┐

│ │ │

LETROZOLE 2.5-5mg CLOMIFENE 50-150mg GONADOTROPHINS

Days 2-6 (1st Line) Days 2-6 (If Letrozole Fail) rFSH/hMG (If Oral Fail)

(↑ Live Birth, ↓ (Traditional 1st Line) (Monitor: US + E2)

Multiples, ↓ OHSS)

│ │ │

└────────────────┴──────────────────────┘

│

▼

MONITORING: US Follicular Tracking (Day 10-12)

TRIGGER: hCG 5000-10000 IU (When Lead Follicle ≥18mm)

TIMING: IUI 36h Post-hCG / Timed Intercourse

LUTEAL SUPPORT: Progesterone (If IUI/IVF)

```

10. Long-Term Health Surveillance

ComplicationScreening IntervalThreshold for Action
Type 2 DiabetesOGTT q1-3yr (Age >30 / BMI >25 / Prior GDM)HbA1c ≥5.7% → Preventive Metformin
CardiovascularBP, Lipids Annually; Carotid IMT if High RiskLDL >3.0 → Statin
Endometrial CancerUltrasound if Abnormal Bleeding; Progestogen Challenge q1-2yr if AmenorrhoeicEndometrial Thickness >5mm → Biopsy
Sleep ApnoeaSTOP-BANG Questionnaire; Sleep Study if High RiskCPAP if AHI >15
Mood DisordersPHQ-9/GAD-7 AnnuallyRefer if Positive
Bone HealthDEXA if Amenorrhoeic >1yr / Steroid UseT-score ≤-2.5 → Bisphosphonate

11. Special Situations

PCOS in Adolescence

ChallengeApproach
Diagnosis DelayWait 2 Years Post-Menarche; PCOM Common in Normal Teens
DiagnosisOligo/Anovulation ≥2 Years Post-Menarche + Hyperandrogenism
ManagementCOCP (1st Line); Metformin if IGT; Lifestyle Emphasis

Pregnancy in PCOS

RiskManagement
Miscarriage1.5-2x HigherProgestogen Support (If Prior Loss)
GDM2-3x RiskEarly OGTT (16-18wk); Metformin if IGT
Pre-eclampsia2-3x RiskAspirin 75-150mg OD from 12w (If >1 Risk Factor)
Preterm Birth↑ RiskCervical Length Monitoring

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point

Phenotype A (Full)Highest Metabolic Risk
Hirsutism mFG>8 = Significant

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
PCOS vs NCCAHNCCAH: 17-OHP >200 (Basal) / >1000 (Stim); Genetic CYP21A2
PCOS vs CushingCushing: Striae, Moon Face, Hypertension, Easy Bruising; Dex Suppression Test
PCOS vs Androgen-Secreting TumourTumour: Rapid Onset, Very High Testosterone (>5 nmol/L), DHEAS ↑↑
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole: ↑ Live Birth, ↓ Multiples, ↓ OHSS; Preferred 1st Line
Metformin in PCOSBMI ≥25 + IGT → Metformin; Not for All PCOS
Ovulation InductionLetrozole 1st Line; Clomifene 2nd; Gonadotrophins 3rd
Endometrial Cancer3-4x Risk → Progestogen Challenge q1-2yr if Oligomenorrhoea
Insulin ResistanceHOMA-IR >2.5; Central Obesity + Acanthosis Nigricans
Acanthosis NigricansInsulin Resistance Marker; Neck, Axillae, Groins

14. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((PCOS))

Diagnosis

Differential

Cushing: Dex Supp; Striae, Moon Face

Tumour: Rapid Virilisation, T >5nmol/L

Phenotypes

A: Oligo + HA + PCOM (Highest Risk)

B: Oligo + HA (No PCOM)

C: HA + PCOM (Ovulatory)

D: Oligo + PCOM (Non-HA)

Management

Lifestyle: 5-10% Wt Loss → 50-60% Ovulation

COCP: 1st Line (Cycle Reg, Hirsutism)

Metformin: BMI≥25/IGT; ↓IR, ↓Androgen

Ovulation: Letrozole (1st) > Clomifene (2nd) > Gonadotrophins

Weight Loss: GLP-1 RA (Sema/Tirzepatide) / Bariatric

Complications

T2DM (4-7x), CVD (2-3x), Endo Ca (3-4x), OSA (5-10x)

Infertility (70-80%)

Mood Disorders (2-3x)

Monitoring

OGTT q1-3yr; Lipids/BP Annual; Endo US if Bleed; DEXA if Amenorrhoeic

```

17. MCQs (10)

  1. PCOS diagnostic criteria (Rotterdam):

B. Absent

C. Only in obese

D. Only in lean

E. Diagnostic criterion

A. Letrozole 1st line; clomiphene 2nd; metformin adjunct; gonadotrophins/IVF if resistant

B. Clomiphene 1st line

C. Metformin alone

D. IVF only

E. Surgery only

  1. PCOS management - hirsutism:

A. COCP 1st line (anti-androgenic progestin); spironolactone/finasteride adjunct; laser

C. Spironolactone alone

D. Laser only

E. No treatment

  1. PCOS metabolic risk:

B. No increased risk

C. Only T2DM

D. Only CVD

E. Only endometrial cancer

  1. PCOS in adolescence:

A. Diagnosis cautious; anovulation + hyperandrogenism (US unreliable); treat symptoms

B. Same as adults

C. Not diagnosed

  1. PCOS and endometrial cancer:

A. Unopposed oestrogen -> endometrial hyperplasia -> cancer; protect with progestin/COCP/MPA

C. Only if obese

D. Only if diabetic

E. Only if nulliparous

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25yo woman: oligomenorrhoea, hirsutism, acne, T=3.5, LH:FSH 3:1, PCO on US. Diagnosis?

D. Laser only

E. No treatment

A. Lifestyle (diet/exercise) 5-10% weight loss; metformin if pre-diabetic

B. Metformin alone

C. Bariatric surgery

D. COCP

E. No intervention

  1. PCOS with endometrial hyperplasia. Management?

A. Progestin (MPA/COCP) to counteract unopposed oestrogen; hysteroscopy if atypical

C. Oestrogen

D. Clomiphene
